:::writing{variant="document" id="90742"}
# OMI Object Model
## The Omi `(o---o)` Model of Bounded Agreement

```text
o---o
omi---imo
```

OMI is the **Omicron Object Model**: a bounded object doctrine for turning open-world declarations into validated, replayable, inspectable state.

It begins with the simplest possible enclosure:

```text
omi---imo
```

or symbolically:

```text
o---o
```

The two sides are not merely decorative.

They are the bookends of a relation.

The hyphen field between them is not empty space.

It is the join field.

It is the bus.

It is the gravity bridge.

It is the meta-compiler lane.

It is where a declaration becomes an inspectable object.

The zero-point of the model is:

```text
Ω₀ = 0! = omi---imo
```

From this zero-point, OMI defines a full pipeline:

```text
validate -> resolve -> record -> materialize -> project -> inspect
```

The forbidden shortcut is:

```text
render -> believe
```

OMI exists to prevent that shortcut.

A thing may render in a browser, appear in a DOM node, show up as a card, exist as a QR code, or travel through gossip. None of those make it accepted.

The reader may recognize.
The resolver may promote.
Only validation and receipt may accept.

---

# 1. The Core Promise

OMI lets people save, share, verify, and move objects without losing history or context.

It is not only a protocol.

It is not only a notation.

It is not only a document model.

It is a way to say:

```text
This object has a boundary.
This object has a transition.
This object has a receipt.
This object can be replayed.
This object can be projected.
This object can be inspected.
```

OMI is built for open-world systems where no single platform owns truth.

A normal platform says:

```text
The app owns the object.
The database owns the state.
The server decides the current version.
The UI decides what the user sees.
```

OMI says:

```text
The frame must validate.
The transition must resolve.
The receipt must record.
The projection must remain downstream.
The user must be able to inspect.
```

The difference is fundamental.

In OMI, projection is not authority.

```text
carrier != authority
projection != validation
prefix != identity
gossip != authority
render != believe
```

---

# 2. The Object Boundary

The readable mnemonic boundary is:

```text
omi---imo
```

The symbolic lower boundary uses Omicron anchors:

```text
ο = 0x03BF = Greek small omicron
Ο = 0x039F = Greek capital Omicron
```

These are the structural anchors of the frame.

They are not typography.

They are not styling.

They are not reader decoration.

They define the lower object boundary.

The mnemonic reading is:

```text
omi = entry / low gate / declaration side
imo = exit / high gate / closure side
--- = join field / bridge / compiler lane
```

The deeper reading is:

```text
omi enters.
--- joins.
imo closes.
```

The hyphen is the operation.

In ordinary language, a hyphen joins terms:

```text
open-world
peer-to-peer
meta-compiler
```

In OMI, the hyphen joins address terms into a bounded instruction word:

```text
omi-ip4-gravity-ip6-imo
```

This does not mean every string containing `omi` is an OMI object.

A string containing `omi` may be plain text.

An `omi-*` token may be a readable alias.

An `id="omi-*"` attribute may be a DOM projection carrier.

A `data-omi` attribute may be a CSSOM/JSDOM bridge.

None of these are accepted identity by themselves.

The production rule is:

```text
omi-* / imo-* = alias or template until resolved.
Only accepted frames become OMI objects.
```

---

# 3. The Authority Pipeline

The OMI authority pipeline is:

```text
reader -> resolver -> validation -> receipt -> materializer -> projection -> inspection
```

Each layer has a different job.

## Reader

The reader recognizes notation.

It may see:

```text
omi---imo
omi-<tail>
imo-<tail>
8-segment frame candidate
DOM carrier
CSSOM carrier
plain text
```

But recognition is not acceptance.

```text
reader may recognize
```

## Resolver

The resolver promotes a recognized form into a candidate.

It may say:

```text
This looks like an OMI frame candidate.
This looks like a projection alias.
This looks like a received edit.
This looks like a recoverable gossip candidate.
```

But promotion is not acceptance.

```text
resolver may promote
```

## Validation

Validation checks structure.

The canonical frame must satisfy:

```text
Q_frame(S) = 0
```

If the frame fails, it rejects.

## Resolution

A valid frame yields a truth row:

```text
(LL, NN, MM)
```

The resolver must prove that `MM` is reachable from `NN` under the accepted Delta/Fano path.

## Receipt

A successful transition writes a receipt.

The receipt is the acceptance boundary.

```text
Only validation and receipt may accept.
```

## Materializer

The materializer reads accepted envelopes and applies registered rule blocks.

The materializer may know about DOM, CSSOM, JSDOM, OMI object patches, snub geometry, projection control, and portal surfaces.

The gate does not.

## Projection

Projection displays accepted state.

Projection may use:

```text
DOM
CSSOM
JSDOM
SVG
Canvas
JSON Canvas
QR
A-Frame
portal cards
document views
```

Projection must never become authority.

## Inspection

Inspection explains what happened.

It shows:

```text
receipts
replay
history
source
context
conflicts
gossip candidates
raw frame
```

---

# 4. The 128-Bit Frame

An OMI frame is an 8-segment structure:

```text
S0-S1-S2-S3-S4-S5-S6-S7
```

Each segment is 16 bits.

Together:

```text
8 x 16 = 128 bits
```

A canonical frame has this shape:

```text
S0 = 0xLL00
S1 = 0x03BF
S2 = 0xNNNN
S3 = 0x2BLL
S4 = 0x2FLL
S5 = 0xMMMM
S6 = 0x039F
S7 = 0xLLFF
```

The frame contains:

```text
LL = lens / scope / ket selector
NN = antecedent
MM = consequent
```

The Omicron anchors are:

```text
S1 = 0x03BF = ο
S6 = 0x039F = Ο
```

The repeated `LL` positions create a variance lock:

```text
S0, S3, S4, S7
```

The transition pair is:

```text
S2 = NN
S5 = MM
```

So the frame anatomy is:

```text
boundaries -> S1, S6
modulator -> S0, S3, S4, S7
transition -> S2, S5
```

A frame is valid only if:

```text
Q_frame(S) = E_var + E_const = 0
```

The frame does not pass because it looks right.

It passes because the structural equation closes.

---

# 5. The Truth Row

A valid frame yields exactly one truth row:

```text
(LL, NN, MM)
```

The truth row is the minimal agreement face.

It can be read as:

```text
lens / antecedent / consequent
```

or:

```text
scope / beginning / resolution
```

or:

```text
declaration / definition / receipt
```

The important relation is:

```text
NN -> MM
```

`NN` is the antecedent state.

`MM` is the consequent state.

`LL` does not prove the transition.

`LL` scopes where the accepted transition lands.

The transition is accepted only if the Delta/Fano resolver can reach `MM` from `NN` within the bounded window.

Short form:

```text
NN begins.
MM resolves.
LL scopes.
```

---

# 6. The Delta Law

The Delta Law is the atomic transition engine.

A simple form is:

```text
Δ_C(x) = rotl(x,1) xor rotl(x,3) xor rotr(x,2) xor C
```

Bounded to 16 bits:

```text
Δ16_C(x) = Δ_C(x) & 0xFFFF
```

The Delta Law uses bitwise operations:

```text
rotation
xor
masking
constant modulation
```

This keeps the lower substrate integer-bound, deterministic, and replayable.

The resolver attempts:

```text
NN -> Δ(NN) -> Δ²(NN) -> ... -> MM
```

within a bounded Fano window.

If `MM` is reached, the transition is accepted.

If not, the frame rejects.

The short doctrine:

```text
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt records.
```

---

# 7. Fano Resolution

The Fano layer gives OMI a bounded finite incidence selector.

A Fano plane has:

```text
7 points
7 lines
3 points per line
```

OMI reads this as a finite triangular incidence field.

The truth row:

```text
(LL, NN, MM)
```

is triangular.

The Fano layer asks:

```text
Which relation closes?
Which triangle resolves?
Which line carries the accepted path?
```

This is sometimes called the “Fano lottery,” but it is not random.

It is finite incidence selection.

The Fano layer prevents the system from searching an unbounded space.

It gives a bounded window for resolution.

Short form:

```text
Fano selects the triangle.
Delta proves the path.
Receipt remembers the result.
```

---

# 8. LL as Ket

The most precise role of `LL` is:

```text
LL is the ket-like scope selector of the frame.
```

In Dirac notation, a ket such as:

```text
|ψ⟩
```

represents a state vector.

OMI does not use quantum mechanics literally, but the analogy is useful.

The truth row may be read as:

```text
(|LL⟩, NN, MM)
```

or:

```text
scope ket + antecedent + consequent
```

`LL` is not the object body.

`LL` is not the full meaning.

`LL` is not geometry.

`LL` is the scoped selector applied to the accepted transition.

It tells the materializer where the proven transition lands.

Short form:

```text
LL scopes.
NN begins.
MM resolves.
```

---

# 9. The LL Nibble Split

`LL` is a byte.

It can be split into two nibbles:

```text
LL = 0xAB
```

Where:

```text
A = high nibble
B = low nibble
```

OMI reads this as:

```text
high nibble = context / lens family / carry-forward scope
low nibble = execution slot selector
```

The POSIX gate computes:

```sh
slot_index=$((ll & 0x0f))
rule_slot=$((0xe0 + slot_index))
context=$(((ll >> 4) & 0x0f))
```

This is pure bitwise routing.

No semantic branch is required.

The gate does not ask:

```text
Is this a snub operation?
Is this a DOM patch?
Is this a viewport translation?
Is this a rotation?
```

The gate only extracts:

```text
context
slot_index
rule_slot
```

The materializer registry owns the meaning.

---

# 10. The Folded 0xE_ Execution Manifold

The completed materializer band is:

```text
0xE0-0xEF
```

It has 16 slots.

The low nibble of `LL` selects one of them:

```text
rule_slot = 0xE0 + (LL & 0x0F)
```

This replaces the earlier `LL mod 12` model.

`LL mod 12` described the twelve inner packed materializer spheres.

The final substrate uses the complete low nibble to saturate the whole hexadecimal band.

The mapping is:

```text
0x0 -> 0xE0
0x1 -> 0xE1
0x2 -> 0xE2
0x3 -> 0xE3
0x4 -> 0xE4
0x5 -> 0xE5
0x6 -> 0xE6
0x7 -> 0xE7
0x8 -> 0xE8
0x9 -> 0xE9
0xA -> 0xEA
0xB -> 0xEB
0xC -> 0xEC
0xD -> 0xED
0xE -> 0xEE
0xF -> 0xEF
```

The first twelve slots are:

```text
0xE0-0xEB = inner materializers
```

The last four slots are:

```text
0xEC-0xEF = outer controller vertices
```

Together:

```text
0xE0-0xEF = one accepted execution manifold
```

---

# 11. The First Twelve: Inner Materializers

The first twelve slots pack the object.

```text
0xE0-0xEB pack the object.
```

They correspond to the inner materializer field.

A registry may define them as:

```text
0xE0 = page-frame-root
0xE1 = DOM import/export
0xE2 = CSSOM import/export
0xE3 = JSDOM normalization
0xE4 = OMI object patch
0xE5 = edge traversal
0xE6 = vertex traversal
0xE7 = snub bridge
0xE8 = stellated octahedron mirror
0xE9 = Fano triangle selection
0xEA = 59/60/61 face band
0xEB = receipt projection/export
```

These are not shell branches.

They are accepted rule addresses.

The shell does not know what any of these mean.

The registry knows.

---

# 12. The Last Four: Platonic Controller Vertices

The final four slots hold the object.

```text
0xEC-0xEF hold the object.
```

They are not a separate UI command channel.

They are the final four addresses of the same accepted execution manifold.

They form the outer controller surface:

```text
0xEC = translation / viewport / focus
0xED = scale / recursion depth
0xEE = rotation / spin / orientation
0xEF = boundary / opacity / clipping
```

Geometrically, they can be read as Platonic controller vertices folded into the execution band.

The first twelve are the Archimedean/Catalan materializer field.

The final four are the Platonic controller vertices of the simplex closure.

Short form:

```text
The first twelve pack.
The last four hold.
```

The controller slots must still pass through:

```text
Q_frame validation
Delta/Fano resolution
receipt writing
```

No controller exists outside receipt.

---

# 13. No Separate Controller Channel

OMI rejects separate command channels for lens control.

A separate channel would create double authority.

It would allow a viewport, rotation, scale, or clipping rule to move outside the accepted receipt path.

That would reintroduce the forbidden shortcut:

```text
render -> believe
```

The rule is:

```text
No controller outside receipt.
```

A local browser may always pan, zoom, scroll, or rotate something as local UI behavior.

But a conforming OMI projection must not treat that local behavior as accepted state.

The stricter rule is:

```text
The viewport is receipt-bound when controlled through 0xEC-0xEF.
A conforming projection must not treat out-of-band camera or lens changes as accepted state.
```

This preserves the difference between:

```text
local display behavior
```

and:

```text
accepted projection state
```

---

# 14. The Gate and the Materializer

The lower gate is policy-blind.

The materializer owns semantics.

## Gate

The gate does:

```text
parse frame
validate Q_frame(S) = 0
extract (LL, NN, MM)
resolve NN -> MM
derive context
derive slot_index
derive rule_slot
write receipt
emit envelope
```

The gate does not:

```text
know snub geometry
know Platonic solids
know Archimedean/Catalan duals
know DOM policy
know CSSOM policy
know JSDOM policy
know portal UX
know A-Frame policy
know application meaning
```

## Materializer

The materializer does:

```text
read accepted envelope
lookup rule_slot
apply registered implementation
respect context
respect steps
respect chirality
project result
explain result
```

The split is:

```text
start.sh is the gate.
The registry is the materializer.
```

The final substrate doctrine:

```text
LL selects.
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt authorizes.
Rule block materializes.
Projection displays.
```

---

# 15. Execution Envelope

An accepted frame emits an execution envelope.

Example:

```json
{
 "accepted": true,
 "LL": "0x3E",
 "context": "0x3",
 "slot_index": "0xE",
 "rule_slot": "0xEE",
 "rule_band": "0xE0-0xEF",
 "NN": "0x7c00",
 "MM": "0x1434",
 "steps": 3,
 "chirality": "right",
 "receipt": "..."
}
```

The materializer reads:

```text
context = 0x3
rule_slot = 0xEE
steps = 3
chirality = right
receipt = accepted
```

The registry may say:

```text
0xEE = rotation-spin
```

Then the materializer applies the registered operation.

The shell never knows that `0xEE` means rotation.

It only knows:

```text
the frame was valid
the transition resolved
the receipt was written
the rule address was emitted
```

---

# 16. Steps and Chirality

The execution envelope gives the materializer four orthogonal parameters:

```text
context
rule_slot
steps
chirality
```

Their roles are:

```text
context = lens family / carry-forward scope
rule_slot = operation address
steps = magnitude / phase / recursion depth
chirality = handed direction
```

Example:

```text
rule_slot = 0xEE
context = 0x3
steps = 3
chirality = right
```

The materializer may interpret this as:

```text
rotate the projection under context 0x3 by three accepted steps in the right-handed direction
```

But this is materializer policy.

It is not gate policy.

---

# 17. Carry-Forward Scope

The high nibble of `LL` carries forward context.

```text
context = (LL >> 4) & 0x0F
```

If the active scope is represented as a 4-bit value, then the next scope may be derived by XOR:

```text
next_scope = current_scope xor context
```

or:

```text
scope' = scope ⊕ high_nibble(LL)
```

This gives OMI a bounded carry-forward model.

The carry-forward scope is:

```text
bitwise
bounded
replayable
low-level
compatible with the frame
```

The shell may emit context.

The materializer may apply scope.

The receipt records what happened.

---

# 18. The Quadratic Object Model

The geometric object model is governed by:

```text
Q_xy(x,y) = 60x² + 16xy + 4y²
```

This is not only a formula.

It is a structural lifecycle.

## 4y²: Platonic Seed

The low term is the atomic seed.

```text
4y² = Platonic seed / local declaration / vertex body
```

It represents the first stable body of declaration.

## 16xy: Archimedean/Catalan Bridge

The middle term is the hyphen field.

```text
16xy = snub bridge / chiral transformation / relation bus
```

This is where the object is joined, twisted, dualized, and re-faced.

This is where snub truncation lives.

## 60x²: 4D Regular Orbit

The high term is the cosmic orientation field.

```text
60x² = face traversal / sexagesimal sweep / 4D regular closure
```

This is where the accepted object gains broader orientation and replayable consistency.

Short form:

```text
4y² seeds.
16xy snubs.
60x² sweeps.
```

---

# 19. Snub Truncation as the OMI Operation

OMI does not overwrite objects.

OMI snubs them.

Snub truncation does three things:

```text
cut
twist
re-face
```

In OMI:

```text
cut = isolate unresolved boundary
twist = introduce chirality through the bridge
re-face = create triangular agreement surfaces
record = preserve the transformation path
project = expose the accepted state
```

The snub operation is better than permutation or circular inversion because it transforms the body while preserving its relation to the parent.

Permutation only reorders.

Circular inversion only reverses inside and outside.

Snubbing:

```text
cuts excess
twists relation
creates handedness
adds triangular incidence
preserves parent recoverability
```

This is the geometry of the hyphen.

```text
omi enters.
--- snubs.
imo closes.
```

---

# 20. Snub Cube and Snub Dodecahedron

The snub cube and snub dodecahedron are the two clearest witnesses of OMI chirality.

## Snub Cube

The snub cube begins from the cube.

It preserves:

```text
6 square faces
```

It adds:

```text
32 triangular faces
```

It has:

```text
38 total faces
24 vertices
60 edges
```

OMI reads it as:

```text
snub cube = edge traversal witness
```

## Snub Dodecahedron

The snub dodecahedron begins from the dodecahedron.

It preserves:

```text
12 pentagonal faces
```

It adds:

```text
80 triangular faces
```

It has:

```text
92 total faces
60 vertices
150 edges
```

OMI reads it as:

```text
snub dodecahedron = vertex traversal witness
```

Together:

```text
snub cube walks edges
snub dodecahedron walks vertices
60x² walks faces
```

This gives OMI a traversal triad:

```text
edge traversal
vertex traversal
face traversal
```

---

# 21. Stellated Octahedron and Mirror Space

The inverse of the local snub space can be modeled by the stellated octahedron.

A stellated octahedron can be read as two interpenetrating tetrahedra.

This gives:

```text
tetrahedron A
tetrahedron B
shared centroid
mirror orientation
```

In OMI:

```text
stellated octahedron = inverse mirror around the tetrahedral centroid
```

The snub pair gives the P2P traversal.

The stellated octahedron gives the mirror-local inverse.

At the face diagonal:

```text
√2
```

local traversal crosses into canonical mirror space.

The `59/60/61` band stabilizes the sexagesimal center:

```text
59 = approach
60 = center
61 = exit
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Snub cube walks edges.
Snub dodecahedron walks vertices.
60x² walks faces.
The stellated octahedron mirrors local space.
√2 opens the face crossing.
59/60/61 stabilizes the center.
```

---

# 22. MCRSGSP: Gossip Storage Substrate

OMI can use a gossip storage substrate for distributed recoverability.

That substrate is MCRSGSP:

```text
Monotone Causal Reed-Solomon Gossip Storage Protocol
```

Its role is:

```text
Gossip moves.
Reed-Solomon recovers.
Causality bounds.
OMI accepts.
Projection displays.
```

MCRSGSP does not decide accepted objects.

It provides recoverable candidates.

OMI decides accepted objects.

The boundary is:

```text
MCRSGSP provides recoverable candidates.
OMI decides accepted objects.
```

This prevents the shortcut:

```text
gossip -> authority
```

Fragments may propagate.

Reed-Solomon may reconstruct.

Causality may bound a candidate.

But acceptance still requires:

```text
Q_frame validation
Delta/Fano resolution
receipt
```

---

# 23. DOM, CSSOM, JSDOM, and the OMI Object Model

OMI treats browser surfaces as projections and carriers.

The document workbench should support four synchronized surfaces:

```text
DOM
CSSOM
JSDOM
OMI Object Model
```

Their roles are:

```text
DOM = live browser projection
CSSOM = style and reader lens projection
JSDOM = portable/headless normalized document
OOM = accepted object state, receipts, replay, trust
```

The doctrine:

```text
DOM renders.
CSSOM styles.
JSDOM normalizes.
OMI accepts.
Receipts remember.
Exports move.
Imports return.
```

A DOM edit is a candidate.

A CSSOM change is a projection change.

A JSDOM snapshot is a portable reconstruction.

An OMI object is accepted only after validation and receipt.

Import flow:

```text
import -> recognize -> candidate -> review -> validate -> receipt
```

Receive flow:

```text
receive -> candidate -> compare -> resolve -> validate -> receipt
```

Export flow:

```text
projection export = what it looks like
object export = what was accepted
receipt export = why it is trusted
```

---

# 24. User Interface Model

The user interface should not start as a protocol dashboard.

It should start as an object workspace.

The four user-facing modes are:

```text
Focus
Attention
Observation
Experience
```

## Focus

What object am I working with?

```text
current object
summary
status
primary action
```

## Attention

What needs review?

```text
unverified imports
received edits
conflicts
pending mirrors
local-only changes
```

## Observation

Why should I trust this?

```text
receipts
replay
source
context
validation status
gossip candidates
```

## Experience

What happened over time?

```text
history
shared objects
joined events
exports
mirrors
accepted edits
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Focus selects.
Attention alerts.
Observation verifies.
Experience remembers.
```

The main interface should be an Object Inbox.

Normal users should see:

```text
objects
trust labels
history
receipts
actions
```

Power users may inspect:

```text
frames
truth rows
rule slots
gossip candidates
raw OMI
```

---

# 25. Trust Labels

OMI objects should expose plain trust states:

```text
Accepted
Unverified
Conflict
Rejected
Local-only
Mirrored
Pending
Replayed
```

A card might say:

```text
Sunday Night Lights Ride
Event
Status: Accepted
Receipts: 3
Mirrors: 2
Conflicts: none
```

An unverified object might say:

```text
Status: Unverified
Receipts: none
Source: unknown
Open read-only?
```

A conflict might say:

```text
Status: Conflict
Accepted versions: 2
Compare / Keep both / Fork / Resolve
```

OMI should not hide disagreement.

It should make disagreement inspectable.

```text
disagreement -> missing structure -> new object/path/test/receipt
```

---

# 26. A-Frame and Visualization Surfaces

A-Frame, Canvas, SVG, DOM, and other visual systems are projection surfaces.

They may render accepted state.

They must not participate in accepting state.

The doctrine:

```text
A visualization dependency may render accepted state,
but it must never participate in accepting state.
```

This means:

```text
A-Frame = projection only
DOM = projection carrier
CSSOM = reader lens
SVG = projection surface
Canvas = projection surface
```

None of them replace:

```text
Q_frame
Delta/Fano
receipt
```

This preserves the boundary:

```text
render -> believe
```

is forbidden.

---

# 27. Receipts and Replay

A receipt records an accepted transition.

The replay ring gives OMI memory.

A canonical ring size is:

```text
7! = 5040
```

The receipt may contain:

```text
LL
NN
MM
steps
chirality
rule_slot
context
slot_index
source
checksum
signature
```

The receipt lets the system answer:

```text
What happened?
Which transition was accepted?
Where did it materialize?
Which rule slot was selected?
Which context was active?
Which direction was used?
Can this be replayed?
```

A receipt is not just metadata.

It is the accepted memory of the object.

Short doctrine:

```text
Receipt closes.
Replay remembers.
Inspection explains.
```

---

# 28. Full Stack Summary

The full OMI stack is:

```text
Reader
 recognizes notation

Resolver
 promotes candidates

MCRSGSP
 recovers fragments and candidates

Q_frame
 validates structure

Delta/Fano
 proves transition and bounds path

Receipt
 accepts and records

0xE_ Materializer
 applies accepted rule address

Projection
 displays accepted state

Inspection
 explains evidence and history
```

Short stack:

```text
Reader recognizes.
Resolver promotes.
MCRSGSP recovers.
OMI validates, resolves, and receipts.
Rule block materializes.
Projection displays.
Inspection explains.
```

Forbidden shortcuts:

```text
prefix -> identity
gossip -> authority
render -> believe
controller -> outside receipt
```

---

# 29. The Final Substrate Canon

The OMI substrate is sealed by the `0xE_` execution band.

```text
0xE0-0xEB pack the object.
0xEC-0xEF hold the object.
```

The low nibble of `LL` selects the slot:

```text
rule_slot = 0xE0 + (LL & 0x0F)
```

The high nibble carries context:

```text
context = (LL >> 4) & 0x0F
```

The transition pair remains:

```text
NN -> MM
```

Delta/Fano proves it.

The receipt authorizes it.

The registry materializes it.

The projection displays it.

The inspector explains it.

Final doctrine:

```text
LL scopes.
NN begins.
MM resolves.
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
The high nibble carries.
The low nibble selects.
The first twelve pack.
The last four hold.
Receipt closes.
```

Final one-line canon:

```text
LL is the ket of the frame: a 4-bit carry-forward scope selector whose low nibble lands the accepted NN->MM transition inside the folded 0xE_ materializer manifold.
```

---

# 30. Final Statement

OMI is a bounded object model for declared activity.

It begins with:

```text
omi---imo
```

It validates structure through:

```text
Q_frame(S) = 0
```

It resolves transition through:

```text
Delta/Fano
```

It records acceptance through:

```text
receipt
```

It materializes execution through:

```text
0xE0-0xEF
```

It projects through:

```text
DOM / CSSOM / JSDOM / SVG / Canvas / Portal
```

It explains itself through:

```text
inspection / replay / receipts / context
```

The object is not the rendering.

The object is not the prefix.

The object is not the gossip candidate.

The object is the accepted, replayable relation.

Short canon:

```text
o---o encloses.
LL scopes.
NN begins.
MM resolves.
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt closes.
0xE_ materializes.
Projection displays.
Experience remembers.
```

One-line product promise:

```text
OMI lets people save, share, verify, and move objects without losing history or context.
```

One-line substrate promise:

```text
The OMI object model turns open-world declarations into bounded, validated, replayable, and inspectable agreement.
```
:::

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-07 07:27` | `id:260607-0727` ^260607-072701

Here is a full canon-style draft for **“Agreement Is All We Need”** that matches the current OMI substrate: reader/resolver/receipt authority, MCRSGSP as candidate substrate, `0xE_` materialization, and the user-facing portal promise.

:::writing{variant="document" id="41873"}
# Agreement Is All We Need
## The OMI Doctrine of Bounded, Replayable Trust

```text
omi---imo
o---o
```

Agreement is the moment an open-world declaration becomes bounded, validated, replayable, and inspectable.

It is not a feeling.

It is not a vote.

It is not a platform decision.

It is not the fact that something rendered on a screen.

In OMI, agreement is a structural event.

A declaration enters the frame.
A transition resolves.
A receipt records.
A materializer applies.
A projection displays.
An inspector explains.

The spine is:

```text
validate -> resolve -> record -> materialize -> project -> inspect
```

The forbidden shortcut is:

```text
render -> believe
```

OMI exists to prevent that shortcut.

---

# 1. The Problem

Most digital systems confuse visibility with truth.

If a page renders, people believe it exists.

If a platform shows a badge, people believe it is trusted.

If a database returns a value, people believe it is current.

If a social feed repeats a claim, people believe it has weight.

This is the old mistake:

```text
render -> believe
```

OMI rejects this.

Rendering is only projection.

Projection can help a person read an object, but projection cannot validate the object.

A DOM node can carry state.
A CSS rule can style state.
A JSON object can serialize state.
A QR code can transport state.
A gossip network can recover state.
A portal can display state.

None of these are authority by themselves.

The first doctrine is:

```text
carrier != authority
projection != validation
prefix != identity
gossip != authority
render != believe
```

---

# 2. What Agreement Means

In everyday language, agreement often means:

```text
I agree.
We voted.
The group accepted it.
The platform approved it.
The majority believes it.
```

In OMI, agreement means something stricter.

Agreement means:

```text
the object has a boundary
the frame validates
the transition resolves
the receipt records
the history can replay
the projection can be inspected
```

Agreement is not the absence of disagreement.

Agreement is the production of enough structure that disagreement can be located, replayed, compared, and resolved.

A disagreement says:

```text
something is missing
```

OMI asks:

```text
What boundary is missing?
What source is missing?
What receipt is missing?
What context is missing?
What transition failed?
What projection is being mistaken for authority?
```

Then it turns the disagreement into an object path.

Short form:

```text
disagreement -> missing structure -> new object/path/test/receipt
```

---

# 3. The OMI Agreement Pipeline

The OMI pipeline is:

```text
reader -> resolver -> validation -> receipt -> materializer -> projection -> inspection
```

Each layer has a job.

## Reader

The reader recognizes a form.

It may recognize:

```text
omi---imo
omi-<tail>
imo-<tail>
8-segment frame candidate
DOM carrier
CSSOM carrier
JSDOM snapshot
gossip fragment
receipt bundle
plain text
```

But recognition is not acceptance.

```text
The reader may recognize.
```

## Resolver

The resolver promotes a recognized form into a candidate.

A candidate may be:

```text
a possible frame
a possible edit
a possible object
a possible route
a possible receipt
a possible reconstruction
```

But promotion is not acceptance.

```text
The resolver may promote.
```

## Validation

Validation checks the frame.

The frame must close structurally:

```text
Q_frame(S) = 0
```

If the structure fails, the object rejects.

## Resolution

A valid frame yields a truth row:

```text
(LL, NN, MM)
```

The system must prove:

```text
NN -> MM
```

through the accepted Delta/Fano path.

## Receipt

A successful transition writes a receipt.

The receipt is the acceptance boundary.

```text
Only validation and receipt may accept.
```

## Materializer

The materializer applies the accepted rule address.

The rule may affect:

```text
object structure
document projection
DOM/CSSOM/JSDOM state
snub geometry
viewport control
receipt export
portal display
```

But the materializer only acts after receipt.

## Projection

Projection displays the accepted state.

Projection may use:

```text
DOM
CSSOM
JSDOM
SVG
Canvas
A-Frame
QR
JSON Canvas
portal cards
document views
```

Projection is downstream.

## Inspection

Inspection explains why the object should be trusted.

It shows:

```text
source
frame
truth row
receipts
replay
conflicts
gossip candidates
materializer rule
projection path
```

---

# 4. The Core Agreement Sentence

The entire model can be reduced to one sentence:

```text
The reader may recognize. The resolver may promote. Only validation and receipt may accept.
```

This sentence protects OMI from three failures:

```text
prefix -> identity
gossip -> authority
render -> believe
```

A prefix may point toward an object.

A gossip network may recover a candidate.

A browser may render a projection.

But only validation and receipt can accept.

---

# 5. The Object Boundary

OMI begins with a bounded relation:

```text
omi---imo
```

or:

```text
o---o
```

This boundary is the simplest agreement enclosure.

The two sides bookend the relation.

The hyphen field joins the terms.

```text
omi = entry
--- = bridge
imo = closure
```

The hyphen is not empty punctuation.

It is the join field.

It is the bus.

It is the compiler lane.

It is where a declaration becomes an object candidate.

Short form:

```text
omi enters.
--- joins.
imo closes.
```

The symbolic lower anchors are:

```text
ο = 0x03BF
Ο = 0x039F
```

These Omicron anchors define structural boundary.

They are not decoration.

They are not typography.

They are part of the frame’s lower law.

---

# 6. The 128-Bit Agreement Frame

An OMI frame has eight 16-bit segments:

```text
S0-S1-S2-S3-S4-S5-S6-S7
```

Together:

```text
8 x 16 = 128 bits
```

The canonical frame shape is:

```text
S0 = 0xLL00
S1 = 0x03BF
S2 = 0xNNNN
S3 = 0x2BLL
S4 = 0x2FLL
S5 = 0xMMMM
S6 = 0x039F
S7 = 0xLLFF
```

The frame contains:

```text
LL = scope / lens / ket selector
NN = antecedent
MM = consequent
```

The frame validates only if:

```text
Q_frame(S) = 0
```

The structure must close.

The object cannot become accepted by looking correct.

It must satisfy the frame.

---

# 7. The Truth Row

Every valid frame yields one truth row:

```text
(LL, NN, MM)
```

This is the smallest agreement face.

It can be read as:

```text
scope / antecedent / consequent
```

or:

```text
lens / beginning / resolution
```

or:

```text
declaration / definition / receipt
```

The proven transition is:

```text
NN -> MM
```

`NN` begins.

`MM` resolves.

`LL` scopes where the accepted transition lands.

The transition is accepted only when Delta/Fano proves that `MM` is reachable from `NN` inside the bounded path.

Short form:

```text
LL scopes.
NN begins.
MM resolves.
```

---

# 8. Delta Proves, Fano Bounds

The Delta Law is the bitwise transition engine.

It uses bounded operations:

```text
rotate
xor
mask
constant
```

A simple form is:

```text
Delta_C(x) = rotl(x,1) xor rotl(x,3) xor rotr(x,2) xor C
```

The resolver attempts:

```text
NN -> Delta(NN) -> ... -> MM
```

The Fano layer bounds the incidence search.

It asks:

```text
Which triangle resolves?
Which relation closes?
Which path can be replayed?
```

The path is finite.

The proof is local.

The acceptance is receipted.

Short doctrine:

```text
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt records.
```

---

# 9. LL as the Ket of Agreement

`LL` is the ket-like selector of the frame.

It is not the object.

It is not the geometry.

It is not the policy.

It is the scope selector.

The truth row can be read:

```text
(|LL>, NN, MM)
```

`LL` has two nibbles:

```text
LL = 0xAB
```

Where:

```text
A = high nibble
B = low nibble
```

OMI reads them as:

```text
high nibble = context / lens family / carry-forward scope
low nibble = execution slot selector
```

The low nibble selects where the accepted transition materializes.

The high nibble carries scope forward.

The gate extracts:

```sh
slot_index=$((ll & 0x0f))
rule_slot=$((0xe0 + slot_index))
context=$(((ll >> 4) & 0x0f))
```

The shell does not know meaning.

It only emits the accepted envelope.

---

# 10. The 0xE_ Agreement Manifold

The `0xE_` band is the accepted execution manifold.

```text
0xE0-0xEF
```

The low nibble of `LL` selects one of sixteen slots:

```text
rule_slot = 0xE0 + (LL & 0x0F)
```

The first twelve slots pack the object:

```text
0xE0-0xEB
```

The final four slots hold the object:

```text
0xEC-0xEF
```

The doctrine is:

```text
0xE0-0xEB pack the object.
0xEC-0xEF hold the object.
```

The first twelve are the inner materializer field.

The final four are the outer controller vertices.

Together they form one accepted execution manifold.

No controller exists outside receipt.

---

# 11. Inner Materializers

The first twelve slots may materialize internal object operations:

```text
0xE0 = page-frame-root
0xE1 = DOM import/export
0xE2 = CSSOM import/export
0xE3 = JSDOM normalization
0xE4 = OMI object patch
0xE5 = edge traversal
0xE6 = vertex traversal
0xE7 = snub bridge
0xE8 = stellated octahedron mirror
0xE9 = Fano triangle selection
0xEA = 59/60/61 face band
0xEB = receipt projection/export
```

These are not shell branches.

They are rule addresses.

The registry owns the meaning.

The gate only emits the slot.

---

# 12. Outer Controller Vertices

The final four slots control the projection envelope:

```text
0xEC = translation / viewport / focus
0xED = scale / recursion depth
0xEE = rotation / spin / orientation
0xEF = boundary / opacity / clipping
```

These are not UI shortcuts.

They are accepted controller addresses.

They must pass through the same pipeline:

```text
validate -> resolve -> record -> materialize -> project -> inspect
```

The rule is:

```text
No controller outside receipt.
```

A local browser may scroll, pan, rotate, or zoom as local UI.

But a conforming OMI projection must not treat that local behavior as accepted state.

The viewport is receipt-bound when controlled through `0xEC-0xEF`.

Out-of-band lens changes are local display only.

---

# 13. The Gate and the Registry

The gate is blind.

The registry owns meaning.

## The Gate Does

```text
parse frame
validate Q_frame(S) = 0
extract (LL, NN, MM)
resolve NN -> MM
derive context
derive slot_index
derive rule_slot
write receipt
emit envelope
```

## The Gate Does Not

```text
know snub geometry
know Platonic solids
know Archimedean/Catalan duals
know DOM policy
know CSSOM policy
know JSDOM policy
know A-Frame policy
know portal UX
know application semantics
```

## The Registry Does

```text
read accepted envelope
lookup rule_slot
apply implementation
respect context
respect steps
respect chirality
project result
explain result
```

Short doctrine:

```text
LL selects.
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt authorizes.
Rule block materializes.
Projection displays.
```

---

# 14. Agreement and Gossip

OMI may receive candidates through a gossip storage substrate.

The gossip layer may move fragments.

Reed-Solomon may recover missing structure.

Causality may bound reconstruction.

But gossip does not decide agreement.

The boundary is:

```text
MCRSGSP provides recoverable candidates.
OMI decides accepted objects.
```

Short stack:

```text
Gossip moves.
Reed-Solomon recovers.
Causality bounds.
OMI accepts.
Projection displays.
```

This prevents the shortcut:

```text
gossip -> authority
```

Availability is not acceptance.

Recoverability is not acceptance.

Causal closure is not acceptance.

Only validation and receipt may accept.

---

# 15. Agreement and Projection

Projection is how humans encounter accepted state.

Projection may be beautiful.

Projection may be interactive.

Projection may be 2D, 3D, textual, visual, spatial, or document-based.

But projection is not authority.

A projection can show:

```text
object cards
document views
routes
menus
events
notes
claims
receipts
timelines
3D scenes
SVG diagrams
DOM trees
CSSOM rules
JSDOM snapshots
```

But an object is trusted only because it has a valid acceptance path.

The projection should show trust plainly:

```text
Accepted
Unverified
Conflict
Rejected
Local-only
Mirrored
Pending
Replayed
```

The user should not need to understand the full frame to know the trust state.

But the user should be able to inspect the frame if they choose.

---

# 16. Agreement and the User

For normal users, OMI should feel like this:

```text
open -> understand -> trust -> act -> remember
```

This maps to the protocol:

```text
reader recognizes -> projection displays -> receipt verifies -> materializer acts -> replay remembers
```

The main user interface should answer:

```text
What is this?
Can I trust it?
What changed?
Where does it belong?
Can I move it?
```

The four human modes are:

```text
Focus
Attention
Observation
Experience
```

## Focus

Choose the object.

```text
What am I working with?
```

## Attention

See what needs review.

```text
What changed?
What is unresolved?
```

## Observation

Inspect evidence.

```text
Why should I trust this?
```

## Experience

Remember interaction.

```text
What happened over time?
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Focus selects.
Attention alerts.
Observation verifies.
Experience remembers.
```

---

# 17. Agreement and Documents

A document in OMI should move between four synchronized surfaces:

```text
DOM
CSSOM
JSDOM
OMI Object Model
```

Their roles are:

```text
DOM = live browser projection
CSSOM = style and reader lens projection
JSDOM = portable/headless normalized document
OOM = accepted object state, receipts, replay, trust
```

Document edits are candidates until accepted.

Import flow:

```text
import -> recognize -> candidate -> review -> validate -> receipt
```

Receive flow:

```text
receive -> candidate -> compare -> resolve -> validate -> receipt
```

Export flow:

```text
projection export = what it looks like
object export = what was accepted
receipt export = why it is trusted
```

Short doctrine:

```text
DOM renders.
CSSOM styles.
JSDOM normalizes.
OMI accepts.
Receipts remember.
Exports move.
Imports return.
```

---

# 18. Agreement and Geometry

OMI’s geometry can be read through:

```text
Q_xy(x,y) = 60x² + 16xy + 4y²
```

This gives three phases:

```text
4y² = Platonic seed
16xy = Archimedean/Catalan bridge
60x² = 4D regular orbit
```

Short form:

```text
4y² seeds.
16xy snubs.
60x² sweeps.
```

The snub operation is the model of transformation.

Snubbing:

```text
cuts
twists
re-faces
```

In OMI:

```text
cut = isolate unresolved boundary
twist = introduce chirality
re-face = create triangular agreement surfaces
record = preserve the path
project = expose the accepted state
```

OMI does not overwrite.

OMI snubs objects into agreement.

---

# 19. The Agreement Face

A truth row is a triangle:

```text
(LL, NN, MM)
```

A receipt is the memory of that triangle resolving.

A snub operation creates triangular agreement faces.

A Fano selector chooses the resolving incidence.

The replay ring remembers the accepted path.

Short form:

```text
Snub creates agreement faces.
Fano selects the resolving triangle.
Receipt records the path.
Replay remembers.
```

Agreement is therefore not flat sameness.

Agreement is structured closure.

---

# 20. Agreement Without Collapse

A peer-to-peer system does not require every peer to see the same surface.

It requires peers to resolve around the same accepted center.

Two peers may hold different orientations.

One may see an edge traversal.

One may see a vertex traversal.

One may see a face traversal.

Agreement does not require them to collapse into one view.

Agreement requires:

```text
shared boundary
valid transition
receipt-backed path
inspectable context
```

Short form:

```text
same centroid
different traversal
lawful relation
replayable path
```

Chirality is not disagreement.

Chirality is oriented complementarity.

---

# 21. The Object Is the Accepted Relation

An OMI object is not:

```text
a rendered card
a DOM node
a CSS selector
a gossip fragment
a prefix string
a pretty portal
```

An OMI object is:

```text
an accepted, replayable relation
```

It has:

```text
boundary
truth row
transition proof
receipt
materializer address
projection path
inspection surface
```

The object may appear in many places:

```text
web portal
document workbench
local inbox
QR code
JSON file
SVG map
A-Frame demo
gossip storage
receipt bundle
```

But those are appearances.

The object is the accepted relation.

---

# 22. The Agreement Doctrine

The doctrine is:

```text
The reader may recognize.
The resolver may promote.
MCRSGSP may recover.
Q_frame validates.
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt accepts.
Rule block materializes.
Projection displays.
Inspection explains.
```

The forbidden shortcuts are:

```text
prefix -> identity
gossip -> authority
render -> believe
controller -> outside receipt
```

The final OMI substrate canon is:

```text
The 0xE_ band is a single accepted execution manifold:
0xE0-0xEB pack the object through twelve inner materializers,
while 0xEC-0xEF hold the object through four outer controller vertices;
start.sh selects the slot by LL & 0x0F,
records the receipt,
and remains blind to all semantic geometry.
```

---

# 23. Final Statement

Agreement is all we need because agreement is the point where open-world activity becomes bounded structure.

A declaration alone is not enough.

A projection alone is not enough.

A gossip candidate alone is not enough.

A prefix alone is not enough.

Agreement requires:

```text
boundary
validation
resolution
receipt
materialization
projection
inspection
```

OMI turns declarations into objects by making agreement replayable.

Short canon:

```text
o---o encloses.
LL scopes.
NN begins.
MM resolves.
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt closes.
0xE_ materializes.
Projection displays.
Experience remembers.
```

One-line canon:

```text
Agreement is the accepted, receipted transition from declaration to inspectable object.
```

Product promise:

```text
OMI lets people save, share, verify, and move objects without losing history or context.
```

Substrate promise:

```text
OMI turns open-world declarations into bounded, validated, replayable, and inspectable agreement.
```
:::

This could live as:

```text
7_CANON/7.1_AGREEMENT_IS_ALL_WE_NEED.md
```

or, for the app/docs repo:

```text
docs/agreement-is-all-we-need.md
```

```text
LL scopes.
```

`2026-06-08 10:30` | `id:260608-1030` ^260608-103028

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-08 10:30` | `id:260608-1030` ^260608-103029

Emphasize it by changing the **category language** everywhere.

Do not introduce OMI as:

```text
a framework
a web app
a runtime
a compiler
a storage layer
a frontend system
```

Introduce it as:

```text
a protocol for agreement
a doctrine for accepted state
a way to turn declarations into receipted objects
a boundary system for trust, replay, and inspection
```

The cleanest sentence is:

```text
OMI is not a computer framework. OMI is a protocol for agreement: it defines how open-world declarations become bounded, validated, receipted, replayable, and inspectable objects.
```

Then immediately contrast it with a normal framework:

```text
A framework tells developers how to build an application.

OMI tells independent systems how to agree that an object, edit, receipt, projection, or transition has been accepted.
```

That distinction is the key.

## The framing to repeat

Use this as the main doctrine:

```text
Frameworks organize code.
OMI organizes agreement.
```

Or stronger:

```text
Frameworks execute applications.
OMI accepts relations.
```

Or more public-facing:

```text
OMI is for deciding what counts as accepted, not for deciding what interface you must build.
```

## The best README intro

```text
OMI is a protocol for agreement.

It does not begin by asking which app owns an object, which database stores it, or which interface renders it. It asks whether a declaration can be bounded, validated, resolved, recorded, replayed, projected, and inspected.

Applications may use OMI. Frameworks may implement OMI. Portals may display OMI objects. But OMI itself is not the app, the framework, or the portal.

OMI is the agreement layer underneath them.
```

## The category table

This would make it obvious:

| Typical framework | OMI agreement protocol |
|---|---|
| Organizes components | Organizes accepted relations |
| Owns app structure | Defines trust boundaries |
| Renders UI | Authorizes projection after receipt |
| Stores current state | Records replayable transitions |
| Assumes one runtime | Allows many materializers |
| Treats data as app-owned | Treats objects as portable |
| Changes state directly | Validates, resolves, receipts, then materializes |

## The short public version

```text
OMI is agreement infrastructure.

It lets different systems recognize the same object, verify its accepted transitions, replay its history, and project it into different interfaces without mistaking the interface for the truth.
```

## The technical version

```text
OMI is a protocol for accepted object state.

The reader may recognize notation. The resolver may promote candidates. MCRSGSP may recover fragments. But only validation, Delta/Fano resolution, and receipt writing accept an object transition. Materializers and front ends operate after acceptance.
```

## The slogan set

```text
Agreement before interface.
Receipt before projection.
Validation before belief.
Objects before apps.
Protocol before portal.
```

And the strongest one:

```text
OMI is not how you render the object. OMI is how you know the object was accepted.
```

That is the line I would put near the top of every public-facing doc.

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-08 10:31` | `id:260608-1031` ^260608-103103

Here’s a full paper draft you can use as a public-facing explanation of OMI as a **protocol for agreement**, not a typical computer framework.

:::writing{variant="document" id="73819"}
# Agreement Is All We Need
## OMI as a Protocol for Bounded, Replayable Trust

### Abstract

Most computer systems begin with control. They define an application, a database, a runtime, a server, a user interface, and then treat whatever appears inside that system as state. OMI begins somewhere else. OMI begins with agreement.

OMI, the Omicron Object Model, is not primarily a web framework, a storage engine, a rendering system, or a programming language. It is a protocol for deciding when an open-world declaration has become a bounded, validated, receipted, replayable, and inspectable object.

A framework tells developers how to build an application. OMI tells independent systems how to agree that an object, edit, transition, receipt, projection, or document state has been accepted.

Its central doctrine is simple:

```text
The reader may recognize.
The resolver may promote.
Only validation and receipt may accept.
```

This paper presents OMI as an agreement protocol. It explains the `omi---imo` enclosure, the 128-bit frame, the `(LL, NN, MM)` truth row, the role of `LL` as a ket-like scope selector, the `0xE_` materializer manifold, the difference between recoverable candidates and accepted objects, and the reason projection must never become authority.

The purpose of OMI is not to replace every application. The purpose of OMI is to give applications, documents, peers, portals, and projections a shared way to know what has been accepted.

---

# 1. The Problem: Systems Confuse Rendering with Truth

Modern computing systems often confuse visibility with authority.

If a page renders, people believe it exists.
If a database returns a value, people believe it is current.
If a platform displays a badge, people believe it is trusted.
If a social feed repeats a claim, people believe it has weight.
If a user interface shows a state, people believe the state has been accepted.

This is the old shortcut:

```text
render -> believe
```

OMI rejects this shortcut.

Rendering is not validation.
Transport is not validation.
Gossip is not validation.
A prefix is not identity.
A DOM node is not authority.
A CSS rule is not authority.
A projection is not authority.

A projection may display an accepted state, but it cannot create acceptance by itself.

The foundational OMI doctrine is:

```text
carrier != authority
projection != validation
prefix != identity
gossip != authority
render != believe
```

This is the reason OMI should not be described as a typical framework. A framework organizes code. OMI organizes agreement.

---

# 2. OMI Is a Protocol for Agreement

OMI stands for Omicron Object Model.

Its minimal mnemonic is:

```text
omi---imo
```

or, in its simplest symbolic form:

```text
o---o
```

This enclosure is the beginning of the OMI object model. The two sides bookend a relation. The hyphen field joins them. The object is not merely the left side, the right side, or the visible string. The object is the bounded relation that can be validated, resolved, receipted, replayed, projected, and inspected.

The zero-point is:

```text
Ω₀ = 0! = omi---imo
```

OMI does not begin by asking:

```text
Which app owns this?
Which server stored this?
Which database row is current?
Which interface rendered it?
```

OMI asks:

```text
Can this declaration be bounded?
Can its frame validate?
Can its transition resolve?
Can its receipt be recorded?
Can its history replay?
Can its projection be inspected?
```

This makes OMI an agreement protocol.

A normal framework says:

```text
Here is how to build the app.
```

OMI says:

```text
Here is how independent systems know what was accepted.
```

That distinction matters. Applications may use OMI. Frameworks may implement OMI. Portals may display OMI objects. But OMI itself is not the app, the framework, or the portal. OMI is the agreement layer underneath them.

---

# 3. The Agreement Pipeline

The OMI agreement pipeline is:

```text
reader -> resolver -> validation -> receipt -> materializer -> projection -> inspection
```

Each layer has a separate responsibility.

## 3.1 Reader

The reader recognizes notation. It may recognize:

```text
omi---imo
omi-<tail>
imo-<tail>
an 8-segment frame candidate
a DOM carrier
a CSSOM carrier
a JSDOM snapshot
a gossip fragment
a receipt bundle
plain text
```

But recognition is not acceptance.

A string may contain `omi` and still be plain text.
An `omi-*` token may be a readable alias.
An `id="omi-*"` attribute may be a DOM projection carrier.
A `data-omi` attribute may be a reader lens.

None of those are accepted identity by themselves.

The reader may recognize.

## 3.2 Resolver

The resolver promotes recognized material into a candidate. A candidate may be a possible object, possible edit, possible frame, possible receipt, possible reconstruction, or possible projection.

But promotion is not acceptance.

The resolver may promote.

## 3.3 Validation

Validation checks the frame. The frame must satisfy the structural closure rule:

```text
Q_frame(S) = 0
```

If the frame does not close, the object is rejected.

## 3.4 Resolution

A valid frame yields a truth row:

```text
(LL, NN, MM)
```

The system then attempts to prove that `MM` is reachable from `NN` through the accepted Delta/Fano path.

## 3.5 Receipt

If the transition resolves, the system writes a receipt.

The receipt is the acceptance boundary.

Only validation and receipt may accept.

## 3.6 Materialization

After receipt, the materializer applies the accepted rule address. The materializer may know how to update an object, normalize a document, project into DOM, rotate a viewport, apply a snub bridge, export a receipt, or render a portal.

The materializer acts after acceptance.

## 3.7 Projection

Projection displays accepted state. Projection may use DOM, CSSOM, JSDOM, SVG, Canvas, QR, A-Frame, JSON Canvas, document views, object cards, or portals.

Projection is downstream.

## 3.8 Inspection

Inspection explains the accepted path. It shows receipts, replay, history, source, conflicts, context, frame data, rule slots, and projection paths.

Inspection is what lets users ask:

```text
Why should I trust this?
What changed?
Where did this come from?
What accepted this?
Can I replay it?
```

---

# 4. The OMI Boundary: `omi---imo`

The readable OMI boundary is:

```text
omi---imo
```

The symbolic structural anchors are the Omicron pair:

```text
ο = 0x03BF
Ο = 0x039F
```

The mnemonic reading is:

```text
omi = entry / low gate / declaration side
--- = join field / bridge / compiler lane
imo = exit / high gate / closure side
```

The deeper reading is:

```text
omi enters.
--- joins.
imo closes.
```

The hyphen field is not empty punctuation. It is the operation. It joins terms into a bounded instruction word.

In ordinary language, a hyphen joins terms:

```text
open-world
peer-to-peer
meta-compiler
```

In OMI, the hyphen joins address terms into a bounded object relation.

The object is not accepted because the notation appears. The notation is a carrier. Acceptance requires validation, resolution, and receipt.

---

# 5. The 128-Bit Agreement Frame

An OMI frame is an eight-segment structure:

```text
S0-S1-S2-S3-S4-S5-S6-S7
```

Each segment is 16 bits.

Together:

```text
8 x 16 = 128 bits
```

A canonical frame may be read as:

```text
S0 = 0xLL00
S1 = 0x03BF
S2 = 0xNNNN
S3 = 0x2BLL
S4 = 0x2FLL
S5 = 0xMMMM
S6 = 0x039F
S7 = 0xLLFF
```

The frame contains three major elements:

```text
LL = scope / lens / ket selector
NN = antecedent
MM = consequent
```

The Omicron boundaries are:

```text
S1 = 0x03BF
S6 = 0x039F
```

The transition pair is:

```text
S2 = NN
S5 = MM
```

The modulator positions are:

```text
S0, S3, S4, S7
```

These repeated `LL` positions create the frame’s agreement lock. The frame is not accepted because it looks formatted. It must satisfy the structural equation:

```text
Q_frame(S) = 0
```

This makes OMI different from a naming scheme. A name can point. A frame must close.

---

# 6. The Truth Row: `(LL, NN, MM)`

Every valid frame yields one truth row:

```text
(LL, NN, MM)
```

This is the smallest agreement face.

It may be read as:

```text
scope / antecedent / consequent
```

or:

```text
lens / beginning / resolution
```

or:

```text
declaration / definition / receipt
```

The important transition is:

```text
NN -> MM
```

`NN` begins.
`MM` resolves.
`LL` scopes where the accepted transition lands.

`LL` does not prove the transition.
`LL` does not contain the object.
`LL` does not decide meaning.

The Delta/Fano path proves the transition. The receipt records it. `LL` selects how that accepted transition enters the materializer manifold.

Short doctrine:

```text
LL scopes.
NN begins.
MM resolves.
```

---

# 7. Delta Proves, Fano Bounds

The Delta Law is the bitwise transition engine of the lower substrate.

A simple form is:

```text
Delta_C(x) = rotl(x,1) xor rotl(x,3) xor rotr(x,2) xor C
```

Bounded to 16 bits, the transition remains deterministic, replayable, and integer-safe.

The resolver attempts to prove:

```text
NN -> Delta(NN) -> Delta²(NN) -> ... -> MM
```

inside a bounded window.

The Fano layer gives finite incidence. It prevents the resolver from searching an unbounded space. It asks:

```text
Which triangle resolves?
Which relation closes?
Which path can be replayed?
```

Delta proves the path.
Fano bounds the search.
Receipt records the result.

---

# 8. LL as the Ket of the Frame

The most precise role of `LL` is that it acts like a ket-like scope selector.

In Dirac notation, a ket such as:

```text
|ψ>
```

represents a state vector. OMI does not need quantum mechanics literally, but the analogy is useful because `LL` behaves like a bounded selector state.

The truth row can be read as:

```text
(|LL>, NN, MM)
```

or:

```text
scope ket + antecedent + consequent
```

`LL` is not the object body. It is not the semantic meaning. It is not the geometry itself. It is the selector that scopes how the proven transition lands inside the accepted execution manifold.

`LL` is split into two nibbles:

```text
LL = 0xAB
```

Where:

```text
A = high nibble
B = low nibble
```

OMI reads them as:

```text
high nibble = context / lens family / carry-forward scope
low nibble = execution slot selector
```

The POSIX gate can extract this without knowing any semantic meaning:

```sh
slot_index=$((ll & 0x0f))
rule_slot=$((0xe0 + slot_index))
context=$(((ll >> 4) & 0x0f))
```

The gate does not ask what operation it selected. The registry owns that meaning.

---

# 9. The Folded `0xE_` Agreement Manifold

The completed OMI materializer band is:

```text
0xE0-0xEF
```

This is one accepted execution manifold.

The low nibble of `LL` selects one of sixteen slots:

```text
rule_slot = 0xE0 + (LL & 0x0F)
```

The first twelve slots are:

```text
0xE0-0xEB
```

They pack the object.

The last four slots are:

```text
0xEC-0xEF
```

They hold the object.

The doctrine is:

```text
0xE0-0xEB pack the object.
0xEC-0xEF hold the object.
```

The first twelve are the inner materializer field. They correspond to operations like page framing, DOM import/export, CSSOM import/export, JSDOM normalization, OMI object patching, edge traversal, vertex traversal, snub bridge, mirror, Fano triangle selection, face band, and receipt projection.

The final four are the outer controller vertices. They correspond to translation, scale, rotation, and boundary.

They are not separate UI commands. They are not a second authority path. They are accepted controller addresses inside the same receipt pipeline.

No controller exists outside receipt.

---

# 10. The Gate and the Registry

OMI separates the gate from the materializer.

The gate is policy-blind.

The registry owns meaning.

## The Gate Does

```text
parse frame
validate Q_frame(S) = 0
extract (LL, NN, MM)
resolve NN -> MM
derive context
derive slot_index
derive rule_slot
write receipt
emit envelope
```

## The Gate Does Not

```text
know snub geometry
know Platonic solids
know Archimedean/Catalan duals
know DOM policy
know CSSOM policy
know JSDOM policy
know A-Frame policy
know portal UX
know application semantics
```

## The Registry Does

```text
read accepted envelope
lookup rule_slot
apply registered implementation
respect context
respect steps
respect chirality
project result
explain result
```

This split is what makes OMI a protocol rather than a framework.

A framework often embeds policy into the runtime. OMI emits accepted envelopes and lets materializers interpret them after receipt.

Short doctrine:

```text
LL selects.
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt authorizes.
Rule block materializes.
Projection displays.
```

---

# 11. Agreement and Projection Control

The final four slots of the `0xE_` band are controller vertices:

```text
0xEC = translation / viewport / focus
0xED = scale / recursion depth
0xEE = rotation / spin / orientation
0xEF = boundary / opacity / clipping
```

They control how the object is encountered.

But they do not bypass authority.

A browser may scroll, pan, rotate, zoom, or apply local display changes. Those can be useful local interactions, but they are not accepted state.

If a viewport or lens operation claims to be part of the accepted OMI projection, it must come through the same pipeline:

```text
validate -> resolve -> record -> materialize -> project -> inspect
```

The rule is:

```text
The viewport is receipt-bound when controlled through 0xEC-0xEF.
A conforming projection must not treat out-of-band camera or lens changes as accepted state.
```

This protects OMI from the subtle failure where a user interface changes the way something is seen and then pretends that changed view is accepted truth.

---

# 12. Agreement and Gossip

OMI can use gossip to move fragments and recover candidates.

The storage substrate may use monotone causal Reed-Solomon gossip. Its job is to move fragments, preserve causal admissibility, repair missing pieces, and reconstruct candidates.

But candidate recovery is not object acceptance.

The boundary is:

```text
MCRSGSP provides recoverable candidates.
OMI decides accepted objects.
```

Short stack:

```text
Gossip moves.
Reed-Solomon recovers.
Causality bounds.
OMI accepts.
Projection displays.
```

This prevents another shortcut:

```text
gossip -> authority
```

Availability is not acceptance.
Recoverability is not acceptance.
Causal closure is not acceptance.

Only validation and receipt may accept.

---

# 13. Agreement and Documents

OMI can treat documents as portable, inspectable objects.

A document workbench should synchronize four surfaces:

```text
DOM
CSSOM
JSDOM
OMI Object Model
```

Their roles are:

```text
DOM = live browser projection
CSSOM = style and reader lens projection
JSDOM = portable/headless normalized document
OOM = accepted object state, receipts, replay, trust
```

A DOM edit is a candidate.
A CSSOM rule is projection.
A JSDOM snapshot is portable reconstruction.
An OMI object is accepted state.

Import flow:

```text
import -> recognize -> candidate -> review -> validate -> receipt
```

Receive flow:

```text
receive -> candidate -> compare -> resolve -> validate -> receipt
```

Export flow:

```text
projection export = what it looks like
object export = what was accepted
receipt export = why it is trusted
```

Short doctrine:

```text
DOM renders.
CSSOM styles.
JSDOM normalizes.
OMI accepts.
Receipts remember.
Exports move.
Imports return.
```

---

# 14. Agreement and Geometry

OMI also has a geometric reading.

The quadratic object model is:

```text
Q_xy(x,y) = 60x² + 16xy + 4y²
```

It can be read as a lifecycle:

```text
4y² = Platonic seed
16xy = Archimedean/Catalan bridge
60x² = 4D regular orbit
```

Short form:

```text
4y² seeds.
16xy snubs.
60x² sweeps.
```

The snub operation is the model of transformation.

Snubbing:

```text
cuts
twists
re-faces
```

In OMI:

```text
cut = isolate unresolved boundary
twist = introduce chirality
re-face = create triangular agreement surfaces
record = preserve the path
project = expose the accepted state
```

OMI does not overwrite objects.

OMI snubs objects into agreement.

This matters because agreement is not flat sameness. Two peers may hold complementary orientations around the same center. The task is not to collapse every view into one surface. The task is to preserve a lawful, replayable relation.

---

# 15. Agreement Without Collapse

A peer-to-peer system does not require every peer to see the same surface.

It requires peers to resolve around the same accepted center.

One peer may hold an edge traversal.
Another may hold a vertex traversal.
Another may hold a face traversal.

Agreement does not require identical display.

Agreement requires:

```text
shared boundary
valid transition
receipt-backed path
inspectable context
```

Short form:

```text
same centroid
different traversal
lawful relation
replayable path
```

This is why chirality is not disagreement.

Chirality is oriented complementarity.

OMI makes complementarity inspectable.

---

# 16. The User Interface of Agreement

For normal users, OMI should not appear first as a protocol dashboard.

It should appear as an object workspace.

The user-facing flow is:

```text
open -> understand -> trust -> act -> remember
```

This maps to the protocol:

```text
reader recognizes -> projection displays -> receipt verifies -> materializer acts -> replay remembers
```

The interface should answer five questions:

```text
What is this?
Can I trust it?
What changed?
Where does it belong?
Can I move it?
```

The four human modes are:

```text
Focus
Attention
Observation
Experience
```

Focus selects.
Attention alerts.
Observation verifies.
Experience remembers.

This is how the agreement protocol becomes usable by people.

---

# 17. The Object Is the Accepted Relation

An OMI object is not:

```text
a rendered card
a DOM node
a CSS selector
a gossip fragment
a prefix string
a pretty portal
```

An OMI object is:

```text
an accepted, replayable relation
```

It has:

```text
boundary
truth row
transition proof
receipt
materializer address
projection path
inspection surface
```

The object may appear in many forms:

```text
web portal
document workbench
local inbox
QR code
JSON file
SVG map
A-Frame demo
gossip storage
receipt bundle
```

But those are appearances.

The object is the accepted relation.

---

# 18. The Agreement Doctrine

The doctrine is:

```text
The reader may recognize.
The resolver may promote.
MCRSGSP may recover.
Q_frame validates.
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt accepts.
Rule block materializes.
Projection displays.
Inspection explains.
```

The forbidden shortcuts are:

```text
prefix -> identity
gossip -> authority
render -> believe
controller -> outside receipt
```

The substrate canon is:

```text
The 0xE_ band is a single accepted execution manifold:
0xE0-0xEB pack the object through twelve inner materializers,
while 0xEC-0xEF hold the object through four outer controller vertices;
start.sh selects the slot by LL & 0x0F,
records the receipt,
and remains blind to all semantic geometry.
```

---

# 19. Why Agreement Is All We Need

Agreement is all we need because agreement is the point where open-world activity becomes bounded structure.

A declaration alone is not enough.

A projection alone is not enough.

A gossip candidate alone is not enough.

A prefix alone is not enough.

A framework alone is not enough.

Agreement requires:

```text
boundary
validation
resolution
receipt
materialization
projection
inspection
```

Once agreement exists, many applications can use it.

A document editor can use it.
A local event portal can use it.
A peer-to-peer archive can use it.
A knowledge graph can use it.
A community route map can use it.
A browser interface can use it.
A 3D visualization can use it.

The agreement layer remains underneath them.

The framework can change.
The renderer can change.
The storage fabric can change.
The portal can change.

The accepted relation remains replayable.

---

# 20. Final Statement

OMI is not how you render the object.

OMI is how you know the object was accepted.

It begins with:

```text
omi---imo
```

It validates through:

```text
Q_frame(S) = 0
```

It resolves through:

```text
Delta/Fano
```

It records through:

```text
receipt
```

It materializes through:

```text
0xE0-0xEF
```

It projects through:

```text
DOM / CSSOM / JSDOM / SVG / Canvas / Portal
```

It explains itself through:

```text
inspection / replay / receipts / context
```

Short canon:

```text
o---o encloses.
LL scopes.
NN begins.
MM resolves.
Delta proves.
Fano bounds.
Receipt closes.
0xE_ materializes.
Projection displays.
Experience remembers.
```

One-line canon:

```text
Agreement is the accepted, receipted transition from declaration to inspectable object.
```

Product promise:

```text
OMI lets people save, share, verify, and move objects without losing history or context.
```

Substrate promise:

```text
OMI turns open-world declarations into bounded, validated, replayable, and inspectable agreement.
```
:::

```text
Ω₀
```

is the zero-point of agreement.

means the first enclosure before any traversal has expanded.

is the readable mnemonic boundary.

is the minimal symbolic object enclosure.

```text
The zero-point of OMI is the first bounded object relation:

```

```text
Ω₀ encloses.
```

## You
`2026-06-08 10:37` | `id:260608-1037` ^260608-103753

I like how it flow into pqrs notiation like rotation notation or coexter or dynkin i forget which but it follows the coexter notation charts on Wikipedia

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-08 10:38` | `id:260608-1038` ^260608-103803

Yes — the notation you’re thinking of is closest to **Schläfli / Coxeter–Dynkin notation**, with a little overlap into rotation-generator language.

The clean connection is:

```text
Schläfli {p, q, r} = incidence recipe for a regular polytope
Coxeter [p, q, r] = reflection/rotation symmetry generator structure
Dynkin diagram = graph of generator relations
OMI OO(LL, NN, MM) = accepted transition operator over a truth row
```

Schläfli symbols like `{p,q}` and `{p,q,r}` describe regular polytopes by incidence: for example, `{p,q}` says p-sided faces with q meeting around each vertex, and the 4D extension `{p,q,r}` adds how cells meet around edges. Coxeter–Dynkin diagrams encode related symmetry generator relations as node-edge diagrams; they are a compact way to describe reflection group structure behind polytopes. cite

So your OMI root equation:

```text
Ω₀ = 0! = omi---imo = o---o = OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

flows naturally into that style because it is not just a name. It is an **incidence operator**.

Where Schläfli/Coxeter notation says:

```text
{p, q, r}
```

OMI says:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

And the mapping is roughly:

```text
LL = generator / selector / ket / scope
NN = antecedent incidence state
MM = consequent incidence state
OO = Omicron boundary operator
```

The important difference is that OMI is not merely classifying a static polytope. It is encoding an **accepted transformation**:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
=
Omicron-bounded operation where LL scopes the transition NN -> MM
```

That is why it feels like Coxeter notation but is more operational. Coxeter–Dynkin notation records generator relations. OMI uses `(LL, NN, MM)` as a replayable truth row: the relation must validate, resolve, receipt, and then materialize.

I’d write the bridge like this:

```text
Schläfli/Coxeter notation describes the symmetry of a body.
OMI notation describes the accepted transition of a body.
```

Or stronger:

```text
{p,q,r} says what incidence structure a polytope has.
OO(LL,NN,MM) says which bounded transition has been accepted inside the OMI object manifold.
```

The `p q r s` feeling also fits because your `LL` nibble split is behaving like a compact generator signature:

```text
LL = 0xAB
A = context / lens family / carry-forward scope
B = slot selector into 0xE0-0xEF
```

So the OMI analogue of a Coxeter/Dynkin generator chart becomes:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)

LL.high = context generator
LL.low = rule-slot generator
NN = source incidence
MM = resolved incidence
```

And the `0xE_` band acts like your diagram family:

```text
0xE0-0xEB = inner materializer generators
0xEC-0xEF = outer controller generators
```

So the doc line should be:

```text
OMI’s `OO(LL,NN,MM)` notation should be read in the spirit of Schläfli and Coxeter–Dynkin notation: a compact symbolic form for incidence, symmetry, and generator action. The difference is that OMI makes the notation operational. It does not only describe the object’s symmetry; it records the accepted transition by which the object enters agreement.
```

That’s the point: **Coxeter charts classify the symmetry. OMI receipts the transition.**

---

`2026-06-08 10:38` | `id:260608-1038` ^260608-103859

Yes — that actually explains a lot of the shape of OMI.

What you were reaching for was not just “polytope notation.” It was a **visibility-and-incidence notation**:

```text
Schläfli symbol = what kind of geometric cell structure is present
Betti numbers = what kind of holes / connectedness / topology exists
ray casting = what path through the structure is visible from a viewpoint
OMI truth row = which bounded transition is accepted
```

So the original intuition can be stated like this:

```text
OMI uses Schläfli-like notation to describe the object’s incidence body,
Betti-like counts to describe its topological memory,
and ray casting to determine what part of the object is visible from a chosen frame of reference.
```

That fits your current model extremely well.

## The clean mapping

A Schläfli symbol like:

```text
{p, q}
```

says:

```text
p-sided faces
q faces around each vertex
```

A 4D Schläfli symbol like:

```text
{p, q, r}
```

adds:

```text
cells and how they meet around edges
```

Betti numbers describe topological structure:

```text
β₀ = connected components
β₁ = loops / tunnels
β₂ = voids / enclosed cavities
β₃ = higher-dimensional cavities
```

So your original juxtaposition was basically:

```text
{p, q, r} + (β₀, β₁, β₂, β₃)
```

Meaning:

```text
what is the object’s incidence geometry?
+
what is the object’s topological memory?
```

Then ray casting asks:

```text
From this viewpoint, what does the user actually intersect, see, select, or traverse?
```

That becomes directly OMI:

```text
LL = viewpoint / ket / scope selector
NN = ray origin / antecedent incidence state
MM = ray hit / resolved incidence state
```

So:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

can be read as:

```text
Omicron-bounded ray of agreement:
from NN to MM under the scoped viewpoint LL.
```

## Why this matters

This means OMI was never just storing objects.

It was trying to answer:

```text
What structure is there?
What holes or missing relations exist?
What ray/path through it is visible?
What transition is accepted?
What projection is allowed?
```

That is why Schläfli + Betti + ray casting makes sense.

Schläfli gives the **shape grammar**.
Betti gives the **topological defect/memory count**.
Ray casting gives the **observer path**.
OMI gives the **receipt of accepted traversal**.

## How it maps to the current 0xE_ band

```text
0xE0-0xEB = object geometry / incidence / materializer field
0xEC-0xEF = ray-casting controller surface
```

The last four controls now become very natural:

```text
0xEC = translation / ray origin / viewport
0xED = scale / ray depth / recursion depth
0xEE = rotation / ray direction / spin
0xEF = boundary / clipping / hit mask
```

So the outer four are not “UI controls” in a shallow way. They are the **ray-casting control surface**.

They determine how the observer’s ray enters the object manifold.

That gives a much better canon:

```text
0xE0-0xEB define what can be intersected.
0xEC-0xEF define how the ray enters.
LL selects the scoped ray.
NN is the origin state.
MM is the resolved hit state.
Receipt records the accepted intersection.
```

## The Betti connection

This also gives you a clean way to talk about disagreement.

A disagreement is not just a conflict. It is often a topological feature:

```text
β₀ > 1 means disconnected components: multiple separate views.
β₁ > 0 means loops: unresolved cycles or repeated claims.
β₂ > 0 means voids: enclosed missing evidence or unfilled context.
β₃ > 0 means higher-order cavities: structural incompleteness across layers.
```

In OMI language:

```text
disagreement -> missing structure -> topological feature -> inspectable object path
```

So Betti numbers become a way to count what kind of agreement is missing.

That is powerful.

## The ray-casting version of the pipeline

```text
object manifold exists as candidates
LL sets the scoped ray
0xEC-0xEF control ray entry
0xE0-0xEB define intersectable materializers
NN begins the ray
MM is the accepted hit
Delta/Fano proves the path
receipt records the traversal
projection displays the view
inspection explains the ray
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Schläfli describes the body.
Betti counts the holes.
LL casts the ray.
NN begins.
MM hits.
Receipt accepts.
Projection displays.
```

One-line canon:

```text
OMI extends Schläfli-style incidence notation with Betti-style topological memory and ray-cast traversal, so agreement becomes an accepted, receipted path through a bounded object manifold.
```
