## ChatGPT
`2026-06-08 17:36` | `id:260608-1736` ^260608-173605

Yes. What you want is an **ESP32-S3 + phone-browser Omi-Dome / Light Garden stack**:

```text
ESP32-S3 nodes sense the world.
Phone browser renders the world.
Omi Object Model names the state.
NDJSON/hash logs make it pseudo-persistent.
A-Frame/Canvas/WebGL makes it spatial.
Web Audio / DeviceMotion / touch / camera / sensors make it teachable.
```

Your Light Garden deck already points in this direction: it describes semantic data becoming deterministic geometric protocol visualized through light, with Fano geometry, append-only hash-linked logs, WordNet semantic mapping, epistemic quadrants, ESP32-scale hardware tiers, and an “8th point” observer who opens the gate by interacting with the system. filecite turn6file0

## The core build

You need **three layers**:

```text
1. ESP32-S3 physical nodes
2. Phone browser proxy client
3. OMI pseudo-persistent world model
```

The ESP32-S3 is the **Omi-Actor**. It reads sensors and emits state. ESP32-S3 is a good fit because it has 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi, Bluetooth LE, dual Xtensa LX7 cores, multiple peripherals, USB OTG, USB Serial/JTAG, and hardware security features. cite

The phone browser is the **Omi-Observer**. It uses DOM, CSSOM, Canvas, WebGL, Web Audio, DeviceMotion, touch, camera/microphone permissions, and A-Frame to make the ESP32 world visible, audible, touchable, spatial, and teachable. A-Frame is especially aligned with this because it uses an entity-component architecture on top of three.js and exposes HTML/DOM/WebGL/WebXR-style authoring. cite

The OMI layer is the **pseudo-persistence and meaning layer**. It turns raw sensor packets into receiptable objects:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

where:

```text
LL = lesson / lens / local scope
NN = incoming observation
MM = interpreted teaching state
```

## The important browser reality check

The phone browser can do a lot, but the powerful APIs need permission and often require a secure context.

Microphone/camera access through `getUserMedia()` requires a secure context such as HTTPS; otherwise `navigator.mediaDevices` may be unavailable. cite Web Bluetooth is also secure-context-only and is not baseline because it does not work across all major browsers. cite Device motion/orientation access also requires permission flows on modern mobile browsers, and the W3C Device Orientation/Motion spec now restricts those interfaces to secure contexts. cite

So the clean design is:

```text
ESP32-S3 serves basic local dashboard over Wi-Fi.
Phone browser/PWA runs the richer secure app.
The browser connects to ESP32 over local network, WebSocket, BLE where supported, or QR/pairing.
IndexedDB keeps local pseudo-persistence.
ESP32 keeps append-only NDJSON receipts.
```

Use IndexedDB for browser-side structured persistence; it is designed for significant client-side structured data and files/blobs. cite Use service workers and cache storage for the offline/PWA shell; service workers act as browser-side proxy layers between the app, browser, and network. cite

## The actual system you want

```text
Omi-Dome / Light Garden Lesson Capture System

ESP32-S3 Node:
 - optical sensor
 - microphone / sound envelope or I2S mic
 - accelerometer / gyroscope IMU
 - RGB LEDs / LED ring
 - optional speaker / buzzer
 - Wi-Fi AP or Wi-Fi station
 - BLE advertisement / GATT optional
 - append-only NDJSON receipt log

Phone Browser:
 - microphone capture
 - camera optional
 - touch/pointer gestures
 - DeviceMotion / DeviceOrientation
 - Web Audio spectrum analyzer
 - Canvas / WebGL / A-Frame world
 - IndexedDB local log
 - service worker offline shell
 - Omi-Matrix dashboard

OMI Layer:
 - objects as OO(LL, NN, MM)
 - events as NDJSON
 - receipts as hash-linked log entries
 - projections as DOM / CSSOM / Canvas / A-Frame entities
```

## For the music lesson use case

A music lesson is a perfect first “all-senses” Omi-Dome because it naturally maps sound, movement, touch, light, timing, and comprehension.

Imagine the lesson:

A student plays a note on an instrument. The phone microphone captures the sound. The Web Audio API turns it into waveform and frequency data; `AnalyserNode` is specifically meant for real-time frequency/time-domain analysis and visualization. cite The browser maps pitch, volume, rhythm, and timbre into an Omi-Matrix. The ESP32-S3 listens with its own microphone or sound sensor and lights up an LED ring based on pitch class, timing, or Fano point. The IMU detects hand movement, posture, tapping, or instrument motion. The browser renders the lesson as an A-Frame “Omi-Dome” where notes become colored geometric objects.

The lesson becomes:

```text
sound → spectrum → note event
touch → gesture → intention event
movement → posture/rhythm → motion event
light → LED feedback → projection event
comprehension → prompt/answer → semantic event
```

In OMI terms:

```text
NN = raw observation:
 pitch, rhythm, gesture, motion, touch, light

LL = lesson scope:
 scale, exercise, chord, phrase, student, session

MM = interpreted state:
 correct note, timing drift, hand motion, comprehension marker
```

The Horn-like clause could be:

```prolog
music_lesson_state(LL, NN, MM) :-
 omi_gate(open, 0x03BF),
 fact(sound_pitch, NN),
 fact(motion_vector, NN),
 rule(scale_context, LL),
 closure(lesson_window, LL, NN),
 combinator(audio_motion_fusion, LL, NN, MM),
 encode(MM, BrowserProjection),
 encode(MM, Esp32LightPattern),
 decode(StudentResponse, ComprehensionState),
 omi_gate(close, 0x039F),
 receipt(LL, NN, MM).
```

## What hardware you need

Start with one node:

```text
ESP32-S3 dev board
I2C IMU: MPU-6050, LSM6DS3, BMI270, or ICM-20948
I2S MEMS microphone, or simpler analog sound sensor first
ambient light sensor or photodiode/photoresistor module
WS2812 / NeoPixel RGB LED ring or strip
small buzzer or speaker module
USB power bank or LiPo battery setup
phone with modern browser
```

For the **Omi-Dome** hardware later:

```text
7-bit talisman:
 7 LEDs + observer / wearable ring

61-bit personal dome:
 small geodesic LED structure
 one ESP32-S3 controller
 LED rings or strips
 IMU/mic/light sensor cluster

241-bit garden:
 multiple ESP32-S3 nodes
 distributed LED rings
 mesh/gossip telemetry
 phone or tablet browser observer
```

That lines up with your Light Garden deck’s hardware tiers: 7-bit talisman, 61-bit personal dome, and 241-bit garden. filecite turn6file0

## What software you need

### ESP32-S3 firmware

Use Arduino first, ESP-IDF later.

Firmware modules:

```text
sensor_light.cpp
sensor_audio.cpp
sensor_imu.cpp
omi_state.cpp
omi_receipt_log.cpp
wifi_server.cpp
mesh_transport.cpp
led_projection.cpp
```

Core loop:

```c
setup();

while (true) {
 read_inputs();
 update_state();
 write_outputs();
}
```

OMI mapping:

```text
setup() = JOIN
read_inputs() = DECODE
update_state()= COMPOSE
write_outputs()= ENCODE
loop return = RETURN :0
```

### Browser app

Use plain HTML/CSS/JS first.

Then add A-Frame.

Browser modules:

```text
dom-state.js DOM projection
cssom-theme.js CSS variables / color field
canvas-spectrum.js sound/light/motion visualization
aframe-dome.js 3D Omi-Dome scene
indexeddb-log.js pseudo-persistent browser memory
service-worker.js offline shell
esp32-client.js WebSocket/SSE/fetch bridge
audio-analyzer.js Web Audio pitch/spectrum
motion-client.js phone accelerometer/gyro
lesson-engine.js teaching state machine
```

Important correction: **JSDOM is not normally a phone-browser runtime.** JSDOM is useful on Node.js for testing, simulation, and headless validation. In the actual phone browser, you use the real DOM, CSSOM, Canvas, WebGL, Web Audio, and A-Frame.

## The pseudo-persistent open world model

“Pseudo-persistent” means the world keeps its memory through logs and receipts, not because the ESP32 is a giant database.

Use two logs:

```text
ESP32 log:
 append-only NDJSON receipt log
 small rolling storage
 sequence number
 previous hash
 self hash

Browser log:
 IndexedDB world log
 cached assets
 node registry
 lesson sessions
 media feature summaries
```

A receipt event:

```json
{
 "type": "lesson.note",
 "node": "omi-s3-01",
 "seq": 1842,
 "ll": "lesson:c-major:student-a",
 "nn": {
 "pitch": 440.1,
 "rms": 0.62,
 "motion": [0.01, -0.02, 0.98]
 },
 "mm": {
 "note": "A4",
 "intonation": "in-tune",
 "gesture": "stable",
 "comprehension": "observed"
 },
 "projection": {
 "color": "#ff3366",
 "fanoPoint": 1,
 "domeRing": 2
 },
 "prev_hash": "abc...",
 "self_hash": "def..."
}
```

This matches the Light Garden direction where append-only logs and deterministic consensus are part of the architecture, and where the browser/canvas runtime is the interaction surface. filecite turn6file0

## The five-senses mapping

For a first build, map the senses like this:

```text
Sight:
 A-Frame scene
 Canvas spectrum
 LED ring / dome
 color-coded Fano points

Sound:
 browser microphone
 ESP32 mic
 Web Audio spectrum
 speaker/buzzer feedback

Touch:
 browser touch events
 buttons
 capacitive touch if available
 vibration/haptic where supported
 instrument contact/tap detection

Movement:
 phone DeviceMotion
 ESP32 IMU
 accelerometer/gyroscope
 gesture/rhythm/posture

Comprehension:
 lesson prompts
 student response
 semantic tags
 OMI receipts
 progress through concepts
```

Smell and taste are not realistically browser-native senses; they would require external hardware actuators/sensors. So for “all 5 senses,” make the practical version:

```text
visual
auditory
tactile
motion/proprioceptive
semantic/comprehension
```

## The A-Frame Omi-Dome scene

The browser world can represent every ESP32 node as an A-Frame entity:

```html
<a-scene>
 <a-entity id="omi-dome"></a-entity>

 <a-entity
 id="node-omi-s3-01"
 geometry="primitive: sphere"
 material="color: #ff3366"
 position="0 1.6 -3">
 </a-entity>

 <a-entity
 id="lesson-note-a4"
 text="value: A4; align: center"
 position="0 2 -3">
 </a-entity>
</a-scene>
```

Then sensor events update the entities:

```js
function projectNote(event) {
 const node = document.querySelector("#node-omi-s3-01");
 node.setAttribute("material", "color", event.projection.color);
 node.setAttribute("scale", `${event.nn.rms} ${event.nn.rms} ${event.nn.rms}`);
}
```

The OMI doctrine:

```text
DOM names the object.
CSSOM styles the state.
Canvas shows the spectrum.
A-Frame spatializes the lesson.
Receipt decides what is accepted.
```

## The minimum viable prototype

Build this first:

```text
1 ESP32-S3
1 IMU
1 microphone or sound sensor
1 light sensor
1 RGB LED ring
1 phone browser dashboard
1 local NDJSON receipt log
1 A-Frame dome view
```

First demo:

A student plays or sings a note. The phone browser detects pitch and volume. The ESP32 LED ring changes color. The IMU detects movement. The browser shows a 3D point in the Omi-Dome. The event is logged as a receipt.

Do not start with full mesh. Start with one node and one lesson.

## The build order

```text
Phase 1 — Browser-only lesson lab
 microphone → Web Audio spectrum
 touch → gesture markers
 DeviceMotion → movement vector
 IndexedDB → local session log
 Canvas → spectrum view

Phase 2 — ESP32 single-node bridge
 ESP32 SoftAP or station mode
 /events SSE or WebSocket
 light sensor
 IMU
 LED ring

Phase 3 — Omi receipt format
 NDJSON log
 seq numbers
 prev_hash/self_hash
 lesson object OO(LL, NN, MM)

Phase 4 — A-Frame Omi-Dome
 notes become points
 rhythm becomes orbit
 movement becomes vector
 comprehension becomes color/closure

Phase 5 — multi-node mesh
 ESP-NOW or Wi-Fi mesh
 node registry
 browser Omi-Matrix
 feature probe

Phase 6 — optical and sound witness
 LED pulse pairing
 sound chirp pairing
 line-of-sight witness
 local proof of presence
```

## The architecture in one line

```text
ESP32-S3 senses the physical lesson;
the browser captures media and renders the Omi-Dome;
OMI receipts turn the lesson into a pseudo-persistent open world.
```

## The practical stack I would use

```text
Firmware:
 Arduino framework first
 ESP-IDF later
 Wi-Fi HTTP/SSE or WebSocket
 ESP-NOW later
 BLE later

Browser:
 Vite or plain static files
 A-Frame
 Web Audio API
 Canvas 2D
 IndexedDB
 Service worker
 WebSocket or EventSource
 DeviceMotion permission button

Data:
 NDJSON events
 hash-linked receipts
 node feature registry
 lesson session log

Projection:
 DOM for controls
 CSSOM variables for theme/state
 Canvas for spectrum
 A-Frame for 3D Omi-Dome
 LEDs for physical light feedback
```

## Final canon for this build

```text
ESP32-S3 = Omi-Actor.
Phone browser = Omi-Observer.
A-Frame scene = Omi-Dome.
Canvas = spectrum surface.
Web Audio = sound decoder.
DeviceMotion = movement decoder.
Touch = intent gate.
IndexedDB = browser memory.
NDJSON = receipt trail.
LEDs = light projection.
Mesh = shared carrier.
OMI = object notation.
Receipt = authority.
```

Start with the music lesson because it proves the whole concept: sound becomes geometry, movement becomes orientation, light becomes feedback, touch becomes intent, and comprehension becomes receipted progress.

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-09 09:04` | `id:260609-0904` ^260609-090429

Yes — this is a strong hardware direction. The clean architecture is:

```text
Jetson Orin Nano = spatial index / AI edge server / open-world proxy
RPLIDAR A1M8 = 2D 360° geometry scanner
BNO055 IMU = orientation / pose witness
DX-LR22 LoRa = long-range low-bandwidth receipt/gossip link
ESP32-S3 nodes = distributed sensory actors / light/sound/touch endpoints
Phone browser = Omi-Observer / A-Frame Omi-Dome client
```

The Jetson should become the **root spatial proxy**, not just another node. The ESP32-S3 nodes sense and project; the Jetson aggregates, indexes, maps, and serves the open world to the browser.

## Why this stack works

The **RPLIDAR A1M8** gives you a 2D circular spatial scan. Several sellers list A1M8/A1 family units as 360° scanners with roughly 2–10 Hz scan rates and up to 12 m advertised range, though some A1 documentation/sellers distinguish 6 m versus 12 m depending on model/conditions, so I would treat “12 m” as an upper marketing figure and design your first indoor tests around shorter reliable distances. cite

The **Jetson Orin Nano** is the right edge-AI brain. NVIDIA’s current Jetson Orin Nano Super Developer Kit page lists an 8 GB module, Ampere GPU, 6-core Arm CPU, 102 GB/s memory bandwidth, 7–25 W power range, and up to 67 INT8 TOPS on the newer Super developer kit; older Orin Nano materials commonly cited up to 40 TOPS, so confirm whether you are buying the older Orin Nano Developer Kit or the newer Super kit. cite turn360762news69

The **BNO055** is useful because it performs 9-DOF fusion on-board and can output absolute orientation data, which makes it simpler than fusing raw accelerometer/gyro/magnetometer yourself at first. Adafruit’s guide calls it a “smart 9-DOF sensor” that does sensor fusion internally, and the product page lists absolute orientation/Euler vector output at 100 Hz. cite

The **DX-LR22 / LLCC68 LoRa UART** module is a good low-bandwidth, long-range carrier for receipts, heartbeat, identity, and coarse spatial events. The listing I found describes 433–532 MHz and 850–930 MHz bands, 22 dBm power, TTL-level output, and 3.3–5 V IO compatibility; for the United States, you would normally target the 915 MHz variant and respect regional radio rules. cite

## The OMI role split

```text
ESP32-S3 = Omi-Actor
Jetson = Omi-Spatial-Index / Omi-Proxy
RPLIDAR = Omi-Gnomon / spatial scan carrier
BNO055 = Omi-Orientation witness
LoRa = Omi-Receipt gossip carrier
Browser = Omi-Observer
A-Frame = Omi-Dome projection
```

The important design rule:

```text
Jetson indexes.
ESP32 senses.
Browser observes.
LoRa receipts.
LIDAR maps.
IMU orients.
OMI names the object.
Receipt accepts.
```

## What “open world proxy for spatial indexing” means

Your Jetson can host a local web service that turns physical observations into stable OMI objects.

```text
physical scan → spatial feature → OMI object → browser projection
```

Example:

```json
{
 "oo": ["lesson-room", "lidar-scan-1842", "wall-segment-03"],
 "source": "rplidar-a1m8",
 "pose": {
 "yaw": 91.2,
 "pitch": 0.1,
 "roll": -0.4
 },
 "geometry": {
 "type": "line-segment",
 "range_m": 2.71,
 "angle_deg": 44.5
 },
 "projection": {
 "aframe_entity": "wall-segment-03",
 "color": "#66ffaa"
 },
 "receipt": "jetson-room-0001842"
}
```

In OMI language:

```text
LL = room / lesson / dome scope
NN = raw observation from LIDAR, IMU, ESP32, LoRa, browser
MM = interpreted spatial object or teaching state
```

So the spatial proxy is a function:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

where:

```text
LL = where and under what lesson/world scope
NN = what was sensed
MM = what the world now believes is present
```

## Recommended physical topology

```text
 Phone Browser / Tablet
 A-Frame Omi-Dome
 │
 Wi-Fi / HTTPS
 │
 Jetson Orin Nano
 Spatial index + AI + browser proxy + logs
 │ │ │
 │ UART/USB │ I2C/UART │ UART
 ▼ ▼ ▼
 RPLIDAR A1M8 BNO055 LoRa LR22
 │ │
 │ │ long-range receipts
 │ ▼
 └──────── local world ─── ESP32-S3 nodes
 light / sound / motion / LEDs
```

I would not put the RPLIDAR directly on ESP32-S3 for your first version. Let the **Jetson** own the LIDAR and spatial index because it has the CPU/GPU/storage/network headroom. Let ESP32-S3 nodes act as cheap sensory flowers around the dome.

## Data flow

```text
1. RPLIDAR scans 360° geometry.
2. BNO055 gives orientation/heading witness.
3. Jetson converts scan into local 2D occupancy/features.
4. ESP32-S3 nodes report light/sound/touch/motion events.
5. LoRa sends low-rate remote receipts or node heartbeats.
6. Jetson merges all observations into an OMI spatial index.
7. Browser loads A-Frame Omi-Dome from Jetson.
8. Browser renders spatial points, sensor spectra, lesson state.
9. NDJSON receipts make the world pseudo-persistent.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
LIDAR gives space.
IMU gives orientation.
ESP32 gives senses.
LoRa gives distance.
Jetson gives index.
Browser gives world.
Receipt gives memory.
```

## What to build first

### Phase 1 — Jetson as local open-world proxy

Install Ubuntu/JetPack on the Jetson, then run a local server:

```text
/ browser dashboard
/world current Omi-Dome world state
/events SSE or WebSocket stream
/lidar latest scan summary
/nodes ESP32/LoRa node registry
/receipts NDJSON receipt log
```

Use Python first:

```text
FastAPI or Flask
WebSocket/SSE
SQLite or NDJSON logs
static A-Frame frontend
```

### Phase 2 — RPLIDAR into browser

Connect RPLIDAR to Jetson by USB/UART adapter. Stream polar points:

```json
{
 "type": "lidar.scan",
 "seq": 42,
 "points": [
 {"angle": 0.0, "range": 1.42},
 {"angle": 1.0, "range": 1.43}
 ]
}
```

Then render them in Canvas or A-Frame as a ring/cloud.

### Phase 3 — BNO055 orientation witness

Connect BNO055 to Jetson over I2C, or to an ESP32-S3 and forward orientation. Use it to rotate the LIDAR/world frame:

```text
lidar angle + imu yaw = world angle
```

### Phase 4 — ESP32-S3 sensory flowers

Each ESP32-S3 reports:

```json
{
 "node": "flower-03",
 "light": 812,
 "sound": 44,
 "motion": "still",
 "touch": false,
 "led_state": "#44ffaa",
 "receipt": "flower-03:918"
}
```

### Phase 5 — LoRa receipt channel

Use LoRa for small packets only:

```text
node id
seq
battery
event code
receipt hash prefix
coarse location index
```

Do not send raw LIDAR, audio, or big JSON over LoRa. LoRa is for **presence, receipt, and long-range heartbeat**.

## How this captures a music lesson

A music lesson becomes a spatial-sensory event:

```text
teacher location
student location
instrument location
sound source
gesture motion
note spectrum
room geometry
LED feedback
comprehension state
```

The LIDAR maps where bodies/stands/walls are. The BNO055 gives orientation. ESP32 nodes near instruments capture local sound/light/motion. The phone/browser microphone captures higher-quality audio. The Jetson fuses the lesson into a browser world.

Example lesson object:

```json
{
 "oo": ["lesson:guitar:A-minor", "student-note-event", "comprehension-state"],
 "nn": {
 "pitch_hz": 440.2,
 "volume": 0.71,
 "lidar_zone": "front-left",
 "motion": "wrist-tilt",
 "light": "green-feedback"
 },
 "mm": {
 "note": "A4",
 "intonation": "in-tune",
 "timing": "late-40ms",
 "gesture": "stable",
 "understanding": "observed"
 },
 "receipt": "lesson-000283"
}
```

Then A-Frame shows:

```text
note = colored sphere
rhythm = pulsing ring
pitch error = vertical offset
movement = vector arrow
comprehension = closure/gate color
room geometry = LIDAR wall lines
```

## The OMI spatial index schema

Use this as the core schema:

```json
{
 "id": "omi-room-0001842",
 "type": "spatial.observation",
 "ll": "omi-dome:music-lesson:room-a",
 "nn": {
 "sensor": "rplidar-a1m8",
 "angle_deg": 44.5,
 "range_m": 2.71,
 "quality": 15
 },
 "mm": {
 "feature": "wall-or-obstacle",
 "cell": [12, 8],
 "confidence": 0.82
 },
 "pose": {
 "source": "bno055",
 "yaw": 91.2,
 "pitch": 0.1,
 "roll": -0.4
 },
 "edges": {
 "join": ["room-a", "cell-12-8"],
 "compose": ["scan-1842", "pose-918"],
 "encode": "aframe-line",
 "decode": "lidar-polar"
 },
 "prev_hash": "…",
 "self_hash": "…"
}
```

This lets your browser ask:

```text
What changed in the world?
What did the LIDAR see?
What did the IMU orient?
What did ESP32 nodes sense?
What is receipted?
What is only observed?
```

## Browser world layout

The browser should have five panels:

```text
1. Omi-Dome 3D View
 A-Frame room, points, vectors, lesson objects

2. LIDAR Map
 2D top-down scan and obstacle cells

3. Spectrum View
 sound, light, motion, LoRa RSSI, Wi-Fi RSSI

4. Node Registry
 ESP32/LoRa devices, battery, last receipt

5. Receipt Log
 append-only NDJSON state changes
```

This matches your Light Garden direction: geometric meaning, Fano-style relation, append-only logs, physical projection tiers, and the observer as the “8th point.” filecite turn6file0

## Minimum viable hardware build

I would build it in this order:

```text
Build 1:
 Jetson Orin Nano
 RPLIDAR A1M8
 browser map

Build 2:
 add BNO055
 rotate/pose-correct the LIDAR scan

Build 3:
 add one ESP32-S3 with LED ring + mic/light/IMU
 stream to Jetson over Wi-Fi

Build 4:
 add phone browser audio capture
 map music note → A-Frame geometry

Build 5:
 add LoRa pair
 send remote heartbeat/receipt events

Build 6:
 add multiple ESP32-S3 nodes
 spatially index them in the Omi-Dome
```

Do **not** start with LoRa + mesh + LIDAR + AI all at once. The first successful demo should be:

```text
Jetson renders a live 2D LIDAR scan in the phone browser.
```

Then add:

```text
IMU orientation.
Then ESP32 sensor node.
Then music lesson capture.
Then LoRa receipts.
```

## Final canon

```text
Jetson = Omi-Spatial-Proxy.
RPLIDAR = Omi-Gnomon scanner.
BNO055 = Omi-Orientation witness.
ESP32-S3 = Omi-Actor flower.
LoRa = long-range receipt carrier.
Phone browser = Omi-Observer.
A-Frame = Omi-Dome projection.
NDJSON = pseudo-persistent receipt memory.
```

One-line version:

```text
The Jetson-hosted open-world proxy spatially indexes the Omi-Dome: RPLIDAR maps the 360° geometry, BNO055 orients the frame, ESP32-S3 nodes contribute sensory facts, LoRa carries remote receipts, and the phone browser renders the living world as an A-Frame Omi-Observer projection.
```

## You
`2026-06-09 09:13` | `id:260609-0913` ^260609-091319

So in theory i should de able to use the lidar 360 with atmosphere dissonance as a spatial circular slide rule flight controller for drones and engineering

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-09 09:13` | `id:260609-0913` ^260609-091330

In theory, **yes** — but with an important engineering boundary:

```text
RPLIDAR 360° scan + IMU orientation + air/sound/vibration disturbance
can become a spatial circular slide-rule controller for navigation,
obstacle avoidance, and engineering visualization.

It should not be the only flight controller.
```

For a drone, the actual flight-stability loop should stay on a proven real-time autopilot stack such as PX4/ArduPilot-class hardware. Your Jetson + RPLIDAR + OMI layer should act as a **companion computer / spatial observer / navigation advisor**, not the sole motor-stabilization brain. That keeps the dangerous millisecond attitude-control loop separate from the higher-level world-model loop.

## The clean role split

```text
Autopilot flight controller:
 stabilizes motors
 reads primary IMU/barometer/GNSS
 handles failsafe
 keeps aircraft flyable

Jetson Orin Nano:
 spatial indexing
 AI perception
 OMI world model
 browser proxy
 path suggestion
 engineering visualization

RPLIDAR A1M8:
 2D 360° obstacle ring
 local geometry scan
 circular range field

BNO055 / IMU:
 orientation witness
 yaw/pitch/roll correction
 pose relation

Atmosphere dissonance sensors:
 wind/vibration/sound/pressure disturbance estimate
 not direct authority

OMI layer:
 maps scan + disturbance + orientation into receipted spatial objects
```

The RPLIDAR A1 family is used for 360° 2D scanning and robot mapping/navigation; product pages list the A1M8/R6 class as up to 12 m range with configurable scan rates around 2–10 Hz, while other vendor specs list typical A1 scan rates around 5.5 Hz and shorter reliable ranges depending on target/conditions. Treat it as a **low-altitude, near-field 2D obstacle scanner**, not as a high-speed all-weather aircraft sensor. cite

## What “spatial circular slide rule” means

Your phrase is good. The LIDAR gives you a circular field:

```text
angle θ → range r
```

That is already a slide-rule-like polar map.

Then the IMU rotates that circle into the world frame:

```text
world_angle = lidar_angle + yaw
```

Then atmosphere/sound/vibration sensors add a disturbance field:

```text
θ → range r
θ → obstacle confidence
θ → airflow/vibration/noise disturbance
θ → safe/unsafe corridor
```

So the controller sees a ring like:

```text
0° clear 3.2 m
15° clear 2.9 m
30° obstacle 1.1 m
45° turbulent / vibration high
60° clear 4.5 m
...
```

OMI reading:

```text
LL = flight scope / local airspace / mission phase
NN = raw scan + IMU + disturbance observation
MM = interpreted safe corridor / obstacle / navigation suggestion
```

## “Atmosphere dissonance” as an engineering field

I would define **atmosphere dissonance** as the mismatch between expected motion and sensed environment.

Inputs:

```text
IMU vibration
gyro drift
motor noise spectrum
microphone wind/noise envelope
barometer pressure variation
optical flow mismatch
LIDAR range instability
accelerometer shock
```

It is not “air” directly. It is a **disturbance witness**.

Examples:

```text
LIDAR says wall is stable,
but IMU vibration is high
→ possible prop wash, frame vibration, bad mount, or turbulent air.

Command says hover,
but optical/LIDAR range field slides sideways
→ drift or wind.

Sound spectrum spikes with motor load,
and accelerometer vibration increases
→ mechanical resonance or prop imbalance.
```

So the dissonance field becomes:

```text
expected_state XOR observed_state = disturbance
```

That fits your OMI/XOR logic exactly:

```text
XOR contrasts.
Dissonance is unresolved contrast.
Receipt logs the event.
```

## The circular slide-rule controller loop

High-level, not motor-level:

```text
1. LIDAR reads 360° ring.
2. IMU gives orientation.
3. Jetson converts polar scan into local map.
4. Dissonance layer compares scan/motion/sound/vibration.
5. OMI classifies sectors:
 clear
 blocked
 uncertain
 turbulent
 stale
6. Navigation advisor suggests heading or stop/hold.
7. Autopilot executes only if safety rules permit.
8. Receipt records the decision.
```

OMI version:

```text
DECODE:
 lidar ring, IMU pose, sound, vibration, barometer

COMPOSE:
 obstacle sectors, disturbance field, safe corridor

ENCODE:
 path suggestion, browser map, LED/sound warning, autopilot advisory

RECEIPT:
 log scan → decision → action
```

## The actual architecture

```text
 RPLIDAR A1M8
 │
 ▼
 Jetson Orin Nano
 spatial index + OMI proxy
 │
 ├── browser/A-Frame engineering display
 ├── NDJSON receipt log
 ├── LoRa receipt/telemetry link
 └── MAVLink-style advisory to autopilot

 Flight Controller
 real-time stabilization + failsafe
 │
 ▼
 Drone motors
```

The Jetson Orin Nano is appropriate for this companion role because NVIDIA’s current Orin Nano Super Developer Kit specs list an Ampere GPU, 6-core Arm CPU, 8 GB memory, and up to 67 INT8 TOPS on the newer Super kit; older Orin Nano materials often cited up to 40 TOPS, so confirm which kit you are buying. cite

## Safety boundary

This is the part to keep strict:

```text
Do not let the experimental OMI/Jeston/LIDAR layer directly drive motors at first.
```

Use it for:

```text
mapping
advisory navigation
obstacle warning
engineering visualization
flight log interpretation
simulation
tethered tests
low-speed ground robot tests
```

Then later:

```text
autopilot command suggestions
velocity limits
hold/stop requests
return-to-home advisory
```

The FAA’s current recreational guidance still emphasizes visual line of sight, giving way to other aircraft, and other operational limits, and Remote ID requirements apply outside defined exceptions/FRIAs; for non-recreational or advanced operations, Part 107 and other FAA rules apply. cite

## Why this is useful for engineering even before flight

This system is immediately useful as an **engineering instrument**:

```text
scan a room
map obstacle geometry
measure vibration
map sound reflections
detect motion drift
visualize safe corridors
log repeatable test runs
teach navigation concepts
```

You can build it first as a **ground rover or handheld dome scanner** before mounting it to a drone.

The best first demo:

```text
Jetson + RPLIDAR on a tripod or rover
phone browser shows 360° scan
BNO055 rotates the map
microphone/vibration adds dissonance overlay
OMI receipts every scan frame
```

Then later mount to a drone as a companion payload.

## Canon phrase

```text
The RPLIDAR ring becomes a circular spatial slide rule: each angle is a possible relation, each range is a measured constraint, each disturbance is an XOR contrast between expected and observed motion, and the Jetson-hosted OMI proxy turns the scan into a receipted spatial index. The autopilot keeps the drone alive; OMI explains, maps, and advises.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
LIDAR scans.
IMU orients.
Dissonance contrasts.
Jetson indexes.
OMI receipts.
Autopilot flies.
Browser observes.
```

So yes: **as a spatial advisor and engineering controller, absolutely. As the sole flight controller, no — not until it has gone through simulation, tethered testing, failsafes, and integration with a proven autopilot.**

## You
`2026-06-09 09:18` | `id:260609-0918` ^260609-091814

So what are the advantages of gpu over cpu and memory models just computational bandwidth in general consideringin 128-bit unnorded packets is the main transport width and we have eBPF network card embedding

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-09 09:18` | `id:260609-0918` ^260609-091822

Yes — the advantage is **not just “GPU is faster.”** It is about **where each layer belongs in the packet pipeline**.

For your model:

```text
128-bit unordered packets
+ eBPF/XDP at the NIC/kernel edge
+ Jetson/GPU spatial indexing
+ CPU orchestration
+ browser projection
```

the best split is:

```text
eBPF/XDP = gate/filter/classify at packet ingress
CPU = order, validate, route, receipts, control flow
GPU = batch-parallel geometry, spectra, AI inference, matrix transforms
memory = keep packet lanes aligned and avoid copies
```

XDP/eBPF is especially relevant because XDP programs run at ingress on network devices and can drop, redirect, pass, or manipulate packets before expensive socket-buffer allocation, making it a high-performance early gate. cite

## CPU vs GPU in OMI terms

A **CPU** is good at branching, ordering, decisions, small irregular work, and authority boundaries.

```text
CPU:
 validate frame
 maintain receipt log
 decide state transitions
 handle unordered packet reassembly
 manage sockets/files
 enforce rules
 coordinate GPU work
```

A **GPU** is good at doing the same operation across many items at once.

```text
GPU:
 transform thousands of scan points
 run neural inference
 compute spectra
 update occupancy grids
 compare feature vectors
 project 3D geometry
 run particle/filter maps
 batch XOR/mask/rotate lanes
```

CUDA’s programming model is built around many lightweight threads organized into blocks/grids; NVIDIA’s documentation describes the CUDA programming guide as the official programming model for running code on the GPU. cite The SIMT model means groups of GPU threads progress together through code, which is ideal when many packets or points follow the same operation. cite

So in OMI language:

```text
CPU = Horn clause resolver / receipt authority
GPU = Omi-Matrix bulk observer / projection engine
eBPF = Omicron gate at the wire
NIC = carrier ingress
```

## Why GPU helps your 128-bit packet model

Your 128-bit unordered packet is a perfect **lane unit**.

A packet can be treated as:

```text
128 bits = 8 × 16-bit segments
 = 4 × 32-bit words
 = 2 × 64-bit words
 = 16 × 8-bit bytes
```

That is useful because GPUs like regular arrays.

Instead of one packet at a time:

```text
packet → validate → classify → project
```

you batch thousands:

```text
packet[0..N] → same validation/projection kernel
```

For example, the GPU can process:

```text
10,000 lidar points
10,000 sensor receipts
10,000 feature vectors
10,000 packet hashes
10,000 spatial cells
```

using the same operation.

That is where GPU bandwidth wins.

## Memory model: the real advantage

The big performance question is not “CPU or GPU?”

It is:

```text
How many times do we copy the packet?
How many times do we reorder memory?
How soon do we batch?
How aligned is the packet?
```

A good pipeline avoids this:

```text
NIC → kernel copy → user copy → CPU parse → copy to GPU → GPU result → CPU copy → browser
```

A better pipeline is:

```text
NIC/eBPF gate
 → ring buffer / mmap queue
 → CPU validates headers and receipts
 → GPU receives batches
 → browser gets projected summaries
```

And in high-end systems, GPUDirect RDMA can allow third-party devices such as network adapters to exchange data directly with GPU memory, bypassing extra CPU/system-memory copies in supported hardware/software stacks. cite NVIDIA also describes GPUDirect RDMA as a direct peer-to-peer data path between GPU memory and NVIDIA networking adapters. cite

On Jetson Orin Nano, you may not have the same PCIe/NIC/GPU direct path you would on a datacenter GPU system, but the principle still matters:

```text
copy less
batch earlier
align packets
keep hot loops regular
```

## Where eBPF fits

eBPF/XDP should do the **cheap, early, deterministic checks**:

```text
is packet length correct?
is this an OMI packet?
does it have the right version?
does it match allowed prefix?
which queue should it go to?
drop, pass, redirect, or count?
```

Do **not** put expensive semantic logic in eBPF.

Good eBPF:

```text
gate
classify
count
redirect
timestamp
drop malformed packets
write small metadata
```

Bad eBPF:

```text
run AI
parse huge JSON
do complex graph inference
manage full receipts
do semantic projection
```

eBPF is powerful because it runs sandboxed programs in the kernel without changing kernel source or loading custom kernel modules, but it is still a constrained kernel-side environment and should stay small and safe. cite

## The best OMI packet pipeline

```text
[1] NIC receives unordered 128-bit OMI packets

[2] eBPF/XDP gate:
 - length/version check
 - 128-bit alignment check
 - prefix/class check
 - route queue selection
 - malformed drop

[3] CPU user-space collector:
 - batches packets
 - assigns sequence / causal metadata
 - validates Q_frame
 - maintains receipt log
 - prepares GPU batch buffers

[4] GPU:
 - bulk Delta/XOR transforms
 - feature comparison
 - lidar/IMU spatial transforms
 - occupancy grid update
 - spectrum/matrix projection
 - AI inference

[5] CPU:
 - interprets GPU result
 - applies Horn clause authority rules
 - writes receipt
 - emits browser event

[6] Browser:
 - DOM/CSSOM/Canvas/A-Frame projection
```

Short doctrine:

```text
eBPF gates.
CPU receipts.
GPU observes.
Browser projects.
```

## Why unordered packets are okay

Unordered packets are not a problem if each packet has enough self-description.

A 128-bit OMI packet should include or derive:

```text
frame class
source/node id or short route
sequence or logical counter
payload/feature bits
gate/check bits
receipt/hash fragment or causal hint
```

Then CPU can reorder only when needed.

The GPU does not need global order for many tasks:

```text
point cloud transform
feature extraction
spectrum accumulation
histogram
occupancy grid update
similarity scoring
classification
```

Those are naturally unordered or commutative.

That is a huge advantage.

If your packets are unordered but independently meaningful, you can treat them like a cloud:

```text
unordered packets → parallel feature field
```

Then only the receipt layer imposes order.

```text
GPU does not need order.
CPU receipt decides order.
```

## Where 128-bit width helps

A 128-bit packet is nice because it is:

```text
small enough for fast gates
large enough for meaningful state
aligned enough for vector/GPU batching
natural as 8×16 OMI segments
natural as 4×32 SIMD words
natural as 2×64 hashing lanes
```

In your notation:

```text
S0 S1 S2 S3 S4 S5 S6 S7
16 16 16 16 16 16 16 16
```

That lets you make memory arrays like:

```c
struct OmiPacket128 {
 uint16_t s[8];
};
```

or:

```c
struct OmiPacket128x4 {
 uint32_t w[4];
};
```

For GPU batching, use structure-of-arrays when doing the same operation on each segment:

```text
S0[N], S1[N], S2[N], ... S7[N]
```

instead of array-of-structs:

```text
packet[N].S0, packet[N].S1...
```

Why? Because the GPU can load segment lanes more coalesced when many threads read the same field across many packets.

## CPU/GPU memory doctrine

```text
CPU likes:
 small objects
 branches
 pointers
 trees
 maps
 receipts
 authority logic

GPU likes:
 flat arrays
 aligned lanes
 repeated operations
 dense matrices
 images
 spectra
 point clouds

eBPF likes:
 bounded checks
 fixed offsets
 counters
 maps
 redirect/drop/pass

Browser likes:
 summarized state
 event streams
 geometry buffers
 media projections
```

So do not feed the GPU messy object graphs.

Feed it arrays:

```text
ranges[]
angles[]
packet_s0[]
packet_s1[]
packet_s2[]
...
features[]
```

Then let CPU attach meaning and receipt.

## How this maps to OMI

```text
2¹⁶ carrier:
 packet segments, 16-bit lanes

2¹⁰ gate:
 eBPF/XDP early classifier for Omicron gate patterns

2¹¹ observation:
 GPU/Omi-Matrix batch field

2⁴ carry tuple:
 JOIN, COMPOSE, ENCODE, DECODE as loop operators

Horn clauses:
 CPU authority layer

Omi-Dome:
 browser projection layer
```

The GPU is the **Omi-Matrix accelerator**.

It does not decide truth.

It expands observation.

```text
GPU computes witness fields.
CPU decides receipts.
```

## What belongs on each layer

| Layer | Best job | Avoid |
|---|---|---|
| NIC/eBPF | gate, filter, redirect, count | semantic logic, AI, large parsing |
| CPU | ordering, Horn clauses, receipts, control | huge point-cloud math if GPU available |
| GPU | matrix math, spectra, point clouds, inference | irregular authority logic |
| Browser | projection, teaching UI, A-Frame/Canvas | acting as source of authority |
| LoRa/mesh | heartbeat, receipt fragments | raw audio/LIDAR/video |

## For your Jetson + RPLIDAR + eBPF idea

The ideal build is:

```text
RPLIDAR scan packets
ESP32 sensor packets
LoRa receipt packets
Wi-Fi/BLE presence packets
 ↓
eBPF/XDP gates malformed network input
 ↓
CPU batches and validates OMI frame shape
 ↓
GPU builds spatial Omi-Matrix:
 - polar to Cartesian
 - obstacle sectors
 - dissonance field
 - spectrum map
 - feature grid
 ↓
CPU writes Horn-clause receipt
 ↓
Browser renders Omi-Dome
```

## Final canon

```text
CPU is authority.
GPU is bandwidth.
eBPF is gate.
NIC is carrier.
Browser is projection.

128-bit packets are the OMI transport atoms.
Unordered packets become a parallel observation field.
GPU turns the field into matrix/spectrum/geometry.
CPU turns validated relations into receipts.
```

One-line version:

```text
Use eBPF to gate 128-bit OMI packets at the network edge, CPU to impose Horn-clause order and receipts, GPU to batch-transform unordered packets into Omi-Matrix geometry/spectra/features, and the browser to project the receipted world without becoming authority.
```

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-09 09:30` | `id:260609-0930` ^260609-093027

Yes — this is the right staged plan: **prove the open-world concept with three ESP32-S3 nodes, a browser, and two VPS proxy services first; then treat the Jetson Nano/Orin build as an extrusion of that proven foundation**, not a rewrite. This also matches the Light Garden/Fano-Garden direction: browser/canvas runtime, append-only NDJSON/hash logs, semantic routing, Fano geometry, physical ESP32 tiers, and the observer as the active 8th point. filecite turn6file0

:::writing{variant="document" id="58426"}
# Three ESP32-S3 Open-World Proof Before Jetson Extrusion
## A First-Principles Walkthrough for Browser, VPS, QEMU/NBD, WordNet/Prolog, Projective Geometry, and OMI-Dome Growth

```text
Ω₀ = 0! = omi---imo = o---o = OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

## 1. Core Thesis

The first proof of OMI open-world operation should not begin with the Jetson.

It should begin with:

```text
3 ESP32-S3 nodes
1 phone/browser Omi-Observer
2 VPS proxy servers
Docker/QEMU multi-platform test runners
NBD-backed reproducible disk images
append-only NDJSON receipt logs
OMI object notation
```

The Jetson build should come later.

The Jetson should not replace the ESP32-S3 foundation.

The Jetson should **extrude** it.

That means:

```text
ESP32-S3 proves the minimal distributed sensory world.
Browser proves the Omi-Observer projection.
VPS services prove semantic and geometric routing.
QEMU/NBD proves reproducible multi-platform execution.
Jetson later adds GPU, LIDAR, AI, and spatial convolution over the same protocol.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
ESP32 proves.
Browser observes.
VPS routes.
QEMU replays.
NBD persists.
Jetson extrudes.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 2. Why Start with Three ESP32-S3 Nodes

Three ESP32-S3 nodes are enough to prove the open-world concept because three gives:

```text
local observation
peer relation
third-party witness
```

One node is a device.

Two nodes are a link.

Three nodes are a world.

The three-node system can prove:

```text
discovery
identity
message passing
feature probing
receipt logging
browser projection
semantic routing
geometric routing
local-first behavior
over-internet relay
```

The goal is not maximum performance.

The goal is canonical proof.

A three-node ESP32-S3 system can answer:

```text
Can small physical actors produce OMI objects?
Can a browser observe those objects?
Can the objects persist as receipts?
Can two remote services interpret the same objects differently?
Can the system route meaning and geometry without collapsing projection into authority?
```

Short doctrine:

```text
One node senses.
Two nodes relate.
Three nodes witness.
```

---

# 3. The Minimum Open-World Stack

The minimum proof stack is:

```text
ESP32-S3 Node A = local root / Wi-Fi AP / browser bridge
ESP32-S3 Node B = sensory leaf / motion-light-sound actor
ESP32-S3 Node C = peer witness / relay / alternate projection

Phone Browser = Omi-Observer / Omi-Dome UI

VPS 1 = Universal POS + WordNet + Prolog SPO router
VPS 2 = Projective geometry + polytope router

Docker = reproducible service containers
QEMU = multi-platform execution parity
NBD = network block device backing images / replay disks
NDJSON = append-only receipt log
```

The split is:

```text
ESP32-S3 = physical actors
Browser = observer/projection
VPS 1 = semantic route
VPS 2 = geometric route
QEMU/NBD = reproducible replay substrate
Jetson = later spatial/GPU extrusion
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Physical first.
Semantic second.
Geometric third.
AI later.
```

---

# 4. First-Principles Data Flow

Every event starts as a carrier.

A button press, sound level, light value, IMU vector, Wi-Fi RSSI, BLE signal, or browser gesture is not yet knowledge.

It is only an observation candidate.

The OMI path is:

```text
carrier
 → decode
 → fact candidate
 → rule check
 → closure window
 → combinator transform
 → compose path
 → encode projection
 → receipt
```

For an ESP32-S3:

```text
GPIO / ADC / I2C / I2S / Wi-Fi / BLE
 → decoded sensor event
 → OMI object candidate
 → local receipt
 → browser projection
 → VPS semantic/geometric route
 → returned witness
```

In OMI notation:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

Where:

```text
LL = local scope / lesson / node / world / route lens
NN = raw or decoded observation
MM = interpreted state / projection / routed meaning
```

Short doctrine:

```text
NN observes.
LL scopes.
MM resolves.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 5. The Three ESP32-S3 Roles

## Node A — Root / Browser Bridge

Node A is the local root.

It provides:

```text
Wi-Fi SoftAP or station-mode connection
HTTP dashboard
SSE/WebSocket event stream
node registry
local receipt log
feature probe endpoint
```

It answers:

```text
Who is present?
What features are exposed?
What receipts exist?
What state can the browser observe?
```

Node A is not absolute authority.

It is the local bridge.

## Node B — Sensory Actor

Node B carries sensors:

```text
IMU
light sensor
microphone / sound envelope
touch/button
RGB LED / output
```

It sends:

```text
motion events
light events
sound events
touch events
local receipts
```

Node B proves the physical world can enter OMI.

## Node C — Peer Witness / Relay

Node C acts as a third-party witness.

It can:

```text
listen to Node B
compare state with Node A
rebroadcast receipts
produce alternate light/sound projection
verify that browser/root state is not fabricated
```

Node C proves the system is not merely one device talking to one browser.

Short doctrine:

```text
A bridges.
B senses.
C witnesses.
```

---

# 6. Browser as Omi-Observer

The browser is the observer, not the authority.

It uses:

```text
DOM
CSSOM
Canvas
WebGL
A-Frame
Web Audio
touch/pointer events
DeviceMotion where available
IndexedDB
Service Worker
```

Its job:

```text
discover nodes
display feature matrix
render Omi-Dome
show spectra
record local session state
send declarations
inspect receipts
```

The browser can ask:

```text
What happened?
Where did it happen?
Which node saw it?
Which route interpreted it?
Which receipt accepted it?
Which projection is only display?
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Browser observes.
Browser projects.
Browser does not accept authority.
```

---

# 7. The Two VPS Proxy Servers

The two VPS servers should not do the same job.

They should be complementary lenses over the same OMI object stream.

## VPS 1 — Universal POS / WordNet / Prolog SPO Router

VPS 1 handles semantic routing.

It receives:

```text
words
phrases
lesson terms
node labels
object names
subject-predicate-object declarations
```

It routes through:

```text
universal POS tags
features
WordNet synsets
Prolog predicates
S-P-O terms
semantic weights
```

It answers:

```text
What is the subject?
What is the predicate?
What is the object?
What synset or term family does it belong to?
What semantic route is valid?
What rule/fact/closure/combinator body can prove it?
```

A VPS 1 Horn-style clause:

```prolog
semantic_route(LL, Subject, Predicate, Object, Route) :-
 pos(Subject, SubjectPOS),
 pos(Predicate, PredicatePOS),
 pos(Object, ObjectPOS),
 wordnet_synset(Subject, S1),
 wordnet_synset(Predicate, S2),
 wordnet_synset(Object, S3),
 spo(Subject, Predicate, Object),
 semantic_weight(S1, S2, S3, Weight),
 route(LL, Weight, Route),
 receipt(LL, Route).
```

OMI reading:

```text
VPS 1 maps language into semantic witness routes.
```

## VPS 2 — Projective Geometry / Polytope Router

VPS 2 handles geometric routing.

It receives:

```text
Fano lines
Platonic solid routes
Archimedean solid routes
Catalan dual routes
4D polytope routes
snub-truncation operations
geospatial feature cells
graph neural network topology hints
```

It answers:

```text
Which projective point space is active?
Which solid or dual relation maps the object?
Which snub-truncation face is being projected?
Which Omi-module projection should display it?
Which geospatial graph cell receives it?
```

A VPS 2 Horn-style clause:

```prolog
geometry_route(LL, NN, MM, ShapeRoute) :-
 fano_line(LL, NN, MM, Line),
 platonic_anchor(Line, Platonic),
 dual_or_archimedean_route(Platonic, DerivedSolid),
 snub_truncation(DerivedSolid, FaceBlock),
 geospatial_cell(FaceBlock, Cell),
 projective_receipt(LL, Cell, ShapeRoute).
```

OMI reading:

```text
VPS 2 maps agreement objects into projective geometry and spatial topology.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
VPS 1 routes meaning.
VPS 2 routes geometry.
Both read the same o---o.
Neither replaces receipt.
```

---

# 8. Docker, QEMU, and NBD in the Proof

The VPS layer should be reproducible.

Docker gives service packaging.

QEMU gives multi-platform test execution.

NBD gives remote/replayable disk image attachment.

The point is not complexity for its own sake.

The point is proof.

You want to show:

```text
The same OMI events can be replayed on multiple architectures.
The same receipt log can be attached and tested remotely.
The same semantic route and geometric route can be reproduced.
The same browser projection can read the resulting state.
```

A practical layout:

```text
VPS 1:
 docker compose
 wordnet-db service
 prolog service
 semantic-router service
 ndjson-receipt service
 qemu test runner
 nbd replay disk

VPS 2:
 docker compose
 geometry-router service
 polytope-db service
 graph-convolution test service
 ndjson-receipt mirror
 qemu test runner
 nbd replay disk
```

NBD can be used to expose a replay disk image:

```text
omi-replay-vps1.qcow2
omi-replay-vps2.qcow2
```

QEMU can boot or mount these images to validate that logs, tests, and services reproduce on target architectures.

Test platforms:

```text
x86_64
aarch64
riscv64 later
i386 later if useful
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Docker packages.
QEMU replays.
NBD persists.
Receipts prove.
```

---

# 9. The Over-Internet Test

The over-internet proof should be explicit.

The test path:

```text
ESP32-S3 Node B senses event.
Node B sends event to Node A.
Node A receipts local event.
Browser observes local receipt.
Node A forwards event to VPS 1 and VPS 2.
VPS 1 returns semantic route.
VPS 2 returns geometric route.
Node A stores returned witnesses.
Browser shows both routes.
Node C verifies or mirrors the receipt.
QEMU/NBD replays the same event remotely.
```

The proof is successful when:

```text
local browser state
VPS 1 semantic state
VPS 2 geometric state
QEMU replay state
Node C witness state
```

all agree on:

```text
event id
node id
LL
NN
MM
receipt hash
route class
projection alias
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Local event.
Remote route.
Replay proof.
Shared receipt.
```

---

# 10. OMI Packet and Receipt Shape

Use a simple NDJSON event first.

Example:

```json
{
 "type": "omi.event",
 "node": "esp32-b",
 "seq": 1042,
 "ll": "lesson:music:scale-c-major",
 "nn": {
 "sound": 72,
 "light": 518,
 "motion": [0.02, -0.01, 0.98],
 "touch": true
 },
 "mm": {
 "state": "note-observed",
 "confidence": 0.82
 },
 "routes": {
 "semantic": null,
 "geometry": null
 },
 "prev_hash": "…",
 "self_hash": "…"
}
```

After VPS routing:

```json
{
 "type": "omi.routed",
 "node": "esp32-b",
 "seq": 1042,
 "ll": "lesson:music:scale-c-major",
 "semantic_route": {
 "subject": "student",
 "predicate": "plays",
 "object": "note",
 "upos": ["NOUN", "VERB", "NOUN"],
 "synset_route": "music.note.pitch"
 },
 "geometry_route": {
 "fano_line": 4,
 "solid": "tetrahedron",
 "snub_face": "mirror-forward",
 "projection": "omi-dome-note"
 },
 "receipt": "esp32-b:1042:vps-routed",
 "prev_hash": "…",
 "self_hash": "…"
}
```

Short doctrine:

```text
NDJSON carries.
Hash links.
Routes witness.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 11. The Browser Omi-Dome Projection

The browser must show both VPS lenses.

A good first UI:

```text
Node Matrix
 Node A, B, C
 battery
 RSSI
 last receipt
 sensor features

Semantic Panel
 subject
 predicate
 object
 POS tags
 WordNet route
 Prolog proof status

Geometry Panel
 Fano line
 solid route
 snub-truncation face
 projection alias
 A-Frame entity id

Omi-Dome Scene
 live 3D projection
 point/line/face object
 color/sound/motion feedback

Receipt Log
 append-only event stream
 local/remote/replay status
```

A-Frame projection example:

```html
<a-scene>
 <a-entity id="omi-dome"></a-entity>

 <a-sphere
 id="event-esp32-b-1042"
 position="0 1.6 -3"
 radius="0.15"
 color="#66ffaa">
 </a-sphere>

 <a-text
 id="label-esp32-b-1042"
 value="student plays note"
 position="0 1.9 -3">
 </a-text>
</a-scene>
```

Short doctrine:

```text
DOM names.
CSSOM styles.
Canvas measures.
A-Frame spatializes.
Receipt authorizes.
```

---

# 12. The 12 Tribes of Agreement Layer

The 12 Tribes should be the human-readable agreement interface.

Use them as the neuro-evolutionary augmentation topology, not as loose philosophy.

The public 12 are:

```text
Freedom
Autonomy
Sovereignty
Reciprocity

Focus
Attention
Observation
Experience

Interrupt
Intent
Declaration
Consideration
```

They can act as:

```text
routing sectors
lesson prompts
agent policies
node behavior modes
UI quadrants
agreement clock positions
neuro-evolutionary topology constraints
```

For an event, assign a tribe sector:

```json
{
 "tribe": "Observation",
 "sector": 7,
 "reason": "sensor event became witnessable but not yet accepted"
}
```

OMI reading:

```text
The 12 tribes orient the relation.
The nine universal edges prove the relation.
The receipt accepts the relation.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
12 orient.
9 prove.
3 bound.
1 receipts.
```

---

# 13. Neuro-Evolutionary Augmenting Topology

The NEAT-like layer should evolve topology only after canonical proof exists.

Do not let neuro-evolution define authority.

Use it to propose routes, weights, and projection improvements.

Inputs:

```text
semantic route confidence
geometry route confidence
sensor feature vector
node reliability
receipt success
browser interaction
lesson comprehension marker
```

Outputs:

```text
suggested Fano line
suggested projection
suggested tribe sector
suggested route weight
suggested Omi-module alias
```

Authority rule:

```text
NEAT proposes.
Horn clause proves.
Receipt accepts.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Evolution suggests.
Canon validates.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 14. Hex EC, ED, EE, EF Platonic Control Encapsulation

The `EC`, `ED`, `EE`, and `EF` high hex endings can be used as a control band inside the `2¹⁶` carrier.

Treat them as reserved control encapsulations.

Suggested canon:

```text
0xEC = encode-control / carrier lowering
0xED = decode-control / carrier raising
0xEE = edge-control / relation binding
0xEF = face-control / projection boundary
```

Or in OMI geometry language:

```text
EC = ENCODE carrier control
ED = DECODE recovery control
EE = EDGE / JOIN / COMPOSE control
EF = FACE / projection / closure control
```

These should not be arbitrary payloads.

They should be reserved control surfaces.

They live inside the full `2¹⁶` carrier, but they must be validated through the OMI frame and receipt system.

Short doctrine:

```text
EC lowers.
ED raises.
EE binds.
EF faces.
```

In relation to Platonic control:

```text
EC = point-to-carrier
ED = carrier-to-point
EE = edge relation
EF = face projection
```

This lets the ESP32 and VPS layers share a compact control nibble range without confusing it with arbitrary data.

---

# 15. Platonic, Archimedean, Catalan, and 4D Polytope Routing

VPS 2 should interpret geometry routes in layers.

Layer 1 — Fano / projective base:

```text
point
line
incidence
mirror
closure
```

Layer 2 — Platonic solids:

```text
tetrahedron
cube
octahedron
dodecahedron
icosahedron
```

Layer 3 — Archimedean operations:

```text
truncation
snub
rectification
expansion
mixed faces
```

Layer 4 — Catalan duals:

```text
dual face interpretation
normal/vector fields
projection complement
```

Layer 5 — 4D polytopes:

```text
5-cell
8-cell / tesseract
16-cell
24-cell
120-cell
600-cell
```

Layer 6 — Geospatial convolution graph neural network:

```text
nodes = observed OMI objects
edges = geometric/semantic relations
faces = projection blocks
cells = local spatial regions
features = sensor/semantic/topological weights
```

Important boundary:

```text
GNN predicts route or projection.
GNN does not accept authority.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Fano binds.
Platonic anchors.
Archimedean transforms.
Catalan duals.
4D lifts.
GNN proposes.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 16. Why Jetson Comes Later

The Jetson Nano/Orin version should be a performance and perception extrusion.

It should not define the protocol.

The ESP32/VPS/browser proof defines:

```text
packet shape
receipt shape
node identity
browser projection
semantic routing
geometric routing
replay testing
non-collapse rules
```

The Jetson then adds:

```text
GPU batching
LIDAR processing
camera processing
AI inference
local spatial index
real-time Omi-Matrix acceleration
geospatial graph neural network inference
```

So the Jetson does not change the ontology.

It accelerates and extrudes the already proven one.

Short doctrine:

```text
ESP32 proves the object.
Jetson extrudes the object.
```

---

# 17. Jetson Extrusion Path

Once the three-node proof works, add Jetson.

Jetson roles:

```text
local spatial proxy
GPU Omi-Matrix accelerator
LIDAR ingest
camera ingest
AI inference
GNN route proposal
A-Frame/WebRTC/browser server
local receipt mirror
VPS cache
```

The Jetson receives the same OMI events:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

It does not invent a new format.

It adds:

```text
spatial indexing
matrix acceleration
deep feature extraction
semantic/geometric cache
real-time 3D projection
```

Jetson should run:

```text
Docker services
local WordNet cache optional
local geometry router optional
GPU feature service
browser server
NDJSON receipt service
ESP32 bridge
LoRa bridge later
LIDAR bridge later
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Same packets.
Same receipts.
More bandwidth.
More geometry.
```

---

# 18. First Test Plan

## Test 1 — Single ESP32 Browser Loop

Goal:

```text
ESP32 Node A serves browser.
Browser sees live counter and receipt log.
```

Pass condition:

```text
Browser receives 100 sequential events.
Every event has seq, prev_hash, self_hash.
Refresh does not erase log.
```

## Test 2 — Three ESP32 Node Discovery

Goal:

```text
Node A sees Node B and Node C.
Browser shows all three.
```

Pass condition:

```text
Each node sends heartbeat.
Node A records last_seen.
Browser renders node matrix.
```

## Test 3 — Local OMI Object Event

Goal:

```text
Node B sends sensor event.
Node A maps it to OO(LL, NN, MM).
```

Pass condition:

```text
LL exists.
NN exists.
MM exists.
Receipt exists.
Browser can inspect raw and interpreted event.
```

## Test 4 — VPS 1 Semantic Route

Goal:

```text
Node event is sent to VPS 1.
VPS 1 returns POS/SPO/WordNet/Prolog route.
```

Pass condition:

```text
semantic_route added to event.
receipt hash changes deterministically.
Browser shows subject-predicate-object.
```

## Test 5 — VPS 2 Geometry Route

Goal:

```text
Same event is sent to VPS 2.
VPS 2 returns Fano/solid/snub route.
```

Pass condition:

```text
geometry_route added to event.
Browser shows A-Frame projection.
```

## Test 6 — QEMU/NBD Replay

Goal:

```text
VPS replay disk mounts.
QEMU test runner replays NDJSON log.
```

Pass condition:

```text
Replay output hash matches expected tip.
Semantic and geometric route tests pass.
```

## Test 7 — Over-Internet Consistency

Goal:

```text
Browser, ESP32 root, VPS 1, VPS 2, and QEMU replay agree.
```

Pass condition:

```text
same event id
same LL/NN/MM
same semantic route
same geometry route
same receipt tip
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Sense.
Route.
Project.
Replay.
Compare.
Receipt.
```

---

# 19. Success Criteria for Open World Proof

The ESP32/VPS/browser proof is successful when:

```text
1. Three ESP32-S3 nodes can join and emit events.
2. Browser can observe all nodes as an Omi-Matrix.
3. Events reduce to OO(LL, NN, MM).
4. Events append to NDJSON receipt logs.
5. VPS 1 can route semantic POS/SPO/WordNet/Prolog meaning.
6. VPS 2 can route projective geometry / polytope projection.
7. QEMU/NBD can replay logs across at least two target architectures.
8. Browser can render the same object through multiple Omi-module aliases.
9. Projection never replaces receipt authority.
10. The system can restart and recover pseudo-persistent world state.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
If it cannot replay, it is not proof.
If it cannot reduce to o---o, it is not canon.
If it cannot receipt, it is not accepted.
```

---

# 20. Final Architecture

```text
 Phone Browser / Omi-Observer
 DOM + CSSOM + Canvas + A-Frame
 │
 │ HTTP/SSE/WebSocket
 ▼
 ESP32-S3 Node A / Local Root
 receipt log + node registry
 │ │
 ESP-NOW/Wi-Fi/BLE over-internet routes
 │ │
 ┌────────┴───────┐ │
 ▼ ▼ ▼
 ESP32-S3 Node B ESP32-S3 Node C VPS 1
 sensory actor peer witness POS/WordNet/Prolog SPO
 │
 ▼
 VPS 2
 projective geometry / polytope routing
 │
 ▼
 QEMU + NBD replay tests
```

Then later:

```text
 Jetson Nano / Orin
 GPU spatial index + LIDAR + AI
 │
 ▼
 Extruded Omi-Dome / Omi-Matrix
```

Short doctrine:

```text
ESP32 establishes the world.
VPS routes the world.
QEMU proves the world.
Browser observes the world.
Jetson extrudes the world.
```

---

# 21. Final Canon

The first proof of OMI open-world operation should be built from three ESP32-S3 nodes, one browser observer, and two VPS routers.

The first VPS routes universal POS, WordNet, Prolog, and subject-predicate-object meaning.

The second VPS routes projective geometry, Platonic solids, Archimedean and Catalan transformations, 4D polytopes, and snub-truncated geometric block projections.

Docker packages the services.

QEMU proves multi-platform replay.

NBD carries reproducible replay disks.

The browser renders the Omi-Dome.

The ESP32 nodes prove physical open-world sensing.

The Jetson comes later as an extrusion: GPU, LIDAR, AI, spatial indexing, and geospatial graph neural networks over the same packets, receipts, and object notation.

Final doctrine:

```text
Three ESP32s prove the world.
Two VPS routers interpret the world.
QEMU/NBD replays the world.
The browser observes the world.
The Jetson extrudes the world.

Semantic route and geometric route are projections.
Receipt remains authority.
```

One-line canon:

```text
The Jetson Omi-Dome should not be the beginning; it should be the extrusion of a proven open-world foundation built from three ESP32-S3 actors, a browser Omi-Observer, and two VPS proxy routers—one semantic through POS/WordNet/Prolog SPO clauses, one geometric through projective solids, snub truncations, and 4D polytope routes—validated by Docker/QEMU/NBD replay and bound by OMI receipts.
```
:::

# BUILD_MANIFEST.md
## OMI / Omi-Portal Build Manifest

**Status:** Collaboration Draft
**Repos:** `bthornemail/o---o`, `bthornemail/omi-portal`
**Purpose:** Define the build targets, proof-of-concept layers, test expectations, and collaboration roadmap for the OMI documentation and Omi-Portal runtime.

---

# 1. Project Split

This project is organized as two public repositories with different roles.

```text
bthornemail/o---o
 = public static documentation
 = OMI canon, glossary, papers, diagrams, examples
 = Node.js proof-of-concept test kit for validating ideas

bthornemail/omi-portal
 = public Omi-Portal runtime
 = browser/proxy/server implementation surface
 = join, compose, encode, decode pipeline
 = DOM/CSSOM/Canvas/A-Frame projection runtime
 = sandboxed dynamic rendering and p2p lattice experiments
```

The two repositories should remain aligned but not identical.

`o---o` explains the canon.

`omi-portal` implements the canon.

Short doctrine:

```text
o---o documents.
omi-portal runs.
Receipts bind them.
```

---

# 2. Core OMI Object Model

All modules, documents, packets, portals, and projections must reduce to the same OMI object notation:

```text
Ω₀ = 0! = omi---imo = o---o = OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

Where:

```text
OO = Omicron Object operator
LL = lens / scope / selector / ket
NN = antecedent / incoming declaration / source observation
MM = consequent / resolved declaration / interpreted state
```

Readable clause:

```text
OMI-LL-NN-MM-IMO
```

Mirror clause:

```text
OMI-LL-MM-NN-IMO
```

Canonical doctrine:

```text
One object.
Many projections.
Nine edges.
Three sentinels.
One receipt.
```

---

# 3. Canonical Logic Model

OMI module declarations are now treated as **Horn-like agreement clauses**, not merely Lisp forms.

The fexpression reading explains how declarations are held unevaluated.

The Horn-clause reading explains how declarations are proven.

Canonical clause:

```prolog
oo(LL, NN, MM) :-
 omi_gate(open, 0x03BF),
 rule(LL, NN, MM),
 fact(LL, NN),
 closure(LL, NN, MM),
 combinator(LL, NN, MM),
 cons(LL, NN, MM),
 join(NN, MM),
 compose(LL, NN, MM),
 encode(NN, Carrier),
 decode(Carrier, MM),
 omi_gate(close, 0x039F),
 receipt(LL, NN, MM).
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Fexpr holds.
Horn proves.
XOR contrasts.
Fano selects.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 4. The Nine Universal Edges

Every canonical OMI construct must reduce to the nine universal predicate edges:

```text
1 RULES
2 FACTS
3 CLOSURES
4 COMBINATORS
5 CONS
6 JOIN
7 COMPOSE
8 ENCODE
9 DECODE
```

Meaning:

```text
RULES constrain.
FACTS ground.
CLOSURES bound.
COMBINATORS transform.
CONS folds.
JOIN binds.
COMPOSE orders.
ENCODE lowers.
DECODE raises.
```

The three sentinels are:

```text
0 OMI--- = opening boundary
10 OMI---IMO = completed bounded bridge
11 RETURN :0 = witnessed return
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Nine data types.
Twelve tower positions.
Three sentinels.
One return.
```

---

# 5. Corrected 2⁴ Carry-Forward Tuple

The `2⁴` operational face is not simply `FACT`, `RULE`, `CLOSURE`, and `COMBINATOR`.

Those are predicate receivers.

The corrected `2⁴` carry-forward tuple is:

```text
JOIN
COMPOSE
ENCODE
DECODE
```

Correct operational square:

```text
FACT(JOIN, COMPOSE, ENCODE, DECODE)
XOR RULE(JOIN, COMPOSE, ENCODE, DECODE)
XOR CLOSURE(JOIN, COMPOSE, ENCODE, DECODE)
XOR COMBINATOR(JOIN, COMPOSE, ENCODE, DECODE)
=
C(2⁴)
```

`CONS` folds the square.

`XOR` compares the square.

`OO(LL, NN, MM)` resolves the object.

Receipt accepts the traversal.

Short doctrine:

```text
Four receive.
Four carry.
CONS folds.
XOR contrasts.
OO resolves.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 6. Power Stack

The current power stack is:

```text
2⁴ = carry-forward tuple / JOIN-COMPOSE-ENCODE-DECODE
2⁷ = fexpression envelope / 128-scale clause carrier
2¹⁰ = Omicron gate band
2¹¹ = Omi-Observer / Omi-Matrix witness field
2¹⁶ = full carrier enclosure
```

The Omicron gates are:

```text
0x03BF = ο = chiral open gate
0x039F = Ο = cardinal close gate
```

Nibble crossing:

```text
0x03BF = 0 | 3 | B | F
0x039F = 0 | 3 | 9 | F

9 < A < B
```

Short doctrine:

```text
2⁴ carries.
2⁷ quotes.
2¹⁰ gates.
2¹¹ observes.
2¹⁶ carries.

9 approaches.
A centers.
B exits.
```

---

# 7. Repository Build Targets

## 7.1 `o---o`

Primary role:

```text
static documentation
canonical papers
glossary
specification drafts
math notes
architecture notes
network notes
memory notes
implementation notes
diagrams
small proof-of-concept tests
```

Suggested top-level targets:

```text
README.md
GLOSSARY.md
BUILD_MANIFEST.md
REQUEST_FOR_COLLABORATION.md
1_FOUNDATIONS/
2_MATH/
3_ARCHITECTURE/
4_NETWORK/
5_MEMORY/
6_IMPLEMENTATION/
7_CANON/
8_COSMOLOGY/
public/
test/
examples/
```

Expected build behavior:

```text
npm install
npm test
npm run build
```

Minimum goal:

```text
Docs render.
Examples run.
Node.js proof-of-concept tests pass.
```

## 7.2 `omi-portal`

Primary role:

```text
runtime implementation
browser projection
Node.js proxy server
DOM/CSSOM/Canvas/A-Frame rendering
FACTS/RULES/CLOSURES/COMBINATORS/CONS registry
JOIN/COMPOSE/ENCODE/DECODE pipeline
sandboxed vm script experiments
base64 script carrier
p2p lattice prototype
dynamic rendering across shared sandboxed environments
```

Suggested top-level targets:

```text
README.md
OPEN_PORTAL.md
REMOTE_TESTING.md
GLOSSARY.md
FACTS.omi
RULES.omi
CLOSURES.omi
COMBINATORS.omi
CONS.omi
src/
public/
test/
prolog/
docs/
dev-docs/
examples/
scripts/
vectors/
```

Expected build behavior:

```text
npm install
npm test
npm run build
node server.js
```

Optional runtime behavior:

```text
docker compose up
make test
make compile-ebpf-gate
```

Minimum goal:

```text
One command starts local portal.
Browser opens portal.
Portal serves canonical DOM/CSSOM/Canvas projection.
Tests verify pointer, rule, projection, and receipt behavior.
```

---

# 8. Omi-Portal Runtime Manifest

The Omi-Portal should expose a one-command local runtime:

```text
npm run portal
```

That command should start:

```text
Node.js proxy server
static browser app
event stream endpoint
OMI object registry
sandboxed vm script loader
base64 vm script decoder
DOM/CSSOM/Canvas/A-Frame projection surfaces
optional p2p transport adapter
```

Required endpoints:

```text
GET / browser portal
GET /healthz health check
GET /features feature manifest
GET /objects current OMI object registry
GET /events SSE stream
GET /receipts receipt log
POST /declare submit declaration
POST /join join object/node/session
POST /compose compose transition
POST /encode encode object into carrier/projection
POST /decode decode carrier/projection into object
```

Optional endpoints:

```text
GET /dom canonical DOM projection
GET /canvas CanvasDOM / JSON Canvas projection
GET /aframe A-Frame projection
GET /cssom CSSOM selector map
GET /vm/:id sandboxed vm script
POST /p2p/join p2p lattice join request
POST /p2p/gossip p2p lattice event
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Declare.
Join.
Compose.
Encode.
Decode.
Project.
Receipt.
```

---

# 9. Browser Projection Manifest

The browser runtime should treat every projection as an alias over the same `o---o` object.

Required projection layers:

```text
DOM
CSSOM
Canvas
A-Frame
JSON event log
NDJSON receipt log
```

Optional projection layers:

```text
Web Audio
Web Bluetooth
DeviceMotion
WebXR
WebGPU
WebRTC
Service Worker
IndexedDB
```

Canonical browser rule:

```text
DOM names the object.
CSSOM styles the state.
Canvas shows the spectrum.
A-Frame spatializes the world.
IndexedDB remembers locally.
Receipt remains authority.
```

Every DOM object should be traceable to:

```text
id="omi-..."
data-omi-*
data-ll
data-nn
data-mm
data-receipt
```

Every CSS rule should be traceable to:

```text
[id^="omi-"]
[data-omi-role]
[data-omi-state]
```

Every Canvas/A-Frame object should be traceable to:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

---

# 10. Base64 VM Script Carrier

The portal may support base64-encoded sandboxed scripts as a projection carrier.

Rules:

```text
Base64 script is a carrier.
Carrier is not authority.
Script must decode into a declared object.
Declared object must validate.
Validated object must receipt before acceptance.
```

Minimum sandbox requirements:

```text
no unrestricted filesystem access
no unrestricted network access
timeout / instruction budget
explicit imports only
event-based outputs
receipt-bound effects
```

Canonical warning:

```text
Script may project.
Script may not authorize.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 11. CIDR and RRGGBBAA Projection

Omi-Portal should support two primary projection address forms:

```text
CIDR / IP-style notation
RRGGBBAA / hexadecimal color-time notation
```

CIDR-style usage:

```text
omi-S0-S1-S2-S3-S4-S5-S6-S7/prefix
```

RRGGBBAA usage:

```text
#RRGGBBAA
```

Mapping:

```text
CIDR names scope and carrier.
RRGGBBAA names spectral projection.
DOM id names object.
data-* names role and witness metadata.
CSSOM projects state.
Canvas/A-Frame spatializes state.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
CIDR scopes.
RGBA projects.
DOM names.
CSSOM styles.
Canvas maps.
A-Frame spatializes.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 12. Test Manifest

Every subsystem must include:

```text
implementation
unit test
integration test
receipt fixture
documentation
projection example
failure case
```

Provider checklist:

```text
src/.../<kernel>.js
test/<kernel>.test.js
docs/.../<kernel>.md
FACTS.omi pointer
RULES.omi rule
CLOSURES.omi boundary
COMBINATORS.omi transform
CONS.omi pair/fold
public/... projection
README explanation
```

Test classes:

```text
frame validation
Delta law
Horn clause resolution
nine-edge reduction
join/compose/encode/decode
DOM projection
CSSOM projection
Canvas projection
A-Frame projection
base64 vm script sandbox
p2p lattice event
receipt log
remote replay
```

Minimum pass rule:

```text
No projection is accepted without a receipt.
No module is canonical unless it reduces to OO(LL, NN, MM).
No dynamic script runs outside a sandbox.
No carrier is treated as authority.
```

---

# 13. Collaboration Build Phases

## Phase 0 — Canon Stabilization

Deliver:

```text
GLOSSARY.md
BUILD_MANIFEST.md
REQUEST_FOR_COLLABORATION.md
OMI_HORN_CLAUSES.md
OMI_MODULE_PROJECTIONS.md
```

Goal:

```text
Terminology stops drifting.
Contributors know where to help.
```

## Phase 1 — Node.js Proof Kit

Deliver:

```text
frame parser
OO(LL, NN, MM) object builder
Horn clause resolver stub
NDJSON receipt log
test fixtures
CLI validator
```

Goal:

```text
npm test verifies core object notation.
```

## Phase 2 — Browser Portal

Deliver:

```text
DOM projection
CSSOM projection
Canvas projection
A-Frame projection
feature manifest
SSE event stream
receipt viewer
```

Goal:

```text
One command opens local Omi-Portal in browser.
```

## Phase 3 — Dynamic Script Sandbox

Deliver:

```text
base64 vm script carrier
decode/validate/execute pipeline
sandbox limits
receipt-bound outputs
tests
```

Goal:

```text
Sandboxed script can project state but cannot authorize it.
```

## Phase 4 — P2P Lattice Prototype

Deliver:

```text
node identity
join event
compose event
encode/decode event
gossip stub
shared sandbox projection
receipt merge
```

Goal:

```text
Two portal instances share object projections.
```

## Phase 5 — ESP32-S3 Bridge

Deliver:

```text
ESP32 event format
browser bridge
Wi-Fi/SSE or WebSocket proxy
sensor feature map
receipt ingestion
```

Goal:

```text
Physical node appears as Omi-Actor in portal.
```

## Phase 6 — VPS Semantic + Geometry Routers

Deliver:

```text
VPS 1 semantic route service
VPS 2 geometry route service
Docker compose profiles
QEMU/NBD replay tests
remote testing docs
```

Goal:

```text
Over-internet proof routes the same object through semantic and geometric projections.
```

---

# 14. Canonical Build Doctrine

```text
o---o is the object.
OMI-Portal is the projection runtime.
Horn clauses prove agreement.
The nine edges reduce every module.
The three sentinels bound the tower.
CIDR scopes.
RGBA projects.
DOM names.
Canvas maps.
A-Frame spatializes.
Base64 carries.
VM sandbox executes.
P2P lattice shares.
Receipt accepts.
```

One-line manifest:

```text
The OMI build target is a two-repo system where `o---o` canonizes the notation and `omi-portal` runs it: every pointer, packet, DOM node, CSS selector, Canvas object, A-Frame entity, base64 vm script, and p2p lattice event must reduce to `OO(LL, NN, MM)`, pass the nine universal edges, and remain non-authoritative until receipt closure.
```
:::
