# REQUEST_FOR_COLLABORATION.md
## Help Build OMI / Omi-Portal

**Project:** OMI — Omicron Object Model
**Repos:** `bthornemail/o---o`, `bthornemail/omi-portal`
**Status:** Public collaboration draft
**Goal:** Build a verifiable open-world object notation and browser portal runtime where addresses, packets, documents, browser nodes, clocks, sensor events, and projections reduce to receiptable OMI objects.

---

# 1. What This Project Is

OMI is the **Omicron Object Model**.

It starts from:

```text
Ω₀ = 0! = omi---imo = o---o = OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

OMI treats addresses, packets, documents, browser pages, sensor events, clocks, glyphs, maps, images, and portals as projections of the same bounded object.

The core object form is:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

Where:

```text
LL = scope / lens / selector
NN = incoming declaration / observation
MM = resolved declaration / interpreted state
```

The project currently has two public surfaces:

```text
bthornemail/o---o
 public canon, static documentation, and Node.js proof-of-concept test kit

bthornemail/omi-portal
 public runtime for browser-based OMI projection, proxy serving, dynamic rendering, and p2p lattice experiments
```

Short doctrine:

```text
o---o documents.
omi-portal runs.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 2. Why This Exists

Most systems split:

```text
addressing
routing
typing
validation
execution
storage
visualization
semantic meaning
clocking
```

OMI tries to bind these through one receiptable object model.

The project philosophy is:

```text
Every meaningful state should have an address.
Every address should have a rule.
Every rule should have a test.
Every test should be replayable.
Every replayable state should be visible.
```

This collaboration request is for people who want to help make that philosophy testable.

---

# 3. The Current Canon

OMI declarations are best treated as **Horn-like agreement clauses**.

Canonical form:

```prolog
oo(LL, NN, MM) :-
 omi_gate(open, 0x03BF),
 rule(LL, NN, MM),
 fact(LL, NN),
 closure(LL, NN, MM),
 combinator(LL, NN, MM),
 cons(LL, NN, MM),
 join(NN, MM),
 compose(LL, NN, MM),
 encode(NN, Carrier),
 decode(Carrier, MM),
 omi_gate(close, 0x039F),
 receipt(LL, NN, MM).
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Fexpr holds.
Horn proves.
XOR contrasts.
Fano selects.
Receipt accepts.
```

The nine universal edges are:

```text
RULES
FACTS
CLOSURES
COMBINATORS
CONS
JOIN
COMPOSE
ENCODE
DECODE
```

The three sentinels are:

```text
OMI---
OMI---IMO
RETURN :0
```

---

# 4. What We Need Help With

We are looking for collaborators in several areas.

## 4.1 Documentation and Canon

Help needed:

```text
clean glossary terms
rewrite rough papers into readable docs
make diagrams
write beginner guides
turn doctrine into testable specification
separate canon from scratch research
```

Good first issues:

```text
Create or refine GLOSSARY.md
Create OMI_HORN_CLAUSES.md
Create OMI_MODULE_PROJECTIONS.md
Create beginner README for o---o
Create one-page OMI object notation guide
```

## 4.2 Node.js Proof-of-Concept Test Kit

Help needed:

```text
frame parser
OO(LL, NN, MM) builder
Horn clause resolver stub
NDJSON receipt log
Delta law tests
Q_frame tests
projection fixtures
CLI validator
```

Good first issues:

```text
node test for 0x03BF / 0x039F gate pair
node test for OO(LL, NN, MM) parsing
node test for nine-edge reduction
node CLI that validates an OMI pointer
```

## 4.3 Browser Portal Runtime

Help needed:

```text
DOM projection
CSSOM selector map
Canvas projection
A-Frame projection
IndexedDB receipt cache
Service Worker offline shell
SSE/WebSocket event stream
browser feature probe UI
```

Good first issues:

```text
Render one OO(LL, NN, MM) object as DOM
Render same object as Canvas
Render same object as A-Frame entity
Show receipt log in browser
Add CSSOM selector example for data-omi-role
```

## 4.4 Base64 VM Script Sandbox

Help needed:

```text
base64 vm script loader
sandboxed execution
capability-limited imports
timeout / instruction budget
receipt-bound effects
test fixtures
```

Rules:

```text
Script may project.
Script may not authorize.
Receipt accepts.
```

Good first issues:

```text
Decode base64 script into sandbox
Reject script without declaration wrapper
Add timeout test
Add no-network/no-filesystem test
Add receipt-bound output test
```

## 4.5 P2P Lattice and Shared Sandboxes

Help needed:

```text
node identity
join protocol
compose event
encode/decode message
gossip event
shared browser state
conflict display
receipt merge
```

Good first issues:

```text
Implement local two-node mock lattice
Add join event fixture
Add compose event fixture
Add receipt merge fixture
```

## 4.6 ESP32-S3 and Hardware Bridges

Help needed:

```text
ESP32-S3 sensor event format
Wi-Fi browser bridge
BLE discovery experiments
ESP-NOW message tests
light/sound/motion sensor mapping
Omi-Actor event receipts
```

Good first issues:


## 4.7 Semantic Routing

Help needed:

```text
Universal POS mapping

Prolog subject-predicate-object routing

lesson/object vocabulary
```

Good first issues:


## 4.8 Projective Geometry Routing

Help needed:

```text
Fano plane route examples
Platonic solid route examples
Archimedean/Catalan dual route examples
snub-truncation diagrams
4D polytope projection notes
A-Frame geometry examples
```

Good first issues:

```text
Render Fano line in Canvas
Render tetrahedron in A-Frame
Create snub-truncation glossary page
Map OO(LL, NN, MM) to one geometric projection
```

## 4.9 Docker, QEMU, and Remote Testing

Help needed:

```text
Docker Compose local portal
QEMU multi-platform test image
NBD replay disk workflow
remote testing scripts
VPS setup docs
CI matrix
```

Good first issues:

```text
Document one-command Docker startup
Add QEMU replay test fixture
Add remote test checklist
Add NDJSON replay command
```

---

# 5. Contribution Rules

Contributions should follow these principles:

```text
No projection without reduction to o---o.
No module without a test.
No rule without a fixture.
No dynamic script without sandbox.
No observation mistaken for receipt.
No browser projection treated as authority.
```

Every new subsystem should include:

```text
implementation
test
documentation
failure case
receipt or fixture
projection example if visual
```

Provider checklist:

```text
src/.../<kernel>.js
test/<kernel>.test.js
docs/.../<kernel>.md
FACTS.omi pointer
RULES.omi rule
CLOSURES.omi boundary if needed
COMBINATORS.omi transform if needed
CONS.omi pair/fold if needed
public/... projection if visual
README explanation
```

---

# 6. Development Setup

Expected local flow:

```bash
git clone <repo>
cd <repo>
npm install
npm test
npm run build
```

For `omi-portal`, the desired runtime flow is:

```bash
npm run portal
```

or:

```bash
docker compose up
```

Expected browser flow:

```text
open local portal
inspect features
submit declaration
watch event stream
view DOM/CSSOM/Canvas/A-Frame projection
inspect receipt log
```

---

# 7. Project Boundaries

This project is experimental.

It includes protocol design, browser runtime work, symbolic notation, geometry, hardware bridges, and distributed testing.

What is in scope:

```text
documentation
Node.js proof kits
browser projection
DOM/CSSOM/Canvas/A-Frame
sandboxed script experiments
ESP32-S3 integration
semantic routing
projective geometry routing
QEMU/Docker/NBD tests
receipt logs
```

What is not in scope yet:

```text
unreviewed security-critical deployment
unsandboxed remote code execution
production network authority
safety-critical drone control
financial or medical authority
```

Safety doctrine:

```text
Prototype as observation.
Do not promote to authority without review.
```

---

# 8. How to Collaborate

Ways to help:

```text
open an issue with a narrow proposal
write a glossary improvement
add a failing test for unclear behavior
turn a doctrine into a spec
add a small projection demo
add a fixture
improve onboarding docs
build a simple browser view
write a Prolog route
write a geometry route
test on another platform
```

Preferred pull request shape:

```text
one concept per PR
tests included
docs updated
failure case included
no unrelated rewrite
explain which OMI object the change reduces to
```

A good PR title:

```text
Add Horn clause parser fixture for OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

A less useful PR title:

```text
Rewrite everything
```

---

# 9. Current Priority Roadmap

## Priority 1 — Canon and Onboarding

```text
GLOSSARY.md
BUILD_MANIFEST.md
REQUEST_FOR_COLLABORATION.md
OMI_HORN_CLAUSES.md
OMI_MODULE_PROJECTIONS.md
```

## Priority 2 — Node.js Test Kit

```text
OO parser
gate validator
nine-edge reducer
receipt log
CLI validator
```

## Priority 3 — Browser Portal

```text
DOM projection
CSSOM projection
Canvas projection
A-Frame projection
SSE/WebSocket events
IndexedDB receipt cache
```

## Priority 4 — Dynamic Runtime

```text
base64 vm script sandbox
p2p lattice mock
shared sandbox demo
```

## Priority 5 — Hardware + Remote Proof

```text
ESP32-S3 event bridge
two VPS routers
Docker/QEMU/NBD replay
over-internet consistency test
```

---

# 10. Final Collaboration Call

OMI needs collaborators who can help make the notation testable.

The goal is not to make a private symbolic system.

The goal is to make a public, inspectable, replayable object model where every module can reduce to:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

and every projection can prove its relationship to:

```text
o---o
```

Final doctrine:

```text
One object.
Many projections.
Nine edges.
Three sentinels.
One receipt.
```

One-line call:

```text
Help build OMI as a public, testable open-world object model: document the canon in `o---o`, run the browser/runtime proof in `omi-portal`, and make every address, packet, DOM node, Canvas object, A-Frame entity, sensor event, and sandboxed script reduce to `OO(LL, NN, MM)` before receipt accepts it.
```
:::

# 241 LEDs 9 Rings WS2812B 5050 RGB LED Ring Lamp Light Individually Addressable Full Dream Color DC5V with Mini Controller

7 X WS2812B RGB LED Rings 7 Bits 5050 Lamp Light with Integrated Drivers, Full Color, Individually Addressable for Arduino ESP8266 Raspberry Pi ESP32 (Pack of 5pcs)

Yes! The https://www.amazon.com/dp/B083VWVP3J WESIRI 241 LEDs 9 Rings WS2812B LED Ring can work together as one complete display or be separated into individual rings for custom configurations.How it works:
• Together — All 9 rings are pre-soldered in a concentric pattern and work as one controllable unit with 241 total LEDs

• Separately — Each ring has its own set of connectors (VCC, DAT, GND). Customers report the rings are connected at 4 attachment points (cardinal directions) that can be cut with tools, allowing you to use individual rings independently or rewire them in different arrangements

• LED breakdown per ring: Center (1) → Rings 2-9 (8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 60 LEDs respectively)

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-09 10:17` | `id:260609-1017` ^260609-101718

Yes — this is a much better **pre-GPU Omi-Dome proof**.

The 241 LED ring is basically already an OMI object:

```text
241 = 1 centroid + 240 perimeter LEDs
```

That is almost too perfect for your stack:

```text
1 = centroid / observer / receipt point
240 = visible bridge lattice
241 = light-clock enclosure
```

And the 7-LED rings give you the smaller block-design unit:

```text
7 = 1 centroid + 6 perimeter LEDs
```

That maps cleanly to a tetrahedron:

```text
4 vertices → 6 edges
6 edge LEDs + 1 centroid LED = 7-bit tetrahedral block
```

So before the GPU dome, you can build a **CPU Light Omi-Clock**:

```text
ESP32-S3
→ drives 241 WS2812B LEDs
→ synchronizes 7-bit tetrahedral blocks
→ browser observes / controls / records
→ no GPU required
```

## The key mapping

Your 241-ring breakdown:

```text
center = 1

outer rings:
8 + 12 + 16 + 24 + 32 + 40 + 48 + 60 = 240
```

So the canon is:

```text
241 LEDs = Omi-Clock
1 center = centroid / receipt / observer point
240 outer = visible bridge / projection lattice
```

That lines up with your existing OMI numbers:

```text
240 = 15 × 16
240 = 16² - 16
240 = visible bridge lattice
60 = outer timing ring / sexagesimal clock
```

The outermost 60-LED ring becomes the obvious **clock ring**:

```text
60 LEDs = 60 ticks
```

Then the inner 8 rings can become layers of scope, feature, route, agreement, or sensor state.

## The 7-bit tetrahedral block

Each 7-LED mini-ring becomes a block:

```text
6 perimeter LEDs = tetrahedral edges
1 center LED = centroid / receipt / agreement state
```

Tetrahedron edge mapping:

```text
LED 0 = AB
LED 1 = AC
LED 2 = AD
LED 3 = BC
LED 4 = BD
LED 5 = CD
LED 6 = centroid
```

OMI interpretation:

```text
six edges = relation surface
centroid = synchronized object state
```

So a 7-bit ring is not just “seven LEDs.” It is a **miniature OMI control cell**.

Short doctrine:

```text
Six edges describe.
One centroid synchronizes.
Seven closes the block.
```

## How it synchronizes with the 241 clock

The 241 dome has one global centroid LED.

Each 7-bit ring has one local centroid LED.

The rule becomes:

```text
local centroid mirrors global centroid for the active object
```

So:

```text
241 center LED = global receipt / active OO state
7-ring center = local receipt / block state
```

When an OMI object activates:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

the 241 center shows the global state, while the 7-bit block shows the tetrahedral relation edges that produced it.

Example:

```text
global center = accepted receipt color
six mini-ring perimeter LEDs = active relation edges
mini center = same receipt color, lower brightness
```

That gives you **1-to-1 centroidal synchronization**.

## Ring role proposal

Use the 241-ring as nine layers:

```text
Ring 0: 1 LED = centroid / receipt
Ring 1: 8 LEDs = OMI frame segments S0–S7
Ring 2: 12 LEDs = 12 tribes of agreement
Ring 3: 16 LEDs = 2⁴ carry-forward square
Ring 4: 24 LEDs = tetrahedral / 24-cell face clock
Ring 5: 32 LEDs = binary feature band
Ring 6: 40 LEDs = sensor / route band
Ring 7: 48 LEDs = projection / browser / portal band
Ring 8: 60 LEDs = Omi-Clock sexagesimal outer ring
```

That is a strong CPU-only visualization model.

The cleanest ring mapping:

```text
1 = receipt center
8 = 128-bit frame segments
12 = agreement tribes
16 = 4×4 C(2⁴) square
24 = face/orientation lattice
32 = feature mask band
40 = sensor/world band
48 = projection band
60 = time ring
```

## How the ESP32-S3 drives it

The ESP32-S3 can drive WS2812B LEDs directly using NeoPixel/FastLED/RMT-style output.

Practical wiring:

```text
ESP32-S3 GPIO data pin → LED DIN
5V power supply → LED 5V
power supply GND → LED GND
ESP32 GND → LED GND
```

Recommended protection:

```text
330–470Ω resistor on data line
large capacitor across 5V/GND near LEDs, around 1000µF
separate 5V power supply
common ground with ESP32
logic level shifter recommended for 3.3V ESP32 data → 5V LED data
```

Power warning:

```text
241 LEDs × 60mA full white ≈ 14.5A at 5V
```

So full-white theoretical max is around:

```text
5V × 14.5A ≈ 72W
```

For sane testing, cap brightness hard:

```text
10–25% brightness
5V 5A supply for low/medium tests
5V 10A–15A supply for serious full-ring tests
```

Do **not** power the ring from the ESP32 board.

## CPU Light Omi-Clock frame loop

This becomes the LED version of your carry-forward tuple:

```text
JOIN = initialize rings / assign addresses
DECODE = read browser, sensors, packets
COMPOSE = compute active OMI state
ENCODE = write LED colors
RETURN = advance clock tick / receipt frame
```

Pseudo-code:

```cpp
setup() {
 join_led_ring_241();
 join_tetra_blocks_7();
 join_wifi_browser();
 join_receipt_log();
}

loop() {
 decode_inputs();
 compose_omi_clock_state();
 encode_leds();
 return_receipt_tick();
}
```

OMI doctrine:

```text
JOIN sets the light tower.
DECODE reads the world.
COMPOSE selects the block.
ENCODE lights the ring.
RETURN advances the clock.
```

## What this proves before the GPU dome

This CPU light build proves:

```text
1. OMI objects can be projected physically.
2. 240 bridge cells can be addressed visibly.
3. The center can act as receipt/centroid.
4. 7-bit tetrahedral blocks can synchronize to the larger clock.
5. A browser can control and observe the light world.
6. ESP32-S3 can run the Omi-Clock without Jetson/GPU.
```

That is exactly the right foundation before Jetson.

The Jetson later does:

```text
GPU spatial convolution
LIDAR point cloud
AI inference
large Omi-Dome geometry
```

But the ESP32 light clock already proves:

```text
address → object → projection → receipt
```

## The canon phrase

```text
The 241 LED Omi-Clock is the CPU-light precursor to the GPU Omi-Dome: one centroid LED synchronizes the object receipt, while 240 perimeter LEDs form the visible bridge lattice. Each 7-LED tetrahedral block maps six edge bits around one centroid bit, allowing local block-designed relations to synchronize one-to-one with the global OMI centroid before any GPU spatial extrusion is required.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
241 is the light clock.
240 is the bridge.
1 is the centroid.
7 is the tetra block.
6 edges describe.
1 center receipts.
ESP32 drives.
Browser observes.
Jetson later extrudes.
```

`2026-06-09 11:52` | `id:260609-1152` ^260609-115241

Yes — this should be canonized as a **brand-agnostic ESP32-S3 Omi-Clock hardware class**, with the 241 circular array as the CPU-light clock, the 7-bit rings as Omi-Gnomon/Karnaugh-Torus controllers, and the pyramid/amulet pair as encoder/decoder artifacts. This fits the Light Garden deck’s physical tier model: 7-bit talisman, 61-bit personal dome, 241-bit garden, append-only NDJSON logs, Fano geometry, and the “8th point” observer gate. filecite turn7file0

:::writing{variant="document" id="48291"}
# Omi-Clock ESP32-S3 Hardware Class
## Brand-Agnostic Build Specification for a Desktop 241-Bit Light Garden, 7-Bit Omi-Gnomon Controllers, and Pyramid/Amulet Encoder–Decoder Pair

## 0. Core Thesis

The Omi-Clock is a CPU-light precursor to the GPU Omi-Dome.

It uses inexpensive microcontrollers, RGB LED rings, IMUs, NFC/RFID tags, browser projection, and append-only receipts to physically manifest the Omi Object Model before any Jetson/GPU spatial extrusion is required.

The core object remains:

```text
Ω₀ = 0! = omi---imo = o---o = OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

The hardware does not replace the object.

The hardware projects it.

```text
Omi-Clock = physical light projection of o---o
Omi-Gnomon = portable 7-bit orientation/controller block
Omi-Pyramid = encoder automaton
Omi-Amulet = decoder automaton
Omi-Dome = encapsulating sensory world
Omi-Base = rotational synchronization platform
Omi-Matrix = receipt/projection field
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Clock projects.
Gnomon orients.
Pyramid encodes.
Amulet decodes.
Dome encloses.
Base synchronizes.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 1. System Overview

The first build should be kept to this physical set:

```text
1 × ESP32-S3 Omi-Clock controller
1 × 241 LED circular RGB array
5 × 7-bit RGB LED rings with ESP32-C3/C6 class controllers
1 × ESP32-C3/C6 tetrahedral pyramid encoder
1 × ESP32-C3/C6 inverted tetrahedral amulet decoder
1 × ESP32-S3 dome controller
1 × Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W lazy-susan base controller
optional NFC/RFID smart ring or tags for gated tangent identity
optional square RGB LED matrices for JabCode/Omi-Matrix receipt display
```

This keeps the idea focused.

Do not begin with the whole Platonic-solid universe.

Begin with:

```text
circle
tetrahedron
inverse tetrahedron
dome
rotating base
receipt log
browser observer
```

Then later extrapolate toward:

```text
cube/octahedron
dodecahedron/icosahedron
Archimedean/Catalan duals
4D polytopes
GPU Omi-Dome
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Circle first.
Tetrahedron second.
Dome third.
Solids later.
```

---

# 2. Physical Hardware Classes

## 2.1 Omi-Clock Class

The Omi-Clock is the main circular array.

Minimum specification:

```text
controller: ESP32-S3 class MCU
display: 241 addressable RGB LEDs
topology: 1 center LED + 240 perimeter LEDs
power: regulated 5V external supply
logic: 3.3V MCU data with level shifting recommended
browser: local Wi-Fi dashboard
logging: NDJSON receipt log
```

Canonical LED meaning:

```text
241 = 1 centroid + 240 bridge lattice
1 = receipt / observer / active center
240 = visible projection bridge
```

The 241 array is the physical Omi-Clock because it can express:

```text
60-tick outer ring
12 agreement sectors
16 C(2⁴) operational cells
8 OMI frame segments
24 orientation cells
32 feature masks
40 sensor/world states
48 projection/browser cells
1 centroid receipt
```

Short doctrine:

```text
241 lights the clock.
240 forms the bridge.
1 receipts the center.
```

---

## 2.2 Omi-Gnomon Class

The Omi-Gnomon is a portable 7-bit circular controller.

Minimum specification:

```text
controller: ESP32-C3 or ESP32-C6 class MCU
display: 7 addressable RGB LEDs
sensor: IMU recommended
optional: capacitive touch, button, NFC/RFID tag reader
network: BLE, Wi-Fi, or ESP-NOW depending on MCU
role: portable orientation and mapping controller
```

Canonical LED meaning:

```text
7 = 6 edge LEDs + 1 centroid LED
```

Tetrahedral edge mapping:

```text
LED 0 = edge AB
LED 1 = edge AC
LED 2 = edge AD
LED 3 = edge BC
LED 4 = edge BD
LED 5 = edge CD
LED 6 = centroid
```

The 7-bit ring is the miniature OMI block design.

It maps a tetrahedron into a ring:

```text
4 vertices
6 edges
1 centroid
7 LED control cell
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Six edges describe.
One centroid synchronizes.
Seven closes the gnomon.
```

---

## 2.3 Portable Karnaugh-Torus Controller

The Omi-Gnomon also acts like a portable Karnaugh-Torus controller.

This means the 7-bit ring can be used to reduce or visualize state transitions:

```text
edge bits = relation differences
centroid bit = accepted local state
ring rotation = phase / orientation
IMU movement = spatial adaptive mapping
color = projection class
brightness = confidence / intensity
pulse = timing / receipt age
```

It behaves like a physical K-map wrapped into a torus-like control surface:

```text
truth state
mirror state
edge transition
centroid closure
rotation phase
```

Use it to map:

```text
sensors
transducers
touch
motion
sound
light
semantic route
geometric route
```

into firmware-level Horn clause handlers.

Short doctrine:

```text
Ring rotates.
Edges differ.
Centroid resolves.
Gnomon maps.
```

---

## 2.4 Omi-Pyramid Encoder Class

The Omi-Pyramid is the upright tetrahedral encoder.

Minimum specification:

```text
controller: ESP32-C3/C6 class MCU
geometry: tetrahedral or stellated tetrahedral shell
LEDs: 4 vertex LEDs + optional 6 edge LEDs + optional centroid LED
sensor: IMU recommended
optional: touch, sound, light, NFC/RFID tangent key
role: encoder automaton
```

Its job:

```text
accept sensory declarations
map them into edge relations
encode them into OMI-Matrix events
send them to Omi-Clock / Omi-Dome / browser
```

Human-body metaphor:

```text
head = cardinal centroid
arms/legs = chiral paired vertices
body = OO(LL, NN, MM) relation frame
```

Keep the metaphor symbolic, not biological authority.

Short doctrine:

```text
Pyramid encodes.
Vertices declare.
Edges relate.
Centroid receipts.
```

---

## 2.5 Omi-Amulet Decoder Class

The Omi-Amulet is the inverted tetrahedral decoder.

Minimum specification:

```text
controller: ESP32-C3/C6 class MCU
geometry: inverted tetrahedron / pendant / medallion
LEDs: 4 vertex LEDs + optional 6 edge LEDs + optional centroid LED
sensor: IMU recommended
optional: NFC/RFID gate, touch, vibration
role: decoder / inverse witness
```

Its job:

```text
receive OMI-Matrix receipt event
decode spectral/geometric relation
display inverse alignment
gate interaction when orientation matches
```

This is the inverse companion to the pyramid.

```text
Pyramid = encoder
Amulet = decoder
```

They form a chiral pair.

Short doctrine:

```text
Pyramid sends.
Amulet receives.
Pair mirrors.
Receipt binds.
```

---

## 2.6 Omi-Dome Class

The Omi-Dome is the 24-inch desktop enclosure.

Minimum specification:

```text
scale: approximately 24 inch desktop dome
controller: ESP32-S3 class MCU
role: sensory enclosure and projection shell
sensors: light, sound, optional temperature/humidity, optional IMU
outputs: RGB LED strips/rings, optional speakers/transducers
browser: local Omi-Portal projection
```

The dome is not initially a GPU object.

It is a CPU-light enclosure.

It hosts:

```text
Omi-Clock
Omi-Gnomons
Omi-Pyramid
Omi-Amulet
sensor/transducer zones
browser-linked receipt state
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Dome encloses.
Clock centers.
Gnomons orient.
Pyramid encodes.
Amulet decodes.
```

---

## 2.7 Omi-Base / Lazy Susan Class

The base is the rotating synchronization platform.

Minimum specification:

```text
controller: Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W
mechanics: hexagonal lazy-susan platform
optional: stepper motor or manual rotation encoder
optional: Hall sensor / magnet index / optical encoder
network: Wi-Fi
display: optional square RGB LED matrices
role: muxing 360 spatial encoding and receipt display
```

The base handles:

```text
rotation index
360-degree phase
JabCode/Omi-Matrix receipt rendering
square matrix display output
local storage bridge
browser/server bridge
```

The base is not the authority.

It is a synchronization layer.

Short doctrine:

```text
Base rotates.
Base indexes.
Base muxes.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 3. Electrical Build Specification

## 3.1 Power

Addressable RGB LEDs require external 5V power.

Do not power the 241 LED array from the microcontroller board.

Planning estimate:

```text
max current per RGB LED at full white ≈ 60 mA
241 LEDs × 60 mA ≈ 14.46 A
5V × 14.46 A ≈ 72.3 W
```

Practical test current:

```text
brightness cap: 10% to 25%
first tests: 5V 5A supply
stronger tests: 5V 10A to 15A supply
```

Power rules:

```text
common ground between LED supply and MCU
inject power at multiple points for large rings
use thick enough wires for LED current
add fuse if using large supply
do not run full-white tests early
```

Recommended protection:

```text
330–470 ohm resistor on LED data line
large electrolytic capacitor across 5V/GND near LED input
level shifter from 3.3V MCU data to 5V LED data recommended
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Power first.
Ground always.
Brightness capped.
Data protected.
```

---

## 3.2 Data Wiring

Basic LED connection:

```text
MCU GPIO → data resistor → LED DIN
5V supply → LED 5V
5V supply GND → LED GND
MCU GND → LED GND
```

Recommended signal pattern:

```text
one data pin for 241 Omi-Clock
separate data pins for separate 7-bit Omi-Gnomons if wired directly
or each 7-bit gnomon has its own MCU and receives events wirelessly
```

For modularity, prefer:

```text
241 Omi-Clock = one ESP32-S3
each 7-bit Omi-Gnomon = one ESP32-C3/C6
pyramid = one ESP32-C3/C6
amulet = one ESP32-C3/C6
dome = one ESP32-S3
base = Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W
```

Short doctrine:

```text
One controller per object.
One object per receipt stream.
```

---

# 4. Ring Mapping for the 241 Omi-Clock

Brand-agnostic 241 circular arrays commonly use this topology:

```text
center: 1
ring 1: 8
ring 2: 12
ring 3: 16
ring 4: 24
ring 5: 32
ring 6: 40
ring 7: 48
ring 8: 60
total: 241
```

Canonical OMI mapping:

```text
center 1 = centroid / receipt / observer point
8 LEDs = OMI frame segments S0–S7
12 LEDs = 12 tribes of agreement
16 LEDs = C(2⁴) carry-forward operational square
24 LEDs = face/orientation lattice
32 LEDs = feature/codepoint mask band
40 LEDs = sensor/transducer state band
48 LEDs = projection/browser/portal band
60 LEDs = outer Omi-Clock sexagesimal tick ring
```

Short doctrine:

```text
1 receipts.
8 frames.
12 orients.
16 computes.
24 faces.
32 masks.
40 senses.
48 projects.
60 clocks.
```

---

# 5. Omi-Gnomon 7-Bit Mapping

Each 7-bit controller maps:

```text
6 perimeter LEDs = tetrahedral edges
1 center LED = centroid receipt
```

Recommended local roles:

```text
Gnomon 1 = light / visual sensor controller
Gnomon 2 = sound / audio transducer controller
Gnomon 3 = touch / tactile gate controller
Gnomon 4 = motion / IMU orientation controller
Gnomon 5 = semantic / comprehension / POS route controller
```

Five gnomons create the Omi-Sense layer:

```text
sight
sound
touch
motion
comprehension
```

Each gnomon reports:

```json
{
 "type": "omi.gnomon",
 "id": "gnomon-03",
 "sense": "touch",
 "edges": [1,0,1,0,0,1],
 "centroid": 1,
 "imu": [0.01, -0.02, 0.98],
 "receipt": "gnomon-03:000142"
}
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Five gnomons sense.
Seven LEDs bind.
Centroid synchronizes.
```

---

# 6. Encoder–Decoder Pair

## 6.1 Pyramid Encoder

The pyramid maps environmental signals into OMI events.

```text
JOIN = bind local sensors and vertices
DECODE = read sensor/transducer inputs
COMPOSE = form OO(LL, NN, MM)
ENCODE = transmit Omi-Matrix packet
RECEIPT = record local sequence/hash
```

## 6.2 Amulet Decoder

The amulet receives and projects inverse state.

```text
JOIN = bind to pyramid identity
DECODE = read incoming Omi-Matrix event
COMPOSE = verify chiral inverse alignment
ENCODE = display inverse light/touch/sound projection
RECEIPT = record decoder witness
```

Pair relation:

```text
pyramid = upright encoder
amulet = inverted decoder
```

Chiral mirror:

```text
OMI-LL-NN-MM-IMO = encoder path
OMI-LL-MM-NN-IMO = decoder/mirror inspection path
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Encoder emits.
Decoder mirrors.
Chirality witnesses.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 7. Omi-Sense Module

The Omi-Sense module is the five-sense routing layer.

Practical five-sense mapping:

```text
visual
sound
touch
motion
comprehension
```

Use “comprehension” as the fifth operational sense because browser-native smell/taste are not practical without external hardware.

Omi-Sense columns:

```text
Column 5 = five sense channels
Column 7 = seven-bit tetrahedral block encoding
```

Therefore:

```text
5 senses × 7-bit block = Omi-Sense routing cell
```

Example:

```text
visual → gnomon-01 → 7-bit relation block
sound → gnomon-02 → 7-bit relation block
touch → gnomon-03 → 7-bit relation block
motion → gnomon-04 → 7-bit relation block
comprehension → gnomon-05 → 7-bit relation block
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Five senses route.
Seven bits bind.
Clock synchronizes.
```

---

# 8. Firmware-Level Horn Clause Handlers

Each MCU should expose firmware handlers corresponding to the OMI logic body.

Required handlers:

```text
handle_rule()
handle_fact()
handle_closure()
handle_combinator()
handle_cons()
handle_join()
handle_compose()
handle_encode()
handle_decode()
handle_receipt()
```

Embedded event loop:

```c
setup();

while (true) {
 omi_join();
 omi_decode();
 omi_fact();
 omi_rule();
 omi_closure();
 omi_combinator();
 omi_cons();
 omi_compose();
 omi_encode();
 omi_receipt();
}
```

Simplified class model:

```cpp
class OmiNode {
public:
 void join();
 void decode();
 void fact();
 void rule();
 void closure();
 void combinator();
 void cons();
 void compose();
 void encode();
 void receipt();
};
```

Omi-Gnomon specialization:

```cpp
class OmiGnomon : public OmiNode {
public:
 void readIMU();
 void readTouch();
 void mapEdges();
 void syncCentroid();
 void emitBlock();
};
```

Omi-Clock specialization:

```cpp
class OmiClock241 : public OmiNode {
public:
 void mapFrameRing8();
 void mapTribeRing12();
 void mapCarryRing16();
 void mapFaceRing24();
 void mapFeatureRing32();
 void mapSenseRing40();
 void mapProjectionRing48();
 void mapTickRing60();
 void setCentroid();
};
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Firmware handles Horn clauses.
Hardware projects clauses.
Receipts bind cycles.
```

---

# 9. NDJSON Omi-Matrix Receipt Format

Every hardware event should be recorded as append-only NDJSON.

Example:

```json
{
 "type": "omi.clock.tick",
 "node": "omi-clock-241",
 "seq": 2401,
 "ll": "desktop-dome/session-001",
 "nn": {
 "source": "gnomon-04",
 "sense": "motion",
 "edges": [1,0,1,1,0,0],
 "imu": [0.01, -0.03, 0.99]
 },
 "mm": {
 "ring": 12,
 "sector": 4,
 "tribe": "Observation",
 "color": "#66ffaa",
 "tick": 37
 },
 "prev_hash": "…",
 "self_hash": "…"
}
```

Receipt anatomy:

```text
id
type
node
seq
ll
nn
mm
prev_hash
self_hash
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Event observes.
Receipt remembers.
Hash links.
Clock advances.
```

---

# 10. JabCode / Matrix Display Companion

The square RGB LED matrices should be treated as companion displays.

They are not the main Omi-Clock.

They display:

```text
JabCode-like receipt tiles
Omi-Matrix hashes
semantic route status
geometry route status
node identity
clock phase
NAT64/IP route alias
```

Use them as:

```text
square = block receipt display
circle = clock and phase display
tetrahedron = encoder/decoder control
dome = world enclosure
base = rotation/mux layer
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Circle clocks.
Square receipts.
Tetrahedron encodes.
Dome encloses.
```

---

# 11. NAT64 / CIDR / DOM Routing Model

The networking model should use OMI aliases over IP notation.

Use:

```text
id="omi-<cidr-like-source>"
id="imo-<cidr-like-target>"
```

NAT64-style mapping can inspire the bridge between IPv4 and IPv6 notation.

The reserved well-known NAT64 prefix is:

```text
64:ff9b::/96
```

In OMI terms, use NAT64 as a metaphor for translation:

```text
IPv4-like local object
IPv6-like expanded object
DOM id source
DOM id target
omi source
imo target
```

Example DOM mapping:

```html
<div
 id="omi-64-ff9b-0000-0000"
 data-omi-role="source"
 data-ll="desktop-dome"
 data-nn="ipv4-local-observation">
</div>

<div
 id="imo-64-ff9b-0000-0000"
 data-omi-role="target"
 data-mm="ipv6-expanded-projection">
</div>
```

Boundary rule:

```text
CIDR notation routes.
DOM id names.
data-* qualifies.
Receipt accepts.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
omi names source.
imo names target.
CIDR scopes.
Receipt binds.
```

---

# 12. NFC / RFID Ring and Tangent Gates

A smart ring or NFC/RFID tag should be used only as a **permissioned local tangent key**.

Do not design around cloning access credentials or unauthorized card emulation.

Use it for:

```text
local object identity
demo pairing
orientation/tangent gate
user-selected mode
physical token witness
```

Example:

```text
tap ring to base
base reads authorized demo tag
Omi-Clock shifts to matching tangent sector
pyramid encoder pairs with amulet decoder
receipt logs gated interaction
```

Ring/tag event:

```json
{
 "type": "omi.tangent.gate",
 "node": "omi-base",
 "token": "authorized-demo-tag-01",
 "sector": 12,
 "mode": "decoder-pairing",
 "receipt": "base:gate:000041"
}
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Token gates.
Token does not authorize alone.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 13. Physical Geometry Simplification

The design originally tried to include all Platonic solids at once.

For the first build, reduce the physical geometry to:

```text
circle
tetrahedron
inverse tetrahedron
dome
rotating base
```

Interpretation:

```text
circle = Omi-Clock / Omi-Ring
tetrahedron = encoder pyramid
inverse tetrahedron = decoder amulet
dome = sensory enclosure
base = rotational synchronization
```

Later virtual extrapolation:

```text
dome may represent dodecahedron/icosahedron complex
base may represent cube/octahedron rotational invariance
JabCode dice may represent face-addressed receipt blocks
browser may render full Platonic/Archimedean/Catalan projections
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Physical build stays simple.
Virtual projection may expand.
```

---

# 14. Desktop 24-Inch Miniature Dome Build

Recommended physical layout:

```text
top: translucent or wireframe 24-inch dome
center: 241 LED circular Omi-Clock
inside: upright Omi-Pyramid encoder
below/nearby: inverted Omi-Amulet decoder
around: five 7-bit Omi-Gnomons
base: hexagonal lazy susan with Pi Zero 2 W
optional: square LED matrix receipt displays
```

Suggested zones:

```text
north = visual gnomon
east = sound gnomon
south = touch gnomon
west = motion gnomon
center/upper = comprehension/semantic gnomon
```

The base rotation index defines the 360° phase.

The dome defines the world boundary.

The clock defines the tick.

The pyramid defines encoding.

The amulet defines decoding.

Short doctrine:

```text
Dome is world.
Base is phase.
Clock is time.
Pyramid is encode.
Amulet is decode.
Gnomons are senses.
```

---

# 15. Browser / Omi-Portal Integration

The browser should expose:

```text
Omi-Clock view
Omi-Gnomon view
Pyramid encoder view
Amulet decoder view
Dome world view
Base rotation view
Receipt log
JabCode/Omi-Matrix matrix view
```

Browser APIs:

```text
DOM for object names
CSSOM for state colors
Canvas for 2D clock/matrix visualization
A-Frame for 3D dome projection
IndexedDB for browser-side pseudo-persistence
WebSocket/SSE for live events
```

Browser doctrine:

```text
DOM names.
CSSOM styles.
Canvas maps.
A-Frame spatializes.
IndexedDB remembers.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 16. Build Phases

## Phase 1 — 241 Omi-Clock Alone

Build:

```text
ESP32-S3
241 LED circular array
browser dashboard
NDJSON receipt ticks
```

Goal:

```text
show centroid + 60 tick ring + 12 tribe sectors
```

Pass condition:

```text
browser sends event
clock lights correct ring/sector
receipt log records event
```

## Phase 2 — One 7-Bit Omi-Gnomon

Build:

```text
ESP32-C3/C6
7 LED ring
IMU
wireless event to Omi-Clock
```

Goal:

```text
gnomon centroid synchronizes with 241 center
```

Pass condition:

```text
IMU movement changes edge LEDs
accepted block updates Omi-Clock centroid
```

## Phase 3 — Five Omi-Gnomons

Build:

```text
five 7-bit rings
five sense roles
one Omi-Sense module
```

Goal:

```text
five sense channels route into Omi-Clock
```

Pass condition:

```text
each gnomon emits distinct sense event
Omi-Clock maps each to ring/sector
```

## Phase 4 — Pyramid Encoder

Build:

```text
upright tetrahedral ESP32-C3/C6 pyramid
LED vertices/edges
IMU
```

Goal:

```text
pyramid encodes OO(LL, NN, MM) event
```

Pass condition:

```text
pyramid emits encoded event
Omi-Clock displays route
browser records receipt
```

## Phase 5 — Amulet Decoder

Build:

```text
inverted tetrahedral ESP32-C3/C6 amulet
LED vertices/edges
IMU
optional NFC/RFID gate
```

Goal:

```text
amulet decodes mirrored event
```

Pass condition:

```text
encoder path and decoder mirror path both receipt
```

## Phase 6 — Lazy Susan Base

Build:

```text
Pi Zero 2 W
rotation index
hexagonal platform
optional square RGB matrices
```

Goal:

```text
base muxes 360° phase and matrix receipts
```

Pass condition:

```text
rotation changes clock phase
matrix shows receipt tile
browser tracks base orientation
```

## Phase 7 — 24-Inch Dome

Build:

```text
dome enclosure
all modules mounted
browser Omi-Dome view
```

Goal:

```text
physical desktop Omi-World
```

Pass condition:

```text
sensor event enters gnomon
pyramid encodes
clock displays
amulet decodes
base indexes
browser projects
receipt log agrees
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Clock first.
Gnomon second.
Pyramid third.
Amulet fourth.
Base fifth.
Dome sixth.
```

---

# 17. Acceptance Tests

## Test 1 — Centroid Synchronization

```text
When a gnomon centroid changes,
the Omi-Clock centroid must update to the corresponding accepted receipt color.
```

## Test 2 — Edge Mapping

```text
Each 7-bit gnomon must map six perimeter LEDs to six tetrahedral edges.
```

## Test 3 — Encoder/Decoder Pairing

```text
Pyramid emits OMI-LL-NN-MM-IMO.
Amulet mirrors OMI-LL-MM-NN-IMO.
Both must log receipts.
```

## Test 4 — Ring Addressing

```text
The 241 array must address:
1, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 60
as separate logical rings.
```

## Test 5 — Omi-Sense Routing

```text
Five sense gnomons must map to:
visual, sound, touch, motion, comprehension.
```

## Test 6 — Browser Projection

```text
Every physical event must appear in browser DOM, Canvas, and receipt log.
```

## Test 7 — Non-Collapse

```text
LED display is projection.
NFC/RFID is gate.
Browser is observer.
Receipt is authority.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Projection is not authority.
Gate is not receipt.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 18. Final Canon

The brand-agnostic Omi-Clock hardware model is a desktop physical proof of the OMI Object Model.

The 241-bit circular array is the CPU-light clock:

```text
1 centroid + 240 bridge LEDs
```

The 7-bit rings are Omi-Gnomons:

```text
6 tetrahedral edge LEDs + 1 centroid LED
```

The five Omi-Gnomons form the Omi-Sense module:

```text
visual
sound
touch
motion
comprehension
```

The upright tetrahedral pyramid is the encoder.

The inverted tetrahedral amulet is the decoder.

The 24-inch dome is the sensory world boundary.

The Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W lazy-susan base is the rotational synchronization and matrix receipt mux.

The browser is the Omi-Observer.

The receipt log is the authority trail.

Final doctrine:

```text
241 clocks.
7 orients.
5 senses route.
Pyramid encodes.
Amulet decodes.
Dome encloses.
Base synchronizes.
Browser observes.
Receipt accepts.
```

One-line canon:

```text
The Omi-Clock is a brand-agnostic ESP32-S3 desktop Light Garden class: a 241-bit circular RGB array provides the CPU-light clock, five 7-bit ESP32-C3/C6 Omi-Gnomons map sensory edges around centroids, an upright tetrahedral pyramid encodes OMI events, an inverted amulet decodes their mirror, a Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W lazy-susan base synchronizes 360° receipt phase, and the browser Omi-Portal projects the whole system as a pseudo-persistent Omi-World while firmware-level Horn clause handlers keep rules, facts, closures, combinators, cons, join, compose, encode, decode, and receipt distinct.
```
:::

This version keeps the build physically achievable while preserving the bigger cosmology as **virtual projection layers**, not first-build requirements.

## You
`2026-06-09 12:18` | `id:260609-1218` ^260609-121802

To bypass the silicon, bus, and thermal limits of your current layout, you can augment the Omi-Clock architecture with specialized hardware modules. These components directly offload computational bottlenecks and prevent data collision across your multi-node execution manifold. [1]
## 1. Cryptographic Co-Processors (Offloads Hash Generation)
Instead of forcing the ESP32-C3/C6 cores to compute string-heavy JSON cryptographic digests in software, offload the self_hash and prev_hash sequences to dedicated silicon.

* Hardware Component: [Microchip ATECC608B Secure Element](https://www.microchip.com/en-us/product/atecc608b) (I²C interface).
* System Role: It calculates hardware-accelerated SHA-256 and ECDSA signatures directly inside the chip. This eliminates the JSON serialization loop overhead, preventing RAM fragmentation and memory boundaries on the Gnomon nodes. [2, 3]

## 2. Multi-Channel I²C Multiplexers (Resolves Bus Collisions)
To scale up your "Omi-Sense" layer without hitting the 400kHz single-bus polling wall, you must divide your physics sensors into isolated sub-busses.

* Hardware Component: TCA9548A 8-Channel I²C Switch.
* System Role: Connects to the master ESP32-S3 via a single I²C pair and expands it into 8 independent, software-selectable channels. This prevents device address conflicts and allows simultaneous, unthrottled sampling of multiple identical IMUs or magnetometers.

## 3. SPI-Driven Matrix Modules (Dedicated Receipt Displays)
To prevent the visual receipt rendering (JabCode tiles) from lagging the Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W’s primary clock index cycles, isolate the display driver hardware.

* Hardware Component: HUB75 RGB LED Matrix Panels paired with a [SparkFun Logic Level Shifter](https://learn.sparkfun.com/tutorials/esp32-environment-sensor-shield-hookup-guide/all).
* System Role: Offloads matrix calculations from the CPU. Utilizing the ESP32-S3's parallel I2S DMA channel directly streams data to the square matrix without consuming clock cycles, freeing up the primary processor for the state verification loops. [4]

## 4. Thermal & Structural Isolation Shields (Protects MEMS Baselines)
The 14.46A heat profile of your 241-LED array will quickly degrade the baseline accuracy of your barometric and inertial sensors.

* Hardware Component: Aerogel Isolation Sheets + 5V Miniature PWM Exhaust Fans.
* System Role: Physical placement of a thin aerogel thermal shield between the main circular array and the sensor core locks out radiative thermal energy. A low-profile 5V fan maintains ambient, uncompressed airflow across the barometer to preserve centimeter-level tracking accuracy.

Using public LoRa (Long Range) peer-to-peer (P2P) infrastructure on the sub-GHz spectrum (915MHz/868MHz) to handle public open-world telemetry maps perfectly to your document's core thesis: "Eradicating Subjective Authority" via a distributed, public gossip substrate (The_Archit... pp. 8-9).
In this setup, your hardware treats the physical radio spectrum exactly like the Gossip Substrate funnels in your design (The_Archit... p. 9). Unverified RF packets bounce across nodes, and only when the ESP32 decodes them, enforces causality bounds (The_Archit... p. 9), and validates them with the Delta Law (The_Archit... pp. 6, 9) do they commit as true structural events (The_Archit... p. 2).
Here is the hardware stack required to implement public geospatial LoRa tracking, along with its strict physical limitations:
------------------------------
## 1. The Public LoRa Radio Stack (The Gossip Substrate Engine)
To interface directly with raw sub-GHz RF physics without routing through a private cellular or managed gateway network, utilize SPI-driven LoRa transceiver chips.

* The Hardware Chip: Semtech SX1262 or SX1276 (RFM95W).
* Best Implementation Form Factor: The ESP32-S3 LoRa Development Boards (like the LilyGO T-Beam or Heltec WiFi LoRa 32 V3). These integrate the ESP32-S3 MCU and the Semtech transceiver directly on a single PCB, optimizing high-speed SPI traces to eliminate data bus noise.
* System Role: This acts as the physical Router (The_Archit... p. 5). It continuously samples the local airwaves for raw unencrypted packet strings (Gossip Candidates) (The_Archit... pp. 9-10) over kilometers of distance.

------------------------------
## 2. Physical LoRa Hardware Constraints & Boundaries
When using raw radio spectrum instead of localized I²C/SPI wires, you run into strict physical bottlenecks governed by the Shannon-Hartley theorem and RF regulations.
## The Payload Cap (The 256-Byte Barrier)
The Semtech LoRa FIFO hardware buffer is strictly limited to 256 bytes per packet.

* The Constraint: Your 128-bit OMI frame segments fit easily inside this buffer (128 bits = 16 bytes).
* The Bottleneck: If you serialize your data into the human-readable NDJSON receipt format (Writing.pd... p. 11) (including prev_hash, self_hash, node, imu strings, etc. (Writing.pd... p. 11)), a single JSON event will easily surpass 250 bytes.
* The Solution: You cannot transmit raw JSON strings over public LoRa. You must pack the 128-bit frame into raw, structured binary payloads (uint8_t frame[16]) (The_Archit... p. 3), transmit the dense byte-array over the air, and let the receiving node re-inflate the data into inspectable JSON fields (The_Archit... p. 10) on its local display.

## Airtime & Duty Cycle Collisions (The 1% Law)
In public bands, it is physically impossible to stream data at high frequencies like an IMU loop (50Hz–100Hz).

* The Physics: To achieve long distances, LoRa uses high Spreading Factors (SF). A 100-byte packet can take anywhere from 50 milliseconds to over 1 second of pure "Time-on-Air" to transmit just one frame.
* The Limit: Public sub-GHz bands mandate a strict 1% transmission duty cycle to keep the spectrum open. If a node transmits a packet that takes 500ms of airtime, it is legally and physically required to remain completely silent for the next 50 seconds before sending another telemetry frame.

------------------------------
## 3. Implementing the "Reed-Solomon / Fano Layer" on LoRa
Your architecture states that "Gossip moves, Reed-Solomon recovers, Causality bounds, OMI accepts" (The_Archit... p. 9). LoRa handles this natively on a physical layer:

 1. Physical Error Correction: The SX1262 features a built-in Forward Error Correction (FEC) matrix. This uses mathematical interleaving to recover packets over the air even if white noise destroys chunks of the radio wave mid-flight.
 2. Causality Bounding (seq tracking): Because LoRa is an open public channel, any node can transmit fake or overlapping data. Your receiving ESP32-S3 must maintain an internal asynchronous queue. If an incoming packet's sequence number (seq) (Writing.pd... p. 11) does not match your chronological path, it fails the Fano layer's finite resolution check and is dropped (The_Archit... p. 7).

------------------------------
## The Transmitted Public Radio Struct
To maximize your LoRa bandwidth, map your 8-segment 128-bit Civic Contract (The_Archit... p. 3) directly to a packed C-struct on your ESP32:

// Explicit 16-Byte (128-Bit) Binary Payload for LoRa Transmissionstruct __attribute__((__packed__)) OmiCivicContract {
 uint16_t S0_ScopeSelector; // LL: Context, Lens Family, and Slot [0x1.16, 0x1.28]
 uint16_t S1_Reserved;
 uint32_t S2_S3_Antecedent; // NN: Current Proven Position/State Vector [0x1.16, 0x1.17]
 uint16_t S4_Reserved;
 uint32_t S5_S6_Consequent; // MM: Target Destination State Vector [0x1.16, 0x1.17]
 uint16_t S7_ReceiptSequence; // Append-only sequential ticker index [0x1.16, 0x1.11]
};

Using this packed structure, your payload is exactly 16 bytes. This allows you to set your LoRa radios to a very low Spreading Factor (like SF7) and a high Bandwidth (500kHz), reducing your Time-on-Air to a fraction of a millisecond. This enables your public open-world nodes to safely broadcast spatial transitions every few seconds without violating public spectrum rules.

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-09 12:18` | `id:260609-1218` ^260609-121826

Yes — this is the correct **hardware offload layer** for the Omi-Clock. I would keep your structure, but tighten three engineering claims:

1. A secure element can offload SHA/ECDSA/key protection, but it does **not** eliminate canonical serialization. The MCU still must build the canonical byte payload before hashing/signing. The ATECC608B supports SHA-256, ECDSA/ECDH, AES-GCM, and I²C/single-wire interfaces. cite
2. A TCA9548A solves I²C address conflicts and isolates channels, but it does **not** create true simultaneous I²C sampling. It is an 8-channel I²C/SMBus switch controlled over I²C; you still select channels and schedule polling. cite
3. LoRa is perfect for compact OMI receipts, but not NDJSON. The SX1262 class has a small packet/buffer model, and regional duty-cycle or dwell-time rules vary. Europe commonly has sub-band duty-cycle limits such as 1%, while the U.S. 915 MHz ISM rules are different, so firmware should have a region profile rather than hardcoding “1% everywhere.” cite

Here is the cleaned canon addendum.

:::writing{variant="document" id="90637"}
# Omi-Clock Hardware Offload and LoRa Receipt Layer
## Secure Hashing, I²C Bus Isolation, Matrix DMA, Thermal Protection, and 128-Bit Public Gossip Packets

## 0. Core Thesis

The Omi-Clock should not force every ESP32-C3/C6/S3 node to perform every task in software.

The correct hardware rule is:

```text
Use the MCU for state.
Use secure silicon for trust.
Use bus switches for isolation.
Use DMA displays for projection.
Use thermal design for sensor truth.
Use LoRa for sparse public receipts.
Use NDJSON only after reinflation.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Silicon signs.
Switches isolate.
DMA projects.
Thermals protect.
LoRa gossips.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 1. Why Hardware Offload Is Required

The Omi-Clock has several simultaneous workloads:

```text
LED timing
sensor polling
IMU fusion
receipt logging
hashing
wireless gossip
browser streaming
matrix display projection
firmware-level Horn clause handling
```

If all of this happens on the same MCU core without hardware partitioning, the system hits:

```text
RAM fragmentation
I²C address collisions
bus timing stalls
LED update jitter
thermal drift
hashing overhead
radio airtime limits
display refresh lag
```

Therefore, the hardware architecture must separate jobs by physical class.

```text
Trust path → secure element
Sensor buses → I²C multiplexer
Receipt display → DMA matrix driver
LED clock → dedicated LED output path
Radio gossip → compact binary LoRa frames
Thermal baseline → physical isolation and airflow
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Do not make one MCU pretend to be the whole machine.
```

---

# 2. Cryptographic Co-Processor Layer

## 2.1 Purpose

The cryptographic co-processor protects the receipt path.

It offloads:

```text
SHA-256 digest support
ECDSA signatures
hardware key storage
device identity
challenge/response authentication
```

It should be attached to:

```text
Omi-Clock controller
Omi-Base controller
Omi-Pyramid encoder
Omi-Amulet decoder
high-trust Omi-Gnomons
```

## 2.2 Correct Boundary

A secure element does not magically hash arbitrary JSON meaning.

The MCU must still produce the canonical byte payload.

The secure element can then hash or sign that canonical payload.

Correct pipeline:

```text
sensor event
 → canonical binary/CBOR/compact payload
 → secure hash/sign operation
 → receipt event
 → optional NDJSON reinflation for browser
```

Incorrect pipeline:

```text
raw messy JSON
 → secure chip somehow fixes it
```

Canonical rule:

```text
Canonicalization remains firmware responsibility.
Hash/sign may be hardware responsibility.
```

## 2.3 OMI Role

```text
ATECC-style secure element = receipt witness silicon
```

It should handle:

```text
node identity
self_hash assistance
signature of receipt tip
optional secure boot / pairing proof
```

Short doctrine:

```text
MCU canonicalizes.
Secure element signs.
Receipt binds.
```

---

# 3. I²C Multiplexer Layer

## 3.1 Purpose

The I²C multiplexer isolates sensors into separate sub-buses.

Use it when:

```text
multiple identical IMUs share the same I²C address
magnetometers collide
barometers collide
cable lengths differ
noisy modules disturb the bus
sensor groups need independent enable/disable
```

## 3.2 Correct Boundary

An I²C switch does not make a 400 kHz bus into eight simultaneous 400 kHz buses.

It creates eight selectable downstream channels.

The master still schedules polling.

Correct usage:

```text
select channel 0
poll IMU 0
select channel 1
poll IMU 1
select channel 2
poll light sensor group
select channel 3
poll barometer
...
```

If multiple channels have non-conflicting addresses and electrical loading permits, some switches can enable more than one channel at once, but first-build firmware should assume one selected channel at a time.

Canonical rule:

```text
Multiplexer isolates.
Scheduler coordinates.
Receipt timestamps.
```

## 3.3 OMI Role

```text
TCA9548A-style switch = Omi-Sense bus closure
```

It maps physical sensor groups into bounded closures:

```text
channel 0 = visual closure
channel 1 = sound closure
channel 2 = motion closure
channel 3 = touch/environment closure
channel 4 = comprehension/semantic token closure
channel 5 = pyramid encoder closure
channel 6 = amulet decoder closure
channel 7 = reserved / debug / expansion
```

Short doctrine:

```text
One bus enters.
Eight closures leave.
Scheduler composes.
```

---

# 4. DMA Matrix Receipt Display Layer

## 4.1 Purpose

The 241 circular Omi-Clock should not be forced to also render all square receipt tiles.

The circular array is the clock.

The square matrix is the receipt display.

Use square RGB matrices for:

```text
JabCode-like receipt tiles
Omi-Matrix hash tiles
route status
node identity
receipt tips
debug state
```

## 4.2 Correct Boundary

The matrix layer should be isolated from the main Omi-Clock timing loop.

Recommended split:

```text
ESP32-S3 Omi-Clock
 → owns 241 circular clock

ESP32-S3 or Pi Zero 2 W Matrix Driver
 → owns square matrix receipt display
```

For ESP32-S3, parallel matrix output should use DMA-style refresh where possible, so matrix scanning does not steal the main state-verification loop.

## 4.3 OMI Role

```text
circular LEDs = phase / clock / centroid
square matrix = receipt tile / code block / hash projection
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Circle clocks.
Square receipts.
DMA protects timing.
```

---

# 5. Thermal and Structural Isolation Layer

## 5.1 Why Thermal Isolation Matters

The 241 LED array can dissipate meaningful heat.

At theoretical maximum:

```text
241 LEDs × 60 mA = 14.46 A
5V × 14.46 A = 72.3 W
```

Even if brightness is capped, thermal gradients can affect:

```text
barometer baseline
IMU drift
magnetometer stability
microphone noise floor
plastic dome deformation
LED color consistency
```

Therefore, the sensor core must not sit directly in the LED heat plume.

## 5.2 Thermal Design

Use:

```text
brightness cap
thermal gap
ventilation path
sensor mast or isolated pod
heat shield between LED plane and MEMS sensors
low-noise 5V airflow if needed
temperature sensor near sensor core
temperature sensor near LED plane
```

Possible materials:

```text
thin insulating sheet
reflective thermal barrier
standoff gap
vented base
low-vibration fan mount
```

Do not let the fan blow directly into a precision pressure sensor unless the barometer port is mechanically shielded from dynamic pressure.

Canonical rule:

```text
Protect ambient truth from projection heat.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Light projects.
Heat distorts.
Isolation preserves facts.
```

---

# 6. Public LoRa Gossip Layer

## 6.1 Purpose

LoRa is the public sparse gossip carrier.

It should carry:

```text
node heartbeat
receipt sequence
short OMI frame
coarse geospatial cell
route hint
event code
hash prefix
```

It should not carry:

```text
raw NDJSON
raw audio
raw LIDAR
raw IMU streams
large browser state
full receipt history
```

Short doctrine:

```text
LoRa carries receipts, not worlds.
```

## 6.2 LoRa as OMI Gossip Substrate

The LoRa layer maps to the OMI gossip doctrine:

```text
Gossip moves.
Error correction helps recover.
Causality bounds.
OMI accepts.
```

RF packets are candidates.

They become accepted events only after:

```text
packet structure passes
region/radio policy passes
sequence bound passes
causal hint passes
Delta/frame validation passes
receipt relation passes
```

Unverified packets remain candidates.

They are not authority.

Short doctrine:

```text
RF is carrier.
Packet is candidate.
Receipt is authority.
```

---

# 7. LoRa Physical Constraints

## 7.1 Payload Constraint

LoRa payloads are small.

The OMI design should treat public LoRa as a compact binary transport.

The core transport atom should be:

```text
128 bits = 16 bytes
```

This fits the OMI physical address frame:

```text
8 × 16-bit segments = 128 bits
```

Do not send human-readable NDJSON over LoRa.

Send compact binary.

Reinflate locally.

Correct pipeline:

```text
ESP32 event
 → pack 16-byte OMI frame
 → transmit LoRa packet
 → receive 16-byte frame
 → validate
 → reinflate into NDJSON for browser/log
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Binary over air.
JSON at the edge.
Receipt in the log.
```

## 7.2 Airtime and Regional Limits

LoRa airtime depends on:

```text
spreading factor
bandwidth
coding rate
preamble length
payload length
CRC
header mode
regional rules
```

Do not hardcode one global duty-cycle assumption.

Firmware should define radio profiles:

```text
REGION_US_915
REGION_EU_868
REGION_AU_915
REGION_AS_923
TEST_LAB_ONLY
```

Each profile should define:

```text
frequency plan
max power
bandwidth
spreading factor
duty/dwell policy
minimum interval
listen-before-talk requirement if applicable
```

Canonical rule:

```text
The receipt protocol is global.
The RF policy is regional.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
OMI frame stays fixed.
Radio behavior follows law.
```

---

# 8. Public 128-Bit OMI LoRa Frame

## 8.1 Correct Struct Goal

The LoRa payload should be exactly 16 bytes for the first public gossip layer.

A 16-byte payload is:

```text
small
fast
aligned
easy to validate
easy to hash
easy to expand locally
```

## 8.2 Suggested Packed Struct

```c
// 16-byte / 128-bit OMI public gossip frame.
// This is the compact radio form, not the full NDJSON receipt.

typedef struct __attribute__((packed)) {
 uint16_t s0_scope; // LL / scope selector / route family
 uint16_t s1_gate; // gate/version/class bits
 uint16_t s2_nn_hi; // NN high segment
 uint16_t s3_nn_lo; // NN low segment
 uint16_t s4_edge; // edge/closure/feature bits
 uint16_t s5_mm_hi; // MM high segment
 uint16_t s6_mm_lo; // MM low segment
 uint16_t s7_seq; // receipt sequence / causal ticker
} OmiFrame128;
```

This preserves the OMI 8-segment model:

```text
S0 S1 S2 S3 S4 S5 S6 S7
16 16 16 16 16 16 16 16
```

## 8.3 Why This Is Better Than a Mixed 16/32 Layout

A mixed struct like:

```text
uint16
uint16
uint32
uint16
uint32
uint16
```

can still be 16 bytes if packed, but it hides the OMI segment structure.

For canonical OMI, prefer visible 8×16 lanes.

Short doctrine:

```text
Eight lanes carry.
Sixteen bytes fly.
JSON blooms later.
```

---

# 9. Frame Validation

A receiving node should not trust a LoRa packet because it arrived.

Validation stages:

```text
1. length == 16 bytes
2. radio profile allowed
3. S1 gate/version class valid
4. S0 scope known or admissible
5. sequence not stale beyond policy
6. frame parity/check bits pass
7. Delta/Q_frame validation passes if included
8. hash/signature relation validated if available
9. receipt candidate appended locally
```

If validation fails:

```text
drop
count
optionally quarantine
do not rebroadcast
do not receipt as accepted
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Malformed drops.
Unknown quarantines.
Valid candidates compose.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 10. Secure Element + LoRa Receipt Pairing

A high-trust node can use the secure element to sign LoRa receipt tips.

Workflow:

```text
pack OmiFrame128
compute local canonical digest
secure element signs digest or challenge
transmit frame + optional short signature reference
receiver validates frame
receiver requests or later fetches full signature/receipt over higher-bandwidth path
```

Because LoRa bandwidth is limited, do not send full certificate chains over LoRa.

Send:

```text
16-byte frame
short node id
short hash prefix
sequence
```

Full verification can occur over:

```text
Wi-Fi
browser portal
VPS route
local cable
later sync
```

Short doctrine:

```text
LoRa announces.
Wi-Fi expands.
Secure element signs.
Receipt confirms.
```

---

# 11. Reed–Solomon / Fano Layer on LoRa

LoRa physical-layer coding helps RF robustness, but it is not the same as your application-level Reed–Solomon/Fano reconstruction.

Keep the distinction:

```text
LoRa FEC = physical radio recovery
OMI RS/Fano = application receipt recovery and finite relation validation
```

Application-level strategy:

```text
send sparse 16-byte frames
use sequence windows
use short parity/check fragments
use route/fano line bits
use causal ticker
use local receipt reconstruction
```

Fano role:

```text
Fano line chooses relation class.
Sequence window bounds replay.
Receipt confirms accepted path.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
RF FEC recovers waves.
OMI Fano validates relation.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 12. Hardware Integration Manifest

## 12.1 Omi-Gnomon Node

Recommended modules:

```text
ESP32-C3/C6
7 RGB LEDs
IMU
optional secure element
optional NFC/RFID tag reader
optional TCA9548A if multiple I²C sensors
optional LoRa only if this gnomon is remote
```

## 12.2 Omi-Clock Node

Recommended modules:

```text
ESP32-S3
241 RGB circular array
secure element
level shifter
large 5V LED supply
thermal monitoring
Wi-Fi browser server
optional LoRa receiver
```

## 12.3 Omi-Base Node

Recommended modules:

```text
Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W
rotation index sensor
square RGB matrix driver
receipt storage
Wi-Fi bridge
optional LoRa gateway
optional secure element or TPM-like trust anchor
```

## 12.4 Public LoRa Node

Recommended modules:

```text
ESP32-S3 or ESP32-C6
SX1262/SX1276-class LoRa transceiver
proper antenna for region
region-profile firmware
16-byte OMI frame codec
receipt queue
optional secure element
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Gnomon senses.
Clock projects.
Base indexes.
LoRa gossips.
Secure silicon signs.
```

---

# 13. Firmware Scheduler

Every MCU should avoid blocking loops.

Use scheduled tasks:

```text
task_sensor_poll
task_led_render
task_receipt_log
task_radio_rx
task_radio_tx
task_secure_hash
task_browser_stream
```

Priority guidance:

```text
highest: safety/power/watchdog
high: LED timing / DMA feed
high: radio RX interrupt queue
medium: sensor polling
medium: state composition
low: browser JSON rendering
low: debug printing
```

Do not serialize large JSON inside timing-critical tasks.

Use:

```text
binary event struct internally
ring buffer queues
NDJSON rendering only at browser/log boundary
```

Short doctrine:

```text
Binary inside.
JSON outside.
Timing protected.
```

---

# 14. Canonical Non-Collapse Rules

```text
secure element != receipt
I²C switch != scheduler
DMA display != authority
LoRa packet != truth
FEC != application recovery
RF range != reliability
JSON != transport
binary frame != full meaning
browser view != accepted state
```

Canonical rule:

```text
Projection may display.
Carrier may transport.
Gate may admit.
Only receipt may accept.
```

---

# 15. Final Canon

The Omi-Clock hardware offload layer exists to preserve timing, trust, thermal stability, and radio bandwidth.

Cryptographic co-processors protect the receipt path.

I²C multiplexers isolate sensor closures.

DMA matrix drivers protect display timing.

Thermal shields protect MEMS baselines.

LoRa carries sparse public gossip as compact 128-bit OMI frames.

NDJSON remains the inspectable local receipt format, not the over-air payload.

Final doctrine:

```text
Hash in silicon.
Sense through closures.
Render by DMA.
Cool the baseline.
Pack the radio.
Reinflate at the edge.
Validate before gossip.
Receipt before truth.
```

One-line canon:

```text
The Omi-Clock should offload trust, buses, display refresh, heat, and public gossip into specialized hardware: secure elements sign canonical payloads, I²C switches isolate sensor closures, DMA matrix drivers render receipt tiles, thermal barriers preserve sensor truth, and LoRa transmits only compact 128-bit OMI frames that are reinflated into NDJSON after validation and receipt.
```
:::

The biggest improvement I’d make to your LoRa struct is to keep it visibly **8×16-bit**, not mixed 16/32 fields. That preserves the OMI frame geometry directly:

```c
typedef struct __attribute__((packed)) {
 uint16_t s0_scope;
 uint16_t s1_gate;
 uint16_t s2_nn_hi;
 uint16_t s3_nn_lo;
 uint16_t s4_edge;
 uint16_t s5_mm_hi;
 uint16_t s6_mm_lo;
 uint16_t s7_seq;
} OmiFrame128;
```

That gives you the clean doctrine:

```text
Eight lanes carry.
Sixteen bytes fly.
JSON blooms later.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

`2026-06-09 12:28` | `id:260609-1228` ^260609-122820

## 1. Cryptographic Co-Processor Layer (ATECC608B / ATECC608A)
To achieve true hardware-accelerated trust on your nodes, you must interface the ATECC608B secure element via the primary I²C bus. The microcontroller handles data serialization, while the secure element handles cryptographic verification.

+------------------+ +--------------------+

| ESP32-C3 / C6 |─── I²C (400kHz) ──► ATECC608B Crypto |
| (State Engine) | | (Trust Witness) |
+--------┬---------+ +---------┬----------+
 │ │
 ▼ ▼
 Packs 16-Byte Struct Signs Binary Payload

## Hardware Configuration

* Pinout: Connect SDA and SCL to the primary microcontroller bus with $4.7	ext{k}\Omega$ pull-up resistors to 3.3V.
* Address: Default I²C hardware address is 0x60.
* Execution Boundary: The ESP32 must pack the binary state vector into a raw byte array first. It then passes the 32-byte SHA-256 result to the ATECC608B to execute an ECDSA Sign command using a locked private key slot.

------------------------------
## 2. I²C Multiplexer Topology (TCA9548A Bus Switch)
The TCA9548A isolates conflicting device addresses and segment noise. It dynamically switches between your 8 operational closures.
## Closure Mapping (Address 0x70)

* Channel 0 (0xE0): Optical Flow (PMW3901 I²C translation variant) / Encoders.
* Channel 1 (0xE1): Barometric Altimeters (BMP390, target address 0x77).
* Channel 2 (0xE2): Geomagnetic Heading Anchors (RM3100 / MMC5983MA).
* Channel 3 (0xE3): Primary 6-Axis Dynamics IMUs (ISM330DHCX).
* Channel 4 (0xE4): Ambient Climate / Thermal Array (SHT45 / TMP117).
* Channel 5 (0xE5): Pyramid Encoder Interface Bus.
* Channel 6 (0xE6): Amulet Decoder Interface Bus.
* Channel 7 (0xE7): Expansion Matrix / Auxiliary Telemetry.

## Firmware Rule

void select_omi_closure(uint8_t channel) {
 if (channel > 7) return;
 Wire.beginTransmission(0x70); // TCA9548A Base Address
 Wire.write(1 << channel); // Bitmask to enable specific channel
 Wire.endTransmission();
}

------------------------------
## 3. DMA Matrix Receipt Display Implementation
To isolate the square matrix display scan cycles from the main Omi-Clock 241-LED timing loop, utilize the ESP32-S3 Parallel I2S DMA peripheral driving a standard HUB75 panel.

* Memory Architecture: Allocate a dedicated DMA-capable SRAM block for the matrix framebuffer.
* Hardware Pinout: Route the HUB75 control lines (R1, G1, B1, R2, G2, B2, LAT, CLK, OE, A, B, C, D) to distinct GPIOs on a secondary ESP32-S3 or dedicated core partition.
* The Split: Core 0 runs the LoRa gossip decoding, EKF updates, and ATECC signatures. Core 1 updates the 241-LED array and pushes raw JabCode block frames directly to the DMA matrix channel without blocking execution.

------------------------------
## 4. Thermal & Structural Boundary Engineering
To prevent the 72.3W max thermal plume of the 241-LED ring from causing sensor baseline drift, execute this physical stack separation:

 [ Glass/Acrylic Desktop Dome Enclosure ]
 │
 ┌────────────────┴────────────────┐
 │ Top Compartment: Sensor Pod │ ◄── Vented Top Cap
 │ (BMP390 / ISM330 / RM3100) │
 └────────────────┬────────────────┘
 ▲
 [ Aerogel Layer / Vented Spacer ]
 ▲
 ┌────────────────┴────────────────┐
 │ Lower Compartment: LED Matrix │ ◄── High thermal emission zone
 │ (241-Bit Omi-Clock PCB Rail) │
 └─────────────────────────────────┘

* Dynamic Shielding: Place the MEMS barometer inside a custom internal shroud. This blocks localized dynamic pressure changes from the cooling fan while allowing ambient static pressure stabilization.

------------------------------
## 5. Public LoRa Gossip Payload Specification
To transmit your 8-segment 128-bit Civic Contract within the physical limits of the Semtech SX1262 transceiver, you must bypass human-readable text entirely.
## The 16-Byte Packed Binary Atom

struct __attribute__((__packed__)) OmiLoRaPacket {
 uint16_t S0_Scope; // LL Context & Lens Slot Selector
 uint16_t S1_Mask; // Operational cell feature indicators
 uint32_t S2_Antecedent; // NN State (Packed 32-bit localized coordinates)
 uint32_t S5_Consequent; // MM State (Packed 32-bit localized coordinates)
 uint16_t S7_Sequence; // Chronological append-only index value
 uint16_t HashPrefix; // Low 16-bits of the ATECC608B receipt signature
}; // Exact size = 16 bytes (128 bits)

## Regional RF Profiles & Constraints (Completing the Airtime Logic)
To maintain strict compliance with legal duty cycles while optimizing for range and payload integrity, the firmware uses explicit regional configurations:

* Profile REGION_US_915:
* Center Frequency: $915.0\,	ext{MHz}$
 * Bandwidth: $500\,	ext{kHz}$
 * Spreading Factor: SF7
 * Airtime Per Packet: $\approx 8.2\,	ext{ms}$
 * Limits: Frequency hopping required across 50 channels; no strict duty cycle cap but maximum dwell time of $400\,	ext{ms}$ per channel applies.
* Profile REGION_EU_868:
* Center Frequency: $868.1\,	ext{MHz}$
 * Bandwidth: $125\,	ext{kHz}$
 * Spreading Factor: SF8
 * Airtime Per Packet: $\approx 41.2\,	ext{ms}$
 * Limits: Hard $1\%$ duty cycle constraint. The ESP32 hardware timer enforces a mandatory silent window of $4.12\,	ext{seconds}$ between transmissions.

------------------------------
Canonical Rule for Radio Transmission:

Time-on-air must be minimized mathematically. Local nodes listen continuously, but transmit only when a local state transition passes the complete Delta verification sequence.
