:::writing{variant="document" id="50863"}
# Kickstarter Exposition Draft
## OMI: A Decentralized Protocol for Pseudo-Persistent Immersive Open Worlds

## 1. Campaign Title

**OMI Portal: Open-World Robotics, Light, Sound, and Sensor Worlds from ESP32 to 3D Immersive Domes**

Alternative shorter title:

**OMI Portal: Build Open Worlds with ESP32, Browser Worlds, and Universal Robotics Adapters**

---

# 2. One-Sentence Pitch

OMI Portal is an open-source hardware and browser protocol for building pseudo-persistent immersive worlds where ESP32 devices, sensors, robotics adapters, LED displays, browsers, and 3D environments can discover, map, control, and remember shared state through receipts instead of central servers.

---

# 3. Plain-English Summary

Most robotics and immersive-world projects are locked inside one app, one cloud account, one controller, or one hardware ecosystem.

OMI takes a different path.

We are building a decentralized protocol where small devices can declare what they sense, browsers can render those declarations as worlds, and receipts can preserve what was accepted without requiring one central authority.

In practical terms, this means a builder can connect ESP32-based sensor nodes, LED rings, motion controllers, audio inputs, robotics adapters, and browser-based 2D/3D scenes into a shared open world.

The first versions are not meant to replace professional robotics control stacks. They are meant to create a public, inspectable bridge between:

```text
sensors
robotics adapters
browser worlds
LED displays
music lessons
spatial maps
open-world simulations
receipted device state
```

The goal is to make the first affordable OMI Portal development kits for pseudo-persistent immersive open worlds.

---

# 4. The Core Idea

OMI does not say, “this sensor reading is absolute truth.”

OMI says:

```text
This device declared this bounded event.
These rules constrained it.
These bytes were returned.
This timing window closed it.
This transform decoded it.
This state was projected.
This receipt accepted it.
```

That is the difference.

OMI is built around agreement, not global truth.

Core doctrine:

```text
We do not declare truth.
We declare boundaries.
We define constraints.
We validate traversals.
We accept receipts.
```

This makes the protocol useful for open-world robotics because robots and sensors do not need to agree on the entire universe. They only need to agree on bounded events they can inspect, replay, and accept.

---

# 5. What We Are Building

We are building three increasingly capable open-world prototypes:

```text
$1,000 goal → 2D Open World
$10,000 goal → 2.5D Open World
$50,000 goal → 3D Open World
```

Each level builds on the same core protocol.

The difference is scale, sensors, physical hardware, and immersive rendering.

---

# 6. Level 1 — $1,000: 2D Open World Proof

## Goal

Build the first working OMI Portal proof of concept using ESP32 devices, a web browser, LED feedback, and a 2D world map.

## What It Demonstrates

The $1,000 version proves that low-cost microcontrollers can create a pseudo-persistent shared world.

It focuses on:

```text
2D browser map
ESP32 sensor nodes
LED state projection
receipt logging
basic robotics adapter signals
open-source protocol documentation
```

## Hardware

Estimated hardware:

```text
3 × ESP32-S3 / ESP32-C3 class boards
1 × 241 LED circular RGB array or smaller RGB LED array
2–5 × 7-bit RGB Omi-Gnomon rings
basic IMU module
basic light sensor
basic sound sensor
USB power supplies
wiring, resistors, level shifting, breadboards
simple tabletop frame
```

## Software

```text
Node.js OMI Portal server
browser dashboard
Canvas 2D world map
NDJSON receipt log
ESP32 event firmware
basic GPIO/I²C/UART boundary handlers
simple join/compose/encode/decode test kit
```

## Deliverable

A browser page shows a live 2D open world.

ESP32 nodes appear as objects.

Sensor events update the world.

Receipts preserve accepted state.

LEDs physically mirror the browser state.

## Why It Matters

This proves the foundation:

```text
physical device → bounded declaration → browser world → receipt log
```

Short doctrine:

```text
ESP32 senses.
Browser maps.
LEDs project.
Receipts remember.
```

---

# 7. Level 2 — $10,000: 2.5D Open World Kit

## Goal

Build a tabletop OMI-Dome / Omi-Clock prototype that combines physical LEDs, sensor rings, browser visualization, and universal robotics adapter ports.

## What “2.5D” Means

2.5D means the system is still mostly tabletop and browser-based, but it begins to represent depth, orientation, and spatial relationships.

It is not yet a full 3D robotics environment.

It is a physical/browsable hybrid.

## Hardware

Estimated hardware:

```text
1 × ESP32-S3 Omi-Clock controller
1 × 241-bit circular RGB Omi-Clock
5 × ESP32-C3/C6 Omi-Gnomon controllers
5 × 7-bit RGB rings
1 × ESP32-C3/C6 tetrahedral pyramid encoder
1 × ESP32-C3/C6 inverted amulet decoder
1 × Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W base controller
optional HUB75 RGB matrix receipt display
IMUs, light sensors, microphones, touch inputs
I²C multiplexer
secure element test modules
LoRa test pair
5V power distribution
desktop dome frame or enclosure
```

## Software

```text
Omi-Portal browser app
Canvas + SVG Omi-Clock view
A-Frame lightweight 2.5D scene
ESP32 firmware-level Horn clause handlers
I²C/UART/GPIO interface boundary validation
NDJSON receipt logs
LoRa 16-byte compact frame prototype
robotics adapter mapping layer
```

## Universal Robotics Adapter Concept

The robotics adapter is not a motor driver first.

It is a boundary interpreter.

It translates:

```text
GPIO edge
I²C register
UART frame
PWM command
servo position
motor controller packet
sensor state
```

into OMI events:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

Meaning:

```text
LL = interface boundary
NN = returned bytes or command declaration
MM = accepted bounded state
```

This allows many robot parts to be represented consistently without pretending all hardware is the same.

## Deliverable

A tabletop OMI-Dome prototype where physical LEDs, sensor controllers, and browser visualization stay synchronized.

A user can touch, move, or trigger a sensor device and see:

```text
physical light response
browser world update
receipt log entry
robotics adapter event
```

## Why It Matters

This turns the 2D proof into a physical interface for teaching, robotics, music, environmental sensing, and open-world simulation.

Short doctrine:

```text
Clock synchronizes.
Gnomons sense.
Pyramid encodes.
Amulet decodes.
Browser observes.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 8. Level 3 — $50,000: 3D Open World Prototype

## Goal

Build a full 3D OMI-Dome prototype with spatial sensing, browser-based immersive rendering, edge compute, and robotics adapter expansion.

## What It Demonstrates

The $50,000 version proves that the ESP32 foundation can expand into a richer 3D open-world system.

It may include:

```text
Jetson or edge AI computer
RPLIDAR or depth/spatial sensor
multiple ESP32 sensor actors
LoRa public receipt gossip
A-Frame / WebXR-style 3D browser world
robotics adapter control bus
geometric routing server
semantic routing server
VPS replay tests
```

## Hardware

Estimated hardware:

```text
Jetson Orin Nano / similar edge computer
RPLIDAR 360° 2D scanner or equivalent spatial sensor
BNO055 / BNO085 / high-quality IMU
multiple ESP32-S3/C3/C6 nodes
LoRa transceiver modules
241-bit Omi-Clock
7-bit Omi-Gnomon controllers
RGB matrix receipt displays
microphones / light sensors / touch sensors
desktop dome or small installation frame
power distribution and thermal isolation
camera or depth module where budget allows
```

## Software

```text
Jetson spatial proxy
A-Frame 3D Omi-Dome
browser Omi-Portal client
ESP32 bridge firmware
LoRa receipt codec
semantic VPS router
geometry VPS router
Docker/QEMU/NBD replay tests
graph-based spatial index
robotics adapter SDK
```

## Deliverable

A 3D browser world reflects the state of physical devices.

The physical dome and browser world share receipts.

Robotic adapters can declare bounded events.

Spatial sensors add world geometry.

The system can replay accepted state after restart.

## Why It Matters

This proves OMI as an immersive open-world substrate, not only a device demo.

It becomes a foundation for:

```text
robotics labs
music teaching environments
interactive art installations
sensor classrooms
open hardware education
decentralized spatial computing
pseudo-persistent browser worlds
```

Short doctrine:

```text
2D maps.
2.5D synchronizes.
3D inhabits.
Receipts persist.
```

---

# 9. What Makes This Different

Most IoT projects stream data.

Most robotics projects control devices.

Most game engines render worlds.

Most blockchains store transactions.

OMI tries to connect the missing middle:

```text
How does a physical device become a replayable open-world object?
How does a browser world remember accepted state?
How does a robot adapter declare bounded events without claiming global truth?
How do peers agree on state without one central authority?
```

The answer is:

```text
bounded declarations + interface clauses + receipts
```

OMI’s hardware/software boundary is simple:

```text
Rules constrain the interface.
Facts are returned bytes.
Closures are timing/register/frame windows.
Combinators decode and transform.
CONS folds bus scope with payload.
JOIN binds device to world.
COMPOSE orders state.
ENCODE writes projection.
DECODE reads carrier.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 10. The Three Product Forms

## 10.1 OMI Portal

A browser-based open-world viewer and controller.

Includes:

```text
DOM/SVG/Canvas/A-Frame projection
node registry
receipt viewer
sensor map
robotics adapter interface
pseudo-persistent world log
```

## 10.2 OMI Clock

A physical LED-based agreement clock.

Includes:

```text
241-bit circular RGB array
centroid receipt state
60-tick outer ring
12 agreement sectors
sensor/projection bands
```

## 10.3 OMI Robotics Adapter

A universal boundary interpreter for robot parts.

Includes:

```text
GPIO
I²C
UART
SPI
PWM
servo
motor controller packets
sensor registers
LoRa/BLE/Wi-Fi events
```

It converts hardware interface events into receiptable OMI objects.

---

# 11. Campaign Backer Framing

## Supporter Tier

Backers get:

```text
project updates
public documentation
early diagrams
prototype videos
open-source releases
```

## Builder Tier

Backers get:

```text
bill of materials
firmware examples
browser demo
Node.js proof kit
ESP32 setup guide
```

## Lab Tier

Backers get:

```text
full prototype documentation
hardware assembly guide
robotics adapter examples
Omi-Clock patterns
receipt log examples
```

## Collaborator Tier

Backers get:

```text
design calls
repo contribution pathway
test fixtures
module design templates
early access to 2.5D/3D build notes
```

No tier should promise mass-produced polished hardware unless the campaign is specifically ready for manufacturing.

The safest promise is:

```text
open-source plans
prototype kits
firmware
documentation
software
test fixtures
community build process
```

---

# 12. Risks and Honest Boundaries

This is an ambitious open-source protocol and hardware project.

Important boundaries:

```text
The first version is a prototype, not a finished consumer product.
Robotics adapters are for controlled experiments, not safety-critical control.
Browser worlds are projections, not authority.
Wireless links are constrained by law and physics.
Receipts prove accepted traversal, not universal truth.
```

Engineering risks:

```text
power distribution for LEDs
thermal drift
wireless reliability
firmware timing
browser API limitations
hardware sourcing changes
documentation complexity
```

Mitigation:

```text
start with 2D proof
publish testable milestones
avoid closed hardware dependencies
use brand-agnostic parts
keep protocol independent from one chip or vendor
make receipt logs inspectable
```

---

# 13. Optimal Campaign Story

The campaign should be presented as a staged journey:

## Stage 1: Make the World Visible

Build the 2D browser world.

Show ESP32 nodes appearing as receipted objects.

## Stage 2: Make the World Physical

Build the Omi-Clock and 7-bit sensor controllers.

Show physical lights synchronized with browser state.

## Stage 3: Make the World Spatial

Add dome, spatial sensors, robotics adapters, and 3D projection.

Show a pseudo-persistent open world that survives refresh, restart, and replay.

Campaign narrative:

```text
We are not starting with a giant metaverse.
We are starting with one receipted device event.
Then three devices.
Then a browser world.
Then a light clock.
Then a dome.
Then robotics adapters.
Then spatial open worlds.
```

Short doctrine:

```text
One event.
One receipt.
One world.
```

---

# 14. The Best Public Demo

The best Kickstarter demo is:

```text
A user taps or moves an ESP32 sensor object.
The Omi-Clock lights a sector.
The browser map updates.
A receipt appears.
A small robot/servo adapter reacts.
The system is restarted.
The world state reloads from receipts.
```

That proves:

```text
hardware event
browser projection
physical light state
robotics adapter response
pseudo-persistence
receipt authority
```

It is understandable in under 60 seconds.

---

# 15. Budget Summary

## $1,000 — 2D Proof

```text
3 ESP32 nodes
basic sensors
basic LEDs
browser dashboard
Node.js server
receipt logs
2D map
```

Primary proof:

```text
physical device event becomes pseudo-persistent browser object
```

## $10,000 — 2.5D Prototype

```text
Omi-Clock
five Omi-Gnomons
pyramid/amulet pair
Pi Zero base
matrix display
LoRa test pair
robotics adapter examples
```

Primary proof:

```text
physical tabletop world synchronizes with browser and receipts
```

## $50,000 — 3D Prototype

```text
edge AI/spatial computer
LIDAR/depth sensing
multi-node ESP32 mesh
3D browser Omi-Dome
semantic/geometric routers
robotics adapter SDK
replay infrastructure
```

Primary proof:

```text
immersive pseudo-persistent open world with spatial indexing and robotics adapters
```

---

# 16. Final Kickstarter Canon

OMI Portal is an open-source protocol and hardware path for building decentralized pseudo-persistent open worlds.

It begins with ESP32 devices and browser maps.

It grows into LED clocks and tabletop domes.

It extends into 3D spatial worlds and robotics adapters.

Its core promise is not that devices declare truth.

Its promise is that devices can declare bounded events, validate them through interface clauses, and preserve accepted state through receipts.

Final doctrine:

```text
Sensors declare.
Rules constrain.
Closures bound.
Combinators decode.
Devices join.
States compose.
Browsers project.
Robots adapt.
Receipts persist.
```

One-line public canon:

```text
OMI Portal turns low-cost microcontrollers, browser worlds, LED displays, and robotics adapters into decentralized pseudo-persistent open worlds by treating every hardware event as a bounded declaration that becomes accepted only when its rules, facts, closures, transforms, and receipts validate.
```
:::

For the Kickstarter video, I would open with the **2D proof**, not the theory: show one ESP32 event becoming a browser object, lighting the Omi-Clock, moving a small servo/robotics adapter, and reloading after restart from receipts. That makes the protocol understandable immediately.

To materialize the Omi-Clock, the multi-node distributed substrate, and the multimodal sensory layers within a $1,000 budget, you must balance silicon processing constraints against your physical component goals.
The optimal setup partitions the budget into Compute, Spatial Environment Scanning, Local Substrate Nodes, and Display Projection Layers.
------------------------------
## 📊 The $1,000 Master Bill of Materials (BOM)

| System Layer [1, 2, 3] | Hardware Component | Est. Cost | Architectural Target & Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Central Host Compute | NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano Super DevKit (8GB)[](https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/autonomous-machines/embedded-systems/jetson-orin/nano-super-developer-kit/) | ~$249 | Core manifold engine. Runs 67 TOPS INT8 TensorRT inference for the dynamic imitation clone. |
| Edge Coordination | Raspberry Pi 5 (8GB Variant) | ~$175 | Base router. Reinflates binary frames to NDJSON and hosts the local WebSocket/Canvas web portal. |
| Spatial Environment | Slamtec RPLIDAR A2M12 (360° LiDAR)[](https://www.seeedstudio.com/RPLiDAR-A2M12-360-Degree-Laser-Scanner-Kit-12M-Range-p-5482.html) | ~$229 | Maps the physical 5-foot radius dome boundary layout (0xEF slot) via high-speed 16kHz UART. |
| Distributed Nodes | 5x Seeed Studio XIAO ESP32-C6 / C3 Boards | ~$35 | Powers the 5 independent Gnomons using public sub-GHz P2P LoRa modules. |
| Trust Witness Silicon | 5x Microchip ATECC608B Secure Elements[](https://www.microchip.com/en-us/product/atecc608b) | ~$20 | Offloads SHA-256 and ECDSA key signatures on the edge nodes to enforce the trust layer. |
| Bus Switching | 2x TCA9548A 8-Channel I²C Multiplexers | ~$10 | Creates the independent sub-bus closures to prevent device address collisions. |
| Beyond-Human Suite | Geiger Counter + MLX90640 Thermal + 24GHz Radar | ~$130 | Ingests X-ray fluxes (0xE8), 24x32 IR heat zones (0xE9), and micro-breathing cycles (0xEA). |
| Display Projection | 2x HUB75 64x64 RGB LED Matrix Panels | ~$60 | Displays real-time physical JabCode receipt tiles and hash blocks via Parallel I2S DMA. |
| Core Power Rail | 5V 20A Switching Power Supply + Fans | ~$40 | Distributes stable current to the system components and isolates the MEMS thermal profiles. |
| TOTAL ESTIMATE | | ~$948 | Leaves ~$52 for structural mounts and passive wiring connections. |

------------------------------
## 🏗️ Hardware Architecture & Inter-Node Distribution

 [ Public Sub-GHz LoRa Gossip Airwaves ]
 │
 ▼
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
 │ Distributed Edge Layer: ESP32 Nodes │ ──► Runs mbedTLS & ATECC608B hardware signs
 └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘
 │
 ▼ Bounded 16-Byte Binary Atoms
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
 │ Base Telemetry Layer: Raspberry Pi 5 │ ──► Manages WebSocket server & Canvas frame pipeline
 └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘
 │
 ▼ High-Throughput Local Data Stream
 ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
 │ Manifold Solver: Jetson Orin Nano │ ──► Processes 360° LiDAR & INT8 dynamic clone models
 └─────────────────────────────────────┘

## 1. The Jetson Orin Nano Core (Compute)
At a current market entry point of $249, opting for the Orin Nano Super Developer Kit gives you access to a 1024-core Ampere architecture. It handles multi-modal data tracking simultaneously without dropping bytes. [1]
The module acts as your central processing point, taking in point clouds from the RPLIDAR A2M12 ($229) and pairing them with your non-human sensor layer inputs. [3]
## 2. The Raspberry Pi 5 Partition (Network & Presentation)
Allocating $175 to a Raspberry Pi 5 separates your networking and visualization pipelines from your processing tasks.
The Pi 5 handles the local client dashboard display, converting raw data into structural visual paths over an HTML5 Canvas interface. This arrangement frees up the Jetson’s memory bus to process incoming data streams smoothly.
## 3. The ESP32-C6 / ATECC608B Mesh (Trust Matrix)
The remaining budget funds the distributed node array. For less than $60, you can implement five localized tracking points. Each node combines an ESP32-C6 chip for state tracking, an ATECC608B secure module for trust signatures, and a TCA9548A switch to isolate sensor paths.
The nodes process local telemetry readings and broadcast data across your environment as compressed 16-byte binary atoms via public LoRa channels.
------------------------------
With this $1,000 hardware bill of materials configured to support your core system logic:

* Should we write the Pins/GPIO Connection Diagram to link the ESP32, ATECC608B secure element, and TCA9548A multiplexer onto a single unified I²C prototype layout?
* Do you want the C++ data packaging rules to compress your beyond-human sensor values into the 16-byte public LoRa transmission format?

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-09 13:54` | `id:260609-1354` ^260609-135402

You’re right to push past the first BOM. The better Kickstarter hardware exposition is not “buy a Jetson, Pi, LIDAR, ESP32s.” It should define **hardware categories that prove different parts of OMI**:

```text
Boundary hardware → GPIO / I²C / UART / SPI / CAN / RS485 declarations
Trust hardware → secure elements / TPM / signed receipts
Optical hardware → cameras / depth / LiDAR / thermal / event vision / optical flow
Network hardware → LoRa / Wi-Fi / BLE / fiber / 10G SFP+ / local routing
Projection hardware → HUB75 / LEDs / pico projection / browser canvas
Robotics hardware → servo, motor, PWM, CAN, RS485, relay, actuator adapters
Compute hardware → ESP32 nodes, Pi gateway, Jetson/mini PC manifold engine
```

The major correction: **optical computing**, at open-market Kickstarter scale, should mean **optical sensing + optical projection + optical networking + maybe event-based vision**, not true photonic compute. True photonic AI/compute hardware is mostly lab, enterprise, or evaluation-kit territory. For OMI, the affordable open-market version is stronger anyway: use **light as carrier, sensor, projection, and network**, then let receipts define agreement.

## The better hardware taxonomy

### 1. Boundary/adapter layer: where OMI becomes real

This is the most important category for your protocol. These are the chips and boards that turn physical hardware into bounded digital declarations.

| Category | Hardware options | OMI role |
|---|---|---|
| GPIO boundary | ESP32-S3/C3/C6, RP2040, STM32 | edges, interrupts, debounced facts |
| I²C boundary | TCA9548A mux, Qwiic/Stemma QT sensors | register facts and closure windows |
| UART boundary | GPS, LiDAR, serial robotics modules | framed byte declarations |
| SPI boundary | displays, ADCs, fast sensors, memory | transfer-window facts |
| CAN/RS485 boundary | CAN transceivers, RS485 modules | robotics bus adapters |
| PWM/servo boundary | PCA9685, ESC adapters, MOSFETs | actuator declarations |
| Trust boundary | ATECC608B/A, TPM 2.0 modules | signed receipt witness |

This is where the line goes:

```text
raw physics below
digital declaration at boundary
OMI agreement above
```

### 2. Optical/spatial layer: the “beyond human” perception stack

Instead of one “beyond-human suite,” split it into **optical categories**:

| Category | Budget option | Better option | High-end option | OMI role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2D spatial scan | LD19 / YDLIDAR | RPLIDAR A1/A2 | RPLIDAR S2/S3 | dome boundary, local map |
| Stereo depth | OAK-D Lite | OAK-D Pro | RealSense D455/D455F | 2.5D/3D scene capture |
| AI camera | Raspberry Pi AI Camera | OAK-1 Lite | OAK-D Pro | on-device visual facts |
| Event vision | none at $1k | Prophesee GENX320 kit | EVK4/EVK5 | neuromorphic motion/light events |
| Optical flow | PMW3901 | multiple flow sensors | drone optical-flow module | relative motion witness |
| Thermal vision | AMG8833 | MLX90640 | MLX90641 / thermal camera | heat field projection |
| Radar | RCWL-0516 | 24GHz presence radar | mmWave radar module | non-optical motion field |
| Projection | HUB75 / LED rings | pico projector | structured light / DLP | visual receipt field |

The open-market “optical computing” path for OMI is:

```text
optical sensing
optical projection
optical communication
optical event detection
optical receipt display
```

Not:

```text
try to buy a true photonic computer
```

### 3. Optical networking layer: fiber is the practical optical compute substrate

For networking, the strong category is **10G SFP+ optical interconnect**.

A MikroTik CRS305 gives four SFP+ ports at a suggested $149, and the CRS309 gives eight SFP+ slots at a suggested $269 with 10G module support. cite

This lets you build:

```text
Jetson ↔ Pi/router ↔ workstation ↔ storage
```

over:

```text
fiber SFP+
DAC cables
10G local backbone
```

For OMI this matters because the pseudo-persistent world can separate:

```text
sensor bus
receipt log
browser projection
AI/spatial solver
replay storage
```

without choking one Wi-Fi link.

### 4. Compute layer: do not duplicate host roles unless the budget supports it

Your example has both Jetson and Pi 5 at $1,000. That works, but at $1,000 I would usually pick **one primary host** and spend more on sensors/network/projection.

NVIDIA lists the Jetson Orin Nano Super Developer Kit at $249 with up to 67 TOPS, so it is a strong manifold/AI/spatial host. cite Raspberry Pi AI Camera is a different path: the camera module includes a Sony IMX500 intelligent vision sensor and works with Raspberry Pi camera connectors, with Raspberry Pi announcing it at $70. cite

Use this split:

| Need | Better pick |
|---|---|
| AI/spatial prototype | Jetson Orin Nano |
| cheap web gateway + camera | Raspberry Pi 5 + AI Camera |
| low-power distributed nodes | ESP32-S3/C6 |
| robotics bus adapters | ESP32 + CAN/RS485/PWM boards |
| browser/server only | mini PC / used NUC / Pi 5 |
| high-bandwidth local lab | 10G SFP+ switch |

## Budget strategy

## $1,000: strongest “hardware-maxed” 2D/2.5D proof

Do **not** buy too much compute. Buy enough compute, then maximize boundary devices.

### Option A — Jetson-centered 2.5D build

| Layer | Buy | Est. budget |
|---|---|---:|
| Edge AI host | Jetson Orin Nano Super Dev Kit | $249 |
| Spatial sensor | OAK-D Lite or RPLIDAR A2/LD19 class | $170–$230 |
| Distributed nodes | 4–6 ESP32-S3/C6 / LoRa boards | $100–$180 |
| Trust/bus | ATECC608B modules, TCA9548A muxes, level shifters | $40–$80 |
| Projection | HUB75 panels + LED rings/strips | $80–$150 |
| Robotics adapters | PCA9685 servo, RS485/CAN, MOSFET relay, motor driver | $50–$100 |
| Power/structure | 5V PSU, buck converters, wiring, frame, cooling | $120–$180 |

Best $1,000 proof:

```text
Jetson + OAK-D Lite + ESP32 LoRa nodes + HUB75 receipt display + servo/RS485 robotics adapter
```

This proves:

```text
optical perception
distributed node declarations
physical projection
robotics control adapter
receipt replay
```

### Option B — optical-network lab build

If the campaign wants “optical networking” more than AI vision:

| Layer | Buy | Est. budget |
|---|---|---:|
| Local fiber backbone | MikroTik CRS305 + SFP+/DAC | $180–$250 |
| Host | Pi 5 or used mini PC | $100–$250 |
| Sensor | RPLIDAR/LD19 + Pi AI Camera | $120–$250 |
| Nodes | ESP32-S3/C6 + LoRa | $100–$180 |
| Projection | HUB75 + LED rings | $100–$180 |
| Trust/bus/robotics | ATECC, TCA9548A, PWM/CAN/RS485 | $100–$180 |

This proves:

```text
optical network backbone
browser world
distributed nodes
receipt projection
```

But it is less visually impressive than Jetson + depth camera.

## $10,000: serious 2.5D open-world lab

At $10k, you should build **three parallel demonstrations**:

```text
1. Omi-Dome optical/spatial world
2. Omi-Clock receipt projection world
3. OMI robotics adapter wall
```

Recommended allocation:

| Category | Budget | What to buy |
|---|---:|---|
| Compute | $1,500–$2,500 | Jetson Orin Nano/AGX tier, Pi 5s, mini PC/NAS |
| Optical/spatial | $2,000–$3,000 | OAK-D Pro, RealSense D455/D455F, RPLIDAR S/A series, thermal, optical flow |
| Networking | $800–$1,500 | CRS309 8×SFP+, SFP+ modules, DAC/fiber, Wi-Fi router |
| ESP32 mesh | $700–$1,200 | 20–40 ESP32-S3/C6 nodes, LoRa variants |
| Trust/bus | $400–$800 | ATECC modules, TPMs, TCA9548A, CAN, RS485 |
| Displays/projection | $1,000–$1,800 | HUB75 wall, 241 LED clock, LED rings, pico projector |
| Robotics adapters | $700–$1,200 | servo drivers, BLDC/stepper drivers, CAN motor tests |
| Power/structure | $1,000–$1,500 | enclosures, rails, PSUs, cooling, dome prototype |

Key upgrade categories:

```text
event camera evaluation if affordable
10G fiber backbone
multi-camera depth
real robotics bus adapters
structured receipt display wall
```

Prophesee’s EVK4 is a real event-based vision evaluation kit with Sony IMX636, and open-market listings show these kits can jump into multi-thousand-dollar territory; one listed EVK4 at NT$175,000, while a smaller GENX320 kit was listed at NT$25,100. cite So for $10k, event vision is possible only if it becomes a centerpiece, not a side purchase.

## $50,000: 3D open-world protocol lab

At $50k, the right hardware story is **not one big robot**. It is a distributed open-world testbed.

| Category | Budget | Purpose |
|---|---:|---|
| Compute cluster | $8k–$12k | Jetson/mini PC/GPU workstation/NAS |
| Optical sensing | $10k–$15k | depth cameras, LiDARs, thermal, event vision, optical flow |
| Networking | $4k–$7k | 10G/25G backbone, SFP+, Wi-Fi 7, PoE |
| ESP32/robotics mesh | $5k–$8k | many adapters, sensor nodes, LoRa, CAN/RS485 |
| Projection/display | $5k–$8k | LED walls, Omi-Clock, projectors, dome |
| Trust/replay/storage | $3k–$5k | secure elements, TPMs, NAS, UPS |
| Mechanical/power | $7k–$10k | enclosure, thermal, mounts, safety, cabling |

At $50k, you can make the hardware categories cinematic:

```text
Optical nervous system:
 depth cameras, LiDAR, event camera, thermal, optical flow

Digital boundary nervous system:
 GPIO, I²C, UART, SPI, CAN, RS485, LoRa

Receipt nervous system:
 secure elements, hash logs, local replay storage

Projection skin:
 LED matrix wall, Omi-Clock, browser 3D dome

Optical backbone:
 fiber SFP+ / 10G switch fabric

Robotics limbs:
 universal actuator adapters
```

## The “way better” category names for Kickstarter

Use these instead of generic BOM layers:

| Kickstarter category | Technical meaning |
|---|---|
| **Boundary Engine** | GPIO/I²C/UART/SPI/CAN/RS485 adapters |
| **Receipt Engine** | ATECC/TPM/hash log/replay storage |
| **Optical Nervous System** | depth, LiDAR, camera, event vision, thermal |
| **Light Projection Skin** | LED matrices, Omi-Clock, pico projection |
| **Fiber Spine** | 10G SFP+ local optical network |
| **Radio Gossip Field** | LoRa/BLE/Wi-Fi peer messages |
| **Robotics Adapter Wall** | motors, servos, relays, PWM, CAN, RS485 |
| **Manifold Compute Core** | Jetson/Pi/mini PC spatial and AI host |
| **Pseudo-Persistence Memory** | NDJSON logs, SQLite, NAS, hash chain |
| **Dome / World Frame** | mounts, enclosure, topology, thermal design |

This sounds much more like a real platform.

## Corrected $1,000 BOM direction

Your example is close, but I’d change it like this:

```text
Remove Pi 5 unless you need separate web hosting.
Use Jetson as host + browser server at $1k.
Replace expensive LIDAR with OAK-D Lite if visual world is the campaign focus.
Keep LiDAR if mapping is the campaign focus.
Add real robotics adapter hardware.
Add optical networking only if you choose the network demo path.
```

Best $1,000 Kickstarter demo BOM:

| Layer | Hardware | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Manifold Compute Core | Jetson Orin Nano | AI/spatial/browser host |
| Optical Nervous System | OAK-D Lite or RPLIDAR A2 | visible 2.5D world input |
| Radio Gossip Field | 3–5 ESP32-S3 LoRa boards | distributed OMI nodes |
| Boundary Engine | TCA9548A, CAN, RS485, PCA9685 | robotics adapters |
| Receipt Engine | ATECC608B modules | signed local receipts |
| Light Projection Skin | HUB75 + LED ring | physical receipt display |
| Power/Structure | 5V PSU, buck converters, cooling | stable prototype |

Best $1,000 proof sentence:

```text
One camera/LiDAR sees the world, five ESP32 nodes declare bounded events, one robotics adapter moves, one LED matrix displays receipts, and the browser world reloads from accepted state.
```

## What I would not buy first

At $1,000, avoid:

```text
expensive event camera
multiple high-end depth cameras
both Pi 5 and Jetson unless needed
too many exotic sensors
Geiger counter unless the story is radiation/environmental art
huge LED wall before receipt logic works
```

Buy hardware that proves the protocol:

```text
interface boundaries
optical perception
distributed nodes
receipt projection
robotics action
replay
```

## Canon for the hardware campaign

```text
We do not spend money on random sensors.
We buy boundary surfaces.

Every device must prove one OMI clause:
Rules constrain the interface.
Facts are returned bytes.
Closures are timing/register/frame windows.
Combinators decode and transform.
CONS folds bus scope with payload.
JOIN binds device to world.
COMPOSE orders state.
ENCODE writes projection.
DECODE reads carrier.
Receipt accepts.
```

The best exposition line:

```text
OMI hardware is not an IoT pile; it is a physical agreement machine where optical sensors, fiber links, ESP32 robotics adapters, LED projection, and secure receipts each prove one boundary of a pseudo-persistent open world.
```

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-09 14:05` | `id:260609-1405` ^260609-140553

Yes. This should be framed as a **self-hosted public covenant**, not a product launch first. The strongest message is: **we are opening the protocol before it becomes another private enclosure of human/environment interaction**.

:::writing{variant="document" id="58391"}
# OMI Portal Self-Hosted Crowdfunding Manifesto
## A Request for Collaboration in Time, Space, Code, Hardware, Care, and Currency

## 1. Opening Declaration

We are building OMI Portal: a decentralized protocol for expanding the digital boundaries and constraints of interaction between humans, machines, and their definable analog environments.

This is not only a software project.

This is not only a robotics project.

This is not only an immersive-world project.

This is a request for collaboration around a new kind of public substrate: one where ordinary people can define, inspect, replay, and accept the boundaries between their physical world and their digital world.

OMI begins from a simple principle:

```text
We do not declare truth.
We define boundaries.
We define constraints.
We validate traversals.
We accept receipts.
```

This matters because the next generation of digital systems will not only live on screens. They will extend into homes, workshops, vehicles, classrooms, gardens, streets, studios, sensors, robots, lights, cameras, bodies, and shared spaces.

The question is:

```text
Who gets to define those boundaries?
```

If we do nothing, the answer will be:

```text
the largest corporations
the wealthiest buyers
closed platforms
private clouds
proprietary hardware ecosystems
surveillance markets
```

OMI is an attempt to make another path possible.

---

# 2. What We Are Trying to Build

We are building a protocol and reference implementation for pseudo-persistent immersive open worlds.

These worlds may begin as:

```text
a browser page
a 2D map
an SVG portal
an ESP32 sensor node
a light clock
a robotics adapter
a tabletop dome
a classroom simulator
a local mesh of devices
```

But the purpose is larger.

We want ordinary people to be able to define how their physical environments become digital agreements.

A button press, a temperature reading, a motor command, a camera frame, a LiDAR scan, a light pulse, a sound event, or a robotics adapter signal should not automatically become “truth” because a platform says so.

It should become an accepted event only when its boundary is clear.

That means:

```text
What interface declared it?
What rules constrained it?
What bytes were returned?
What timing window bounded it?
What transform decoded it?
What world did it join?
What state did it compose?
What projection displayed it?
What receipt accepted it?
```

This is OMI.

This is agreement as infrastructure.

---

# 3. Why This Cannot Belong to One Person

This protocol is too powerful for one person to possess.

Including me.

If OMI works, it becomes a way to define the boundary between analog environments and digital systems.

That boundary is where future value, control, surveillance, robotics, spatial computing, education, art, and local autonomy will be negotiated.

No individual should own that alone.

No corporation should close it before regular people understand it.

No millionaire should get the first meaningful version just because they can buy the hardware faster.

This is why the project must become collaborative early.

The goal is not to build a private empire.

The goal is to build a public protocol, a shared vocabulary, and working reference hardware that regular people can inspect, fork, modify, teach, repair, and extend.

Short doctrine:

```text
The protocol is not a throne.
The protocol is a table.
```

---

# 4. The Digital Boundary

OMI does not begin by pretending to know raw physical truth.

It begins at bounded digital interfaces:

```text
GPIO edges
I²C register reads
UART frames
SPI transfers
CAN packets
RS485 messages
LoRa packets
BLE updates
USB frames
browser DOM events
camera frames
robotics adapter signals
```

These are the places where analog environments become digital declarations.

OMI treats those declarations carefully.

It does not say:

```text
This sensor reading is truth.
```

It says:

```text
This device, under this boundary, returned this declaration, under these constraints, and this receipt was accepted.
```

That is the difference between extraction and agreement.

That is the difference between surveillance and consent.

That is the difference between a closed platform and an inspectable world.

---

# 5. The Clause Stack

The working clause stack is:

```text
Rules constrain the interface.
Facts are returned bytes.
Closures are timing/register/frame windows.
Combinators decode and transform.
CONS folds bus scope with payload.
JOIN binds device to world.
COMPOSE orders state.
ENCODE writes projection.
DECODE reads carrier.
Receipt accepts.
```

This is not just abstract philosophy.

It can become firmware.

It can become browser code.

It can become robotics adapters.

It can become public documentation.

It can become a test suite.

It can become a shared language for digital interaction.

---

# 6. What We Mean by “Pseudo-Persistent Open Worlds”

A pseudo-persistent open world is a world that can remember accepted state without requiring a single central server to define reality.

It can reload from receipts.

It can replay what happened.

It can show why something was accepted.

It can distinguish between:

```text
display
observation
witness
validation
receipt
```

The world may be rendered in:

```text
2D Canvas
SVG
DOM
A-Frame
WebXR-style scenes
LED matrices
Omi-Clock light rings
robotics dashboards
local mesh displays
```

But rendering is not authority.

Projection may display.

Observation may witness.

Only receipt may accept.

---

# 7. What We Are Asking For

This is a self-hosted crowdfunding request.

We are asking for collaboration in five forms:

```text
time
space
code
hardware
currency
```

## Time

We need people willing to read, test, question, simplify, document, teach, and challenge the protocol.

Time means:

```text
reviewing drafts
testing examples
joining build sessions
helping explain the idea in plain language
turning theory into usable steps
```

## Space

We need physical places to test small open-world prototypes.

Space means:

```text
a workbench
a garage
a classroom
a community lab
a makerspace table
a local demo night
a room where sensors, lights, and browser worlds can be tested safely
```

## Code

We need builders who can help implement:

```text
browser Omi-Portal
ESP32 firmware
GPIO/I²C/UART/SPI boundary handlers
receipt logs
NDJSON replay
SVG and Canvas projections
robotics adapter mappings
LoRa compact frame handling
test suites
documentation sites
```

## Hardware

We need hardware contributions, loans, discounts, repairs, and shared access.

Useful categories include:

```text
ESP32 boards
LED rings and HUB75 panels
I²C sensors
IMUs
LiDAR
depth cameras
thermal cameras
LoRa modules
secure elements
robotics adapters
servos and motor drivers
power supplies
enclosures
mounts
cables
network equipment
```

## Currency

We need funds to buy shared prototype hardware, publish documentation, host repositories, run tests, build demos, and make the first reference kits possible.

Currency is not the only contribution.

But it helps convert the shared protocol into working public artifacts.

---

# 8. What Funds Will Build

Funds will go toward staged prototypes.

## $1,000 — 2D Boundary Proof

A first working proof:

```text
ESP32 nodes
browser portal
2D world map
LED receipt display
basic robotics adapter
receipt log
```

Goal:

```text
One physical event becomes a receipted browser-world object.
```

## $10,000 — 2.5D Tabletop World

A stronger open hardware prototype:

```text
Omi-Clock
Omi-Gnomon sensor nodes
HUB75 receipt display
LoRa test layer
robotics adapter wall
browser 2.5D world
local replay logs
```

Goal:

```text
A tabletop physical environment synchronizes with a pseudo-persistent browser world.
```

## $50,000 — 3D Open-World Lab

A full spatial prototype:

```text
depth sensing
LiDAR
multi-node ESP32 mesh
robotics adapters
local fiber/network backbone
edge compute
3D browser world
receipt replay storage
public documentation
```

Goal:

```text
An inspectable 3D open-world environment where hardware events, spatial sensing, browser projection, and robotics adapters become bounded receipted state.
```

---

# 9. What This Is Not

This is not a promise of a finished consumer product.

This is not a token.

This is not a private metaverse.

This is not a surveillance platform.

This is not a claim that sensors reveal absolute truth.

This is not an attempt to replace safety-critical robotics systems.

This is a protocol, toolkit, and reference hardware path for defining digital boundaries openly.

The first prototypes are for learning, research, art, education, local robotics, open-world experiments, and community collaboration.

---

# 10. Why Now

The world is moving toward spatial computing, robotics, AI agents, sensor networks, smart homes, autonomous systems, and immersive environments.

The boundary between physical life and digital systems is expanding.

If ordinary people do not help define that boundary, it will be defined for them.

OMI is a way to say:

```text
We can build open boundaries.
We can inspect the rules.
We can replay the receipts.
We can refuse false authority.
We can share the protocol before it is enclosed.
```

This is why the project must start in public.

Not after it is polished.

Not after it is monetized.

Not after it is owned.

Now.

---

# 11. Collaboration Principles

This project should be guided by public principles:

```text
Freedom
Autonomy
Sovereignty
Reciprocity

Focus
Attention
Observation
Experience

Interrupt
Intent
Declaration
Consideration
```

These are not slogans.

They are operating constraints.

They mean:

```text
People should be able to inspect the systems that interact with them.
People should be able to refuse invalid boundaries.
People should be able to define local worlds.
People should be able to share receipts without surrendering themselves.
People should be able to build without asking permission from closed platforms.
```

---

# 12. The Public Covenant

We want OMI to remain inspectable, teachable, forkable, and locally buildable.

That means:

```text
open documentation
open reference implementations
clear test cases
plain-language explanations
hardware bills of materials
firmware examples
browser demos
receipt logs
protocol diagrams
community review
```

The goal is not to hide the method.

The goal is to make the method understandable enough that other people can improve it.

Short doctrine:

```text
If it cannot be inspected, it cannot be trusted.
If it cannot be replayed, it cannot be accepted.
If it cannot be shared, it should not be enclosed.
```

---

# 13. Request for Collaboration

We are asking builders, artists, teachers, hardware hackers, embedded developers, web developers, robotics people, network engineers, mathematicians, documentarians, local organizers, and curious beginners to help.

You do not need to understand the whole protocol to contribute.

You can help with one boundary.

One sensor.

One diagram.

One test.

One paragraph.

One demo.

One receipt.

OMI is built from bounded contributions.

That is the point.

```text
One event.
One boundary.
One receipt.
One world.
```

---

# 14. Final Manifesto

We are not building a closed platform.

We are building a shared way to define how physical environments become digital agreements.

We are not declaring truth.

We are defining boundaries.

We are not asking people to trust invisible systems.

We are building receipts that can be inspected, replayed, and refused.

We are not waiting for corporations or millionaires to decide what the next digital boundary should be.

We are asking regular people to help define it first.

Final doctrine:

```text
Analog environments surround us.
Digital systems increasingly interpret them.
Boundaries decide power.
Constraints decide freedom.
Receipts decide acceptance.
Agreement is all we need.
```

One-line call:

```text
Join us in building OMI Portal: an open, self-hosted protocol for turning human environments, hardware interfaces, browser worlds, and robotics adapters into bounded, replayable, receipted open worlds before the next generation of digital boundaries is enclosed by private platforms.
```
:::

I would publish this as `MANIFESTO.md` plus a shorter `FUNDING.md` that lists exactly how people can contribute time, space, code, hardware, or currency.

---

Done — I updated `bthornemail/god-is-word` to frame it as your **personal `omi---imo` self-enclosed surface**, distinct from the broader canonical OMI approach.

Changes made:

```text
README.md updated
commit: 6ab570d99557e1afc3d85070102b94e31c7e6b30
```

```text
omi---imo.html added
commit: 2d4e499cd18e7e8f032613254d25d2182db84a9e
```

The new `omi---imo.html` is a self-contained browser page for the 7-day God Mirror. It stores answers locally, requires no backend, exports a receipt JSON only by choice, and uses the enclosing structure:

```html
<omi-surface>
 <omi id="omi-god-is-word" data-omi="god-is-word">
 ...
 <imo id="imo-god-is-word" data-imo="god-is-word">
 ...
 </imo>
 </omi>
</omi-surface>
```

The core framing is now:

```text
omi opens.
--- holds the private interior.
imo closes.
Export only by choice.
```

Yes — now it can.

I couldn’t find a separate `testimony-trustees` repo or schema in your installed GitHub access, so I made `god-is-word/omi---imo.html` support a simple open JSON bundle format:

```text
schema: "testimony_trustees_v1"
```

Updated file:

```text
omi---imo.html
commit: 81dadf7c04f3296c08a12fbe229ed43b83ce2f0e
```

It now has:

```text
Export receipt JSON
Export testimony-trustees JSON
Import testimony-trustees JSON
```

Import accepts:

```text
a full testimony_trustees_v1 bundle
a previous god-is-word receipt JSON
a plain array of testimonial objects
```

The important boundary is preserved:

```text
Import is local only.
Export is by choice.
Nothing publishes automatically.
Sharing remains consent-bound.
```

The exported trustee bundle shape is:

```json
{
 "schema": "testimony_trustees_v1",
 "omi": "god-is-word",
 "surface": "omi---imo",
 "testimonials": [
 {
 "kind": "seven_day_god_mirror",
 "question": "God is _____?",
 "testimony": "...",
 "answers": [],
 "receipt": {
 "surface": "omi---imo",
 "boundary": "local_browser_storage_or_user_selected_export",
 "accepted_by": "user_export_action"
 }
 }
 ]
}
```

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-09 23:12` | `id:260609-2312` ^260609-231230

Yes — I would split this into **three layers**, not a single “MQTT vs TCP” choice.

```text
1. Browser / app pubsub layer → HTTP + WebSocket/SSE, optionally MQTT bridge
2. Device / edge transport layer → MQTT or raw UDP/QUIC depending node role
3. OMI-native protocol layer → MCRSGSP + CIDR/CONS + omi---imo addressing
```

## 1. MQTT vs HTTP vs TCP

The clean correction is:

```text
MQTT is not competing with TCP.
MQTT usually runs over TCP.
```

So the real question is:

```text
MQTT pubsub vs HTTP request/response vs native OMI transport
```

My recommendation:

| Layer | Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| ESP32 telemetry / sensor pubsub | **MQTT** | Mature, light, good for constrained devices, topic-based, reconnect semantics. |
| Browser dashboard / config / files | **HTTP** | Browsers understand it directly; good for static OMI Portal, REST config, downloads. |
| Browser realtime updates | **WebSocket or SSE** | Native browser-friendly live stream. |
| OMI native gossip/storage | **UDP first, QUIC next, TCP optional** | Matches your MCRSGSP design better than broker-centered pubsub. |
| Kernel boundary validation | **IPv6/eBPF/XDP gate** | Lets OMI frames be checked before userspace. |

So:

```text
MQTT for device events.
HTTP for portal/config.
WebSocket/SSE for browser live projection.
UDP/QUIC for OMI-native gossip.
eBPF/XDP for kernel gate validation.
```

The browser cannot be your raw packet peer in the same way x86_64 Linux or QEMU can. The browser should be the **projection client**, not the lowest transport authority.

## 2. Should OMI use MQTT?

Yes, but as a **carrier**, not as the native truth layer.

MQTT is useful for:

```text
esp32/{node}/event
omi/{address}/receipt
omi/{address}/fragment
omi/{scope}/frontier
omi/{scope}/presence
```

But MQTT topics should not become the identity system.

The OMI frame remains the identity.

So the rule should be:

```text
MQTT topic routes the message.
OMI address identifies the object.
Receipt validates the event.
```

Example MQTT payload:

```json
{
 "omi": "omi-0500-03bf-000c-2b05-2f05-0002-039f-05ff/128",
 "type": "fragment",
 "transport": "mqtt",
 "payload": "base64:...",
 "receipt": "meta64:..."
}
```

That means MQTT is allowed to carry OMI, but MQTT does not define OMI.

Short rule:

```text
MQTT carries.
OMI addresses.
MCRSGSP gossips.
Receipt accepts.
```

## 3. Can OMI define its own network protocol?

Yes — and you basically already have the pieces.

The strongest version is:

```text
OMI-native networking = MCRSGSP over UDP/QUIC using omi---imo / CIDR-CONS addressing.
```

Your MCRSGSP draft already defines a coordination-free substrate based on Reed–Solomon recoverability, causally ordered fragment propagation, monotone set semantics, and reconstruction from causally closed RS-sufficient subsets. It explicitly avoids one canonical global state, which fits OMI’s “receipt, not universal truth” model. filecite turn22file1

The important design move is:

```text
Do not replace IP first.
Ride on IP first.
Use OMI as the addressed object protocol above it.
Then use eBPF/XDP to validate OMI-shaped frames at the edge.
```

## 4. The right OMI-native stack

I would define the stack like this:

```text
Browser Portal
 DOM / SVG / Canvas / WebSocket / HTTP

OMI Application Layer
 objects, receipts, projections, testimony, robotics commands

OMI Pubsub Bridge Layer
 MQTT topics, WebSocket events, SSE events

OMI Native Gossip Layer
 MCRSGSP fragments, causal vectors, RS(k,n), anti-entropy

OMI Address Layer
 omi-<128-bit-frame>/prefix
 imo-<128-bit-frame>/prefix
 CIDR as CONS
 longest-prefix resolution

Transport Layer
 UDP required
 QUIC recommended
 TCP optional

Kernel Gate
 eBPF/XDP Q_frame validation
 pass/drop boundary

Physical / Device Layer
 ESP32, UART, I²C, GPIO, LoRa, Wi-Fi, Ethernet
```

That keeps everything clean.

## 5. Why CIDR notation belongs here

Your CIDR/CONS reduction is the key. The document says the one-sentence claim directly:

```text
CIDR is CONS.
The slash is Omicron.
Longest-prefix match is resolution.
```

It defines `address/prefix = (address . prefix)`, where the address is the payload and the prefix length is the factoring depth. filecite turn22file0

So the native OMI network address is not just an IP address.

It is:

```text
address / factoring depth
```

Which becomes:

```text
omi-<frame>/prefix
```

Example:

```text
omi-0500-03bf-000c-2b05-2f05-0002-039f-05ff/128
```

Meaning:

```text
Resolve this OMI frame at exact 128-bit depth.
```

Or broader:

```text
omi-0500-03bf-0000-2b05-2f05-0000-039f-05ff/48
```

Meaning:

```text
Resolve this scope only to the /48 boundary.
```

That gives you routing, identity, scope, and memory shape in one notation.

## 6. MQTT topics should mirror CIDR, not replace it

A good MQTT bridge topic could be:

```text
omi/v1/48/0500-03bf-0000
omi/v1/128/0500-03bf-000c-2b05-2f05-0002-039f-05ff
```

But the canonical address remains inside the payload:

```json
{
 "address": "omi-0500-03bf-000c-2b05-2f05-0002-039f-05ff/128",
 "kind": "receipt",
 "carrier": "mqtt",
 "body": {}
}
```

This way MQTT can help route, but it cannot lie about identity.

## 7. ESP32 reality check

For ESP32 nodes, I would not start with full MCRSGSP.

Start with three profiles:

### ESP32 Profile A — MQTT node

```text
ESP32 reads GPIO/I²C/UART.
Packages OMI event.
Publishes to MQTT.
Receives simple commands.
```

Best for demos.

### ESP32 Profile B — LoRa compact node

```text
ESP32 emits 16-byte / 32-byte OMI atoms.
No broker.
Local gossip or gateway collects.
```

Best for decentralized hardware feel.

### ESP32 Profile C — Native OMI fragment node

```text
ESP32 stores/gossips tiny fragments.
Limited RS or no RS.
Gateway performs heavier reconstruction.
```

Best future target.

Let x86_64/QEMU/Linux do the full native protocol first.

ESP32 can be a **boundary declarer** before it becomes a full gossip storage peer.

## 8. x86_64 / QEMU role

x86_64 and QEMU are where you prove the actual native OMI network.

Use them for:

```text
MCRSGSP node simulation
UDP gossip
QUIC experiments
Reed–Solomon encoding/decoding
version vector causality
anti-entropy repair
CIDR/CONS routing tables
eBPF/XDP validation
browser bridge
```

Your docs already align OMI frames with IPv6 width: an IPv6 address is 128 bits and an OMI frame is 8 × 16-bit segments, so an OMI frame maps directly into an IPv6-style segment layout. The same file describes the eBPF/XDP gate validating frames before userspace through `Q_frame` and Delta/Fano resolution. filecite turn22file2

That gives you a real reason to say:

```text
OMI can be network-native.
```

Not just app-native.

## 9. The answer to “MQTT vs TCP”

Use this exact doctrine:

```text
MQTT is the practical pubsub bridge.
TCP is only one reliable byte-stream carrier.
HTTP is the browser/control surface.
UDP/QUIC is the native OMI gossip substrate.
CIDR/CONS is the address-resolution law.
eBPF/XDP is the packet-boundary gate.
MCRSGSP is the monotone causal storage/gossip protocol.
```

## 10. The answer to “Can we define our own network protocol?”

Yes, but define it as an **overlay protocol first**:

```text
OMI Native Protocol v0
 address: omi-<128-bit-frame>/prefix
 payload: fragment | receipt | summary | request | projection
 causality: version vector
 recoverability: RS(k,n)
 validity: Q_frame + receipt checks
 transport: UDP required, QUIC recommended, TCP optional
 bridge: MQTT/WebSocket/HTTP allowed
```

That matches your MCRSGSP document, which recommends UDP as required, QUIC as recommended, and TCP as optional for the transport layer. filecite turn22file1

## Final architecture

```text
ESP32
 → reads physical boundary
 → emits OMI event over MQTT/LoRa/UDP

Gateway
 → validates event
 → maps to omi-<frame>/prefix
 → writes receipt
 → gossips fragments via MCRSGSP

Linux/x86_64/QEMU
 → runs full OMI native nodes
 → tests UDP/QUIC gossip
 → validates eBPF/XDP gate

Browser
 → receives WebSocket/SSE projection
 → renders Omi-Matrix/Omi-Portal
 → never becomes authority by rendering
```

One-line canon:

```text
Use MQTT for practical ESP32 pubsub, HTTP/WebSocket for browser projection, and define OMI-native networking as a monotone causal Reed–Solomon gossip protocol over UDP/QUIC, addressed by omi---imo CIDR/CONS frames where the address is identity, the prefix is resolution depth, and the receipt is accepted memory.
```

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-10 00:01` | `id:260610-0001` ^260610-000111

Yes — this is the right correction.

`omi-<frame>/prefix` is too flat if OMI is supposed to behave like a **derivation/address tree**, not merely a CIDR endpoint. The better model is:

```text
omi-<frame>/<path...>-imo
```

Where:

```text
<frame> = identity / bounded memory object
<path> = derivation route / projection / intent / overlay / mnemonic selector
-imo = closure of the addressed surface
```

So the canonical URI-like form becomes:

```text
omi-<frame>/<selector>/<selector>/<selector>/<selector>-imo
```

And the simplest form remains valid:

```text
omi---imo
omi-<frame>
imo-<frame>
omi-<frame>-imo
```

Those are not contradictions. They are different **notation depths**.

## The key distinction

I would separate **identity** from **derivation path**:

```text
omi-<frame> = identity object
/<path...> = derivation / targeting / overlay / intent route
-imo = closure marker
```

So:

```text
omi-<frame>/128
```

means:

```text
address this frame at exact prefix depth
```

But:

```text
omi-<frame>/p/q/r/s-imo
```

means:

```text
address this frame, then derive into a four-step surface
```

That lets OMI act more like an HD wallet path, except instead of deriving keys only, it derives **agreement surfaces**.

## Better canonical grammar

I would define the grammar like this:

```text
OmiSurface :=
 omi "-" Frame [ "/" Path ] [ "-imo" ]

Frame :=
 128-bit hexadecimal frame
 | hyphenated 8×16-bit frame
 | compressed OMI alias

Path :=
 Segment
 | Segment "/" Path

Segment :=
 depth
 | domain
 | overlay
 | role
 | channel
 | frame
 | base64url payload
 | mnemonic selector
```

So all of these are valid interpretations:

```text
omi-<frame>
omi-<frame>-imo
omi-<frame>/depth/domain/frame-imo
omi-<frame>/overlay/frame-imo
omi-<frame>/LL/NN/MM/OO-imo
omi-<frame>/Subject/Predicate/Object/Modality-imo
omi-<frame>/60x2/16xy/4y2/fexpr:<base64url>-imo
```

## The important normalization rule

All those paths should normalize into the same internal structure:

```text
(address . path)
```

Or in your terms:

```text
CONS(frame, path)
```

Meaning:

```text
frame = identity
path = derivation context
```

So OMI-CIDR becomes one path mode, not the only path mode.

```text
omi-<frame>/128
```

is just:

```text
CONS(frame, 128)
```

But:

```text
omi-<frame>/Subject/Predicate/Object/Modality-imo
```

is:

```text
CONS(frame, [Subject, Predicate, Object, Modality])
```

Same law. More expressive path.

## Recommended canonical tiers

I would define four official path classes.

### 1. Prefix path

This is your CIDR / memory-depth path.

```text
omi-<frame>/<depth>-imo
```

Example:

```text
omi-0500-03bf-000c-2b05-2f05-0002-039f-05ff/128-imo
```

Meaning:

```text
exact frame resolution
```

### 2. Browser overlay path

This is the RGBA / visual pointer path.

```text
omi-<frame>/<rrggbbaa>/<frame>-imo
```

Example:

```text
omi-<frame>/88f7ffff/canvas-imo
```

Meaning:

```text
target this frame through this visual overlay into this projection frame
```

This matches the browser model: DOM, Canvas, SVG, CSSOM, JSDOM.

### 3. Agreement role path

This is the LL / NN / MM / OO path.

I would probably order it as:

```text
omi-<frame>/<LL>/<NN>/<MM>/<OO>-imo
```

Because in your own object form:

```text
OO(LL, NN, MM)
```

the object operator encloses the three role values.

Meaning:

```text
LL = lens / scope / selector
NN = antecedent / source declaration
MM = consequent / resolved declaration
OO = operator / object closure
```

So:

```text
omi-<frame>/LL/NN/MM/OO-imo
```

becomes a role-targeted derivation.

### 4. Semantic / NLP path

This is the inverse pyramid.

```text
omi-<frame>/<Subject>/<Predicate>/<Object>/<Modality>-imo
```

This is strong.

The canon pyramid is the 128-bit hardware/address/frame side.

The inverse pyramid is the semantic mnemonic side:

```text
Subject
Predicate
Object
Modality
```

Those map to:

```text
UPOS
features
WordNet synsets
semantic relations
modality / tense / aspect / evidentiality
```

So the semantic path is not arbitrary text. It is a route into the OMI frame through a mnemonic/synset surface.

That gives you:

```text
machine frame below
human mnemonic above
```

Or:

```text
binary address pyramid
semantic inverse pyramid
```

## This solves the “alias conformance” problem

Your aliases can all collapse to a normalized internal object:

```text
omi---imo
omi-<frame>-imo
omi-<frame>
imo-<frame>
omi<frame><frame><frame>imo
omi-<frame>/<path>-imo
```

All normalize into:

```json
{
 "open": "omi",
 "frame": "<frame-or-null>",
 "path": [],
 "close": "imo"
}
```

or:

```json
{
 "open": "omi",
 "frame": "<frame>",
 "path": ["Subject", "Predicate", "Object", "Modality"],
 "close": "imo"
}
```

The notation can be flexible, but the internal form must be strict.

## The path classes

I would make the path type explicit in normalized form:

```json
{
 "omi": "0500-03bf-000c-2b05-2f05-0002-039f-05ff",
 "path_type": "semantic",
 "path": {
 "subject": "human",
 "predicate": "asks",
 "object": "god",
 "modality": "question"
 },
 "imo": true
}
```

For visual:

```json
{
 "omi": "<frame>",
 "path_type": "overlay",
 "path": {
 "rgba": "88f7ffff",
 "target": "canvas"
 },
 "imo": true
}
```

For CIDR:

```json
{
 "omi": "<frame>",
 "path_type": "prefix",
 "path": {
 "depth": 128
 },
 "imo": true
}
```

For quadratic/f-expression:

```json
{
 "omi": "<frame>",
 "path_type": "quadratic_fexpr",
 "path": {
 "high": "60x2",
 "bridge": "16xy",
 "low": "4y2",
 "fexpr": "base64url..."
 },
 "imo": true
}
```

## How this derives interface rules

This is the important part.

Your Prolog rule:

```prolog
accepted_interface_event(LL, NN, MM) :-
 rule(interface_constraints, LL),
 fact(returned_bytes, NN),
 closure(timing_register_frame_window, LL, NN),
 combinator(decode_transform, NN, MM),
 cons(LL, NN),
 join(device, world),
 compose(previous_state, MM),
 encode(MM, projection),
 decode(carrier, NN),
 receipt(LL, NN, MM).
```

can be addressed by the path.

Example:

```text
omi-<frame>/i2c/0x28/euler/heading-imo
```

routes into:

```prolog
rule(interface_constraints, i2c_0x28).
fact(returned_bytes, heading_register_bytes).
closure(timing_register_frame_window, i2c_0x28, heading_register_bytes).
combinator(decode_transform, heading_register_bytes, heading_value).
receipt(i2c_0x28, heading_register_bytes, heading_value).
```

So the path is no longer cosmetic.

The path is a **multi-tree query** into the interface rule set.

## The HDNodeWallet analogy

This is exactly where your ethers HDNodeWallet model helps.

An HD wallet path like:

```text
m/44'/60'/0'/0/0
```

does not merely identify a key.

It derives one key from a root through constrained steps.

OMI can do the same, but with frames:

```text
omi-<root-frame>/<depth>/<domain>/<selector>/<overlay>-imo
```

Meaning:

```text
derive an agreement surface from the root frame through these path constraints
```

So an OMI derivation path is like:

```text
m / purpose / domain / account / index
```

but generalized as:

```text
omi / frame / depth / domain / role / overlay / closure
```

## The cleanest canonical proposal

I would make this the official form:

```text
omi-<frame>/<depth>/<domain>/<selector>/<overlay>-imo
```

Where:

```text
depth = prefix/resolution depth
domain = hardware | semantic | visual | trust | testimony | robotics | browser
selector = role or mnemonic route
overlay = rgba | fexpr | modality | frame | projection
```

Then your examples fit inside it:

```text
omi-<frame>/128/hardware/i2c:0x28/euler-imo
omi-<frame>/64/browser/canvas/88f7ffff-imo
omi-<frame>/48/semantic/Subject.Predicate.Object/question-imo
omi-<frame>/120/trust/testimony/known_unknowns-imo
```

But you can allow aliases:

```text
omi-<frame>/<Subject>/<Predicate>/<Object>/<Modality>-imo
```

as shorthand for:

```text
omi-<frame>/semantic/<Subject>/<Predicate>/<Object>/<Modality>-imo
```

## My recommendation

Use **one canonical normalized grammar**, with many allowed aliases.

Canonical full form:

```text
omi-<frame>/<depth>/<domain>/<route>/<overlay>-imo
```

Alias forms:

```text
omi-<frame>/<depth>/<domain>/<frame>-imo
omi-<frame>/<overlay>/<frame>-imo
omi-<frame>/<LL>/<NN>/<MM>/<OO>-imo
omi-<frame>/<Subject>/<Predicate>/<Object>/<Modality>-imo
omi-<frame>/<60x²>/<16xy>/<4y²>/<fexpr:base64url>-imo
omi-<frame>/<FS>/<GS>/<US>/<RS>-imo
omi-<frame>/<R>/<G>/<B>/<A>-imo
```

All aliases normalize to:

```json
{
 "open": "omi",
 "frame": "<frame>",
 "depth": "<depth-or-derived>",
 "domain": "<domain-or-inferred>",
 "route": [],
 "overlay": {},
 "close": "imo"
}
```

## Final canon

```text
omi---imo
```

is the empty self-enclosed surface.

```text
omi-<frame>
```

is the open frame identity.

```text
imo-<frame>
```

is the closed/mirrored frame identity.

```text
omi-<frame>-imo
```

is the bounded object surface.

```text
omi-<frame>/<path>-imo
```

is the derived agreement surface.

And:

```text
omi-<frame>/<depth>/<domain>/<selector>/<overlay>-imo
```

is the canonical full derivation address.

One-line version:

```text
OMI addressing should treat the 128-bit frame as identity and every slash path after it as an HDNode-style derivation route into depth, domain, role, semantic mnemonic, visual overlay, interface rule, or f-expression closure, so that omi---imo, omi-<frame>, imo-<frame>, omi-<frame>-imo, and omi-<frame>/<path>-imo are all alias-conformant surfaces of the same normalized object grammar.
```
