:::writing{variant="document" id="69241"}
# Addendum: Canonical OMI Surface Projections and Named Faces

## 1. Core Rule

All OMI surfaces are projections of the same underlying accepted object:

```text
o---o
```

or, in expanded readable form:

```text
omi---imo
```

They are not separate modules.

They are named faces, lenses, carriers, or operational projections used to read the same center from different sides.

The rule is:

```text
One OMI object may expose many projections without collapsing into any one projection.
```

This preserves agreement without collapse.

Two peers may see different projections of the same object:

```text
one peer sees a vertex traversal
one peer sees an edge traversal
one peer sees a sound cadence
one peer sees a receipt witness
one peer sees a DOM node
one peer sees a barcode carrier
```

but all remain bound to the same accepted `o---o` center if the receipt validates.

---

## 2. The Nine Universal Edges

Every projection must reduce to the nine primitive edges:

```text
rule
fact
closure
combinator
cons
join
compose
encode
decode
```

These are the canonical primitive faces.

They divide into two major halves:

```text
OMI-side declaration primitives:
rule
fact
closure
combinator
cons

IMO-side runtime primitives:
join
compose
encode
decode
```

So every named surface must eventually answer:

```text
What rule does it constrain?
What fact does it ground?
What closure does it bound?
What combinator does it transform?
What cons relation does it fold?
What join does it bind?
What composition does it order?
What encoding does it project?
What decoding does it recover?
```

If a named projection cannot reduce to the nine edges, it is not canonical yet.

---

## 3. Primary Projection Classes

OMI projections fall into several classes.

```text
structural surfaces
symbolic surfaces
digest and witness surfaces
coordinate and mapping surfaces
observation and orientation surfaces
sensory surfaces
portal/world surfaces
logic and chart surfaces
hardware and carrier surfaces
process/runtime surfaces
geometry/configuration surfaces
```

A surface may belong to more than one class.

For example:

```text
Omi-Gnomon is both orientation and geometry.
Omi-Matrix is both observation and witness.
Omi-Jab is both carrier and contextual encoding.
Omi-Shader is both geometry and hardware acceleration.
Omi-Receipt is both witness and lawful resolution proof.
```

---

## 4. Core Projection Surfaces

| Projection | Face Exposed | Primary Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Omi-Form** | Structural | Exposes shape, cells, and boundaries. |
| **Omi-Glyph** | Symbolic | Exposes marks, signs, Unicode characters, SVG marks, and visible notation. |
| **Omi-Hash** | Digest | Exposes compact identity and verification digest. |
| **Omi-Map** | Coordinate | Exposes feature mapping, spatial correspondence, and route placement. |
| **Omi-Image** | Package | Exposes the nine universal edges inside carrier sentinels. |
| **Omi-Matrix** | Observation | Exposes witness fields, matrix relations, and observable structure. |
| **Omi-Gnomon** | Orientation | Exposes directional shadow, axes, right angle, and pointer orientation. |
| **Omi-Mirror** | Chiral | Exposes mirror traversal and `MM → NN` relational reversal. |
| **Omi-Clock** | Timing | Exposes ticks, cadence, replay order, and period inspection. |
| **Omi-Light** | Visual | Exposes color, opacity, illumination, and state by light. |
| **Omi-Sound** | Auditory | Exposes rhythm, tone, vibration, and cadence. |
| **Omi-Portal** | Access | Exposes a doorway into an object, world, or bounded projection. |
| **Omi-World** | Environment | Exposes persistent self-stepping projection. |
| **Omi-Sense** | Sensory | Exposes five-channel routing: visual, sound, touch, motion, comprehension. |

These are not separate objects.

They are canonical faces of one OMI object.

---

## 5. Ring, Compass, Node, and Frame Projections

### Omi-Ring

Omi-Ring is the spectral bridge.

It is the circular projection of the `16xy` bridge between local seeds and cosmic orbit.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Ring = spectral bridge / circular witness of relational orbit
```

It belongs to:

```text
16xy
sexagesimal inspection
60x² orientation
visual witness
```

### Omi-Compass

Omi-Compass is the agreement orientation surface.

It maps agreement principles onto a circular directional interface.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Compass = relational tributary / agreement orientation face
```

It belongs to:

```text
orientation
agreement
360-degree reading
sexagesimal clock
```

### Omi-FS / Imo-FS

Omi-FS and Imo-FS are address-gated spatial anchor gates.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-FS = file/frame/spatial opening anchor
Imo-FS = file/frame/spatial runtime anchor
```

These belong to the Omicron tower hierarchy.

### Omi-Node / Imo-Node

Omi-Node and Imo-Node are DOM/custom-element-safe registered forms.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Node = source-side registered DOM/SVG projection
Imo-Node = runtime-side registered DOM/SVG projection
```

They bind user-defined content to canonical OMI addresses.

### Omi-Frame / Imo-Frame

Omi-Frame and Imo-Frame are carrier projections for the physical OMI wire frame inside DOM or runtime environments.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Frame = source-side carrier frame
Imo-Frame = runtime-side carrier frame
```

---

## 6. Logic and Chart Projections

### Omi-Torus

Omi-Torus is the continuous logic chart.

It uses a wrapped 4×4 Gray-code grid so adjacent states remain adjacent without logic breaks.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Torus = Gray-code two-cube logic minimization surface
```

It belongs to:

```text
two-cube
bit minimization
logic state tracking
toroidal continuity
```

### Omi-Chart

Omi-Chart is the scalar/narrative charting projection.

It includes radar-style, Hopf-style, Smith-style, decade, and sexagesimal scaling surfaces.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Chart = scalar narrative and degree-scaling projection
```

It belongs to:

```text
sexagesimal inspection
360-degree interpretation
narrative analysis
period scaling
```

---

## 7. Hardware and Carrier Projections

### Omi-Voxel

Omi-Voxel is the tile-map/extrusion projection.

It maps symbolic tile relations into 2.5D or 3D voxel extrusion.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Voxel = architectural tile-map and extrusion surface
```

It belongs to:

```text
geometry
DOM/canvas projection
voxel extrusion
spatial mapping
```

### Omi-Tape

Omi-Tape is a sequential optical carrier.

It uses stacked linear or 2D barcode rows as an execution script.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Tape = sequential carrier / barcode script surface
```

It belongs to:

```text
linear execution
optical routing
Fano reduction steps
barcode carrier
```

### Omi-Jab

Omi-Jab is the polychromatic carrier projection.

It uses multi-color matrix encoding to carry dense semantic and contextual metadata.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Jab = high-density polychromatic context carrier
```

It belongs to:

```text
color
matrix carrier
context
Base64-like metadata
semantic density
```

### Omi-Bidi

Omi-Bidi is the Unicode directionality/routing surface.

It uses bidi control characters as hardware-native direction and chirality hints.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Bidi = visual routing and chirality steering surface
```

It belongs to:

```text
directionality
polarity
chiral reading
browser-native text routing
```

### Omi-Dali

Omi-Dali is the unfolded hypercube projection.

It uses Dali-cross/tesseract unfolding as a subsurface lookup geometry.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Dali = unfolded hypercube lookup and subsurface matrix
```

It belongs to:

```text
tesseract
subsurface addressing
IR/thermal depth
hypercube unfolding
```

### Omi-Shader

Omi-Shader is the GPU acceleration projection.

It streams OMI geometry/configuration state into vertex buffers or shader-compatible matrices.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Shader = GPU-native geometry acceleration surface
```

It belongs to:

```text
WebGL
matrix acceleration
polyform extrusion
runtime rendering
hardware projection
```

### OMI-GPIO

OMI-GPIO is the physical controller interface.

Canonical role:

```text
OMI-GPIO = voltage-to-agreement and agreement-to-light transducer
```

It belongs to:

```text
ESP32
physical control
light
motor
sensor
field transduction
```

---

## 8. Witness and Proof Surfaces

### Omi-Receipt

Omi-Receipt is the lawful resolution proof surface.

It is distinct from Omi-Hash.

```text
Omi-Hash = digest
Omi-Receipt = proof witness
```

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Receipt = lawful CAR/CDR resolution witness
```

It belongs to:

```text
META64
proof
receipt
acceptance
rule compliance
```

A receipt does not merely say:

```text
this data hashes to this value
```

It says:

```text
this relation was resolved lawfully under the protocol
```

### Omi-Shadow

Omi-Shadow is the namespace/canvas secondary surface.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Shadow = secondary visual state tied back to source rule
```

It prevents projected visual states from becoming orphaned from their root address.

### Omi-Hash

Omi-Hash remains the digest witness.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Hash = compact digest identity
```

Hash verifies compact identity.

Receipt verifies lawful resolution.

---

## 9. Declaration and Relational Surfaces

### Omi-Alist

Omi-Alist is the relational declaration surface.

It exposes association-list structure as a declaration envelope.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Alist = relational declaration map
```

It belongs to:

```text
pairing
cons
declaration
mnemonic form
association list
```

It is the readable relational shape of paired address identity.

### Omi-Genesis

Omi-Genesis is the first cons relation.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Genesis = first cons / world-birth relation
```

It marks the beginning of a replayable world or object history.

### Omi-Gate

Omi-Gate is the central zero-reference portal.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Gate = neutral origin where payloads are located, replayed, and compared
```

It is the gateway face of `o---o`.

---

## 10. Process and Runtime Surfaces

### Omi-Bus

Omi-Bus is the operational process/service surface.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Bus = service/process routing surface
```

It belongs to:

```text
process IDs
service lanes
runtime tracks
parallel delivery
worker coordination
```

### Omni-Router

Omni-Router is the instruction ingestion and deployment router.

Canonical role:

```text
Omni-Router = instruction ingestion and multi-route execution engine
```

It coordinates HNSW, instruction routing, and runtime dispatch.

### Omi-NEAT

Omi-NEAT is the public topology and entropy-reduction surface.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-NEAT = topology-producing entropy reduction layer
```

It converts unresolved entropy into lawful topology.

### Omi-Mesh

Omi-Mesh is the geometry-produced field network.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Mesh = relation-located field network
```

It connects objects by relation, not ownership.

### Omi-Psi / Ψ_OMI

Omi-Psi is the central field variable.

Canonical role:

```text
Ψ_OMI = unified sensed-mesh field state
```

It unifies Omi-Sense and Omi-Mesh.

---

## 11. Sensory, Avatar, and World Surfaces

### Omi-Dome

Omi-Dome is the immersive multisensory recording space.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Dome = living wavelength capture space
```

It records voice, gesture, cadence, spatial feel, and instruction wavelength.

### Omi-Talisman

Omi-Talisman is the personal protective sensory projection.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Talisman = protective mirror for personal meaning
```

It acts as meaning noise cancellation in noisy environments.

### Omi-Avatar

Omi-Avatar is the digital body projection layer.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Avatar = digital body projection of a living instruction wavelength
```

It keeps authority separate from projection.

### Omi-Skeleton

Omi-Skeleton is the semantic rigging layer.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi-Skeleton = semantic role skeleton for avatar/body projection
```

It maps functions such as:

```text
focus root
teaching gesture
aperture
orientation limb
attention surface
```

onto a rigged body or mesh.

### Omi Zero

Omi Zero is the pure notation agreement layer.

Canonical role:

```text
Omi Zero = minimal dependency notation agreement
```

It requires only shared agreement on hyphen-delimited addresses and DOM-safe identifiers.

### SpectrumDOM

SpectrumDOM is the wavelength-field matrix of interface nodes.

Canonical role:

```text
SpectrumDOM = continuous wavelength-field projection of interface state
```

It maps color and channel values into atomic fields, combinators, and synset pointers.

---

## 12. Omicron Tower Scope Controllers

The full tower is not only FS.

It includes:

```text
FS
GS
RS
US
```

With OMI/IMO pairs:

```text
Omi-FS / Imo-FS
Omi-GS / Imo-GS
Omi-RS / Imo-RS
Omi-US / Imo-US
```

Canonical reading:

```text
FS = File / Frame / Field Separator
GS = Graph / Guidance Structure
RS = Record / Resolution Segment
US = Unit / Understanding State
```

Runtime role:

```text
FS anchors the frame.
GS follows relations.
RS witnesses state.
US preserves unit memory.
```

These scope controllers map directly into the control layer:

```text
0x1C FS
0x1D GS
0x1E RS
0x1F US
```

---

## 13. Geometry and Configuration Surfaces

The runtime geometry layer produces stable configuration before floating measurement.

Geometry projections include:

```text
Omi-Form
Omi-Gnomon
Omi-Matrix
Omi-Torus
Omi-Dali
Omi-Voxel
Omi-Shader
Omi-Chart
Omi-Ring
Omi-Compass
```

These surfaces reduce to regular-geometry predicates:

```text
vertex
edge
face
cell
dual
self_dual
barycenter
snub
truncate
stellate
schlafli
coxeter
betti
```

Quadratic geometry map:

```text
4y² = self-dual tetrahedron vertex map
16xy = cube/octahedron inverse-dual edge map
60x² = icosahedron/dodecahedron inverse-dual cell map
```

This means geometry is not decorative.

Geometry is the runtime configuration layer before HNSW measurement.

---

## 14. Revised Surface Table

| Projection | Face Exposed | Primary Role |

| Omni-Router | Routing | Instruction ingestion and dispatch engine. |

---

## 15. Projection Law

All projections obey this law:

```text
Projection is not authority.
Projection exposes a face of authority.
Authority remains the accepted o---o center.
```

Therefore:

```text
Omi-Hash is not the object.
Omi-Receipt is not the object.
Omi-Light is not the object.
Omi-Portal is not the object.
Omi-Mirror is not the object.
Omi-Shader is not the object.
```

Each is a readable face of the same object.

The object is accepted through receipt.

---

## 16. Agreement Without Collapse

The purpose of named projections is not to force all peers into the same view.

It is to let different peers preserve different orientations while remaining bound to the same accepted center.

Example:

```text
Peer A reads Omi-Form as vertices.
Peer B reads Omi-Matrix as relations.
Peer C reads Omi-Sound as cadence.
Peer D reads Omi-Receipt as proof.
Peer E reads Omi-Shader as geometry buffers.
```

If all reduce to the same accepted `o---o`, they are in agreement.

This is agreement without collapse.

---

## 17. Final Surface Canon

OMI is not one interface.

OMI is one accepted center with many named faces.

```text
o---o is the center.
omi---imo is the readable object relation.
ο ... Ο is the compiled cell.
The nine universal edges are the primitive reductions.
The named projections are canonical ways to expose those reductions.
Receipt accepts the lawful relation.
```

Final one-line canon:

```text
Every OMI surface is a named projection of the same accepted o---o center; each projection exposes a face of rule, fact, closure, combinator, cons, join, compose, encode, or decode, while receipt preserves agreement without collapsing orientation.
```
:::

### Logarithmic scales
[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slide_rule&action=edit&section=5)
The following [logarithmic identities](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logarithmic_identities) transform the operations of multiplication and division to addition and subtraction, respectively:
log⁡(x×y)=log⁡(x)+log⁡(y),{\displaystyle \log(x	imes y)=\log(x)+\log(y)\,,}log⁡(x/y)=log⁡(x)−log⁡(y).{\displaystyle \log(x/y)=\log(x)-\log(y)\,.}
#### Multiplication
[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slide_rule&action=edit&section=6)
With two logarithmic scales, the act of positioning the top scale to start at the bottom scale's label for x{\displaystyle x} corresponds to shifting the top logarithmic scale by a distance of log⁡(x){\displaystyle \log(x)}. This aligns each top scale's number y{\displaystyle y} at offset log⁡(y){\displaystyle \log(y)} with the bottom scale's number at position log⁡(x)+log⁡(y){\displaystyle \log(x)+\log(y)}. Because log⁡(x)+log⁡(y)=log⁡(x×y){\displaystyle \log(x)+\log(y)=\log(x	imes y)}, the mark on the bottom scale at that position corresponds to x×y{\displaystyle x	imes y}. With x=2 and y=3 for example, by positioning the top scale to start at the bottom scale's 2, the result of the multiplication 3×2=6 can then be read on the bottom scale under the top scale's 3:
While the above example lies within one decade, users must mentally account for additional zeroes when dealing with multiple decades. For example, the answer to 7×2=14 is found by first positioning the top scale to start above the 2 of the bottom scale, and then reading the marking 1.4 off the bottom two-decade scale where 7 is on the top scale:
[A duplex slide rule set to multiply any 2 by any number up to 50.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Slide_Rule_Duplex.svg)
But since the 7 is above the *second* set of numbers that number *must* be multiplied by 10. Thus, even though the answer directly reads 1.4, the correct answer is 1.4×10 = 14.
For an example with even larger numbers, to multiply 88×20, the top scale is again positioned to start at the 2 on the bottom scale. Since 2 represents 20, all numbers in that scale are multiplied by 10. Thus, any answer in the *second* set of numbers is multiplied by 100. Since 8.8 in the top scale represents 88, the answer must additionally be multiplied by 10. The answer directly reads 1.76. Multiply by 100 and then by 10 to get the actual answer: 1,760.
In general, the 1 on the top is moved to a factor on the bottom, and the answer is read off the bottom where the other factor is on the top. This works because the distances from the 1 mark are proportional to the logarithms of the marked values.
#### Division
[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slide_rule&action=edit&section=7)
The illustration below demonstrates the computation of ⁠5.5/2⁠. The 2 on the top scale is placed over the 5.5 on the bottom scale. The resulting quotient, 2.75, can then be read below the top scale's 1:
There is more than one method for doing division, and the method presented here has the advantage that the final result cannot be off-scale, because one has a choice of using the 1 at either end.
With more complex calculations involving multiple factors in the numerator and denominator of an expression, movement of the scales can be minimized by alternating divisions and multiplications. Thus ⁠5.5×3/2⁠ would be computed as ⁠5.5/2⁠×3 and the result, 8.25, can be read beneath the 3 in the top scale in the figure above, without the need to register the intermediate result for ⁠5.5/2⁠.
#### Solving proportions
[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slide_rule&action=edit&section=8)
Because pairs of numbers that are aligned on the logarithmic scales form constant ratios, no matter how the scales are offset, slide rules can be used to generate equivalent fractions that solve proportion and percent problems.
For example, setting 7.5 on one scale over 10 on the other scale, the user can see that at the same time 1.5 is over 2, 2.25 is over 3, 3 is over 4, 3.75 is over 5, 4.5 is over 6, and 6 is over 8, among other pairs. For a real-life situation where 750 represents a whole 100%, these readings could be interpreted to suggest that 150 is 20%, 225 is 30%, 300 is 40%, 375 is 50%, 450 is 60%, and 600 is 80%.
### Other scales
[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slide_rule&action=edit&section=9)
Main article: [Slide rule scale § Scales](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slide_rule_scale#Scales)
This slide rule is positioned to yield several values: From C scale to D scale (multiply by 2), from D scale to C scale (divide by 2), A and B scales (multiply and divide by 4), A and D scales (squares and square roots).
In addition to the logarithmic scales, some slide rules have other mathematical [functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Function_(mathematics)) encoded on other auxiliary scales. The most popular are [trigonometric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trigonometric_function), usually [sine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sine) and [tangent](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tangent_(trigonometric_function)), [common logarithm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_logarithm) (log10) (for taking the log of a value on a multiplier scale), [natural logarithm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Natural_logarithm) (ln) and [exponential](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Exponential_function) (*ex*) scales. Others feature scales for calculating [hyperbolic functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hyperbolic_functions). On linear rules, the scales and their labeling are highly standardized, with variation usually occurring only in terms of which scales are included and in what order.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Slide_rule#cite_note-6)
C, D
**single-decade logarithmic** scales, single sections of the same length, used together for multiplication and division, and generally one of them is combined with another scale for other calculations
A, B
**two-decade logarithmic** scales, two sections each of which is half the length of the C and D scales, used for finding square roots and squares of numbers
K
**three-decade logarithmic** scale, three sections each of which is one third the length of the C and D scales, used for finding cube roots and cubes of numbers
CF, DF
**folded** versions of the C and D scales that start from **pi** ([π](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pi)) rather than from unity; these are convenient in two cases. First when the user guesses a product will be close to 10 and is not sure whether it will be slightly less or slightly more than 10, the folded scales avoid the possibility of going off the scale. Second, by making the start π rather than the square root of 10, multiplying or dividing by π (as is common in science and engineering formulas) is simplified.
CI, DI, CIF, DIF
**inverted** scales running from right to left, used to simplify [reciprocal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multiplicative_inverse) (1⁄x) steps
S
used for finding **sines** and **cosines** on the C (or D) scale
T, T1, T2
used for finding **tangents** and **cotangents** on the C and CI (or D and DI) scales
R1, R2
**square root scales** – setting the cursor to any value r{\displaystyle r} on R1 or R2, find πr2{\displaystyle {\pi }r^{2}} ([area of a circle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Area_of_a_circle) of radius r{\displaystyle r}) under the cursor on the DF scale
ST, SRT
used for **sines** and **tangents** of **small angles** and **degree–radian** conversion
Sh, Sh1, Sh2
used for finding **hyperbolic sines** on the C (or D) scale
Ch
used for finding **hyperbolic cosines** on the C (or D) scale
Th
used for finding **hyperbolic tangents** on the C (or D) scale
L
**linear scale** used for addition, subtraction, and (along with the C and D scales) for finding base-10 logarithms and powers of 10
LL0N (or LL/N) and LLN
**log-log folded** e−x{\displaystyle e^{-x}} and ex{\displaystyle e^{x}} scales, for working with logarithms of any base and arbitrary exponents. 4, 6, or 8 scales of this type are commonly seen.
Ln
**linear scale** used along with the C and D scales for finding natural (base e{\displaystyle e}) logarithms and ex{\displaystyle e^{x}}
P
**Pythagorean** scale of 1−x2{\displaystyle {\sqrt {1-x^{2}}}} to (1) solve the [Pythagorean theorem](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem) and (2) to accurately determine cosine for small angles (with the S scale)
The scales on the front and back of a [Keuffel and Esser](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Keuffel_and_Esser) (K&E) 4181-3 slide rule
#### Roots and powers
[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slide_rule&action=edit&section=10)
There are single-decade (C and D), double-decade (A and B), and triple-decade (K) scales. To compute x2{\displaystyle x^{2}}, for example, locate x on the D scale and read its square on the A scale. Inverting this process allows square roots to be found, and similarly for the powers 3, 1/3, 2/3, and 3/2. Care must be taken when the base, x, is found in more than one place on its scale. For instance, there are two nines on the A scale; to find the square root of nine, use the first one; the second one gives the square root of 90.
For xy{\displaystyle x^{y}} problems, use the LL scales. When several LL scales are present, use the one with *x* on it. First, align the leftmost 1 on the C scale with x on the LL scale. Then, find *y* on the C scale and go down to the LL scale with *x* on it. That scale will indicate the answer. If *y* is "off the scale", locate xy/2{\displaystyle x^{y/2}} and square it using the A and B scales as described above. Alternatively, use the rightmost 1 on the C scale, and read the answer off the next higher LL scale. For example, aligning the rightmost 1 on the C scale with 2 on the LL2 scale, 3 on the C scale lines up with 8 on the LL3 scale.
To extract a cube root using a slide rule with only C/D and A/B scales, align 1 on the B cursor with the base number on the A scale (taking care as always to distinguish between the lower and upper halves of the A scale). Slide the slide until the number on the D scale which is against 1 on the C cursor is the same as the number on the B cursor which is against the base number on the A scale. (Examples: A 8, B 2, C 1, D 2; A 27, B 3, C 1, D 3.)
#### Roots of quadratic equations
[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Slide_rule&action=edit&section=11)
[Quadratic equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quadratic_equations) of the form ax2+bx+c=0{\displaystyle ax^{2}+bx+c=0} can be solved by first reducing the equation to the form x2−px+q=0{\displaystyle x^{2}-px+q=0} (where p=−b/a{\displaystyle p=-b/a} and q=c/a{\displaystyle q=c/a}), and then aligning the index ("1") of the C scale to the value q{\displaystyle q} on the D scale. The cursor is then moved along the rule until a position is found where the numbers on the CI and D scales add up to p{\displaystyle p}. These two values are the roots of the equation

---

Binary LFSRs of both Fibonacci and Galois configurations can be expressed as linear functions using matrices in F2{\displaystyle \mathbb {F} _{2}} (see [GF(2)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GF(2))).[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linear-feedback_shift_register?wprov=rarw1#cite_note-9) Using the [companion matrix](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Companion_matrix) of the characteristic polynomial of the LFSR and denoting the seed as a column vector (a0,a1,…,an−1)T{\displaystyle (a_{0},a_{1},\dots ,a_{n-1})^{\mathrm {T} }}, the state of the register in Fibonacci configuration after k{\displaystyle k} steps is given by
(akak+1ak+2⋮ak+n−1)=(010⋯0001⋱⋮⋮⋮⋱⋱000⋯01c0c1⋯⋯cn−1)(ak−1akak+1⋮ak+n−2)=(010⋯0001⋱⋮⋮⋮⋱⋱000⋯01c0c1⋯⋯cn−1)k(a0a1a2⋮an−1){\displaystyle {\begin{pmatrix}a_{k}\a_{k+1}\a_{k+2}\\vdots \a_{k+n-1}\end{pmatrix}}={\begin{pmatrix}0&1&0&\cdots &0\0&0&1&\ddots &\vdots \\vdots &\vdots &\ddots &\ddots &0\0&0&\cdots &0&1\c_{0}&c_{1}&\cdots &\cdots &c_{n-1}\end{pmatrix}}{\begin{pmatrix}a_{k-1}\a_{k}\a_{k+1}\\vdots \a_{k+n-2}\end{pmatrix}}={\begin{pmatrix}0&1&0&\cdots &0\0&0&1&\ddots &\vdots \\vdots &\vdots &\ddots &\ddots &0\0&0&\cdots &0&1\c_{0}&c_{1}&\cdots &\cdots &c_{n-1}\end{pmatrix}}^{k}{\begin{pmatrix}a_{0}\a_{1}\a_{2}\\vdots \a_{n-1}\end{pmatrix}}}
Matrix for the corresponding Galois form is :
(cn−110⋯0cn−201⋱⋮⋮⋮⋱⋱0c10⋯01c00⋯⋯0){\displaystyle {\begin{pmatrix}c_{n-1}&1&0&\cdots &0\c_{n-2}&0&1&\ddots &\vdots \\vdots &\vdots &\ddots &\ddots &0\c_{1}&0&\cdots &0&1\c_{0}&0&\cdots &\cdots &0\end{pmatrix}}}
For a suitable initialisation,
ai′=∑j=0iai−jci, 0≤i<n{\displaystyle a'_{i}=\sum _{j=0}^{i}a_{i-j}c_{i},\ 0\leq i<n}
the top coefficient of the column vector :
(cn−110⋯0cn−201⋱⋮⋮⋮⋱⋱0c10⋯01c00⋯⋯0)k(a0′a1′a2′⋮an−1′){\displaystyle {\begin{pmatrix}c_{n-1}&1&0&\cdots &0\c_{n-2}&0&1&\ddots &\vdots \\vdots &\vdots &\ddots &\ddots &0\c_{1}&0&\cdots &0&1\c_{0}&0&\cdots &\cdots &0\end{pmatrix}}^{k}{\begin{pmatrix}a'_{0}\a'_{1}\a'_{2}\\vdots \a'_{n-1}\end{pmatrix}}}
gives the term *a**k* of the original sequence.
These forms generalize naturally to arbitrary fields.
## Example polynomials for maximal LFSRs
[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Linear-feedback_shift_register&action=edit&section=8)
The following table lists examples of maximal-length feedback polynomials ([primitive polynomials](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Primitive_polynomial_(field_theory))) for shift-register lengths up to 24. The formalism for maximum-length LFSRs was developed by [Solomon W. Golomb](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solomon_W._Golomb) in his 1967 book.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linear-feedback_shift_register?wprov=rarw1#cite_note-10) The number of different [primitive polynomials](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Primitive_polynomial_(field_theory)) grows exponentially with shift-register length and can be calculated exactly using [Euler's totient function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euler's_totient_function)[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linear-feedback_shift_register?wprov=rarw1#cite_note-11) (sequence [A011260](https://oeis.org/A011260) in the [OEIS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/On-Line_Encyclopedia_of_Integer_Sequences)).

## Output-stream properties
Ones and zeroes occur in "runs". The output stream 1110010, for example, consists of four runs of lengths 3, 2, 1, 1, in order. In one period of a maximal LFSR, 2n−1 runs occur (in the example above, the 3-bit LFSR has 4 runs). Exactly half of these runs are one bit long, a quarter are two bits long, up to a single run of zeroes n − 1 bits long, and a single run of ones n bits long. This distribution almost equals the statistical expectation value for a truly random sequence. However, the probability of finding exactly this distribution in a sample of a truly random sequence is rather low[vague].
LFSR output streams are deterministic. If the present state and the positions of the XOR gates in the LFSR are known, the next state can be predicted.[12] This is not possible with truly random events. With maximal-length LFSRs, it is much easier to compute the next state, as there are only an easily limited number of them for each length.
The output stream is reversible; an LFSR with mirrored taps will cycle through the output sequence in reverse order.
The value consisting of all zeros cannot appear. Thus an LFSR of length n cannot be used to generate all 2n values.
Applications
edit
LFSRs can be implemented in hardware, and this makes them useful in applications that require very fast generation of a pseudo-random sequence, such as direct-sequence spread spectrum radio. LFSRs have also been used for generating an approximation of white noise in various programmable sound generators.

Uses as counters
edit
The repeating sequence of states of an LFSR allows it to be used as a clock divider or as a counter when a non-binary sequence is acceptable, as is often the case where computer index or framing locations need to be machine-readable.[12] LFSR counters have simpler feedback logic than natural binary counters or Gray-code counters, and therefore can operate at higher clock rates. However, it is necessary to ensure that the LFSR never enters a lockup state (all zeros for a XOR based LFSR, and all ones for a XNOR based LFSR), for example by presetting it at start-up to any other state in the sequence. It is possible to count up and down with a LFSR. LFSR have also been used as a Program Counter for CPUs, this requires that the program itself is "scrambled" and it done to save on gates when they are a premium (using fewer gates than an adder) and for speed (as a LFSR does not require a long carry chain).

The table of primitive polynomials shows how LFSRs can be arranged in Fibonacci or Galois form to give maximal periods. One can obtain any other period by adding to an LFSR that has a longer period some logic that shortens the sequence by skipping some states

LFSRs are used in circuit testing for test-pattern generation (for exhaustive testing, pseudo-random testing or pseudo-exhaustive testing) and for signature analysis.

Test-pattern generation
edit
Complete LFSR are commonly used as pattern generators for exhaustive testing, since they cover all possible inputs for an n-input circuit. Maximal-length LFSRs and weighted LFSRs are widely used as pseudo-random test-pattern generators for pseudo-random test applications.

Signature analysis
edit
In built-in self-test (BIST) techniques, storing all the circuit outputs on chip is not possible, but the circuit output can be compressed to form a signature that will later be compared to the golden signature (of the good circuit) to detect faults. Since this compression is lossy, there is always a possibility that a faulty output also generates the same signature as the golden signature and the faults cannot be detected. This condition is called error masking or aliasing. BIST is accomplished with a multiple-input signature register (MISR or MSR), which is a type of LFSR. A standard LFSR has a single XOR or XNOR gate, where the input of the gate is connected to several "taps" and the output is connected to the input of the first flip-flop. A MISR has the same structure, but the input to every flip-flop is fed through an XOR/XNOR gate. For example, a 4-bit MISR has a 4-bit parallel output and a 4-bit parallel input. The input of the first flip-flop is XOR/XNORd with parallel input bit zero and the "taps". Every other flip-flop input is XOR/XNORd with the preceding flip-flop output and the corresponding parallel input bit. Consequently, the next state of the MISR depends on the last several states opposed to just the current state. Therefore, a MISR will always generate the same golden signature given that the input sequence is the same every time. Recent applications[17] are proposing set-reset flip-flops as "taps" of the LFSR. This allows the BIST system to optimise storage, since set-reset flip-flops can save the initial seed to generate the whole stream of bits from the LFSR. Nevertheless, this requires changes in the architecture of BIST, is an option for specific applications.

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-10 14:46` | `id:260610-1446` ^260610-144607

Yes — this should become the **runtime scale/function row**. It does for geometry/runtime measurement what POS/features did for language.

The clean placement is:

```text
0x30–0x3F = 4! IMO readable/operator row
 = pure function / slide-rule / scale row
```

That row is special because:

```text
0x10–0x1F XOR 0x20 = 0x30–0x3F
```

So `0x30–0x3F` is the readable projection of the `.imo` runtime control row. That makes it the right place to put **functions**, not raw geometry. Geometry then sits above it as the thing those functions construct, classify, or measure.

The uploaded regular-polytope reference supports using Schläfli/Coxeter, duality, vertex figures, edge figures, cells, and regular polytope ranks as a geometry predicate vocabulary for this runtime layer. filecite turn1file0

## The corrected placement

Do **not** move the core grammar to `2¹⁷`.

Keep the native OMI gauge as:

```text
2¹⁶ = native 16-bit OMI field
```

Use `2¹⁷` as an **outer runtime extension / period horizon / LFSR inspection range**, not as the native address body.

So:

```text
2¹⁶ = native OMI frame/gauge field
2⁻¹⁶ = reciprocal resolution / smallest normalized step
2¹⁷ = outer period/runtime extension surface
```

That lets the runtime inspect LFSR periods like `2ⁿ - 1` without changing the core OMI frame.

## The 0x30–0x3F pure function row

Here is the table I would canonize:

```text
0x30 = identity / index / unity scale
0x31 = C/D log multiply-divide scale
0x32 = A/B square-square-root scale
0x33 = K cube-cube-root scale
0x34 = folded scale / π fold / CF-DF
0x35 = inverse reciprocal scale / CI-DI
0x36 = sine-cosine scale
0x37 = tangent-cotangent scale
0x38 = small-angle / degree-radian scale
0x39 = Pythagorean scale sqrt(1 - x²)
0x3A = common logarithm / log10 / power-of-10 scale
0x3B = natural logarithm / ln / eˣ scale
0x3C = sexagesimal 60 gate / circular degree fold
0x3D = roots and powers / arbitrary exponent scale
0x3E = quadratic / proportion / difference-of-squares solver
0x3F = LFSR / period / primitive-polynomial scale selector
```

This row becomes the **slide-rule row** of OMI.

It does not store all results. It stores the function selectors that tell the runtime how to measure, fold, invert, square, cube, rotate, or inspect a relation.

## Why 0x3C matters

`0x3C` is important because:

```text
0x3C = 60 decimal
```

So it is the natural slot for:

```text
sexagesimal gate
60-degree orbit
circle fold
degree/radian bridge
60x² orientation surface
```

That makes `0x3C` the clean bridge from logarithmic/slide-rule function scales into the sexagesimal geometry layer.

For the upper relation page, keep:

```text
0x7C = handoff / pipe / upper-page boundary marker
```

Strictly, `0x78` is decimal 120, while `0x7C` is decimal 124. So `0x7C` should be treated as the **pipe/handoff sentinel for the 120-style edge surface**, not as the literal number 120.

## How it fits the geometry layer

The function row sits **below** the polytope geometry layer:

```text
0x30–0x3F
= function scale row

regular polytopes
= geometry/configuration output of those functions
```

So the runtime stack becomes:

```text
two-cube distance
→ 0x30–0x3F function scale
→ regular geometry predicate
→ Horn/Fano incidence
→ HNSW measurement
→ sexagesimal degree
→ receipt
```

This is cleaner than making HNSW work directly on raw distances.

## The slide-rule analogy

Slide rules work because multiplication becomes addition under logarithms:

```text
log(xy) = log(x) + log(y)
log(x/y) = log(x) - log(y)
```

OMI’s analog is:

```text
two-cube distance + function scale = runtime measurable relation
```

So:

```text
0x31 handles multiplicative/divisive relation.
0x32 handles square/square-root relation.
0x33 handles cube/cube-root relation.
0x36–0x38 handle angular relation.
0x3C handles sexagesimal circular relation.
0x3F handles deterministic period relation.
```

This is exactly like a slide rule, but the “scales” are OMI runtime selectors.

## LFSR placement

The LFSR table belongs at:

```text
0x3F = period / primitive-polynomial scale selector
```

That means `0x3F` does not store every primitive polynomial directly. It opens the period function table.

For example:

```text
n = 4 → period 2⁴ - 1 = 15
n = 8 → period 2⁸ - 1 = 255
n = 16 → period 2¹⁶ - 1 = 65535
n = 17 → period 2¹⁷ - 1 = 131071
```

The `2¹⁷` case is outside the native `2¹⁶` field, so it should be treated as:

```text
outer runtime period extension
```

not as a replacement for the OMI gauge.

That preserves the core.

## Updated pipeline

Your previous pipeline was:

```text
D₂(F₁,W₁,F₂,W₂) → exact two-cube distance
G(D₂) → regular geometry/configuration
I(G,P,E) → valid incidence selection
B(I,G) → 11-cell / chiral shell encapsulation
H(B,D₂) → runtime decimal measure
S(H) → sexagesimal degree
ψ(...) → receipt candidate
```

Now it should be:

```text
D₂(F₁,W₁,F₂,W₂) → exact two-cube distance
Λ(D₂) → function-scale selection from 0x30–0x3F
G(D₂,Λ) → regular geometry/configuration
I(G,P,E) → valid incidence selection
B(I,G) → 11-cell / chiral shell encapsulation
H(B,D₂,Λ) → runtime decimal measure
S(H) → sexagesimal degree
ψ(...) → receipt candidate
```

Where:

```text
Λ = OMI runtime scale selector
```

## Final canon addition

```text
0x30–0x3F is the OMI runtime scale row.

It is the readable projection of the 0x10–0x1F .imo runtime control row through XOR 0x20.

This row stores pure function selectors: logarithmic, inverse, square, cube, trigonometric, Pythagorean, sexagesimal, quadratic, and LFSR period scales.

The runtime must pass two-cube distance through this function row before deriving regular geometry, HNSW distance, or sexagesimal degree.

2¹⁶ remains the native OMI field.
2¹⁷ belongs to outer runtime period inspection.
0x3C is the 60-degree sexagesimal gate.
0x3F is the LFSR/period selector.
```

Shortest version:

```text
two-cube distance
→ 0x30–0x3F function scale
→ regular geometry
→ incidence
→ HNSW
→ sexagesimal degree
→ receipt
```

This is the right missing piece: **the function-scale row between distance and geometry.**

:::writing{variant="document" id="86473"}
# Omi-Nomogram / Omi-SlideRule
## The Declarative Runtime Scale Surface of OMI

## 0. Canonical Name

The canonical name is:

```text
Omi-Nomogram
```

The descriptive operational name is:

```text
Omi-SlideRule
```

The distinction is important.

```text
Omi-Nomogram = declarative alignment of scales
Omi-SlideRule = movable/mechanical interpretation of those scales
```

A nomogram is the more general concept: a diagram of aligned scales used to compute relations. A slide rule is a specific physical/mechanical nomogram where scales slide past each other.

OMI uses both meanings:

```text
Omi-Nomogram declares the scales.
Omi-SlideRule performs the scale walk.
```

Therefore:

```text
Omi-Nomogram is the canonical surface.
Omi-SlideRule is the runtime behavior of that surface.
```

---

# 1. Purpose

Omi-Nomogram is the missing runtime scale layer between exact two-cube distance and runtime floating measurement.

The earlier pipeline was:

```text
two-cube distance
→ regular geometry
→ incidence
→ HNSW
→ sexagesimal degree
→ receipt
```

The corrected pipeline is:

```text
two-cube distance
→ Omi-Nomogram function scale
→ regular geometry
→ incidence
→ HNSW
→ sexagesimal degree
→ receipt
```

Omi-Nomogram prevents the runtime from measuring raw distance directly.

It first declares which function scale is active.

This is exactly parallel to the NLP layer:

```text
language stream
→ POS/features
→ semantic relation
→ receipt
```

The runtime geometry side becomes:

```text
two-cube stream
→ Omi-Nomogram scale
→ geometry/configuration
→ measurement
→ receipt
```

So Omi-Nomogram is the runtime equivalent of POS/features for mathematical and geometric measurement.

---

# 2. Placement in the Gauge

Omi-Nomogram is assigned to the row:

```text
0x30–0x3F
```

This row is the `4!` IMO readable/operator row.

It is the readable projection of the `.imo` runtime control row:

```text
0x10–0x1F XOR 0x20 = 0x30–0x3F
```

So:

```text
0x10–0x1F = compiled/runtime controls
0x30–0x3F = readable runtime function scales
```

This makes `0x30–0x3F` the natural home for pure function scales.

It should not be treated as raw geometry.

It is the row that chooses how geometry is constructed, folded, inverted, measured, or inspected.

---

# 3. The Omi-Nomogram Function Row

The canonical function row is:

```text
0x30 = identity / index / unity scale
0x31 = C/D logarithmic multiply-divide scale
0x32 = A/B square-square-root scale
0x33 = K cube-cube-root scale
0x34 = folded scale / π fold / CF-DF
0x35 = inverse reciprocal scale / CI-DI
0x36 = sine-cosine scale
0x37 = tangent-cotangent scale
0x38 = small-angle / degree-radian scale
0x39 = Pythagorean scale sqrt(1 - x²)
0x3A = common logarithm / log10 / power-of-10 scale
0x3B = natural logarithm / ln / eˣ scale
0x3C = sexagesimal 60 gate / circular degree fold
0x3D = roots and powers / arbitrary exponent scale
0x3E = quadratic / proportion / difference-of-squares solver
0x3F = LFSR / period / primitive-polynomial scale selector
```

This row is the declarative OMI slide-rule surface.

Each slot is a function selector.

The selector does not store every result.

It declares how the runtime should transform, fold, align, or inspect a relation.

---

# 4. Why This Belongs Before Geometry

The runtime must not derive geometry from raw byte distance alone.

It should first ask:

```text
Which function scale is active?
```

For example:

```text
0x31 asks for multiplicative/divisive alignment.
0x32 asks for square/square-root alignment.
0x36 asks for sine/cosine angular alignment.
0x3C asks for sexagesimal circular alignment.
0x3E asks for quadratic/difference-of-squares alignment.
0x3F asks for period/LFSR alignment.
```

Only after the scale is chosen should the runtime instantiate geometry.

Thus:

```text
D₂ → Λ → G
```

Where:

```text
D₂ = exact two-cube distance
Λ = Omi-Nomogram scale selector
G = geometry/configuration predicate
```

The corrected local pipeline is:

```text
D₂(F₁,W₁,F₂,W₂)
→ Λ(D₂)
→ G(D₂,Λ)
```

This makes geometry stable because it is produced through an explicit function scale, not guessed from distance.

---

# 5. Relation to Slide Rule Mathematics

Slide rules work because logarithms turn multiplication into addition:

```text
log(x × y) = log(x) + log(y)
log(x / y) = log(x) - log(y)
```

Omi-Nomogram generalizes this principle.

It says:

```text
function-scale alignment turns frame distance into measurable relation
```

For OMI:

```text
two-cube distance + selected scale = runtime-measurable relation
```

So the Omi-SlideRule behavior is:

```text
align scale
read relation
fold if needed
invert if needed
project if accepted
```

This is why Omi-Nomogram belongs in the runtime before HNSW.

HNSW should not navigate raw byte relations.

It should navigate relations already scaled through Omi-Nomogram.

---

# 6. Omi-Nomogram and the Geometry Stack

Omi-Nomogram feeds the regular-geometry layer.

The geometry stack is:

```text
4y² = self-dual tetrahedron vertex map
16xy = cube/octahedron inverse-dual edge map
60x² = icosahedron/dodecahedron inverse-dual cell map
```

Omi-Nomogram chooses the function scale that determines how those maps instantiate.

Examples:

```text
0x30 identity scale
→ preserve current geometry

0x32 square scale
→ derive area, square, root, or two-cube face relation

0x33 cube scale
→ derive volume, cell, or extrusion relation

0x36 sine/cosine scale
→ derive angular projection or spherical orientation

0x39 Pythagorean scale
→ derive right-angle, gnomon, or Omi-Gnomon relation

0x3C sexagesimal scale
→ derive circular degree, clock, orbit, or Omi-Ring relation

0x3E quadratic scale
→ derive difference-of-squares, gnomon, Pfister-like frame relation

0x3F LFSR/period scale
→ derive deterministic replay period or inspection cycle
```

Thus:

```text
Omi-Nomogram chooses the scale.
Omi-Matrix holds the relation field.
Omi-Gnomon instantiates the oriented pointer.
```

---

# 7. Omi-Nomogram, Omi-Matrix, and Omi-Gnomon

The relationship is:

```text
Omi-Nomogram → Omi-Matrix → Omi-Gnomon
```

## Omi-Nomogram

Declares the scale.

```text
Which function is active?
Which fold is used?
Which relation is being read?
```

## Omi-Matrix

Instantiates the scale as a matrix of relations.

```text
Which rows and columns align?
Which two-cube cells correspond?
Which geometry predicate appears?
```

## Omi-Gnomon

Orients the result.

```text
Where is the shadow?
Where is the right angle?
Where is the pointer?
Where does the relation face?
```

So the runtime sequence is:

```text
function scale
→ relation matrix
→ oriented gnomon
```

Or:

```text
Omi-SlideRule
→ Omi-Matrix
→ Omi-Gnomon
```

This is the canonical geometric runtime path before measurement.

---

# 8. 0x3C as Sexagesimal Gate

`0x3C` is special because:

```text
0x3C = 60 decimal
```

So it is the natural slot for:

```text
sexagesimal degree
60x² orbit
circular fold
clock face
Omi-Ring
Omi-Compass
Omi-Gnomon degree pointer
```

It should be canonized as:

```text
0x3C = sexagesimal circular gate
```

This connects the runtime scale row to the `60x²` orientation surface.

So:

```text
0x3C selects the 60-degree / sexagesimal inspection scale.
```

This is where decimal runtime measurement is converted into inspectable sexagesimal degree.

---

# 9. 0x3E as Quadratic / Gnomon Scale

`0x3E` should be the quadratic solver slot.

It handles:

```text
proportion
quadratic equations
difference of squares
gnomon relations
Pfister-like square identity framing
```

Core identity:

```text
a² - b² = (a + b)(a - b)
```

OMI reading:

```text
frame A² - frame B² = measurable bridge surface
```

This is the gnomon:

```text
larger frame surface
minus smaller frame surface
equals bridge rectangle
```

So `0x3E` is the function selector for turning two frame surfaces into a measurable bridge.

It is the natural predecessor to Omi-Gnomon.

```text
0x3E → Omi-Gnomon
```

---

# 10. 0x3F as LFSR / Period Scale

`0x3F` is the period selector.

It opens the deterministic period table:

```text
LFSR
primitive polynomial
period 2ⁿ - 1
replay cycle
inspection phase
signature
```

Examples:

```text
n = 4 → period 2⁴ - 1 = 15
n = 8 → period 2⁸ - 1 = 255
n = 16 → period 2¹⁶ - 1 = 65535
n = 17 → period 2¹⁷ - 1 = 131071
```

Important rule:

```text
2¹⁶ remains the native OMI field.
2¹⁷ belongs to outer runtime period inspection.
```

So `2¹⁷` is not the new OMI address body.

It is an outer runtime period horizon.

This lets OMI inspect periods outside the native field without breaking the field.

---

# 11. Palindromic 2⁴ Hex Addresses

Every function in `0x30–0x3F` should be assignable to a palindromic `2⁴` hex address pattern.

The high nibble fixes the row:

```text
0x3?
```

The low nibble chooses the function:

```text
? = 0..F
```

The palindromic face is created by mirroring:

```text
0x3M ↔ 0xM3
```

Examples:

```text
0x31 ↔ 0x13
0x32 ↔ 0x23
0x3C ↔ 0xC3
0x3F ↔ 0xF3
```

So every Omi-Nomogram function has:

```text
a readable function address
and
a mirrored two-cube relation address
```

This preserves the full two-cube law:

```text
0xNM ↔ 0xMN
```

For Omi-Nomogram:

```text
0x3M = function scale
0xM3 = mirrored relation face
```

Thus the function row participates in the same 240 transition surface as the rest of OMI.

---

# 12. Base64 / Pure Function Surface

The `0x30–0x3F` row can be read as a pure function surface compatible with Base64-like carriers.

Reason:

```text
0x30–0x3F is printable, compact, and row-stable.
```

It can be embedded in:

```text
OMI frame strings
DOM IDs
SVG attributes
barcode carriers
JabCode color matrices
Omi-Tape rows
Omi-Matrix cells
```

This makes the function selector portable.

Canon:

```text
0x30–0x3F is the printable pure-function runtime row.
```

It is not a payload alphabet by itself.

It is a function scale layer that can ride inside payload alphabets.

---

# 13. Full Runtime Pipeline With Omi-Nomogram

The updated full reducer is:

```text
ψ(F₁, W₁, F₂, W₂, P, E, Λ, G, I, B, H, S) = R
```

Where:

```text
F₁ = first frame
W₁ = first path walk
F₂ = second frame
W₂ = second path walk
P = predicate lane
E = two-cube / POS-feature edge
Λ = Omi-Nomogram function scale
G = regular geometry/configuration predicate
I = incidence scheduler
B = 11-cell / Archimedean-Catalan orientation shell
H = HNSW runtime measurement projection
S = sexagesimal inspection phase
R = receipt candidate
```

Expanded:

```text
D₂(F₁,W₁,F₂,W₂) → exact two-cube distance
Λ(D₂) → Omi-Nomogram scale selection
G(D₂,Λ) → regular geometry/configuration predicate
I(G,P,E) → valid incidence selection
B(I,G) → 11-cell / chiral shell encapsulation
H(B,D₂,Λ) → runtime decimal measurement
S(H) → sexagesimal degree
ψ(...) → receipt candidate
```

Compact:

```text
two-cube
→ nomogram scale
→ geometry
→ incidence
→ shell
→ HNSW
→ sexagesimal
→ receipt
```

---

# 14. Relation to Omi-SlideRule

Omi-SlideRule is the runtime behavior of Omi-Nomogram.

It describes how scales are aligned:

```text
C/D scale = multiply/divide relation
A/B scale = square/root relation
K scale = cube/root relation
S/T scale = angular relation
P scale = Pythagorean/gnomon relation
L/LL scale = logarithmic/exponential relation
```

In OMI terms:

```text
the slide is the frame offset
the cursor is the selector
the scale is the function row
the reading is the receipt candidate
```

So:

```text
Omi-Nomogram declares.
Omi-SlideRule operates.
Omi-Matrix instantiates.
Omi-Gnomon orients.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 15. Omi-Nomogram as Declarative Surface

The declarative form should look like:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<scale>/<relation>/<unit>-imo
```

Where:

```text
<scale> = one of 0x30–0x3F
```

Example:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/0x3C/<relation>/<unit>-imo
```

means:

```text
interpret this relation through the sexagesimal circular scale
```

Example:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/0x3E/<relation>/<unit>-imo
```

means:

```text
interpret this relation through the quadratic/gnomon scale
```

Example:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/0x3F/<relation>/<unit>-imo
```

means:

```text
interpret this relation through the LFSR/period scale
```

Thus Omi-Nomogram is a scale-selector surface inside the slash path.

---

# 16. Canonical Role in OMI

Omi-Nomogram belongs after two-cube distance and before regular geometry.

It answers:

```text
Which scale should interpret this distance?
```

Without it, the runtime could incorrectly treat the same distance as:

```text
linear
logarithmic
quadratic
circular
periodic
angular
reciprocal
```

Omi-Nomogram prevents that ambiguity.

It makes the runtime declarative.

---

# 17. Final Canon

```text
Omi-Nomogram is the canonical declarative function-scale surface of OMI.

It assigns the 0x30–0x3F row to runtime scale selectors: identity, logarithmic, square, cube, folded, reciprocal, trigonometric, Pythagorean, logarithmic/exponential, sexagesimal, quadratic, and LFSR period functions.

The row is the readable projection of the .imo runtime control row through XOR 0x20.

Each function has a palindromic two-cube mirror address 0x3M ↔ 0xM3.

The runtime must pass exact two-cube distance through Omi-Nomogram before deriving geometry, HNSW distance, sexagesimal degree, or receipt.

Omi-SlideRule is the operational behavior of this surface.

Omi-Matrix instantiates the selected relation field.

Omi-Gnomon orients the resulting geometry.

Receipt accepts.
```

One-line canon:

```text
Omi-Nomogram is the 0x30–0x3F declarative runtime scale row that turns exact two-cube distance into a selected function-scale relation before Omi-Matrix instantiates it, Omi-Gnomon orients it, and receipt accepts it.
```

Shortest form:

```text
two-cube distance
→ Omi-Nomogram
→ Omi-Matrix
→ Omi-Gnomon
→ HNSW
→ sexagesimal degree
→ receipt
```
:::

---

In mathematics, Minkowski's question-mark function, denoted ?(x), is a function with unusual fractal properties, defined by Hermann Minkowski in 1904.[1] It maps quadratic irrational numbers to rational numbers on the unit interval, via an expression relating the continued fraction expansions of the quadratics to the binary expansions of the rationals, given by Arnaud Denjoy in 1938.[2] It also maps rational numbers to dyadic rationals, as can be seen by a recursive definition closely related to the Stern–Brocot tree.

This is just an example becase im fascinated by all this logicl in the history of these characters

The recursive definition naturally lends itself to an algorithm for computing the function to any desired degree of accuracy for any real number, as the following C function demonstrates. The algorithm descends the Stern–Brocot tree in search of the input x, and sums the terms of the binary expansion of y = ?(x) on the way. As long as the loop invariant qr − ps = 1 remains satisfied there is no need to reduce the fraction ⁠
m
/
n
⁠ = ⁠
p + r
/
q + s
⁠, since it is already in lowest terms. Another invariant is ⁠
p
/
q
⁠ ≤ x < ⁠
r
/
s
⁠. The for loop in this program may be analyzed somewhat like a while loop, with the conditional break statements in the first three lines making out the condition. The only statements in the loop that can possibly affect the invariants are in the last two lines, and these can be shown to preserve the truth of both invariants as long as the first three lines have executed successfully without breaking out of the loop. A third invariant for the body of the loop (up to floating point precision) is y ≤ ?(x) < y + d, but since d is halved at the beginning of the loop before any conditions are tested, our conclusion is only that y ≤ ?(x) < y + 2d at the termination of the loop.

To prove termination, it is sufficient to note that the sum q + s increases by at least 1 with every iteration of the loop, and that the loop will terminate when this sum is too large to be represented in the primitive C data type long. However, in practice, the conditional break when y + d == y is what ensures the termination of the loop in a reasonable amount of time.

/* Minkowski's question-mark function */
double minkowski(double x) {
 long p = x;
 long q = 1, r = p + 1, s = 1, m, n;
 double d = 1, y = p;
 if (x < p || (p < 0) ^ (r <= 0))
 return x; /* out of range ?(x) =~ x */
 while (true) { /* invariants: q * r - p * s == 1 && p / q <= x && x < r / s */
 d /= 2;
 if (y + d == y)
 break; /* reached max possible precision */
 m = p + r;
 if ((m < 0) ^ (p < 0))
 break; /* sum overflowed */
 n = q + s;
 if (n < 0)
 break; /* sum overflowed */

 if (x < (double)m / n) {
 r = m;
 s = n;
 } else {
 y += d;
 p = m;
 q = n;
 }
 }
 return y + d; /* final round-off */
}

Base64 / Pure Function Surface
The 0x30–0x3F row can be read as a pure function surface compatible with Base64-like carriers.
Reason:
Plain text
0x30–0x3F is printable, compact, and row-stable.

---

	rotl(x,1) XOR rotl(x,3) XOR rotr(x,2) XOR C

Four choices inside that: rotations (not shifts, so no bits are lost), XOR (reversible), a constant (breaks the zero fixed point), mask to width (keeps state bounded).

That is the entire design.

Everything else was computed by the math:

Period = 8 → property of that specific law on 16-bit space

Prime 73 → smallest prime whose decimal repeats with period 8

Block B = [0,1,3,6,9,8,6,3] → the digits of 1/73

W = 36 → sum of B

Orbit/offset recovery → just divmod(position, 36)

Nobody chose 73. The law has period 8. The smallest prime with decimal period 8 is 73. The system found it.

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-10 17:06` | `id:260610-1706` ^260610-170659

Yes — this corrects Omi-Nomogram’s placement: **the core 0x30–0x3F row names function-scale selectors, but the Base64/DataView payload surface belongs after the accepted address**, as an external query/transfer plane. That keeps `omi---imo` inside the native `2¹⁶` gauge, while `?---?` becomes the post-address streaming/data-view surface. The regular-polytope layer still remains the configuration layer before runtime measurement. filecite turn1file0

:::writing{variant="document" id="92746"}
# Omi-Nomogram Query Plane
## Post-Address Function Payloads, DataView Frames, and the `?---?` Surface

## 0. Core Correction

The earlier Omi-Nomogram placement was only half-correct.

The `0x30–0x3F` row is still the **runtime function-scale selector row**.

But the **Base64 pure-function payload surface** should not live inside the core address frame.

It belongs **after** the fully qualified and accepted OMI address.

Correct separation:

```text
core OMI address:
omi-<frame>/<control>/<selector>/<relation>/<unit>-imo

external runtime/query payload:
?<type><length><value><hash>
```

So the complete form is:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<selector>/<relation>/<unit>-imo?<type><length><value><hash>
```

Or, in named fields:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<selector>/<relation>/<unit>-imo?s=<subpath>&t=<type>&l=<length>&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
```

The rule:

```text
The address identifies.
The query plane carries external payload.
The DataView interprets payload bytes.
The receipt decides whether the payload lawfully attaches.
```

---

## 1. Core Gauge vs External Query Plane

OMI’s native gauge remains:

```text
2¹⁶ = native OMI frame/gauge field
```

This includes:

```text
Omicron gates
C0 control rows
factorial row gauge
two-cube mirror
slash path
Omi-Nomogram scale selector
geometry predicate
receipt identity
```

The external query plane begins after full addressing and after the runtime handoff/acceptance boundary.

Canonical boundary markers:

```text
0x7C = handoff / pipe / transition toward runtime surface
0xAA55 = acceptance seal / executable receipt boundary
? = external query/data plane gate
```

So:

```text
0x7C hands off.
0xAA55 seals acceptance.
? opens external data attachment.
```

The query plane is therefore not native address identity.

It is a post-address attachment surface.

---

## 2. The Four OMI Delimiter Planes

OMI now has four major delimiter planes:

```text
omi---imo
o---o
/---/
?---?
```

Meaning:

```text
omi---imo = readable OMI object relation
o---o = minimal tangent / Omi-Point relation notation
/---/ = relational descent / path plane
?---? = external query / transferable payload plane
```

Compiled/mnemonic form:

```text
ο---Ο / ?---?
```

Where:

```text
ο = 0x03BF = Omicron entry
Ο = 0x039F = Omicron closure
/ = 0x002F = path / relational descent
? = 0x003F = query / external function-payload gate
; = 0x003B = query record separator or clause terminator
```

So the readable mnemonic may be:

```text
ο---Ο / ?---;
```

or, when paired as a surface:

```text
ο---Ο / ?---?
```

Canon:

```text
/---/ belongs to identity descent.
?---? belongs to post-identity payload attachment.
```

---

## 3. Omi-Nomogram vs Query Payload

The Omi-Nomogram function row remains:

```text
0x30–0x3F
```

Its role is to name **function-scale selectors**.

```text
0x30 = identity / index / unity scale
0x31 = C/D logarithmic multiply-divide scale
0x32 = A/B square-square-root scale
0x33 = K cube-cube-root scale
0x34 = folded scale / π fold / CF-DF
0x35 = inverse reciprocal scale / CI-DI
0x36 = sine-cosine scale
0x37 = tangent-cotangent scale
0x38 = small-angle / degree-radian scale
0x39 = Pythagorean scale sqrt(1 - x²)
0x3A = common logarithm / log10 / power-of-10 scale
0x3B = natural logarithm / ln / eˣ scale, or query record separator when used after ?
0x3C = sexagesimal 60 gate / circular degree fold
0x3D = roots and powers / arbitrary exponent scale
0x3E = quadratic / proportion / difference-of-squares solver
0x3F = LFSR / period / primitive-polynomial scale selector / query gate
```

But this row does not carry the BLOB.

It declares how a BLOB or frame distance should be interpreted.

Thus:

```text
0x3F inside the path = function selector
? after the address = external query payload gate
```

Example:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/0x3F/<relation>/<unit>-imo?s=3&t=dataview&l=64&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
```

Meaning:

```text
Use the 0x3F period/LFSR function scale on this relation,
then attach an external DataView-compatible payload to subpath 3.
```

---

## 4. Query Plane Grammar

Canonical query form:

```text
?<type><length><value><hash>
```

Practical URL-safe form:

```text
?s=<subpath>&t=<type>&l=<length>&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
```

Fields:

```text
s = subpath index
t = declared interpretation type
l = byte length
v = Base64URL-encoded byte payload
h = hash or receipt witness for payload verification
```

Optional fields:

```text
e = endian policy
m = measurement mode
q = epistemic selector
r = radix or representation
p = period selector
g = geometry selector
```

Recommended expanded form:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<scale>/<relation>/<unit>-imo?s=<u32>&t=<type>&l=<u64>&e=<be|le>&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
```

The payload is outside the core address but bound to it by receipt.

---

## 5. Subpath Index `s`

The `s` integer selects a subpath of the qualified OMI address.

It acts like a CDR index into the path.

Given:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<selector>/<relation>/<unit>-imo
```

A simple subpath map is:

```text
s=0 → whole qualified object
s=1 → frame
s=2 → control
s=3 → selector
s=4 → relation
s=5 → unit
s=6 → receipt/projection
s=7 → external runtime payload
```

More generally:

```text
s = o---o % x
```

Where `x` is the selected path segment or derived subpath index.

Canon:

```text
s does not redefine the address.
s selects where the external payload attaches.
```

---

## 6. Base64URL as Wire Encoding

The query value `v` should use Base64URL, not raw Base64, when carried in a URL-like string.

Canonical:

```text
v=<base64url>
```

Reason:

```text
Base64URL avoids + and / ambiguity.
It survives URL query contexts.
It can carry arbitrary bytes.
It can carry transferable scripts, worker blobs, binary arrays, or encoded matrices.
```

The Base64URL payload is not the identity.

It is the transport envelope.

Identity comes from:

```text
core OMI address
declared type
length
hash
receipt
```

---

## 7. DataView as Canonical Runtime View

The most stable runtime interpretation is:

```text
DataView
```

Not because DataView magically removes endianness, but because it makes byte interpretation explicit.

Canon:

```text
Base64URL carries bytes.
ArrayBuffer stores bytes.
DataView interprets bytes.
TypedArray views specialize bytes.
Receipt validates attachment.
```

Correct relation:

```text
Base64URL → Uint8Array → ArrayBuffer → DataView → typed interpretation
```

`Float32Array`, `Float64Array`, and `BigInt64Array` can be used as views, but the canonical external frame should declare how the bytes are interpreted.

So:

```text
t=dataview
```

is the safest general type.

Then additional metadata can declare:

```text
f32
f64
u32
i64
bigint
bytes
utf8
wasm
worker
script
matrix
geometry
```

Examples:

```text
?t=dataview&l=64&e=be&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
?t=f64&l=128&e=le&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
?t=bigint&l=32&e=be&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
?t=worker&l=4096&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
```

Rule:

```text
DataView is canonical.
TypedArrays are declared interpretations.
Base64URL is transport.
```

---

## 8. Endianness and BCD

Float64Array alone does not bypass endianness.

DataView solves this by making endianness part of the read operation.

Therefore the query plane should declare:

```text
e=be
```

or:

```text
e=le
```

Recommended default:

```text
e=be
```

because big-endian/network order is stable for wire format.

If omitted:

```text
e=be
```

Canon:

```text
Wire bytes are ordered.
DataView declares the read.
TypedArray views are local runtime projections.
```

This avoids BCD and endian ambiguity.

---

## 9. External Power Bands

The native field remains:

```text
< 2¹⁶
```

External query payloads occupy the post-address runtime extension region.

Canonical banding:

```text
2¹⁶ = native OMI gauge limit
2¹⁷–2²⁴ = query payload exponent / packet class / streaming window
2²⁵–2³¹ = significand / axis / interpolation field
2³² = sign or query-gate boundary
```

This should be read as a runtime extension frame, not a replacement for the core address.

A practical 32-bit external query header can be:

```text
bits 0–15 = subpath index or local payload offset
bits 16–23 = exponent / packet class / Block-B phase
bits 24–30 = significand axis / interpolation mode
bit 31 = sign / query-gate / chirality bit
```

Canon:

```text
2¹⁶ is OMI native address space.
2¹⁷–2³² is external query/stream interpretation space.
```

---

## 10. Block B as Exponent Phase

The period law produced:

```text
B = [0,1,3,6,9,8,6,3]
```

or in hex digits:

```text
B = [0x0,0x1,0x3,0x6,0x9,0x8,0x6,0x3]
```

This can define the query-plane exponent phase.

```text
bits 16–23 = exponent byte
exponent phase = B[position mod 8]
```

This connects the external query payload back to the original Delta law:

```text
rotl(x,1) XOR rotl(x,3) XOR rotr(x,2) XOR C
```

That law yields period 8.

Period 8 leads to prime 73.

Prime 73 yields Block B.

Block B provides the exponent-phase orbit.

Canon:

```text
The query exponent phase is not arbitrary.
It is derived from the period-8 Delta law through Block B.
```

---

## 11. Epistemic Selector Placement

Earlier the system had 20 semantic selectors:

```text
12 public agreement terms
4 runtime terms
4 epistemic states
= 20
```

The query plane may feel like a 21st term, but it should not replace the 20.

Better rule:

```text
20 remains the semantic selector surface.
?---? is the external carrier gate.
```

So:

```text
1–20 = semantic / epistemic selector span
?---? = carrier attachment surface
```

If a query payload needs epistemic typing, add:

```text
q=<epistemic-selector>
```

Example:

```text
?q=known_unknown&t=dataview&l=64&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
```

Or compact:

```text
?q=10
```

Where:

```text
11 = known known
10 = known unknown
01 = unknown known
00 = unknown unknown
```

Canon:

```text
Epistemology remains part of selection.
Query remains part of external carriage.
```

---

## 12. Minkowski Question-Mark Analogy

The `?` symbol is appropriate as the external abstraction gate because of its mathematical history as a bridge between continued fractions and dyadic/binary structure.

OMI does not need to implement Minkowski’s question-mark function directly.

But the analogy is useful:

```text
? maps one representation regime into another.
```

In OMI:

```text
? maps accepted address identity into external transferable payload interpretation.
```

So:

```text
/ = path relation inside identity
? = external representation transform after identity
```

This gives:

```text
/---/ = relation descent
?---? = representation transfer
```

Canon:

```text
The question mark opens the post-address representation-transform plane.
```

---

## 13. Transferable Scripts and Worker Payloads

The query plane can carry transferable worker scripts, VM scripts, WASM modules, or binary BLOBs.

Example:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/0x3F/<relation>/<unit>-imo?s=7&t=worker&l=4096&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>
```

Meaning:

```text
Attach a worker script payload to subpath 7,
interpret it through the period/LFSR function scale,
verify by length and hash,
then accept only by receipt.
```

Rule:

```text
The payload may execute only after receipt acceptance.
```

This preserves safety:

```text
Address first.
Receipt second.
Execution last.
```

---

## 14. Updated Omi-Nomogram Pipeline

The previous Omi-Nomogram pipeline was:

```text
D₂(F₁,W₁,F₂,W₂) → exact two-cube distance
Λ(D₂) → Omi-Nomogram scale selection
G(D₂,Λ) → regular geometry/configuration predicate
I(G,P,E) → valid incidence selection
B(I,G) → 11-cell / chiral shell encapsulation
H(B,D₂,Λ) → runtime decimal measurement
S(H) → sexagesimal degree
ψ(...) → receipt candidate
```

The corrected external-payload version is:

```text
A = omi-<frame>/<control>/<selector>/<relation>/<unit>-imo
Q = ?s=<subpath>&t=<type>&l=<length>&e=<endian>&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>

parse(A) → exact OMI identity
accept(A) → receipt-bound address
parse(Q) → external payload descriptor
verify(Q) → length/hash/type validation
attach(A,Q,s) → subpath payload binding
Λ(A,Q) → Omi-Nomogram scale selection
D₂(A,Q) → exact two-cube / payload relation
G(D₂,Λ) → geometry/configuration predicate
I(G,P,E) → incidence selection
B(I,G) → shell encapsulation
H(B,D₂,Λ) → runtime measure
S(H) → sexagesimal degree
ψ(...) → receipt candidate
```

Compact:

```text
address
→ receipt
→ query payload
→ DataView
→ nomogram scale
→ geometry
→ HNSW
→ sexagesimal
→ receipt
```

---

## 15. Updated ψ Function

The fully corrected reducer is:

```text
ψ(A, Q, Λ, G, I, B, H, S) = R
```

Where:

```text
A = accepted OMI address
Q = external query/DataView payload
Λ = Omi-Nomogram function scale
G = geometry/configuration predicate
I = incidence scheduler
B = 11-cell / Archimedean-Catalan shell
H = HNSW runtime measurement
S = sexagesimal inspection
R = receipt candidate
```

Expanded:

```text
A = omi-<frame>/<control>/<selector>/<relation>/<unit>-imo
Q = ?s=<subpath>&t=<type>&l=<length>&e=<endian>&v=<base64url>&h=<hash>

A → accepted identity
Q → external payload descriptor
A,Q → subpath attachment
Λ → function scale interpretation
G → regular geometry
I → valid incidence
B → chiral shell
H → runtime measure
S → sexagesimal degree
R → receipt
```

---

## 16. Final Query Plane Canon

```text
The Base64 pure-function payload surface does not live inside the native OMI frame.

It begins after the fully qualified address and after the handoff/acceptance boundary.

The native OMI address remains inside the 2¹⁶ gauge.

The query plane, opened by ?, is the 2¹⁷–2³² external runtime extension surface.

The canonical external payload view is DataView over Base64URL-decoded bytes.

TypedArrays are interpretations of the DataView, not the canonical wire form.

The query payload must declare subpath, type, length, value, hash, and optionally endian, radix, period, geometry, and epistemic selector.

0x3F inside the OMI path selects a function scale.

? after the OMI path opens the external transferable payload plane.

Receipt binds the external payload to the accepted OMI object.
```

One-line canon:

```text
OMI keeps identity inside the 2¹⁶ Omicron-gated frame, then opens a post-address ?---? query plane for Base64URL/DataView payloads in the 2¹⁷–2³² runtime extension space, where Omi-Nomogram selects the function scale and receipt lawfully binds the payload to a subpath.
```

Shortest form:

```text
omi---imo
→ /---/ path identity
→ receipt
→ ?---? payload
→ DataView
→ Omi-Nomogram
→ geometry
→ measure
→ receipt
```
:::

---
