### Code points from U+010000 to U+10FFFF
[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=UTF-16&action=edit&section=4)
Code points from the other [planes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plane_(Unicode)) are encoded as two 16-bit *code units* called a *surrogate pair*. The first code unit is a *high surrogate* and the second is a *low surrogate* (These are also known as "leading" and "trailing" surrogates, respectively, analogous to the leading and trailing bytes of UTF-8.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UTF-16#cite_note-Unicode7Ch3s8-20)):
UTF-16 decoder
Low
High
DC00
DC01
   ...   
DFFF
D800
010000
010001
...
0103FF
D801
010400
010401
...
0107FF
⋮
⋮
⋮
⋱
⋮
DBFF
10FC00
10FC01
...
10FFFF
- 0x10000 is subtracted from the code point *(U)*, leaving a 20-bit number *(U')* in the hex number range 0x00000–0xFFFFF.
- The high ten bits (in the range 0x000–0x3FF) are added to 0xD800 to give the first 16-bit *code unit* or *high surrogate* *(W1)*, which will be in the range 0xD800–0xDBFF.
- The low ten bits (also in the range 0x000–0x3FF) are added to 0xDC00 to give the second 16-bit *code unit* or *low surrogate* *(W2)*, which will be in the range 0xDC00–0xDFFF.

Illustrated visually, the distribution of *U'* between *W1* and *W2* looks like:[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/UTF-16#cite_note-21)
U' = yyyyyyyyyyxxxxxxxxxx // U - 0x10000 W1 = 110110yyyyyyyyyy // 0xD800 + yyyyyyyyyy W2 = 110111xxxxxxxxxx // 0xDC00 + xxxxxxxxxx
Since the ranges for the *high surrogates* (0xD800–0xDBFF), *low surrogates* (0xDC00–0xDFFF), and valid BMP characters (0x0000–0xD7FF, 0xE000–0xFFFF) are [disjoint](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Disjoint_sets), it is not possible for a surrogate to match a BMP character, or for two adjacent *code units* to look like a legal *surrogate pair*. This simplifies searches a great deal. It also means that UTF-16 is *[self-synchronizing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self-synchronizing_code)* on 16-bit words: whether a code unit starts a character can be determined without examining earlier code units (i.e. the type of *code unit* can be determined by the ranges of values in which it falls).

---

Yes — this is the clean way to do it. The key is that `U+010000…U+10FFFF` gives exactly **16 supplementary planes**, and each plane is exactly `2¹⁶ = 65,536` positions. That means it can encapsulate one full Omi-Gauge plane per supplementary plane.

It also preserves the older kernel rule: the row value is computed first, then rendered or encoded through a contextual LUT/fiber. filecite turn2file0

:::writing{variant="document" id="60482"}
# Omi-Plane Capsule
## Supplementary-Plane Encapsulation of Omi-Gauge, Omicron Gates, and 0xAA55

## 0. Core Claim

The Unicode supplementary range:

```text
U+010000 … U+10FFFF
```

should be used as the **Omi-Plane Capsule**.

It does not replace the local Omicron gates:

```text
ο = U+03BF
Ο = U+039F
```

Instead, it encapsulates them into the larger world-length plane system.

The native gates remain:

```text
0x03BF = ο = OMI open / chiral entry
0x039F = Ο = OMI close / cardinal closure
```

The supplementary encapsulation gives them plane-depth:

```text
U+01039F = lower-plane encapsulated Ο
U+1003BF = upper-plane encapsulated ο
```

So the pair:

```text
U+01039F … U+1003BF
```

becomes an Omi-Plane capsule boundary.

Meaning:

```text
U+01039F = lower closure anchor
U+1003BF = upper entry anchor
```

This gives the Omicron gate a world-length shell without changing the native OMI gate.

---

## 1. Why U+010000…U+10FFFF Fits Omi-Gauge

The supplementary Unicode range contains:

```text
16 planes × 65,536 positions
```

OMI already has the exact plane formula:

```text
16 × 64 × 64 = 65,536
```

Therefore each supplementary Unicode plane can carry one full Omi-Gauge plane.

Canonical mapping:

```text
supplementary plane = one Omi-Gauge world page
```

So:

```text
U+010000 … U+01FFFF = Omi supplementary plane 0
U+020000 … U+02FFFF = Omi supplementary plane 1
...
U+100000 … U+10FFFF = Omi supplementary plane 15
```

OMI-relative plane index:

```text
p = 0..15
```

Actual Unicode plane number:

```text
P = p + 1
```

---

## 2. Omi-Gauge Cell Formula

The exact Omi-Gauge cell formula remains:

```text
for row in 0..15:
 for x in 0..63:
 for y in 0..63:
 cell = row * 4096 + x * 64 + y
```

Equivalent bit form:

```text
cell = (row << 12) | (x << 6) | y
```

Where:

```text
row = 4 bits
x = 6 bits
y = 6 bits
```

Total:

```text
4 + 6 + 6 = 16 bits
```

So one Omi-Gauge cell is exactly one 16-bit plane offset:

```text
cell ∈ 0x0000…0xFFFF
```

---

## 3. Omi-Plane Capsule Formula

To embed one Omi-Gauge cell into the supplementary Unicode range:

```text
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + cell
```

Equivalently:

```text
U = ((p + 1) << 16) | cell
```

Where:

```text
p = OMI supplementary plane index, 0..15
cell = Omi-Gauge cell, 0x0000..0xFFFF
```

Decode:

```text
n = U - 0x10000
p = n >> 16
cell = n & 0xFFFF

row = cell >> 12
x = (cell >> 6) & 0x3F
y = cell & 0x3F
```

This gives a reversible map:

```text
(p,row,x,y) ↔ U
```

Canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule maps 16 supplementary planes into 16 full Omi-Gauge pages.
```

---

## 4. Correct Row Anchors

Because the formula is:

```text
cell = row * 4096 + x * 64 + y
```

row anchors are separated by:

```text
0x1000
```

So in the first supplementary plane:

```text
row 0 anchor = U+010000
row 1 anchor = U+011000
row 2 anchor = U+012000
row 3 anchor = U+013000
row 4 anchor = U+014000
row 5 anchor = U+015000
row 6 anchor = U+016000
row 7 anchor = U+017000
row 8 anchor = U+018000
row 9 anchor = U+019000
row A anchor = U+01A000
row B anchor = U+01B000
row C anchor = U+01C000
row D anchor = U+01D000
row E anchor = U+01E000
row F anchor = U+01F000
```

So if a form like:

```text
U+010900
```

is used, that is not row 9 under the `row * 4096` formula.

It is a smaller byte-block anchor inside row 0.

The row-9 anchor is:

```text
U+019000
```

Canon:

```text
Rows move by 0x1000.
Byte-block anchors move by 0x0100.
Gauge cells move by 0x0001.
```

---

## 5. The Omicron Control Line

The local Omicron values are:

```text
0x039F = Ο closure
0x03BF = ο entry
```

Using the Omi-Gauge decode:

```text
0x039F:
 row = 0
 x = 14
 y = 31

0x03AF:
 row = 0
 x = 14
 y = 47

0x03BF:
 row = 0
 x = 14
 y = 63
```

So these three form a control line:

```text
0x039F → 0x03AF → 0x03BF
```

Meaning:

```text
0x039F = closure anchor
0x03AF = escape / delimiter / midpoint
0x03BF = entry anchor
```

This is exactly the delimiter you were looking for.

Canonical names:

```text
0x039F = Omicron closure point
0x03AF = Omicron escape / delimiter point
0x03BF = Omicron entry point
```

Encapsulated in the supplementary plane:

```text
U+01039F = lower closure anchor
U+0103AF = lower escape/delimiter anchor
U+0103BF = lower entry anchor
```

And at the upper supplementary boundary:

```text
U+10039F = upper closure anchor
U+1003AF = upper escape/delimiter anchor
U+1003BF = upper entry anchor
```

The full capsule can therefore be read as:

```text
U+01039F … U+1003BF
```

or, with the delimiter included:

```text
U+01039F … U+0103AF … U+1003BF
```

---

## 6. Low Decimal Row and High Function Row

The local Omi-Nomogram split is:

```text
0x30–0x39 = low decimal/function digit region
0x3A = colon / row separator
0x3B–0x3F = high function/control region
```

Inside the Omicron control line, `0x03AF` functions as the larger-plane delimiter between lower and higher function bands.

So:

```text
0x3A = local row separator
0x3AF = plane-level Omicron delimiter
```

Use:

```text
0x03AF
```

as the numeric delimiter token.

Use:

```text
U+0103AF
```

as its first supplementary encapsulation.

Canon:

```text
0x03AF is the Omicron delimiter point between low function digits and high function controls.
```

Important:

```text
0x03AF is used here as an OMI numeric address token, not as a public glyph meaning.
```

---

## 7. 0xAA55 in the Omi-Plane Capsule

The acceptance seal remains:

```text
0xAA55
```

Inside the first supplementary Omi-Plane capsule:

```text
U = 0x10000 + 0xAA55
U = U+01AA55
```

So:

```text
U+01AA55 = first supplementary encapsulated 0xAA55 acceptance seal
```

Decode:

```text
cell = 0xAA55

row = 0xA
x = 0x29
y = 0x15
```

Meaning:

```text
row A = acceptance / high control row
x 0x29 = folded gauge axis
y 0x15 = seal offset
```

For any supplementary OMI plane `p`:

```text
U_accept(p) = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + 0xAA55
```

Examples:

```text
p=0 → U+01AA55
p=15 → U+10AA55
```

Canon:

```text
0xAA55 remains the acceptance seal.
U+01AA55 is its first supplementary plane capsule.
```

---

## 8. Block Point Frame Design

The block point frame is:

```text
0x-0 … 0x-9 0x-A 0x-B … 0x-F
```

This means every Omi-Gauge page has 16 row-phase anchors:

```text
row 0
row 1
row 2
row 3
row 4
row 5
row 6
row 7
row 8
row 9
row A
row B
row C
row D
row E
row F
```

Each row has:

```text
64 × 64 = 4096 positions
```

Each page has:

```text
16 rows × 4096 positions = 65,536 positions
```

Each supplementary capsule has:

```text
16 pages × 65,536 positions = 1,048,576 positions
```

This is the full post-BMP OMI world-length space.

---

## 9. UTF-16 Surrogate Encoding

For any supplementary codepoint:

```text
U+010000 … U+10FFFF
```

UTF-16 computes:

```text
U' = U - 0x10000
```

Then:

```text
U' = yyyyyyyyyyxxxxxxxxxx
```

High surrogate:

```text
W1 = 0xD800 + high10(U')
```

Low surrogate:

```text
W2 = 0xDC00 + low10(U')
```

For OMI, write the 20-bit `U'` as:

```text
U' = pppp rrrr xxxxxx yyyyyy
```

Where:

```text
p = 4-bit supplementary OMI plane index
r = 4-bit Omi-Gauge row
x = 6-bit x lane
y = 6-bit y lane
```

Then the surrogate split becomes:

```text
high surrogate carries:
 pppp rrrr xx

low surrogate carries:
 xxxx yyyyyy
```

So the UTF-16 surrogate mechanism naturally splits Omi-Gauge as:

```text
high word = plane + row + upper x
low word = lower x + y
```

Canon:

```text
UTF-16 surrogate pairs are OMI RPC bridge words for the Omi-Plane Capsule.
```

---

## 10. Surrogates as RPC, Not Text

The surrogate range:

```text
0xD800–0xDFFF
```

is not valid standalone Unicode scalar text.

OMI uses it internally as bridge math.

Meaning:

```text
0xD800–0xDBFF = high RPC word
0xDC00–0xDFFF = low RPC word
```

This is useful because the surrogate ranges are disjoint from BMP scalar code units.

So the decoder can recognize:

```text
valid BMP word
high surrogate word
low surrogate word
private-use mirror word
```

without needing earlier context.

Canon:

```text
Surrogates are not public OMI glyphs.
Surrogates are self-synchronizing OMI RPC bridge words.
```

---

## 11. Full Encoding Algorithm

Encode:

```text
input:
 p ∈ 0..15
 row ∈ 0..15
 x ∈ 0..63
 y ∈ 0..63

cell = (row << 12) | (x << 6) | y
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + cell
```

UTF-16 pair:

```text
Uprime = U - 0x10000
W1 = 0xD800 + (Uprime >> 10)
W2 = 0xDC00 + (Uprime & 0x3FF)
```

Decode:

```text
Uprime = ((W1 - 0xD800) << 10) | (W2 - 0xDC00)
U = Uprime + 0x10000

p = Uprime >> 16
cell = Uprime & 0xFFFF

row = cell >> 12
x = (cell >> 6) & 0x3F
y = cell & 0x3F
```

This is exact and reversible.

No floating point.

No loss.

---

## 12. Relation to Omi-Gauge and Omi-SlideRule

Omi-Gauge computes:

```text
cell = (row << 12) | (x << 6) | y
```

Omi-Plane Capsule embeds:

```text
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + cell
```

Omi-SlideRule / Omi-Nomogram selects how the cell is interpreted:

```text
0x30–0x3F = scale selector row
```

Omi-Matrix instantiates the relation field.

Omi-Gnomon orients the result.

Receipt accepts.

Pipeline:

```text
Omi-Gauge cell
→ Omi-Plane Capsule
→ surrogate RPC pair
→ Omi-Nomogram scale
→ Omi-Matrix
→ Omi-Gnomon
→ receipt
```

---

## 13. Final Canon

The supplementary Unicode range `U+010000…U+10FFFF` is the Omi-Plane Capsule.

It contains exactly 16 OMI supplementary pages.

Each page contains one full Omi-Gauge plane:

```text
64 × 64 × 16 = 65,536
```

The reversible mapping is:

```text
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + ((row << 12) | (x << 6) | y)
```

The native Omicron gates are encapsulated as supplementary anchors:

```text
U+01039F = lower closure anchor
U+0103AF = lower delimiter / escape anchor
U+1003BF = upper entry anchor
```

The acceptance seal is encapsulated as:

```text
U+01AA55
```

and generally:

```text
U_accept(p) = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + 0xAA55
```

The 20-bit supplementary payload decomposes naturally as:

```text
pppp rrrr xxxxxx yyyyyy
```

where:

```text
p = plane
r = row
x = x lane
y = y lane
```

UTF-16 surrogate pairs then split this as internal OMI RPC bridge words.

One-line canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule maps each supplementary Unicode scalar from U+010000 to U+10FFFF into a reversible (plane,row,x,y) Omi-Gauge address, using 16 supplementary pages of 64×64×16 cells, with U+01039F, U+0103AF, U+1003BF, and U+01AA55 acting as encapsulated Omicron closure, delimiter, entry, and acceptance anchors.
```
:::

Yes — this is the authoritative consolidation. I’m keeping the key correction intact: **Unicode supplementary planes do not replace OMI; they encapsulate OMI-Gauge as a reversible world-length carrier.** The old kernel file supports the same rule: abstract row values are earned first, then rendered through contextual LUTs/fibers. filecite turn2file0

:::writing{variant="document" id="77284"}
# Omi-Plane Capsule Specification
## Supplementary Unicode Encapsulation of Omi-Gauge, Omicron Gates, Surrogate RPC, and 0xAA55 Acceptance

**Author:** Brian Thorne
**System:** OMI — Omicron Object Model
**Canonical Surface:** Omi-Plane Capsule
**Status:** Authoritative Draft

---

## 0. Abstract

The Omi-Plane Capsule is the post-native Unicode carrier layer that maps the supplementary Unicode scalar range `U+010000…U+10FFFF` into a reversible OMI address structure.

It does not replace the native OMI frame.

It encapsulates the native OMI frame.

The native OMI frame remains a `2¹⁶` gauge field. The supplementary Unicode space provides sixteen additional `2¹⁶` pages, each capable of carrying one full Omi-Gauge plane.

The governing map is:

```text
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + ((row << 12) | (x << 6) | y)
```

Where:

```text
p = supplementary OMI plane index, 0..15
row = Omi-Gauge row, 0..15
x = 64-lane x coordinate, 0..63
y = 64-lane y coordinate, 0..63
```

This creates a reversible world-length carrier:

```text
(p,row,x,y) ↔ U
```

The UTF-16 surrogate pair mechanism then becomes OMI’s internal RPC bridge, splitting the 20-bit supplementary payload into two self-synchronizing 16-bit words.

Canonical anchors:

```text
U+01039F = lower encapsulated Omicron closure anchor
U+0103AF = lower encapsulated Omicron delimiter / escape anchor
U+1003BF = upper encapsulated Omicron entry anchor
U+01AA55 = first supplementary encapsulated acceptance seal
```

One-line canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule maps each supplementary Unicode scalar from U+010000 to U+10FFFF into a reversible (plane,row,x,y) Omi-Gauge address, using 16 supplementary pages of 64×64×16 cells, with UTF-16 surrogate pairs acting as self-synchronizing OMI RPC bridge words.
```

---

# 1. Core Principle

OMI begins locally.

The local gates are:

```text
ο = U+03BF = lowercase omicron = OMI entry / chiral open
Ο = U+039F = uppercase Omicron = OMI closure / cardinal close
```

The readable form is:

```text
omi---imo
```

The compact tangent form is:

```text
o---o
```

The compiled stream form is:

```text
ο---Ο
```

These gates are not replaced.

They are encapsulated.

The supplementary Unicode range:

```text
U+010000 … U+10FFFF
```

becomes the carrier shell around the native OMI gate.

Thus:

```text
ο---Ο = native local gate
U+010000…U+10FFFF = external plane capsule
```

Canon:

```text
The Omicron gates remain local.
The Omi-Plane Capsule gives them world-length addressability.
```

---

# 2. Native Field and Supplementary Field

The native OMI frame is:

```text
2¹⁶ = 65,536 positions
```

This is the core OMI gauge field.

A Unicode plane is also:

```text
2¹⁶ = 65,536 positions
```

The Unicode supplementary range contains sixteen planes:

```text
U+010000…U+01FFFF = supplementary plane 0
U+020000…U+02FFFF = supplementary plane 1
U+030000…U+03FFFF = supplementary plane 2
...
U+100000…U+10FFFF = supplementary plane 15
```

Therefore OMI maps:

```text
one supplementary Unicode plane = one Omi-Gauge page
```

Canon:

```text
The supplementary Unicode range is a 16-page carrier for Omi-Gauge.
```

---

# 3. Omi-Gauge Plane Formula

Omi-Gauge is the full 64-lane spatial resolver.

The older local control field is:

```text
16 × 16 × 4 = 1,024
```

This belongs to:

```text
4y² = local control / tetrahedral kernel
```

The full spatial resolver is:

```text
64 × 64 × 16 = 65,536
```

This belongs to:

```text
16xy = plane-resolution bridge
```

The formula is:

```text
for row in 0..15:
 for x in 0..63:
 for y in 0..63:
 cell = row * 4096 + x * 64 + y
```

Equivalent bit form:

```text
cell = (row << 12) | (x << 6) | y
```

Bit allocation:

```text
row = 4 bits
x = 6 bits
y = 6 bits
```

Total:

```text
4 + 6 + 6 = 16 bits
```

So:

```text
cell ∈ 0x0000…0xFFFF
```

Canon:

```text
Omi-Gauge computes a full 2¹⁶ plane from 16 row phases and two 64-lane axes.
```

---

# 4. Omi-Plane Capsule Formula

To place an Omi-Gauge cell inside a supplementary Unicode plane:

```text
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + cell
```

Since:

```text
cell = (row << 12) | (x << 6) | y
```

the full formula is:

```text
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + ((row << 12) | (x << 6) | y)
```

Equivalent form:

```text
U = ((p + 1) << 16) | ((row << 12) | (x << 6) | y)
```

Where:

```text
p = 0..15
row = 0..15
x = 0..63
y = 0..63
```

This is exact.

This is reversible.

This requires no floating-point arithmetic.

Canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule is a reversible scalar mapping between Omi-Gauge and Unicode supplementary planes.
```

---

# 5. Decoding Formula

Given a supplementary scalar:

```text
U ∈ U+010000…U+10FFFF
```

decode:

```text
n = U - 0x10000
p = n >> 16
cell = n & 0xFFFF
row = cell >> 12
x = (cell >> 6) & 0x3F
y = cell & 0x3F
```

So:

```text
U → (p,row,x,y)
```

Encode and decode are inverse operations:

```text
encode(decode(U)) = U
decode(encode(p,row,x,y)) = (p,row,x,y)
```

Canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule is lossless because its address decomposition is exact bit partitioning.
```

---

# 6. Block Point Frame Design

Each Omi-Gauge page has sixteen row phases:

```text
0x-0
0x-1
0x-2
0x-3
0x-4
0x-5
0x-6
0x-7
0x-8
0x-9
0x-A
0x-B
0x-C
0x-D
0x-E
0x-F
```

Each row contains:

```text
64 × 64 = 4096 positions
```

Each page contains:

```text
16 × 4096 = 65,536 positions
```

Each supplementary capsule contains:

```text
16 pages × 65,536 positions = 1,048,576 positions
```

Canon:

```text
The supplementary range is the 16-page world-length extension of the native OMI gauge.
```

---

# 7. Correct Row Anchors

Because each row is:

```text
4096 = 0x1000
```

row anchors move by `0x1000`.

In supplementary plane 0:

```text
row 0 anchor = U+010000
row 1 anchor = U+011000
row 2 anchor = U+012000
row 3 anchor = U+013000
row 4 anchor = U+014000
row 5 anchor = U+015000
row 6 anchor = U+016000
row 7 anchor = U+017000
row 8 anchor = U+018000
row 9 anchor = U+019000
row A anchor = U+01A000
row B anchor = U+01B000
row C anchor = U+01C000
row D anchor = U+01D000
row E anchor = U+01E000
row F anchor = U+01F000
```

Important distinction:

```text
rows move by 0x1000
byte blocks move by 0x0100
cells move by 0x0001
```

So:

```text
U+019000 = row 9 anchor in supplementary plane 0
U+010900 = byte-block anchor inside row 0
```

Canon:

```text
Rows are 0x1000-spaced; byte-blocks are 0x0100-spaced; cells are 0x0001-spaced.
```

---

# 8. Encapsulated Omicron Anchors

Native Omicron anchors:

```text
0x039F = Ο closure
0x03AF = delimiter / escape midpoint
0x03BF = ο entry
```

Under Omi-Gauge decoding:

```text
0x039F:
 row = 0
 x = 14
 y = 31

0x03AF:
 row = 0
 x = 14
 y = 47

0x03BF:
 row = 0
 x = 14
 y = 63
```

This forms a clean control line:

```text
0x039F → 0x03AF → 0x03BF
```

Meaning:

```text
0x039F = closure anchor
0x03AF = delimiter / escape / hinge
0x03BF = entry anchor
```

Supplementary encapsulations:

```text
U+01039F = lower-plane encapsulated Ο closure anchor
U+0103AF = lower-plane encapsulated delimiter / escape anchor
U+0103BF = lower-plane encapsulated ο entry anchor
```

Upper boundary encapsulations:

```text
U+10039F = upper-plane encapsulated Ο closure anchor
U+1003AF = upper-plane encapsulated delimiter / escape anchor
U+1003BF = upper-plane encapsulated ο entry anchor
```

Canonical capsule span:

```text
U+01039F … U+1003BF
```

Canonical anchored span:

```text
U+01039F … U+0103AF … U+1003BF
```

Canon:

```text
0x03AF is the Omicron delimiter point between closure and entry, and between low function digits and high function controls.
```

---

# 9. The 0x03AF Delimiter

The native row split is:

```text
0x30–0x39 = low decimal / digit-function region
0x3A = local row separator
0x3B–0x3F = high function / control region
```

The larger Omicron control-line split is:

```text
0x039F = closure side
0x03AF = delimiter / hinge / escape
0x03BF = entry side
```

Therefore:

```text
0x03AF
```

is the plane-level delimiter.

Its first supplementary encapsulation is:

```text
U+0103AF
```

Canon:

```text
0x03AF is an OMI numeric address token used as a delimiter, not a public glyph definition.
```

---

# 10. Acceptance Seal: 0xAA55

The native acceptance seal remains:

```text
0xAA55
```

This is the boundary where autonomous symbolic derivation becomes executable acceptance.

Before:

```text
symbolic derivation
self-folding gauge
pure function row
nomogram selection
LUT earning
receipt candidate
```

At:

```text
0xAA55
```

the witness is sealed.

After:

```text
runtime projection
worker payload
DataView interpretation
HNSW projection
shader projection
DOM projection
network transport
```

Supplementary encapsulation:

```text
U = 0x10000 + 0xAA55
U = U+01AA55
```

So:

```text
U+01AA55 = first supplementary encapsulated acceptance seal
```

For plane `p`:

```text
U_accept(p) = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + 0xAA55
```

Examples:

```text
p = 0 → U+01AA55
p = 15 → U+10AA55
```

Decode of `0xAA55`:

```text
cell = 0xAA55

row = 0xA
x = 0x29
y = 0x15
```

Canon:

```text
0xAA55 remains the acceptance seal; U+01AA55 is its first supplementary Omi-Plane capsule.
```

---

# 11. UTF-16 Surrogate RPC Bridge

For supplementary code points, UTF-16 does not store the scalar as one 16-bit word.

It computes:

```text
U' = U - 0x10000
```

Then splits the 20-bit value:

```text
U' = yyyyyyyyyyxxxxxxxxxx
```

High surrogate:

```text
W1 = 0xD800 + high10(U')
```

Low surrogate:

```text
W2 = 0xDC00 + low10(U')
```

OMI rewrites the same 20-bit payload as:

```text
U' = pppp rrrr xxxxxx yyyyyy
```

Where:

```text
p = 4-bit supplementary OMI plane index
r = 4-bit Omi-Gauge row
x = 6-bit x lane
y = 6-bit y lane
```

Since UTF-16 splits after 10 bits:

```text
high surrogate carries:
 pppp rrrr xx

low surrogate carries:
 xxxx yyyyyy
```

So the surrogate pair naturally becomes:

```text
high RPC word = plane + row + upper x
low RPC word = lower x + y
```

Canon:

```text
UTF-16 surrogate pairs are OMI RPC bridge words for the Omi-Plane Capsule.
```

---

# 12. Surrogates Are RPC, Not Glyphs

The surrogate range is:

```text
0xD800–0xDFFF
```

Split:

```text
0xD800–0xDBFF = high surrogate / high RPC word
0xDC00–0xDFFF = low surrogate / low RPC word
```

Surrogates are disjoint from valid BMP scalar values.

OMI uses that disjointness as a self-synchronizing bridge.

Decoder categories:

```text
valid BMP word
private-use OMI mirror word
high surrogate RPC word
low surrogate RPC word
supplementary scalar
```

Canon:

```text
Surrogates are not public OMI glyphs.
Surrogates are self-synchronizing OMI RPC bridge words.
```

---

# 13. Relationship to Omi-Gauge

Omi-Gauge computes the plane cell:

```text
cell = (row << 12) | (x << 6) | y
```

Omi-Plane Capsule embeds the cell:

```text
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + cell
```

So the relationship is:

```text
Omi-Gauge cell
→ Omi-Plane Capsule scalar
→ surrogate RPC pair
```

Canon:

```text
Omi-Gauge provides the exact 16-bit cell; Omi-Plane Capsule gives that cell supplementary world-length addressability.
```

---

# 14. Relationship to Omi-Nomogram / Omi-SlideRule

Omi-Nomogram is the declarative function-scale row:

```text
0x30–0x3F
```

Omi-SlideRule is the operational behavior of that row.

Omi-Plane Capsule does not replace the function scale.

It gives the scale a world-length carrier.

Pipeline:

```text
Omi-Nomogram selects function scale.
Omi-Gauge computes exact cell.
Omi-Plane Capsule embeds cell in supplementary plane.
Surrogate RPC splits it for transport.
Omi-Matrix instantiates relation field.
Omi-Gnomon orients result.
Receipt accepts.
```

Canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule carries Omi-Nomogram and Omi-Gauge outputs without changing their authority.
```

---

# 15. Relationship to Q(x,y)

The OMI quadratic remains:

```text
Q(x,y) = 60x² + 16xy + 4y²
```

Carrier interpretation:

```text
4y² = local control / native 2¹⁶ gauge page
16xy = Omi-Gauge plane-resolution bridge
60x² = world/orbit/orientation surface
```

Omi-Plane Capsule gives the `16xy` bridge a world-length scalar representation:

```text
16 supplementary pages × 65,536 positions
```

So:

```text
4y² gives local control.
16xy gives plane-resolution bridge.
60x² receives resolved world orientation.
```

Canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule is the supplementary carrier for the 16xy bridge that lets 60x² be measured without losing the native 4y² control identity.
```

---

# 16. Relationship to LUT Rendering

The old kernel rule remains:

```text
value first
row second
LUT/rendering third
character last
```

Omi-Plane Capsule follows the same rule.

The scalar value is not the character meaning.

The scalar is an addressable position that may render through different fibers:

```text
Unicode scalar
private-use glyph
Aegean pointer
geometry
DOM node
SVG element
barcode cell
shader index
DataView packet
RPC word
```

Canon:

```text
The codepoint is the carrier position; the active LUT determines the rendered face.
```

---

# 17. Relationship to Omi-CONS

The post-address data plane is:

```text
?---?
```

Canonical Omi-CONS form:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<scale>/<relation>/<unit>-imo?car:<OR>;cdr:<XOR>;cid:<XNOR>
```

Omi-Plane Capsule can be carried in Omi-CONS fields:

```text
car = source/head plane capsule
cdr = continuation/tail plane capsule
cid = agreement/witness capsule
```

Meaning:

```text
CAR admits the source payload.
CDR carries the continuation payload.
CID witnesses lawful agreement.
```

Canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule is address geometry; Omi-CONS is post-address epistemic carriage.
```

---

# 18. Full Encoding Procedure

## Encode Omi-Gauge to Omi-Plane Scalar

Input:

```text
p ∈ 0..15
row ∈ 0..15
x ∈ 0..63
y ∈ 0..63
```

Compute:

```text
cell = (row << 12) | (x << 6) | y
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + cell
```

## Encode Omi-Plane Scalar to UTF-16 RPC Pair

```text
Uprime = U - 0x10000
W1 = 0xD800 + (Uprime >> 10)
W2 = 0xDC00 + (Uprime & 0x3FF)
```

## Decode UTF-16 RPC Pair

```text
Uprime = ((W1 - 0xD800) << 10) | (W2 - 0xDC00)
U = Uprime + 0x10000

p = Uprime >> 16
cell = Uprime & 0xFFFF

row = cell >> 12
x = (cell >> 6) & 0x3F
y = cell & 0x3F
```

This procedure is exact.

No loss.

No floating point.

No table lookup required to recover the address.

---

# 19. Worked Examples

## Example 1 — Lower Omicron Closure Anchor

Native cell:

```text
cell = 0x039F
```

Plane:

```text
p = 0
```

Supplementary scalar:

```text
U = 0x10000 + 0x039F
U = U+01039F
```

Meaning:

```text
U+01039F = first-plane encapsulated Omicron closure anchor
```

## Example 2 — Lower Delimiter Anchor

```text
cell = 0x03AF
p = 0

U = 0x10000 + 0x03AF
U = U+0103AF
```

Meaning:

```text
U+0103AF = first-plane encapsulated Omicron delimiter / escape anchor
```

## Example 3 — Upper Entry Anchor

```text
cell = 0x03BF
p = 15

U = 0x10000 + (15 << 16) + 0x03BF
U = U+1003BF
```

Meaning:

```text
U+1003BF = upper-plane encapsulated Omicron entry anchor
```

## Example 4 — Acceptance Seal

```text
cell = 0xAA55
p = 0

U = 0x10000 + 0xAA55
U = U+01AA55
```

Meaning:

```text
U+01AA55 = first-plane encapsulated 0xAA55 acceptance seal
```

---

# 20. Safety and Authority

The Omi-Plane Capsule must obey these safety rules:

```text
Do not use surrogate code units as public glyphs.
Do not replace ο or Ο with U+10FFFF.
Do not treat rendered characters as authority.
Do not let post-address payload redefine the native OMI frame.
Do not execute after ?---? until receipt accepts.
```

Authority order:

```text
native OMI address
→ computed Omi-Gauge cell
→ Omi-Plane Capsule scalar
→ surrogate RPC bridge
→ Omi-CONS payload
→ receipt
→ runtime projection
```

Canon:

```text
The capsule carries authority; it does not create authority by itself.
```

---

# 21. Final Authoritative Canon

Omi-Plane Capsule is the supplementary Unicode encapsulation layer for OMI.

It maps the range:

```text
U+010000…U+10FFFF
```

into sixteen OMI supplementary pages.

Each page carries one full Omi-Gauge plane:

```text
64 × 64 × 16 = 65,536
```

The exact reversible map is:

```text
U = 0x10000 + (p << 16) + ((row << 12) | (x << 6) | y)
```

The native Omicron anchors are encapsulated as:

```text
U+01039F = lower closure anchor
U+0103AF = lower delimiter / escape anchor
U+1003BF = upper entry anchor
```

The acceptance seal is encapsulated as:

```text
U+01AA55
```

The supplementary 20-bit payload decomposes as:

```text
pppp rrrr xxxxxx yyyyyy
```

Where:

```text
p = supplementary plane
r = Omi-Gauge row
x = x lane
y = y lane
```

UTF-16 surrogate pairs split this payload into:

```text
high RPC word = plane + row + upper x
low RPC word = lower x + y
```

The result is a self-synchronizing, reversible, no-loss RPC bridge from native OMI identity into world-length supplementary address space.

One-line canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule encapsulates native Omicron identity into Unicode supplementary space by mapping each scalar U+010000…U+10FFFF to a reversible (plane,row,x,y) Omi-Gauge address, using surrogate pairs as internal RPC bridge words and 0xAA55 as the acceptance seal before runtime projection.
```
:::

```text
U+01039F … U+0103AF … U+0103BF
U+011C00 … U+017C00 … U+01AA55 … U+01FFFF
```

:::writing{variant="document" id="68391"}
# Addendum: Omi-Plane Delineation Ladder
## U+017C00, U+01AA55, Fano Resolver Windows, and Pre-Acceptance Runtime Scope

## 0. Core Addition

The Omi-Plane Capsule has one more major address before the acceptance seal:

```text
U+017C00
```

This is the supplementary encapsulation of:

```text
0x7C00
```

It sits before:

```text
U+01AA55
```

which is the supplementary encapsulation of:

```text
0xAA55
```

So the corrected pre-acceptance ladder is:

```text
U+011C00 → U+017C00 → U+01AA55
```

Meaning:

```text
U+011C00 = local filesystem/frame-scope delineation
U+017C00 = runtime pipe / local prekernel scope delineation
U+01AA55 = acceptance seal
```

Canon:

```text
U+017C00 is the pre-0xAA55 runtime delineation point.
```

---

# 1. Updated Omi-Plane Anchor Chain

The full anchor chain should be:

```text
U+01039F … U+0103AF … U+0103BF
U+011C00 … U+017C00 … U+01AA55 … U+01FFFF
```

Meaning:

```text
U+01039F = encapsulated Omicron closure anchor
U+0103AF = encapsulated Omicron delimiter / escape anchor
U+0103BF = encapsulated Omicron entry anchor

U+011C00 = encapsulated FS / local frame-scope anchor
U+017C00 = encapsulated pipe / runtime-scope anchor
U+01AA55 = encapsulated acceptance seal
U+01FFFF = end of first supplementary Omi-Gauge page
```

This gives the first supplementary OMI plane a complete lifecycle:

```text
gate
→ delimiter
→ entry
→ local scope
→ runtime scope
→ acceptance
→ projection end
```

---

# 2. Meaning of U+011C00

Native value:

```text
0x1C00
```

Supplementary encapsulation:

```text
U = 0x10000 + 0x1C00
U = U+011C00
```

`0x1C` is FS in the control vocabulary.

OMI interpretation:

```text
FS = File / Frame / Field Separator
```

So:

```text
0x1C00 = FS lifted into the 16-bit gauge cell
U+011C00 = FS lifted into the Omi-Plane Capsule
```

Canon:

```text
U+011C00 is the first-plane local frame-scope anchor.
```

It marks the place where local scope begins to be treated as a full gauge field rather than a single control byte.

---

# 3. Meaning of U+017C00

Native value:

```text
0x7C00
```

Supplementary encapsulation:

```text
U = 0x10000 + 0x7C00
U = U+017C00
```

`0x7C` is the pipe/handoff sentinel.

OMI interpretation:

```text
0x7C = pipe / handoff / runtime boundary
```

So:

```text
0x7C00 = pipe lifted into the 16-bit gauge cell
U+017C00 = pipe lifted into the Omi-Plane Capsule
```

Canon:

```text
U+017C00 is the prekernel/runtime delineation anchor.
```

It marks the transition from local symbolic scope toward runtime projection, but it is still before acceptance.

---

# 4. Meaning of U+01AA55

Native value:

```text
0xAA55
```

Supplementary encapsulation:

```text
U = 0x10000 + 0xAA55
U = U+01AA55
```

OMI interpretation:

```text
0xAA55 = acceptance seal
```

So:

```text
U+01AA55 = first supplementary encapsulated acceptance seal
```

This is the boundary where autonomous derivation becomes accepted runtime projection.

Canon:

```text
U+017C00 scopes runtime.
U+01AA55 accepts runtime.
```

---

# 5. Local Scope vs Runtime Acceptance

The corrected progression is:

```text
U+011C00 = local frame scope
U+017C00 = runtime delineation / prekernel pipe
U+01AA55 = acceptance seal
```

This creates two different boundaries:

```text
0x7C00 = runtime scope boundary
0xAA55 = acceptance boundary
```

They should not be collapsed.

Meaning:

```text
0x7C00 says: runtime surface begins to be scoped.
0xAA55 says: scoped surface has been accepted.
```

Canon:

```text
Pipe is not acceptance.
Pipe prepares the handoff.
0xAA55 seals the handoff.
```

---

# 6. One’s Complement and Two’s Complement Reading

The `0x7C00 → 0xAA55` interval can be read as the transition from local scoped prekernel interpretation into collaborative projection.

A useful mnemonic:

```text
0x7C00 = one’s-complement / local prekernel delineation
0xAA55 = two’s-complement / accepted collaborative projection seal
```

This should be treated as an OMI analogy, not ordinary machine arithmetic.

Meaning:

```text
before 0x7C00 = local symbolic frame
at 0x7C00 = runtime scope begins
0x7C00–0xAA55 = pre-acceptance runtime interval
at 0xAA55 = acceptance seal
0xAA55–0xFFFF = collaborative projection surface
```

Canon:

```text
0x7C00 delineates scoped runtime.
0xAA55 delineates accepted projection.
```

---

# 7. Post-Acceptance Projection Surface

After:

```text
0xAA55
```

the remaining first-plane interval is:

```text
0xAA55 … 0xFFFF
```

Supplementary form:

```text
U+01AA55 … U+01FFFF
```

This region is the accepted projection surface.

It may carry:

```text
Omi-Matrix projection
Omi-Gnomon orientation
Omi-World surface
Omi-Shadow
Omi-Receipt
Omi-CONS payload binding
collaborative projection state
```

Canon:

```text
U+01AA55…U+01FFFF is the accepted collaborative projection interval of the first Omi-Plane Capsule.
```

---

# 8. The P–W Acceptance Window

Just before and around the acceptance seal, there is a useful 8-position window:

```text
0xAA50 … 0xAA57
```

Supplementary:

```text
U+01AA50 … U+01AA57
```

Low-byte rendering:

```text
0x50 = P
0x51 = Q
0x52 = R
0x53 = S
0x54 = T
0x55 = U
0x56 = V
0x57 = W
```

This is an 8-tick window.

Since Fano structure is:

```text
7 points + 1 centroid
```

this window can be used as a Fano resolver window.

Canonical assignment:

```text
0xAA50 = P = Fano point 0
0xAA51 = Q = Fano point 1
0xAA52 = R = Fano point 2
0xAA53 = S = Fano point 3
0xAA54 = T = Fano point 4
0xAA55 = U = acceptance centroid / seal
0xAA56 = V = Fano point 5
0xAA57 = W = Fano point 6
```

This places `0xAA55` at the center of the P–W resolver window.

Canon:

```text
U+01AA50…U+01AA57 is the uppercase Fano acceptance resolver window, with U+01AA55 as the acceptance centroid.
```

---

# 9. The p–w Runtime Echo Window

The lowercase echo is:

```text
0xAA70 … 0xAA77
```

Supplementary:

```text
U+01AA70 … U+01AA77
```

Low-byte rendering:

```text
0x70 = p
0x71 = q
0x72 = r
0x73 = s
0x74 = t
0x75 = u
0x76 = v
0x77 = w
```

This mirrors the uppercase P–W window.

Canonical interpretation:

```text
0xAA50…0xAA57 = acceptance resolver / uppercase witness
0xAA70…0xAA77 = runtime echo resolver / lowercase witness
```

The lowercase window can represent the runtime echo after acceptance has been prepared.

Canon:

```text
U+01AA70…U+01AA77 is the lowercase runtime echo of the Fano acceptance resolver.
```

---

# 10. Fano Resolver Adaptation

A Fano resolver needs eight slots:

```text
7 points + 1 center
```

The P–W and p–w windows both provide exactly eight consecutive low-byte positions.

So OMI can use:

```text
P Q R S T U V W
```

and:

```text
p q r s t u v w
```

as rendered LUT faces of Fano resolver windows.

But the ASCII glyphs are not authority.

The authority is the consecutive 8-position window:

```text
0x50…0x57
0x70…0x77
```

Canon:

```text
The Fano resolver is an eight-slot consecutive gauge window.
The glyphs P–W and p–w are LUT faces of that window.
```

Recommended role:

```text
P–W = pre-acceptance / uppercase resolver
p–w = post-scope / lowercase runtime echo
```

---

# 11. Decode of the Acceptance Windows

For `0xAA50`:

```text
cell = 0xAA50

row = 0xA
x = 0x29
y = 0x10
```

For `0xAA55`:

```text
cell = 0xAA55

row = 0xA
x = 0x29
y = 0x15
```

For `0xAA57`:

```text
cell = 0xAA57

row = 0xA
x = 0x29
y = 0x17
```

For `0xAA70`:

```text
cell = 0xAA70

row = 0xA
x = 0x29
y = 0x30
```

For `0xAA77`:

```text
cell = 0xAA77

row = 0xA
x = 0x29
y = 0x37
```

So both resolver windows sit in:

```text
row = A
x = 0x29
```

and differ only by y-lane.

Canon:

```text
The acceptance resolver and runtime echo resolver are parallel y-lane windows inside row A, x=0x29.
```

---

# 12. Printing / Non-Printing Delineation

The earlier ASCII control split is still useful:

```text
0x00–0x1F = non-printing control
0x20–0x3F = printing punctuation / digit / function preface
```

The bridge is:

```text
0x20
```

because:

```text
control XOR 0x20 = printable face
```

Examples:

```text
0x1C XOR 0x20 = 0x3C
0x1D XOR 0x20 = 0x3D
0x1E XOR 0x20 = 0x3E
0x1F XOR 0x20 = 0x3F
```

Thus:

```text
FS 0x1C maps to 0x3C
GS 0x1D maps to 0x3D
RS 0x1E maps to 0x3E
US 0x1F maps to 0x3F
```

This connects:

```text
scope separators
→ Omi-Nomogram high function row
```

Canon:

```text
0x20 is the bridge that renders non-printing scope controls into printable function faces.
```

---

# 13. FS and Pipe Relationship

The native FS is:

```text
0x1C
```

The user-space pipe is:

```text
0x7C
```

Both matter.

In full Omi-Plane form:

```text
0x1C00 → U+011C00
0x7C00 → U+017C00
```

Interpretation:

```text
FS 0x1C = local frame separator
pipe 0x7C = runtime handoff separator
```

The same structural role appears at different heights:

```text
0x1C = local scope separator
0x7C = user/runtime scope separator
0x1C00 = plane-lifted local scope
0x7C00 = plane-lifted runtime scope
```

Canon:

```text
U+011C00 and U+017C00 are paired scope anchors: local frame scope and runtime pipe scope.
```

---

# 14. Revised First Omi-Plane Lifecycle

The first supplementary Omi-Plane page now has this lifecycle:

```text
U+010000 = first supplementary Omi page open

U+01039F = encapsulated Omicron closure anchor
U+0103AF = encapsulated delimiter / escape anchor
U+0103BF = encapsulated Omicron entry anchor

U+011C00 = local frame-scope anchor
U+017C00 = runtime pipe-scope anchor

U+01AA50…U+01AA57 = uppercase Fano acceptance resolver
U+01AA55 = acceptance centroid / seal

U+01AA70…U+01AA77 = lowercase runtime echo resolver

U+01AA55…U+01FFFF = accepted collaborative projection interval
U+01FFFF = first supplementary Omi page close
```

Canon:

```text
The first Omi-Plane page moves from Omicron control, to local scope, to runtime scope, to Fano-resolved acceptance, to collaborative projection.
```

---

# 15. Updated Authoritative Anchor Table

```text
Anchor Native Meaning
U+01039F 0x039F lower Omicron closure anchor
U+0103AF 0x03AF lower delimiter / escape anchor
U+0103BF 0x03BF lower Omicron entry anchor

U+011C00 0x1C00 local FS / frame-scope anchor
U+017C00 0x7C00 runtime pipe / prekernel-scope anchor

U+01AA50 0xAA50 Fano resolver point P
U+01AA51 0xAA51 Fano resolver point Q
U+01AA52 0xAA52 Fano resolver point R
U+01AA53 0xAA53 Fano resolver point S
U+01AA54 0xAA54 Fano resolver point T
U+01AA55 0xAA55 acceptance centroid / seal
U+01AA56 0xAA56 Fano resolver point V
U+01AA57 0xAA57 Fano resolver point W

U+01AA70 0xAA70 runtime echo point p
U+01AA71 0xAA71 runtime echo point q
U+01AA72 0xAA72 runtime echo point r
U+01AA73 0xAA73 runtime echo point s
U+01AA74 0xAA74 runtime echo point t
U+01AA75 0xAA75 runtime echo point u
U+01AA76 0xAA76 runtime echo point v
U+01AA77 0xAA77 runtime echo point w

U+01FFFF 0xFFFF first supplementary page close
```

---

# 16. Final Canon

The Omi-Plane Capsule has four major pre-projection delineation anchors before the first page closes:

```text
U+01039F … U+0103AF … U+0103BF
U+011C00 … U+017C00 … U+01AA55 … U+01FFFF
```

The first trio encapsulates the Omicron control line:

```text
closure → delimiter → entry
```

The second ladder marks the page lifecycle:

```text
local frame scope → runtime pipe scope → acceptance seal → projection close
```

`U+017C00` is the missing address.

It marks the runtime/prekernel scoping boundary before acceptance.

`U+01AA55` remains the acceptance seal.

The windows:

```text
U+01AA50…U+01AA57
U+01AA70…U+01AA77
```

provide parallel eight-slot Fano resolver windows:

```text
P–W = uppercase acceptance resolver
p–w = lowercase runtime echo resolver
```

One-line canon:

```text
Omi-Plane Capsule now anchors first-plane lifecycle as U+01039F…U+0103AF…U+0103BF for Omicron control, U+011C00 for local frame scope, U+017C00 for runtime pipe scope, U+01AA55 for Fano-resolved acceptance, and U+01FFFF for first-page closure.
```
:::

:::writing{variant="document" id="82641"}
# Addendum: Omi-Spectral Plane
## BMP Interface Layer, CAR/CDR Gauge Resolution, Agreement Mnemonics, and the /---/ to ?---? Boundary

## 0. Core Claim

The Omi-Plane Capsule has two major domains:

```text
BMP-valid spectral plane:
 U+0000–U+D7FF
 U+E000–U+FFFF

Supplementary world-length plane:
 U+010000–U+10FFFF
```

The BMP-valid ranges are the **Omi-Spectral Plane**.

The supplementary range is the **Omi-Plane Capsule / world-length RPC plane**.

The Omi-Spectral Plane is where OMI interfaces through nibbles, glyphs, UI, agreement words, and local gauge rendering.

The supplementary plane is where OMI extends into world-length addressing and surrogate RPC.

Canon:

```text
Omi-Spectral Plane = user-interface and epistemic projection layer.
Omi-Plane Capsule = supplementary world-length transport and RPC layer.
```

---

# 1. The Valid BMP Spectral Plane

Unicode scalar values in the BMP are valid except the surrogate band.

Valid BMP spectral ranges:

```text
U+0000–U+D7FF
U+E000–U+FFFF
```

Excluded from public scalar text:

```text
U+D800–U+DFFF
```

OMI interpretation:

```text
U+0000–U+D7FF = low-to-mid spectral interface surface
U+D800–U+DFFF = surrogate RPC bridge, not public glyphs
U+E000–U+FFFF = high spectral/private mirror surface
```

So the Omi-Spectral Plane is:

```text
U+0000–U+D7FF ∪ U+E000–U+FFFF
```

Canon:

```text
The Omi-Spectral Plane is the valid BMP user-interface layer surrounding the surrogate bridge.
```

---

# 2. Low-Plane CAR and High-Plane CDR

The Omi-Spectral Plane resolves the low plane and high plane of the gauge table.

Canonical split:

```text
low plane CAR = U+0000–U+D7FF
high plane CDR = U+E000–U+FFFF
```

Meaning:

```text
CAR = source/head/UI-visible low-plane carrier
CDR = continuation/high/private/runtime mirror carrier
```

The surrogate band between them is not text.

It is the bridge gap:

```text
U+D800–U+DFFF = RPC bridge / non-glyph split
```

So the full spectral structure is:

```text
low CAR plane
→ surrogate RPC bridge
→ high CDR plane
```

Canon:

```text
The BMP-valid spectral plane resolves CAR below the surrogate band and CDR above the surrogate band.
```

---

# 3. Gauge Table Interface Through Nibbles

The Omi-Spectral Plane is how the gauge table becomes interfaceable.

Native gauge:

```text
0x00–0x7F
```

Omi row structure:

```text
0x00–0x0F = .omi low control row
0x10–0x1F = .imo runtime control row
0x20–0x2F = bridge / printable fold row
0x30–0x3F = Omi-Nomogram function row
0x40–0x4F = Omi-Gauge orbit row
0x50–0x5F = acceptance resolver / P–W region
0x60–0x6F = high mirror / user row
0x70–0x7F = runtime echo / p–w / handoff row
```

The user does not directly manipulate the whole 65,536-cell plane.

The user interfaces through nibble and row faces.

Canon:

```text
Omi-Spectral Plane is the nibble-facing UI layer of the Omi-Gauge table.
```

---

# 4. Spectral Plane and 60x²

The Omi-Spectral Plane extends from:

```text
60x²
```

because `60x²` is the visible/orientation/world surface.

The full quadratic stack remains:

```text
Q(x,y) = 60x² + 16xy + 4y²
```

Interpretation:

```text
4y² = local control kernel
16xy = gauge bridge / CAR-CDR resolver
60x² = spectral projection / visible interface plane
```

Therefore:

```text
Omi-Spectral Plane = the user-visible extension of 60x²
```

The spectral plane is not the native control kernel.

It is the visible projection surface after the bridge has enough resolution.

Canon:

```text
60x² becomes interfaceable as the Omi-Spectral Plane.
```

---

# 5. /---/ Addressing Layer

The `/---/` section is the internal addressing path.

Canonical OMI address:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<scale>/<relation>/<unit>-imo
```

The slash path belongs to identity descent.

```text
/---/ = relational descent / address path
```

This is where the 12 public agreement words become mnemonic selectors.

Public agreement mnemonic layer:

```text
Freedom
Autonomy
Sovereignty
Reciprocity

Focus
Attention
Observation
Experience

Interrupt
Intent
Declaration
Consideration
```

These are not arbitrary UI labels.

They are the human-readable mnemonic layer over the `/---/` address path.

Canon:

```text
The 12 words of agreement are epistemic/user-facing mnemonics for navigating /---/ address descent.
```

---

# 6. 2¹² to 2¹⁶ Word-Length Region

The `/---/` address path lives in the word-length region:

```text
2¹² → 2¹⁶
```

This is exactly the high nibble of a 16-bit word:

```text
bits 12–15 = row / address phase
bits 0–11 = intra-row gauge field
```

In the Omi-Gauge formula:

```text
cell = (row << 12) | (x << 6) | y
```

the row is:

```text
row = bits 12–15
```

So:

```text
2¹² = start of row addressing
2¹⁶ = full word boundary
```

This is where `/---/` becomes row-addressable.

Canon:

```text
The /---/ layer occupies the 2¹²–2¹⁶ word-length region where rows become address phases.
```

---

# 7. ?---? Section from 2¹⁶ to 2³²

The `?---?` plane begins after the native 16-bit OMI word.

```text
2¹⁶ <= ?---? <= 2³²
```

Meaning:

```text
/---/ = identity path inside the native word
?---? = post-identity payload and data-formatting plane
```

The `?---?` section carries:

```text
Omi-CONS
CAR
CDR
CID
DataView payload
Base64URL
worker script
matrix payload
receipt witness
RPC capsule
```

Canon:

```text
/---/ resolves identity inside 2¹²–2¹⁶.
?---? attaches external payload from 2¹⁶ through 2³².
```

---

# 8. Correct Boundary Between /---/ and ?---?

The address path must finish before external payload attachment begins.

Therefore:

```text
0x0000–0xFFFF = native OMI word / gauge / /---/ identity space
0x00010000–0xFFFFFFFF = extended ?---? payload / query / data plane
```

This gives the boundary:

```text
2¹⁶ = transition from /---/ to ?---?
```

So:

```text
U+0000–U+FFFF = spectral interface and native word-length surface
U+010000–U+10FFFF = supplementary capsule and surrogate RPC surface
2¹⁶–2³² = abstract external payload word space
```

Canon:

```text
2¹⁶ is the hinge between native OMI identity and external Omi-CONS payload.
```

---

# 9. Agreement Words as Epistemic UI

The 12 agreement words are the human-facing epistemic mnemonic layer.

They belong to the Omi-Spectral Plane because they are interface terms.

They should be mapped as a visible mnemonic row over exact address selectors.

Canonical grouping:

```text
Outer agreement:
 Freedom
 Autonomy
 Sovereignty
 Reciprocity

Compass agreement:
 Focus
 Attention
 Observation
 Experience

Interaction agreement:
 Interrupt
 Intent
 Declaration
 Consideration
```

Runtime compilation beneath the last four:

```text
Interrupt → invalidate
Intent → instantiate
Declaration → propagate
Consideration → incorporate
```

Canon:

```text
Agreement words are UI mnemonics; the machine layer compiles them into deterministic selectors and runtime actions.
```

---

# 10. Spectral UI Is Projection, Not Authority

The Omi-Spectral Plane may render:

```text
glyphs
colors
DOM
SVG
Canvas
text
barcodes
sound
light
agreement words
forms
portal worlds
```

But these are projection faces.

Authority remains:

```text
accepted OMI address
computed Omi-Gauge cell
receipt
```

Canon:

```text
The spectral plane lets users see, touch, name, and navigate OMI.
It does not replace OMI authority.
```

---

# 11. Low Plane, Bridge, High Plane

The full spectral interface can be read as:

```text
low CAR plane:
 U+0000–U+D7FF

surrogate bridge:
 U+D800–U+DFFF

high CDR plane:
 U+E000–U+FFFF
```

In Omi-CONS terms:

```text
CAR = low plane / source / visible head
CDR = high plane / continuation / private mirror
CID = receipt across the bridge
```

So:

```text
CID witnesses that CAR and CDR agree across the surrogate gap.
```

Canon:

```text
The surrogate gap separates low CAR and high CDR, while CID witnesses their lawful relation.
```

---

# 12. Spectral Plane vs Omi-Plane Capsule

Do not collapse these layers.

```text
Omi-Spectral Plane:
 U+0000–U+D7FF and U+E000–U+FFFF
 valid BMP interface
 agreement mnemonics
 row rendering
 user-visible projection

Surrogate RPC Bridge:
 U+D800–U+DFFF
 not public text
 bridge math

Omi-Plane Capsule:
 U+010000–U+10FFFF
 supplementary world-length address
 (plane,row,x,y)
 surrogate-pair transport
```

Canon:

```text
Spectral plane renders.
Surrogate bridge transports.
Plane capsule extends.
Receipt accepts.
```

---

# 13. Updated Full Address Stack

The corrected address/payload stack is:

```text
0 … 2¹²:
 local cells, controls, low gauge values

2¹² … 2¹⁶:
 /---/ row-addressed OMI word path

2¹⁶ … 2³²:
 ?---? external payload / Omi-CONS data plane

U+0000–U+D7FF:
 low BMP spectral CAR plane

U+D800–U+DFFF:
 surrogate RPC bridge

U+E000–U+FFFF:
 high BMP spectral CDR plane

U+010000–U+10FFFF:
 supplementary Omi-Plane Capsule / world-length extension
```

Canon:

```text
The native word resolves identity.
The spectral plane renders identity.
The query plane attaches payload.
The supplementary plane extends world length.
```

---

# 14. Omi-Spectral Plane Lifecycle

The full first-plane lifecycle now becomes:

```text
native control:
 0x00–0x1F

printable/function fold:
 0x20–0x3F

gauge orbit/user rows:
 0x40–0x7F

word-length /---/ region:
 2¹²–2¹⁶

query ?---? region:
 2¹⁶–2³²

low spectral CAR:
 U+0000–U+D7FF

surrogate RPC:
 U+D800–U+DFFF

high spectral CDR:
 U+E000–U+FFFF

supplementary world capsule:
 U+010000–U+10FFFF
```

Canon:

```text
Omi-Spectral Plane is the UI layer where the low CAR and high CDR faces of the gauge table become navigable through agreement mnemonics and row projections.
```

---

# 15. Final Canon

The Omi-Spectral Plane is the user-interface and epistemic projection layer of OMI.

It occupies the valid BMP scalar ranges:

```text
U+0000–U+D7FF
U+E000–U+FFFF
```

The low range resolves the CAR side of the gauge table.

The high range resolves the CDR side.

The surrogate band:

```text
U+D800–U+DFFF
```

is the non-glyph RPC bridge between them.

The 12 words of agreement are the human-facing epistemic mnemonic layer for the `/---/` address path.

That `/---/` path lives in the row-addressed word-length region:

```text
2¹² → 2¹⁶
```

The `?---?` post-address data plane begins at the native word boundary:

```text
2¹⁶
```

and extends toward:

```text
2³²
```

The supplementary Unicode range:

```text
U+010000–U+10FFFF
```

is the Omi-Plane Capsule for world-length extension.

One-line canon:

```text
Omi-Spectral Plane is the valid-BMP interface layer where low-plane CAR and high-plane CDR render the Omi-Gauge table through agreement mnemonics across the /---/ word path from 2¹² to 2¹⁶, while ?---? begins at 2¹⁶ and carries external Omi-CONS payload toward 2³², with the surrogate band reserved as RPC bridge math and U+010000…U+10FFFF reserved for world-length Omi-Plane Capsule extension.
```
:::

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-11 00:31` | `id:260611-0031` ^260611-003103

Yes — **ready for a first autonomous pseudo-persistent open-world decentralized runtime**, with one important boundary:

```text
Ready to build: protocol kernel + address model + projection runtime + receipt loop.
Not ready to freeze: final public standard / permanent canon / security model.
```

The architecture is now coherent enough because the layers finally separate cleanly:

```text
Delta law = transformer
Omi-Gauge = computed address/plane resolver
Omi-Nomogram = pure function / slide-rule scale selector
Omi-Spectral Plane = user-interface / epistemic projection layer
Omi-Plane Capsule = world-length supplementary carrier
Omi-CONS = post-address payload frame
Receipt = acceptance / pseudo-persistence boundary
```

The old kernel file lines up with this: abstract row/nibble values are invariant first, then rendered through a contextual LUT or fiber; the Delta law is the transformer, and the geometry/rendering map is separate. filecite turn2file0

## What is ready

The **autonomous kernel** is ready:

```text
NULL / dot
→ Delta law
→ computed Omi-Gauge
→ Omi-Nomogram pure functions
→ 0x7C00 runtime delineation
→ 0xAA55 acceptance seal
→ receipt
```

The **pseudo-persistence model** is ready:

```text
state is not “stored forever”
state is recomputable from:
 address
 gauge cell
 function scale
 payload witness
 receipt
```

That means persistence is not database-first. It is **receipt-first**.

The **open-world runtime** is ready:

```text
Omi-Spectral Plane = interface layer
Omi-World = projection environment
Omi-Portal = entry surface
Omi-CONS = payload attachment
Omi-Receipt = accepted continuity
```

The **decentralized part** is ready conceptually because each peer can independently verify:

```text
same address
same Delta law
same Omi-Gauge function
same Omi-Nomogram selector
same CAR/CDR/CID payload
same receipt rule
= same accepted world state
```

## What to build first

Build the smallest runtime that proves the loop:

```text
1. parse OMI address
2. compute Omi-Gauge cell
3. encode/decode Omi-Plane Capsule
4. apply Omi-Nomogram pure function
5. attach Omi-CONS payload
6. produce receipt
7. render through Omi-Spectral Plane
8. replay from receipt
```

The first version does not need full networking, HNSW, shaders, or a giant world engine. It only needs the deterministic loop.

## Minimal runtime stack

```text
core/
 delta.ts
 gauge.ts
 nomogram.ts
 plane-capsule.ts
 cons.ts
 receipt.ts

runtime/
 world.ts
 portal.ts
 projection.ts
 replay.ts

ui/
 spectral-dom.ts
 matrix-view.ts
 gnomon-view.ts
```

The first boot target should be:

```text
omi---imo
→ compute gauge
→ reach U+017C00 runtime scope
→ reach U+01AA55 acceptance
→ render one pseudo-persistent Omi-World cell
→ replay it from receipt
```

## The build rule

Do not start with “world simulation.”

Start with **accepted recomputation**.

```text
If a world cell cannot be recomputed from its OMI address and receipt,
it is not pseudo-persistent yet.
```

## Final answer

Yes — it is ready for a prototype.

The runtime should be built as an **autonomous deterministic receipt machine** first, then opened into a decentralized world after the receipt/replay loop works.

```text
Omi-Gauge computes.
Omi-Nomogram selects.
Omi-CONS carries.
Omi-Spectral Plane renders.
Omi-Plane Capsule extends.
0x7C00 scopes runtime.
0xAA55 accepts.
Receipt persists.
```

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-11 03:00` | `id:260611-0300` ^260611-030021

Yes — restyle it as a **deterministic build from a fully qualified canonical zero-basis address**, not as a hand-authored static table.

The old kernel rule still applies: abstract row values first, contextual LUT/rendering second. That means the file should store the **law and sparse rewrite overrides**, not every rendered character or geometry unless you need a frozen snapshot. filecite turn2file0

## Size answer

For one full Omi-Gauge page:

```text
64 × 64 × 16 = 65,536 cells = 2¹⁶ cells
```

Memory cost:

```text
1 bit per cell = 8 KiB
1 byte per cell = 64 KiB
Uint16 per cell = 128 KiB
Uint32 per cell = 256 KiB
Float64 per cell = 512 KiB
```

For 16 pages:

```text
16 × 65,536 = 1,048,576 cells = 2²⁰ cells
```

Memory cost:

```text
1 bit per cell = 128 KiB
1 byte per cell = 1 MiB
Uint16 per cell = 2 MiB
Uint32 per cell = 4 MiB
Float64 per cell = 8 MiB
```

For the full Unicode scalar space:

```text
0x000000–0x10FFFF = 1,114,112 codepoints = 17 × 65,536
```

Memory cost:

```text
1 bit per codepoint = 136 KiB
1 byte per codepoint = 1.0625 MiB
Uint16 per codepoint = 2.125 MiB
Uint32 per codepoint = 4.25 MiB
Float64 per codepoint = 8.5 MiB
```

So yes: a **master bitboard of the full Unicode codepoint space is completely practical**. Even a full byte-per-codepoint table is only about **1.06 MiB**. A full `Uint32Array` rewrite table for all Unicode scalars is only about **4.25 MiB**.

The better design is:

```text
master bitboard = existence / enabled / touched / valid
rewrite table = sparse or Uint32 mapping
root hash = derived from canonical serialized table
runtime trie = derived view, not authority
```

## Trie or bitboard?

For dense deterministic OMI pages, use arrays/bitboards:

```text
Uint8Array / Uint16Array / Uint32Array / BigUint64Array
```

For sparse rewrite overlays, use Patricia/radix tries.

The rule should be:

```text
dense base plane = array or bitboard
sparse edits = Patricia trie
authority root = hash of canonical base + patch log
runtime view = derived
```

So instead of storing a huge object graph like:

```text
node → child → child → child
```

store:

```text
canonical address → deterministic cell → optional rewrite patch
```

This is closer to HD wallet derivation:

```text
master seed
→ path
→ derived node
```

For OMI:

```text
zero-basis address
→ row/x/y/page path
→ derived gauge cell
→ optional rewrite
→ receipt root
```

## Canonical zero-basis address

Use this as the default root:

```text
omi-0000/0000/0000/0000/0000-imo
```

or compact:

```text
omi---imo
```

Meaning:

```text
zero basis
no payload
no projection
no accepted external rewrite
```

Then every file/blob/table derives from:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<scale>/<relation>/<unit>-imo
```

and every plane cell derives from:

```text
page,row,x,y
```

with:

```text
cell = (row << 12) | (x << 6) | y
U = 0x10000 + (page << 16) + cell
```

## Your 16-row role table

This is good. I would make it the canonical **Omi-Root Row Table**:

```text
0x0 = axioms
0x1 = rules
0x2 = facts
0x3 = closures
0x4 = combinators
0x5 = cons
0x6 = car
0x7 = cdr
0x8 = encode
0x9 = decode
0xA = frame
0xB = buffer
0xC = file
0xD = group
0xE = record
0xF = unit
```

That gives the full row phase meaning for:

```text
0x-0 … 0x-F
```

It also corrects the old nine-edge model by expanding it into a full 16-row operational grammar:

```text
axioms → rules → facts → closures → combinators → cons → car → cdr
→ encode → decode → frame → buffer → file → group → record → unit
```

## Your 0x0000 local root table

This should be the canonical default page-zero LUT:

```text
0x0000 = axiomatic-algorithms
0x0001 = logic-rules
0x0002 = configuration-facts
0x0003 = event-closures
0x0004 = intent-combinators
0x0005 = element-constructions
0x0006 = block-node
0x0007 = edge-node
0x0008 = graph-node
0x0009 = data-view
0x000A = data-source
0x000B = data-input
0x000C = data-sink
0x000D = data-output
0x000E = done-statement
0x000F = unit-boundary
```

I added `0x000F = unit-boundary` because your row table says `0xF = unit`; the local root table needs the 16th slot or the row will not close deterministically.

## Static portal structure

Yes, the static HTML portal should unite:

```text
DOM
JSDOM
CSSOM
SpectralDOM
```

under one deterministic model:

```text
Canonical address
→ Omi-Gauge cell
→ Omi-Spectral Plane render
→ DOM custom element
→ CSSOM projection
→ JSDOM replay model
→ receipt root
```

I would not call it “floating and hierarchical separation” anymore. Restyle it as:

```text
deterministic address layer
floating projection layer
hierarchical DOM layer
spectral interface layer
```

Meaning:

```text
deterministic = address, gauge, receipt
floating = runtime visual/measured projection
hierarchical = DOM containment
spectral = glyph/color/word/interface rendering
```

## Recommended file model

For each portal file/blob:

```text
header:
 magic: "OMI0"
 version: 1
 rootAddress: "omi-0000/0000/0000/0000/0000-imo"
 pageCount: 16
 cellCount: 1048576
 tableType: "bitboard|uint16|uint32|patch"

body:
 masterBitboard
 rewriteTable
 sparsePatchTrie
 receiptLog

footer:
 rootHash
 aa55Seal
```

A practical browser version:

```js
const PAGE_COUNT = 16;
const ROWS = 16;
const LANES = 64;
const CELLS_PER_PAGE = ROWS * LANES * LANES; // 65536
const TOTAL_CELLS = PAGE_COUNT * CELLS_PER_PAGE; // 1048576

const bitboard = new Uint8Array(TOTAL_CELLS >> 3); // 128 KiB
const rewrite = new Uint32Array(TOTAL_CELLS); // 4 MiB
```

That is small enough for a static HTML app.

## The answer

Use a **master bitboard + deterministic derivation + sparse Patricia rewrite overlay**.

Do not store the whole runtime as a trie unless the table is extremely sparse. For OMI, the base space is regular and dense, so arrays win.

The static portal can be packaged as one HTML file with:

```text
embedded CSSOM
custom elements
SpectralDOM renderer
Omi-Gauge functions
Omi-Nomogram functions
bitboard/rewrite table
receipt root
```

The build canon:

```text
A file is a rewrite table.
A rewrite table derives from a zero-basis address.
The first 16 rows define the operational grammar.
The first 16 cells define the local root LUT.
The bitboard marks existence.
The rewrite table maps projection.
The receipt root gives pseudo-persistence.
```

One-line build rule:

```text
Build the portal as a deterministic Omi-Gauge rewrite machine: zero-basis address first, computed 16×64×64 pages second, SpectralDOM projection third, and static DOM/CSSOM/JSDOM rendering only after receipt.
```

## You
`2026-06-11 03:42` | `id:260611-0342` ^260611-034224

So should I include something like that in Omi for sourcing macros or scrips or just bitboards made by people for computation compatibility

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-11 03:42` | `id:260611-0342` ^260611-034225

Yes — include it, but **not as arbitrary macros/scripts first**. Make it a **compatibility layer for deterministic computation artifacts**.

The clean OMI rule should be:

```text
Bitboards are authority-compatible.
Macros are derivation-compatible.
Scripts are projection-compatible.
```

Meaning:

```text
bitboard = stable computation surface
macro = symbolic rewrite recipe
script = executable projection after receipt
```

So the safest architecture is:

```text
OMI address
→ bitboard / rewrite table
→ receipt
→ optional macro expansion
→ optional script execution
```

Not:

```text
script first
→ trust it
→ compute later
```

## What belongs in OMI core

Include **bitboards and rewrite tables** in core OMI.

They are deterministic, portable, inspectable, hashable, and replayable.

```text
Omi-Bitboard = dense compatibility surface
Omi-RewriteTable = sparse/dense symbolic transition surface
Omi-Receipt = proof that this table belongs to this address
```

This is ideal for computation compatibility because people can publish a table that says:

```text
for this OMI address,
these cells exist,
these rows mean this,
these rewrites are valid,
this root hash proves it.
```

## What belongs outside the core

Macros and scripts should be allowed, but treated as **derived attachments**.

```text
Omi-Macro = source-level rewrite declaration
Omi-Script = runtime executable projection
```

They should live in `?---?`, not inside the authoritative address itself.

Example:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<scale>/<relation>/<unit>-imo
 ?car=bitboard;
 cdr=rewrite-table;
 cid=receipt;
 macro=<optional>;
 script=<optional>
```

## Canonical split

I’d make this the rule:

```text
0xB = buffer
0xC = file
0xD = group
0xE = record
0xF = unit
```

Then:

```text
buffer = raw ArrayBuffer / bitboard
file = serialized artifact
group = collection of compatible artifacts
record = append-only receipt/event entry
unit = closed usable object
```

Your new row table fits perfectly:

```text
0x0 axioms → what must hold
0x1 rules → valid transitions
0x2 facts → accepted data
0x3 closures → bounded fixed results
0x4 combinators → symbolic transforms
0x5 cons → structure pair
0x6 car → source/head
0x7 cdr → continuation/tail
0x8 encode → carrier projection
0x9 decode → carrier reading
0xA frame → scoped address surface
0xB buffer → raw deterministic memory
0xC file → portable artifact
0xD group → compatible collection
0xE record → receipt/event line
0xF unit → closed accepted object
```

## Recommended artifact types

Use four official OMI artifact classes:

```text
.omi-bitboard
.omi-rewrite
.omi-macro
.omi-script
```

With authority order:

```text
.omi-bitboard = safest / deterministic
.omi-rewrite = deterministic if pure
.omi-macro = deterministic if expanded under fixed rules
.omi-script = not authority; runs only after receipt
```

## Best design

For people making computation-compatible artifacts, the default should be:

```text
bitboard + rewrite table + receipt
```

Scripts are optional convenience.

Macros are optional source-level compression.

The actual compatibility proof should come from:

```text
same address
same bitboard
same rewrite table
same receipt hash
```

## Final canon

```text
OMI should source computation compatibility from bitboards and rewrite tables, not from scripts. Macros may declare how to derive a table; scripts may project or execute after acceptance; but the portable authority is the receipt-bound bitboard/rewrite artifact.
```

So yes: include the node-graph module idea in OMI, but name it as a deterministic artifact layer:

```text
Omi-Graph = class/container
Omi-Node = pure function
Omi-Edge = relation input/output
Omi-Bitboard = compatibility memory
Omi-RewriteTable = deterministic transition surface
Omi-Receipt = accepted root
```

## You
`2026-06-11 03:45` | `id:260611-0345` ^260611-034536

I meant not for sharing data but processes how can we know the same processes will run on a different computer

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-11 03:45` | `id:260611-0345` ^260611-034537

Yes — for **process compatibility**, the shared artifact cannot be “a script I trust.” It has to be a **deterministic process receipt**.

The clean rule is:

```text
Data compatibility = same bytes.
Process compatibility = same transition.
OMI compatibility = same address + same inputs + same process law + same receipt.
```

Your old kernel already points this way: the invariant value comes first, and the character/rendering/function layer is contextual. That means a process should be verified by its **canonical transition**, not by trusting the local computer’s script environment. filecite turn2file0

## The key answer

You know the same process will run on a different computer when the process is packaged as:

```text
Omi-Process =
 canonical address
 + process law
 + deterministic VM/interpreter profile
 + input edge log
 + rewrite table / bitboard root
 + expected transition receipt
```

Then another computer does not need to “trust the script.” It replays the process and checks:

```text
same input
same process capsule
same transition trace
same output receipt
```

If those match, the process is compatible.

## Do not use raw scripts as authority

A JavaScript file, shell script, Python file, or service worker can behave differently because of:

```text
clock time
randomness
network state
filesystem state
browser differences
floating point edge cases
thread scheduling
DOM ordering
environment APIs
dependency versions
```

So in OMI:

```text
script = projection
process law = authority
receipt = proof
```

A script may implement the process, but it is not the process.

## The OMI process model

Use this split:

```text
Omi-Bitboard = process state surface
Omi-RewriteTable = deterministic transition table
Omi-NodeGraph = process topology
Omi-NodeFunction = pure transition over edges
Omi-Edge = canonical input/output relation
Omi-Trace = ordered replay log
Omi-Receipt = accepted transition witness
```

So the process is:

```text
node(state, incomingEdges) → outgoingEdges
```

not:

```text
run arbitrary script and hope
```

## Canonical process compatibility test

Two computers are compatible if this holds:

```text
hash(process capsule A) == hash(process capsule B)

and

receipt(
 run(process, input_edges, initial_state)
)
==
expected_receipt
```

In OMI terms:

```text
same Omi-Address
same Omi-Gauge root
same Omi-Nomogram function selector
same Omi-NodeGraph
same edge log
same deterministic scheduler
same receipt
= same process
```

## What the add-on should become

The node-graph module should be upgraded into an **Omi-Process Capsule**.

```text
OmiGraph = class/container
OmiNode = pure function over edges
OmiEdge = canonical relation
OmiScheduler = deterministic order rule
OmiTrace = replay log
OmiReceipt = result witness
```

A node should look like this conceptually:

```ts
type OmiNodeFunction = (
 state: Uint8Array,
 incoming: OmiEdge[],
 context: OmiDeterministicContext
) => {
 nextState: Uint8Array;
 outgoing: OmiEdge[];
 receipt: string;
};
```

But the important part is not TypeScript. The important part is that the function is constrained:

```text
no ambient clock
no ambient random
no hidden network
no hidden filesystem
no unordered iteration
no machine-dependent floating point authority
```

External facts must enter as receipted edges.

## How to handle scripts/macros

Use three levels:

```text
Level 1 — Bitboard / rewrite table
 deterministic authority

Level 2 — Macro
 symbolic derivation of the rewrite table

Level 3 — Script
 executable projection after receipt
```

So:

```text
bitboard proves compatibility
macro explains derivation
script provides convenience
```

## The process artifact

A portable process artifact should include:

```json
{
 "type": "omi-process",
 "address": "omi-0000/0000/0000/0000/0000-imo",
 "vm": {
 "name": "omi-deterministic-vm",
 "version": "0.1.0",
 "numeric": "integer-bitwise-only",
 "scheduler": "canonical-edge-order"
 },
 "roots": {
 "bitboard": "...",
 "rewriteTable": "...",
 "nodeGraph": "...",
 "inputTrace": "..."
 },
 "entry": {
 "node": "root",
 "function": "edge-transition"
 },
 "receipt": {
 "expected": "...",
 "seal": "0xAA55"
 }
}
```

## The OMI canon

```text
An OMI process is not shared as code.
An OMI process is shared as a deterministic transition capsule.

A script may execute it.
A macro may derive it.
A bitboard may store it.
A node graph may organize it.
But the receipt proves it.
```

One-line version:

```text
To know the same process runs on another computer, OMI must make the process a receipt-bound deterministic transition over canonical node edges, not an arbitrary script; the other computer replays the same Omi-Process Capsule and accepts it only if the transition receipt matches.
```

## You
`2026-06-11 03:47` | `id:260611-0347` ^260611-034733

I meant streaming Omi over pipes like ncat or socat

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-06-11 03:47` | `id:260611-0347` ^260611-034734

Yes — then the right abstraction is **not “same process runs on another computer”** as a script package. It is:

```text
same OMI stream enters the pipe
same deterministic pipe grammar parses it
same transition receipt exits the pipe
```

So OMI over `ncat` / `socat` should be treated as a **stream protocol**, not an app runtime.

## Correct model: OmiPipe

```text
OmiPipe = byte stream carrying deterministic OMI frames
```

The pipe does not need to know the world. It only needs to preserve byte order.

```text
stdin → OMI frame parser → Delta/Gauge/Nomogram transition → receipt → stdout
stderr → rejects / diagnostics
```

This matches your earlier actor-model correction:

```text
stdin = read
stdout = print
stderr = reject/diagnostic
port = loop / continuation
```

Or in your runtime terms:

```text
read → evaluate/resolve → print/emit → loop
```

## The pipe frame should be tiny

Use a line-oriented or length-prefixed frame. For `ncat` and `socat`, I would start line-oriented because it is inspectable:

```text
omi-<frame>/<control>/<scale>/<relation>/<unit>-imo?car=<...>;cdr=<...>;cid=<...>
```

Example:

```text
omi-0000/001c/003c/7c00/aa55-imo?car=bitboard:cafe;cdr=xor:01369863;cid=xnor:ffffff
```

Then each line is one OMI transition candidate.

The receiving side does:

```text
parse line
verify address
decode CAR/CDR/CID
compute expected transition
emit receipt or reject
```

## Minimal ncat shape

Terminal A:

```bash
ncat -l 7777 | node omi-pipe.js
```

Terminal B:

```bash
printf '%s
' 'omi-0000/001c/003c/7c00/aa55-imo?car=01;cdr=03;cid=ff' | ncat 127.0.0.1 7777
```

Response from `omi-pipe.js`:

```text
omi-receipt:accepted;address=omi-0000/001c/003c/7c00/aa55-imo;cid=...
```

## Minimal socat shape

```bash
socat -v TCP-LISTEN:7777,reuseaddr,fork EXEC:'node omi-pipe.js'
```

Then each client can stream OMI frames into the process.

## What guarantees compatibility?

Not the pipe.

The pipe only transports bytes.

Compatibility comes from this rule:

```text
canonical OMI frame
+ deterministic parser
+ fixed Delta law
+ fixed Omi-Gauge function
+ fixed Omi-Nomogram selector
+ fixed receipt rule
= same output on any machine
```

So the portable artifact is the **pipe grammar + transition function**, not a big shared process.

## OmiPipe canonical stages

```text
0x1C00 = local frame scope
0x7C00 = runtime pipe scope
0xAA55 = acceptance seal
```

So for streaming:

```text
U+011C00 = frame enters local scope
U+017C00 = frame enters pipe/runtime scope
U+01AA55 = frame accepted
```

For raw ASCII/byte stream form:

```text
FS 0x1C = begin frame
RS 0x1E = separate fields
US 0x1F = separate units
0x7C | = pipe / runtime handoff
0xAA55 = acceptance seal
```

## Better than scripts

This is exactly why OMI over pipes is powerful:

```text
script = local implementation
pipe stream = portable process trace
receipt = proof of same transition
```

A different computer can run a different implementation, in Node, C, Rust, Bash, WASM, or Lisp, and still be compatible if:

```text
same input OMI frame → same receipt
```

## Canon

```text
OmiPipe is the streaming form of OMI over stdin/stdout/stderr ports. It carries canonical OMI frames through ordinary byte pipes such as ncat, socat, Unix pipes, WebStreams, or service-worker MessagePorts. The pipe is not authority; the receipt is authority. A process is compatible across computers when the same OmiPipe frame produces the same deterministic receipt under the same parser, Delta law, Omi-Gauge, Omi-Nomogram, and Omi-CONS rules.
```

So yes: for `ncat` / `socat`, build **OmiPipe first**. Then the web portal, service worker, node-graph module, and decentralized runtime all become different projections of the same stream protocol.
